Evidence: Our automated method provided specificity, sensitivity and accuracy of 100% for the identification of complete genomes for all viral species and genotypes compared to the gold standard, a manual classification.

Our analysis shows that the classification results for the envelope sub-genomic region at the species and genotype level were similar to that obtained using whole-genome sequences and largely in agreement with the gold standard, a manual classification.
For DENV, most of the genotypes were classified with great accuracy (i.e. specificity and sensitivity > 99%) using the envelope gene.
The CHIKV sequences covering the E1 region were accurately classified for all three genotypes (i.e. 100% sensitivity and specificity).
17.Rationale: The passage demonstrates perfect performance for whole genomes but also shows that the ArboTyping method achieved highly accurate classification using sub-genomic regions—particularly the envelope gene for DENV and ZIKV and the CHIKV E1 region—often with specificity and sensitivity near or at 100%. Therefore the claim that accuracy is limited to whole-genome sequences and that sub-genomic performance is insufficient is contradicted by the Passage Body.

18.Option: The geographic expansion of the rabies outbreak to new areas, combined with the lack of timely and accurate information, has created confusion and reduced community engagement in mass dog vaccination campaigns.
Evidence: the number of rabid dogs in Arequipa continues to increase, and vaccination coverage has not only remained below target but has also declined in recent years [6–8].
which in turn contributed to an increase in rabies cases and a geographic expansion of the outbreak to new areas [17].
a lack of timely and accurate information about MDVC logistics, and insufficient planning and resources, further hindered effective rabies control efforts [8].
18.Rationale: While the passage documents the geographic expansion of the outbreak and separately documents lack of timely and accurate information about MDVC logistics, it does not present evidence that the geographic expansion, when considered alongside delayed or absent information, is the most direct systemic explanation for reduced community engagement. The text links expansion as an outcome of decreased MDVC activity and pandemic-related disruptions, but it emphasizes operational and communication failures (e.g., unclear sites, poor publicity, coordination problems) as proximate causes of low participation. Therefore, the specific causal claim that geographic expansion combined with delayed information directly created confusion and reduced engagement is not supported as the most direct explanation in the passage.

18.Option: The lack of trust in health authorities, fueled by misinformation from private veterinarians and conflicting messages about vaccine quality, undermines community confidence and reduces participation in mass dog vaccination campaigns.
Evidence: Community participants also reported being confused by private veterinarians who show up at the communities a day or two before the MDVC to vaccinate, thinking the private veterinarians are part of the MDVC, or being confused by their claims that government-subsidized vaccines are of poor quality.

"...a [private] veterinarian one day told me not to have my dog vaccinated at the MDVC because as it [vaccine] comes from the state, there is no guarantee [about its quality]. The veterinarian was giving the vaccine from a certified company and that’s why it’s not free." (Urban Community Member)
"I had to go and ask: ‘What do you do here, do you vaccinate?" (Urban Community Member)

Community members and the research field team expressed concerns about the accessibility and visibility of fixed vaccination points, noting that locations were often unclear, far from homes, or poorly communicated, hindering access.
18.Rationale: The passage explicitly describes instances where private veterinarians provided conflicting messages that questioned the quality of government-provided vaccines and caused confusion among community members about who was conducting vaccinations. These reports, together with evidence of poor visibility/identification of official vaccinators, support the epidemiological inference that diminished trust in health authorities—fueled by misinformation and mixed messages—undermines community confidence and can reduce participation in MDVCs. The inference is directly derivable from the documented confusion and cited quotes.

18.Option: The inconsistent location of fixed vaccination points, combined with poor communication about schedules and sites, significantly hindered community participation in mass dog vaccination campaigns in Arequipa.
Evidence: Among community members, the most discussed barrier was poor communication about the dates and locations of the MDVC.

Community members and the research field team expressed concerns about the accessibility and visibility of fixed vaccination points, noting that locations were often unclear, far from homes, or poorly communicated, hindering access.

"There is not enough publicity like before when they used to stop at every corner: ‘Don’t forget this Sunday!’ and the diffusion of the campaign was from Monday to Friday, so you already had the chance to have a plan: ‘Oh, this Sunday you have to take Rocky, right?’" (Urban Community Member)

"The lady who has come to vaccinate has gone to the 7A bus stop, behind a little tree. She was hiding there with her vaccines. Whenever they have come to vaccinate, they have always come here to the soccer field." (Periurban Community Member)
"The vaccine may be free, but it is so far that they [my neighbors] say... ‘I’d better pay my five soles here nearby and I don’t have to spend money on a cab’" (Urban Community Member)
18.Rationale: The passage repeatedly identifies poor communication about campaign dates/locations as the single most discussed barrier and documents that fixed vaccination points were often poorly visible, inconsistently located, or inconveniently placed. Those operational deficiencies, together with delayed or absent information, directly explain why community members failed to attend (e.g., arriving when teams were packing up, not knowing location, distance barriers). This inference is explicitly supported by multiple participant quotes and field notes, making Option 2 a correct derivation from the passage.

18.Option: The shortage of trained personnel and reliance on unqualified vaccinators has led to a decline in public confidence and reduced participation in mass dog vaccination campaigns.
Evidence: "Many of the vaccinators from the army did not know how to vaccinate or how to fill out the card or records; many had been replaced at the last minute by untrained people." (Urban Community Member)
"At the time of vaccination, the thermos lid was left open." (Research field Team)

"At the beginning, they do not tell you what they will give the dog. They do not tell you: ‘good afternoon/good morning, we are going to give him this medicine, it is called.... I don’t know - Barrovirus. It is to prevent this disease…’ So sometimes you are afraid to bring your dogs to them." (Urban Community Member)
18.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that vaccinators were sometimes untrained or unprepared and that these deficiencies led to community members feeling uncertain or afraid to bring their dogs. Such loss of confidence in the competence of vaccination teams is a plausible and directly supported explanation for reduced participation. Combined with reports of delayed or absent information (which further undermines confidence and clarity), the shortage of trained personnel is a systemic issue that can be derived from the passage as contributing to low MDVC turnout.

19.Option: Apigenin’s antileishmanial activity is due to its antioxidant properties, as it reduces oxidative stress in infected macrophages and prevents cellular damage.
Evidence: It has been shown that apigenin induces mitochondrial damage and the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) [16–20].
apigenin demonstrated to be non-cytotoxic to murine macrophages at a potent leishmanicidal concentration, with activity that was determined to be ROS-dependent.
19.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that apigenin induces mitochondrial damage and increases ROS, and that its leishmanicidal activity was ROS-dependent. These statements indicate a pro-oxidant mechanism rather than antioxidant activity that reduces oxidative stress. Therefore the claim that apigenin's effect is due to antioxidant properties is not supported by the reported findings.

19.Option: Apigenin induces autophagy in Leishmania-infected macrophages, which is associated with parasite death and contributes to its antileishmanial effect.
Evidence: Apigenin reduced the infection index in a concentration-dependent manner.
Leishmania-infected macrophages treated with apigenin exhibited an increase in double-membrane vesicles and myelin-like membrane inclusions, characteristic of autophagosomes.
19.Rationale: The study reports a concentration-dependent reduction in infection index (antileishmanial effect) and documents ultrastructural changes in treated infected macrophages—an increase in double-membrane vesicles and myelin-like inclusions—explicitly described as characteristic of autophagosomes. These findings support the inference that apigenin induces autophagy in infected macrophages and that this autophagic response is associated with the observed reduction in parasite burden.

19.Option: The antileishmanial effect of apigenin is mediated by the inhibition of mitochondrial membrane potential, as apigenin treatment led to a significant decrease in ΔΨm in infected macrophages.
Evidence: It has been shown that apigenin induces mitochondrial damage and the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) [16–20].

Determination of ΔΨm
Peritoneal macrophages (2 × 10^6 cells/mL) were allowed to adhere to black 96-well tissue culture plates for 1 h at 37°C in an atmosphere of 5% CO2. Non-adherent cells were removed by washing with RPMI-1640 medium. Next, the adherent macrophages were incubated with the indicated concentrations of apigenin (3 μM, 6 μM and 12 μM) for 72 h. Cells were harvested and resuspended in Hank's Balanced Salt Solution (HBSS) and incubated with JC-1 (10 μg/mL) for 30 min at 37°C in an atmosphere of 5% CO2. After washing twice with HBSS, fluorescence was measured spectrofluorometrically at 530 nm and 590 nm using an excitation wavelength of 480 nm. The ratio of values obtained at 590 nm and 530 nm was plotted as the relative ΔΨm.
19.Rationale: Although the methods describe measurement of mitochondrial membrane potential (ΔΨm) and the passage notes that apigenin can induce mitochondrial damage, the Passage Body does not present explicit results showing a significant decrease in ΔΨm after apigenin treatment. Therefore the specific causal claim that the antileishmanial effect is mediated by inhibition of ΔΨm cannot be derived from the presented text.

19.Option: Apigenin exerts its antileishmanial effect by directly inhibiting the growth of L. amazonensis promastigotes in the extracellular environment, as demonstrated by its activity in vitro.
Evidence: In this study, we describe the possible mechanism of action for apigenin, demonstrating that antileishmanial activity in vitro against intracellular amastigotes of L. amazonensis and in vivo, using a murine-model of cutaneous leishmaniasis.
L. amazonensis promastigotes were washed with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), counted using a Neubauer chamber and added to peritoneal macrophages at a multiplicity of infection (MOI) of 3.0.
19.Rationale: The in vitro experiments described target intracellular amastigotes within macrophages; promastigotes are mentioned only as the infective form used to establish infection. The passage does not provide data demonstrating direct inhibition of extracellular promastigote growth by apigenin. Thus the assertion that apigenin's effect is due to direct extracellular promastigote inhibition is not supported by the presented evidence.

19.Option: Apigenin's antileishmanial activity is primarily due to direct cytotoxicity against L. amazonensis amastigotes, as evidenced by its ability to reduce parasite load in vivo without affecting host cell viability.
Evidence: apigenin demonstrated to be non-cytotoxic to murine macrophages at a potent leishmanicidal concentration, with activity that was determined to be ROS-dependent.
Apigenin reduced the infection index in a concentration-dependent manner.

Apigenin treatment was also effective in a murine model of L. amazonensis infection, demonstrating oral bioavailability, as it decreased parasitic load without altering serological toxicology markers.
19.Rationale: While the passage shows that apigenin reduces parasite burden in vitro and in vivo and is not cytotoxic to macrophages, it also states that activity was ROS-dependent and documents autophagosomal features in treated infected macrophages. These findings point toward host-cell mediated mechanisms (ROS generation and induction of autophagy) rather than proving that apigenin's primary mechanism is direct cytotoxicity against amastigotes. The data do not directly demonstrate amastigote killing independent of host-cell processes, so the claim of primary direct cytotoxicity is not supported by the Passage Body.

20.Option: The absence of risk-pooling mechanisms in Nigeria to protect households from financial shocks that could arise from common healthcare expenditures mean that households and more importantly, poorer households are likely to experience a disproportionate burden of the disease.
Evidence: It is commonly a burden to affected households and results to significant reduction in household productivity [2].
Overall, the total cost of lost productivity was US$19.5 (Table 6).

Overall, only about 14% of respondents sought treatment in the one month preceding the survey, 16% in the one year preceding survey and more than half (60%) had never sought any treatment for their condition (Table 3).
20.Rationale: While the passage documents that onchocerciasis is a burden to households and provides quantified costs of lost productivity, it does not provide any information about the presence or absence of risk-pooling mechanisms (e.g., insurance, community funds) in Nigeria or this community. The option asserts the absence of such mechanisms and attributes disproportionate burden to that absence; that causal claim is not contained in the Passage Body. The cited quotes show household burden and costs but do not support the specific inference about lack of risk-pooling, so the conclusion cannot be derived solely from the passage.

20.Option: The reported average daily work loss (represented by total work absence) found in this study is less than those reported in earlier studies, suggesting that the disease is becoming less severe over time in the region.

However, the option goes beyond these descriptive comparisons to infer a temporal trend (disease becoming less severe over time) in the region. Such a causal, temporal inference cannot be drawn from a cross-sectional comparison of separate studies with different settings and methods; the passage provides no longitudinal data or evidence of temporal change in severity. Therefore the suggested conclusion is not supported by the Passage Body.

20.Option: The average monthly cost of productivity loss of US$19.5 from patients and caregivers could be considered a huge economic burden on the affected individuals and their families, largely subsistence farmers whose consumption depends largely on their productive ability.
Evidence: Subsistence farming provides employment for over 85% of the population in the community.
It is commonly a burden to affected households and results to significant reduction in household productivity [2].
Overall, the total cost of lost productivity was US$19.5 (Table 6).
Patients in the poorest quintile lost more productive days on average (9 days) compared to the richest who lost 7 days in a month.
20.Rationale: The passage quantifies the average monthly productivity loss (US$19.5) and explicitly states that most community members rely on subsistence farming (>85%), so household consumption depends on productive ability. The authors also characterize onchocerciasis as a burden that reduces household productivity. From these facts, it is a reasonable epidemiological inference from the Passage Body that the measured monthly productivity loss represents a substantial economic burden for affected subsistence-farming households. The option's wording 'could be considered' appropriately frames this as an interpretation grounded in the provided data and context.

20.Option: The economic burden of onchocerciasis on affected households in Southeast Nigeria is substantial, primarily due to lost productivity rather than direct treatment costs, and is disproportionately higher among the poorest socio-economic groups.
Evidence: Overall, the total cost of lost productivity was US$19.5 (Table 6).

Out of the 48 respondents that reported having sought treatment, the average monthly treatment cost per out-patient visit was US$ 14.0 with drug cost (US$ 10) contributing about 72% of total cost (Table 4).
Overall, about 28% patients reported they have had to miss work in the one month prior to the day of interview due to their onchocerciasis-related illness for an average of 3 days (Table 5).
Patients in the poorest quintile lost more productive days on average (9 days) compared to the richest who lost 7 days in a month.
There were no statistically significant differences in the results (Table 5).
20.Rationale: The Passage Body supports that the economic burden is measurable and that lost productivity (US$19.5) is an important component and that treatment costs for those who sought care averaged US$14 per outpatient visit. This evidence supports the claim that productivity losses are a major contributor to economic burden. However, the option asserts that the burden is 'primarily due to lost productivity rather than direct treatment costs' and that it is 'disproportionately higher among the poorest socio-economic groups.' In contrast, all four miRNAs in the cluster were abundant in adult worms, with females showing higher levels of each miRNA compared to male worms

qRT-PCR showed that hco-miR-5352 could be detected in the ES products and female worm extract but not in gut tissue, consistent with microarray data.
21.Rationale: The passage states hco-miR-5352 is low/absent in dissected adult female gut tissue yet abundant in whole adult females, with higher levels in females than males. These facts together support the epidemiological/biological inference that the miRNA's source is not gut tissue but rather tissues associated with reproduction and eggs, and that it is released in or with eggs/embryos. 21.Option: The high similarity between the miR-5352 cluster sequences in H. polygyrus and T. circumcincta suggests that these parasites may have acquired the cluster through horizontal gene transfer from a common ancestor.

The option's phrasing ('horizontal gene transfer from a common ancestor') is internally contradictory (acquisition from a common ancestor is vertical inheritance, not HGT) and the Passage gives no evidence of HGT. 21.Option: The detection of hco-miR-5352 in the draining lymph node but not in the abomasal tissue indicates that the miRNA is actively transported through the lymphatic system to exert immunomodulatory effects.

exposure of mouse cells to exosomes resulted in downregulation of host genes important for immune responses, such as Dusp1 and il-33r. Those data provided the first example that secreted parasitic nematode miRNAs have the potential to regulate host immunity [9].

Evidence: qRT-PCR showed that hco-miR-5352 could be detected in the ES products and female worm extract but not in gut tissue, consistent with microarray data.
miR-5352 could be detected in eggs/L1 by qRT-PCR at a similar level to that of the adult female (Fig 3B).
For abomasal tissue samples and particularly the lymph node, hco-miR-5352 could be detected at a greater level in infected relative to uninfected animals (P<0.0001)...
the signal from the draining lymph node in infected sheep was significantly higher than that from the abomasal tissue (P = 1.59E-05).

22.Option: The use of zero-inflated models such as ZINB is inadequate for helminth infection data because they fail to account for over-dispersion, as demonstrated by the residual evidence of over-dispersion in the data.
Evidence: Zero-inflated negative binomial (ZINB) models assume that the number of worms among those who are at risk of infection has a negative binomial distribution [6].

The AIC further showed a difference of 10,700 for the NBLH compared to the Poisson and a difference of 19 comparing NBLH with ZINB, thus NBLH is superior among all competing models.
22.Rationale: The passage distinguishes between ZIP (zero-inflated Poisson) models, which were found inadequate because they left over-dispersion, and ZINB models, which by construction include a negative binomial component to handle over-dispersion. The study found the NBLH had better AIC than ZINB, but that does not support the categorical claim that ZINB 'fails to account for over-dispersion'—indeed, ZINB explicitly models over-dispersion. The correct, evidence-based statement is that NBLH fit the study data better than ZINB in this application, not that ZINB inherently fails to handle over-dispersion.

However, these observations do not support the strong conclusion that the NBLH is 'not suitable' for geospatial analysis or that it is 'unable' to capture spatial dependence. The study simply assessed residuals spatially (variogram and kriging) and reported spatial structure; it does not claim that NBLH cannot be used for geospatial modeling nor does it compare spatially-explicit versions of the model. Thus the negative claim about suitability is not derivable from the presented evidence.

22.Option: The negative binomial logit hurdle model is superior to all other models because it uniquely accounts for both over-dispersion and zero inflation through a single unified process, making it the only model capable of accurately estimating infection intensity.
Evidence: the negative binomial hurdle model, which allows for over-dispersion and accommodates the presence of excess zeros through a two-part model has a natural epidemiological interpretation within the case study considered here.

The paper does not claim uniqueness or exclusivity; it compares a set of models and finds NBLH superior for these data. Moreover, NBLH is a two-part model rather than a single unified stochastic process, so the wording is inaccurate and the absolute claim is not supported.

Evidence: This paper demonstrates the applications of hurdle models to helminth epidemiology (S. haematobium) and encourage its wider application in helminth disease control programmes.

hurdle models are robust when over-dispersion is present.

Since NB logit hurdle model offered the best fit to zero inflated helminth data in terms of the AIC (minimum value for all the models fitted) as well as true zero count capturing, it therefore became a natural choice for fitting a final model to model helminth infection intensity and determination of factors that foster infections.

it was expected to observe a disagreement between serological tests and qPCR when serological tests are positive and qPCR is negative, since it is known in the literature that serological tests tend to show negative results later than parasitological tests.

Because the passage contains no direct evidence that post-treatment qPCR positivity reflects only DNA from lysed parasites, the specific causal inference in this option cannot be derived solely from the provided text.

Therefore the stronger causal claim in this option cannot be derived from the provided material.

Simultaneous negative seroconversion of both tests (ELISA and IFAT) ... reached 41.9% (26.5–57.3) of patients at 30 months after treatment (Fig. 3).

The high immunity inferred by the model was maintained through annual flare-ups and constant environmental exposure.

It further notes that vaccinating many people in this context will often result in vaccinating already-immune individuals, reducing marginal impact. These statements support the inference that vaccination impact may be limited under those endemic, environmentally-driven conditions.

Evidence: Our model suggested that vaccination campaigns with small target population sizes would have a limited impact in populations with high immunity (Fig 3C). However, the timing of a pulse of vaccination could substantially influence the impact of vaccination campaigns.

Thus the specific claim that campaigns are most effective if timed to peaks of environmental growth is not supported by the text; the text supports pre-outbreak (low-immunity) timing instead.

27.Option: The modest impact of the intervention in Kalemie was primarily due to the limited scale and incremental implementation of the WASH improvements, which prevented a significant reduction in environmental exposure.

Our model suggested that vaccination campaigns with small target population sizes would have a limited impact in populations with high immunity ... However, the high level of local immunity would result in vaccinating a large proportion of immune individuals, reducing the impact of the vaccination.
We estimated that removing environmental exposure or contamination would have a critical impact on cholera dynamics. These strategies avoided 142,518 cases ... and 134,373 cases ..., respectively.

The passage does not present direct evidence that incremental WASH implementation was the primary reason for modest impact, so this causal claim is not fully supported.

28.Option: The environmental niche models predicted that regions in the Blue Ridge, Mississippi Alluvial Plain, and Southern Florida Coastal Plain are least suitable for human-tick encounters, which aligns with the observed absence of ticks in these areas.
Evidence: Only one model for I. scapularis had an AUC value greater than 0.8 (Table 2). However, e-cadherin aggregated into clusters and was engulfed inside the cells. After 60 min, immunofluorescence showed that claudin 1, e-cadherin, occludin and zo-1 entered the nucleus of the cells, the fluorescence of claudin 1 had disappeared in some cells, the fluorescence of e-cadherin, occludin and zo-1 was still present, and occludin and zo-1 also started to cluster (Fig 7A).

The rTsCatL2-treated group showed bright green fluorescence and cells underwent autophagy; ... Transmission electron microscopy revealed the presence of autophagic vesicles and autophagic lysosomes in Caco-2 cells after incubation with rTsCatL2 (Fig 10B).
32.Rationale: The passage documents that rTsCatL2 exposure causes Caco-2 cells to shrink and lose tight junction integrity, that multiple junctional proteins are internalized (and claudin 1 protein levels substantially decline), and that rTsCatL2 induces autophagy (MDC staining and TEM). These combined cellular effects are consistent with disruption of the intestinal epithelial barrier, so this inference is directly supported by the experimental observations reported in the study.

32.Option: The binding of rTsCatL2 to the cell periphery and its subsequent internalization into the cytoplasm and nucleus indicate that the protein functions as a transcriptional regulator in host cells.
Evidence: rTsCatL2 bound mainly around the cell periphery at the beginning and gradually entered the cytoplasm and nucleus with increasing incubation time; when MBP and E64-treated rTsCatL2 was added to Caco-2 cells, faint fluorescence of Caco-2 cells was detected; whereas no fluorescence was seen in the heat-treated rTsCatL2 group and the PBS group (Fig 6).

After 60 min, immunofluorescence showed that claudin 1, e-cadherin, occludin and zo-1 entered the nucleus of the cells, the fluorescence of claudin 1 had disappeared in some cells, the fluorescence of e-cadherin, occludin and zo-1 was still present, and occludin and zo-1 also started to cluster (Fig 7A).
The result showed that rTsCatL2 interacts with laminin and collagen I but not with claudin 1, e-cadherin, occludin or fibronectin.
32.Rationale: While the study shows that rTsCatL2 binds at the cell periphery and can be detected inside cytoplasm and nucleus over time, there is no direct evidence presented that rTsCatL2 acts as a transcriptional regulator. The data instead emphasize protease activity, interaction with extracellular matrix proteins (laminin, collagen I), and effects on junctional protein localization and autophagy. The presence of the protein in the nucleus does not by itself demonstrate regulatory activity on host gene transcription, and no assays of host transcriptional regulation (e.g., reporter assays, changes in mRNA levels attributable to nuclear rTsCatL2) are reported. Therefore the specific claim that rTsCatL2 functions as a transcriptional regulator is not supported by the passage.

It also documents fear of needles as a driver of preference. While the passage notes that CL causes visible lesions and lifelong scars (indicating aesthetic/ stigma concerns exist), it does not state that aesthetic concerns are the primary driver of formulation preference. Therefore the claim that preferences are primarily driven by aesthetic concerns rather than convenience or burden is not supported by the provided text.

patients preferred oral and topical treatments due to the easier administration and being perceived as ‘fitting’ the disease.

From an epidemiological and product-development perspective, integrating these patient-reported preferences into TPPs is directly supported by the study's purpose and findings and reasonably inferred to improve design, effectiveness (by addressing adherence and acceptability), and accessibility (by addressing cost and logistical barriers).

However, the stronger assertion that 'patient preferences are often overridden by clinical protocols' is not directly supported by the text. individual semi-structured interviews of about one-hour duration, related to their disease experiences, preferences and expectations, were conducted with 33 CL patients at four sites in Austria, Brazil and Colombia.

34.Option: The simulation of parasite development in the SMT suggests that P. vivax parasites spend an average of 25.3 hours as trophozoites and schizonts before termination of the assay, which may explain the variability in EC50 values across different isolates.
Evidence: the mean λ was 19.1 hours (95% CI 11, 29) for rings, 23.1 hours (95% CI 11, 36) for trophozoites, and 2.2 hours (95% CI 0, 7) for the schizonts.

There was a significant negative association between the CQ assay duration (hours) and the ln (EC50) values for P. vivax (r2 = 0.219, p<0.001; Figure 1a).
34.Rationale: While the passage does report mean durations for trophozoites (23.1 h) and schizonts (2.2 h) — which arithmetically sum to 25.3 h — the option frames this as the key methodological consideration and ascribes the variability in EC50 specifically to this summed duration. The passage does not state that the 25.3-hour sum itself is the methodological issue to be addressed; rather it highlights stage diversity and the presence of resistant trophozoites (and the proportions of stages and assay duration) as the confounding factors. Thus the numerical statement is derivable, but the asserted methodological conclusion (that this average 25.3 h is the explanatory/critical methodological factor) is not directly supported as the key consideration in the passage.

34.Option: Because P. falciparum isolates are more synchronous and predominantly contain ring stage parasites, the SMT is more reliable for P. falciparum than for P. vivax, and thus should be prioritized in drug susceptibility testing.

87.5% (189/216) of P. falciparum isolates contained 100% ring stage parasites at 0 hours, compared to only 2.8% (4/141) of P. vivax isolates.
34.Rationale: The passage does show that P. falciparum samples are more synchronous and predominantly ring stages and that SMT was originally developed for P. falciparum. The authors emphasize challenges in applying SMT to P. vivax (stage diversity/confounding) but do not recommend deprioritizing P. vivax testing; nor do they present direct evidence that SMT is inherently more reliable for P. falciparum in all contexts or that prioritization is appropriate. Therefore the recommended prioritization is not directly derivable from the Passage Body.

34.Option: The use of mixed-effects modelling in the dose-response analysis for CQ data ensures that inter-isolate variability is accounted for, which improves the accuracy of EC50 estimates in P. vivax isolates.
Evidence: The sigmoid Emax dose-response curve ... was fitted to all available data simultaneously, with mixed-effects modelling used for CQ data.

Therefore the option's claim — that mixed-effects modelling accounts for inter-isolate variability and thereby improves EC50 estimation accuracy — follows directly from the methods described.

35.Option: Although the over-expression of detoxification enzymes in resistant populations is often associated with gene copy number variations, regulatory variations in upstream regions of these genes also contribute to their up-regulation, as evidenced by allele frequency correlations with transcription levels and predicted effects on transcription factor binding.
Evidence: Comparing the transcription profile of detoxification genes over-transcribed in resistant populations with their copy number variations (CNVs) previously inferred from targeted DNA-seq revealed that most detoxification genes differentially expressed in resistant populations belong to genomic clusters affected by CNV variations associated with resistance [28]. Among them, seven showed a significant positive correlation between their transcription level and their CNV (Fig 2 and S1 Fig).

Because CNV did not fully explain the over-expression of all detoxification genes associated with resistance, their upstream regions were sequenced in order to identify promoter variations potentially associated with their up-regulation.

The sequencing of 1 Kb upstream regions of all detoxification genes using targeted DNA-seq identified 188 variations located upstream detoxification genes over-transcribed in resistant populations (S3 Table). Among them, 23 variations showed a significant correlation between their allele frequency and the transcription level of the downstream gene (r ≥ 0.7 and p ≤ 0.05).

Seven variations were potentially favoring the binding of transcription factors previously identified as enhancers of detoxification genes in resistant populations (Fig 3 and S4 Table). These included two variations likely to have a major effect on the binding of enhancers: the variation at position -642 (respective to ATG) affecting the gene CYP6P12-like AAEL014891 favoring the binding of the HNF-1A element and the variation at position -410 affecting the gene CYP6CB1-like AAEL009018 favoring the binding of the HNF-3 element.
35.Rationale: The passage reports that many over-expressed detoxification genes are located in CNV-affected genomic clusters and that seven genes show a significant positive correlation between expression and CNV, supporting a role for gene dosage. It also states that CNV did not fully explain over-expression, and targeted sequencing of 1 kb upstream identified 23 variations whose allele frequencies significantly correlated with downstream gene transcription; seven of these are predicted to alter transcription factor binding (including two with likely major effects). Together these statements provide direct evidence that both CNVs and regulatory upstream variations contribute to detox gene up-regulation in resistant populations.

Comparing the transcription profile of detoxification genes over-transcribed in resistant populations with their copy number variations (CNVs) previously inferred from targeted DNA-seq revealed that most detoxification genes differentially expressed in resistant populations belong to genomic clusters affected by CNV variations associated with resistance [28]. Among them, seven showed a significant positive correlation between their transcription level and their CNV (Fig 2 and S1 Fig).

Because CNV did not fully explain the over-expression of all detoxification genes associated with resistance, their upstream regions were sequenced in order to identify promoter variations potentially associated with their up-regulation.

Thus, although NS mutations are present and enriched, the evidence indicates multiple mechanisms (CNV, upstream regulatory changes, and some coding changes) contribute to resistance; the passage does not support the stronger claim that coding NS mutations are the primary cause of over-expression.

35.Option: The presence of kdr mutations in resistant populations is sufficient to explain the observed over-expression of detoxification enzymes, as these mutations are consistently found in all resistant strains and are known to confer pyrethroid resistance.
Evidence: Resistance of mosquitoes to pyrethroid insecticides can be the consequence of various mechanisms, such as non-synonymous mutations affecting the voltage-gated sodium channel targeted by these insecticides (knock down resistance mutations, known as kdr mutations), a lower insecticide penetration, its sequestration, and its biodegradation (metabolic resistance) [16, 17]. Knock down resistance mutations and metabolic resistance are known as the two main pyrethroid resistance mechanisms in mosquitoes.

While kdr mutations are well known in Ae. aegypti and can easily be tracked using PCR tools [18], molecular mechanisms underlying metabolic resistance are not yet fully understood with a limited set of robust molecular markers [16, 19].

Previous genotyping of kdr mutations revealed that resistant populations from South-America carried the V1016I and F1534C mutations at high frequencies (V1016I from 77 to 96% and F1534C from 75 to 100%) while the S989P mutation was not detected. Comparing the transcription profile of detoxification genes over-transcribed in resistant populations with their copy number variations (CNVs) previously inferred from targeted DNA-seq revealed that most detoxification genes differentially expressed in resistant populations belong to genomic clusters affected by CNV variations associated with resistance [28].
35.Rationale: The passage distinguishes kdr (target-site) mutations from metabolic resistance mechanisms and indicates both are main mechanisms of pyrethroid resistance. Conversely, the study focuses on genomic (CNV, upstream regulatory) and transcriptomic evidence explaining metabolic (detoxification) over-expression. Therefore, the claim that kdr presence is sufficient to explain detox gene over-expression is not supported by the passage.

Among them, seven showed a significant positive correlation between their transcription level and their CNV (Fig 2 and S1 Fig).

Because CNV did not fully explain the over-expression of all detoxification genes associated with resistance, their upstream regions were sequenced in order to identify promoter variations potentially associated with their up-regulation.

these studies highlighted many candidate genes over-expressed in resistant populations, some of them being later validated as encoding enzymes capable of metabolizing pyrethroids [20, 22]. Evidence: Because CNV did not fully explain the over-expression of all detoxification genes associated with resistance, their upstream regions were sequenced in order to identify promoter variations potentially associated with their up-regulation.

The sequencing of 1 Kb upstream regions of all detoxification genes using targeted DNA-seq identified 188 variations located upstream detoxification genes over-transcribed in resistant populations (S3 Table). Among them, 23 variations showed a significant correlation between their allele frequency and the transcription level of the downstream gene (r ≥ 0.7 and p ≤ 0.05).
Seven variations were potentially favoring the binding of transcription factors previously identified as enhancers of detoxification genes in resistant populations (Fig 3 and S4 Table).

Comparing the transcription profile of detoxification genes over-transcribed in resistant populations with their copy number variations (CNVs) previously inferred from targeted DNA-seq revealed that most detoxification genes differentially expressed in resistant populations belong to genomic clusters affected by CNV variations associated with resistance [28]. Among them, seven showed a significant positive correlation between their transcription level and their CNV (Fig 2 and S1 Fig).

36.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports that CCDC168 was the single gene upregulated across all three infection-related comparisons (infected vs uninfected; high vs uninfected; high vs low). That consistent upregulation across contrasts supports the epidemiological inference that CCDC168 is associated with S. mansoni infection status across intensities and therefore may have an important, putative role in the host response to infection. The option phrases this cautiously ('may play a central role'), which is consistent with the evidence of consistent differential expression rather than proof of mechanism.

36.Option: Children with low S. mansoni infection intensity exhibit a transcriptional profile that is intermediate between uninfected and high-intensity infected individuals, suggesting a dose-dependent response.
Evidence: No significant DEGs were identified among children with low S. mansoni infection intensity compared to the uninfected.
We identified 33 significant DEGs ... among individuals with high infection compared with those with low infection (HL) of which 30 (90.9%) were upregulated and 3 (9.1%) downregulated.
We identified 56 significant DEGs ... among the children with high S. mansoni infection compared to the uninfected ...
36.Rationale: The data show that low-intensity infected children did not have any significant DEGs when compared with uninfected children, i.e. their transcriptional profiles were not distinguishable from uninfected in this analysis. High-intensity infection, by contrast, shows many DEGs versus uninfected and versus low intensity. The absence of detectable differences between low and uninfected argues against an intermediate, dose-dependent transcriptional signature that lies between uninfected and high-intensity; instead, low-intensity appears similar to uninfected based on the reported results.

We identified 33 significant DEGs ... among individuals with high infection compared with those with low infection (HL) ...
Fourteen (14) significant DEGs (9 upregulated and 5 down regulated) were identified among the S. mansoni infected children compared with the uninfected.

This directly supports the inference that low-intensity S. mansoni infection did not produce a detectable transcriptional response in peripheral blood under the study's methods and thresholds.

36.Option: The absence of significant DEGs in the low infection vs. uninfected comparison may be due to the use of POC-CCA, which cannot differentiate between Schistosoma species and may have introduced misclassification bias.
Evidence: POC-CCA was used in the field to classify participants by infection intensity to select and collect blood samples that were sequenced for RNAseq.
Samples were later classified by infection intensity using CAA in the laboratory as described below.

Thus the primary classification for gene expression contrasts was based on CAA results, not solely on POC-CCA. The passage does not state that POC-CCA introduced species misclassification leading to the null LU result; therefore attributing the absence of DEGs to POC-CCA misclassification is not supported by the study methods as described.

36.Option: The low number of differentially expressed genes in S. mansoni infected children may be due to the parasite's ability to suppress inflammatory responses, leading to a relatively weak effect on the host transcriptome.
Evidence: We found that 14 genes were significant differentially expressed between infected and uninfected children of which 9 (64%) were upregulated and 5 (36%) were down regulated among infected individuals compared to uninfected (IU).

36.Rationale: While the data show relatively few DEGs overall and no DEGs in the low vs uninfected contrast, the passage provides no evidence or discussion attributing this pattern to parasite-mediated suppression of inflammatory responses. That explanation is speculative and requires mechanistic evidence not present in the passage. Therefore the claim that immune suppression by the parasite is the cause is not derivable from the provided data.

37.Option: The divergence between the Paraguayan and Argentinean T. trivittatus populations is due to recent climate change and urbanization, which altered venom composition and gene expression.
Evidence: Our study revealed significant toxinological and genetic divergence between the Paraguayan samples and T. trivittatus from Argentina, indicating that they probably comprise unique cryptic species.

Divergence time estimates (using calibration data from [41]) indicate that the T. trivittatus populations from northern Argentina and Paraguay diverged between the middle Miocene and early Pliocene (~15–5 Million years ago (Ma)).
The species’ range extends from central-northern and eastern Argentina to eastern Paraguay and southeast Brazil, and has been predicted to increase in response to ongoing global climate change [6].
37.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports that the populations diverged long ago (middle Miocene to early Pliocene, ~15–5 Ma), which contradicts the claim that divergence is due to recent climate change and urbanization. While the passage notes that the species’ range has been predicted to increase in response to ongoing global climate change, it does not attribute the observed genetic and toxinological divergence to recent climatic or urbanization processes. The timing provided in the phylogenetic analysis argues against a recent origin for the divergence.

37.Option: The Argentinean and Paraguayan populations of T. trivittatus are genetically identical but differ in venom composition due to environmental adaptation.
Evidence: Our study revealed significant toxinological and genetic divergence between the Paraguayan samples and T. trivittatus from Argentina, indicating that they probably comprise unique cryptic species.
Paraguayan T. trivittatus is 8.14% divergent (at the nucleotide level) from T. trivittatus from Argentina, and 8.97% from T. confluens.

Banding patterns in silver-stained SDS-PAGE gels differed between T. trivittatus venoms from Paraguay and Argentina, notably with proteins of 17, 19, 25, and 40 kDa being only present in the Argentinean venom, together with an intense 30-kDa component, and a fraction migrating around 10 kDa only found in the Paraguayan population (Fig 2, left panel).

Evidence: A comparison of these values (Paraguay: 4.16 ± 0.15, 95%CI: 3.95–5.01; Argentina: 3.97 ± 0.06, 95%CI: 3.86–4.16) rendered the difference nonsignificant (F = 1.71, p = 0.198), implying that T. trivittatus venoms from Paraguay and Argentina were similarly recognized by the anti-T. trivittatus (INPB) AV.

DNA of R. raoultii was detected in the ticks from all the different sources but its positive rate in ticks from the domestic animals (32.0%, 180/563) was significantly higher than that from the vegetative covers (10.5%, 76/723) (P = 91.42, P<0.05).

Therefore the claim that prevalence is primarily driven by transfusions and IDU is not supported by the provided evidence.

Together these data support the inference that both vertical (breastfeeding) and sexual transmission contribute to the observed prevalence, particularly among older generations.

Therefore the claim that the presence of both subtypes implies independent introductions instead of family transmission cannot be drawn solely from the passage.

Genetic distance between viral isolates of each family group was 0.0%.... In family 1, the viral isolate sequenced from the index case (OKSP1400) showed 100% identity with two of her sisters… All of them reported having been breastfed.

41.Rationale: The passage documents that being first- and second-generation Japanese immigrants is associated with infection, but it provides direct evidence for familial transmission routes (vertical via breastfeeding and sexual transmission within couples) and molecular identity within families. The passage contains no data or discussion linking environmental exposures (air pollution, urban living) to HTLV-1 risk. Thus attributing higher prevalence to environmental factors rather than family transmission is not supported by the presented evidence.

42.Option: Although PQ8W is well tolerated in most patients, significant falls in haemoglobin can occur after the first dose highlighting the importance of clinical monitoring to identify vulnerable patients with early signs of hemolysis, before clinical deterioration.
Evidence: Overall, 92% (46/50) patients completed all 8-doses of primaquine; the median total mg/kg dose administered was 6.0 mg/kg (range 4.3 − 7.8).

The Hb concentrations over time by G6PD status are shown in Fig 3. The median maximal fall in Hb was -2.0 g/dL (range -5.6 to 0) in the 25 confirmed hemizygous males, -2.0 g/dL (range -4.1 to -1.1) in the 5 confirmed heterozygous females, and -1.7 g/dL (range -3.7 to 0) in the 16 patients without confirmed G6PD deficiency.

The Hb concentration rose thereafter to exceed the median baseline Hb on D28 (Fig 2). The median fall in Hb was -1.5 g/dL (range -4.3 to 2.5) on D3 and -1.6 g/dL (-5.1 to 3.2) on D7.
Two patients had falls in Hb exceeding 5 g/dL (Fig 4).

42.Rationale: The passage documents that most patients completed the PQ8W regimen (92% completion), indicating overall tolerability, and that adverse GI symptoms were similar to controls. Simultaneously, it reports measurable and sometimes large falls in hemoglobin occurring early (median falls by D3 and D7, nadir at D7) and two individual falls >5 g/dL including one SAE with hospitalization for hemolysis-related symptoms. These statements together support the inference that while PQ8W is generally tolerated, significant Hb declines can occur early after initiating treatment, justifying clinical monitoring to detect early hemolysis and prevent deterioration.

42.Option: The 8-week primaquine regimen is ineffective in preventing relapses in patients with severe G6PD deficiency, as evidenced by the high rate of recurrent parasitaemia in patients with known G6PD variants.

42.Rationale: The passage reports only two recurrences among the 50 G6PDd patients over 12 months (12-month incidence risk 5.1%), and explicitly notes that recurrence rates were higher in G6PD normal patients in the main trial. Thus the data do not show a high rate of recurrent parasitaemia among G6PD-deficient patients treated with PQ8W and do not support the claim that PQ8W is ineffective in this group. The option's assertion of ineffectiveness and high recurrence is not supported by the presented outcomes.
