134.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports that groups with a history of eye worm (EW and EWMF) had significantly higher adjusted prevalence ratios for multiple symptoms compared to LN (no signs of loiasis), and it lists neurological manifestations including paresthesia and transient paralysis with their adjusted effect sizes. This directly supports the inference that history of eye worm, even without detectable microfilaremia, is associated with increased reporting of a range of clinical symptoms including neurological ones.

134.Option: Individuals with a history of eye worm migration are more likely to experience symptoms due to the presence of adult worms in the bloodstream, which can be detected through repeated blood sampling during peak migration periods.
Evidence: Adult L. loa filariae can live more than 15 years in their definitive hosts, continuously migrating through the soft tissue and reproducing sexually.
Female worms release microfilariae (mf) which can be found in the peripheral blood of patients, in sometimes very high numbers (up to 300,000 mf/mL) [1,3].
Two Giemsa-stained thick blood smears of 10 μL each were examined under a microscope and, if no mf were found, additional examination of 1 mL whole blood was performed after lysis and centrifugation.
134.Rationale: The passage describes adult L. loa as migrating through soft tissues (not residing in the bloodstream) and states that it is the microfilariae (mf) that are found in peripheral blood. While the methods mention enhanced blood examination to detect mf, nothing in the passage claims that symptoms are due to adult worms circulating in the bloodstream or that repeated blood sampling during peak migration detects adults. Therefore the causal and diagnostic claims in this option are not supported by the provided text.

134.Option: The clinical burden of loiasis is underestimated in endemic regions because individuals with microfilaremia are more likely to be diagnosed and treated, while those with only a history of eye worm are often overlooked despite reporting more severe symptoms.
Evidence: In comparison to LN, each of the two groups with history of eye worm (EW and EWMF) reported several signs and symptoms significantly more often.
The frequency of the reported manifestations was similar between MF and LN subjects.
Only few studies systematically evaluated the clinical picture of loiasis in long-term residents of endemic regions, not least due to the supposedly benign disease course [4,12–17].
134.Rationale: While the passage supports the factual premise that individuals with a history of eye worm report more symptoms and that MF subjects reported manifestations at similar frequency to LN, it contains no data or statements about diagnosis or treatment patterns (who is more likely to be diagnosed or treated) or about underestimation of clinical burden in practice. The claim that microfilaremic individuals are more likely to be diagnosed/treated and that EW-only individuals are overlooked is not derivable from the presented data and therefore is not supported.

134.Option: Microfilaremic individuals, particularly those with high or hyper-microfilaremia, are at increased risk for severe clinical manifestations such as Calabar swelling and urticaria, which are directly linked to elevated microfilarial density.
Evidence: The frequency of the reported manifestations was similar between MF and LN subjects.

Comparing the 3 LP categories among each other, EW and EWMF subjects reported some manifestations more frequently than MF individuals. This was the case for myalgia (1.79 [1.08–2.97] and 1.76 [1.04–2.99], respectively) and Calabar swelling-like manifestations (1.55 [1.00–2.40] and 2.27 [1.45–3.55]).

Comparing the EW and EWMF categories, patients in the EWMF group reported significantly fewer Calabar swelling-like manifestations (0.68 [0.51–0.91]), whereas patients in the EW category indicated more often urticaria (p = 0.062, 1.48 [0.97–2.24]).
HMF and HYMF individuals reported more often an episode of eye worm migration during the previous year compared to LMF (58.2% versus 35.5%; p-adj. = 0.003).
134.Rationale: The data do not support the assertion that microfilaremic individuals (including those with high or hyper-microfilaremia) have increased risk of Calabar swelling or urticaria linked directly to higher microfilarial density. In fact, Calabar swelling-like manifestations were more associated with eye worm history (EW/EWMF), and EWMF individuals reported fewer Calabar swelling-like manifestations compared with EW. MF subjects reported manifestations at similar frequency to LN. Thus the claimed direct link between elevated microfilarial density and those specific severe clinical manifestations is not supported by the passage.

134.Option: The presence of eosinophilia in loiasis is primarily driven by co-infection with Mansonella perstans, rather than by L. loa infection itself, as evidenced by the lack of significant differences in eosinophil counts between infection states after adjustment.
Evidence: LP had higher total WBC counts (median [inter quartile range]: 7.4 [5.9–8.9] vs. 6.7 [5.4–8.4] x103 leucocytes/μL, p-adj.<0.001), as well as higher absolute eosinophils/μL (1.1 [0.5–1.8] vs. 0.6 [0.25–1.3] x103, p-adj.<0.001) and relative eosinophil counts (15.0% [8.0–22.0] vs. 9.2% [4.1–17.0] of total WBCC, p-adj.<0.001) compared to LN.

Comparing all individuals with microfilaremia (MF and EWMF) to patients with history of eye worm only (EW), the two former categories were characterized by higher absolute eosinophil (1.3 [0.8–1.85] and 1.4 [0.8–2.0] vs. 0.8 [0.34–1.6] 103/μL; p-adj. = 0.076 and 0.003) and relative eosinophil counts (18.0% [11.0–23.0] and 17.0% [12.0–24.0] vs. 13.0% [6.0–21.0]; p-adj. = 0.016 and 0.002).

In summary, loiasis was associated with absolute and relative eosinophilia. Extent of eosinophilia was higher in microfilaremic individuals in comparison to only eye worm positives. The level of eosinophilia was associated with the quantity of L. loa microfilaremia.
All intergroup comparisons are adjusted to sex, age and Mansonella PCR.
134.Rationale: The passage shows that loiasis (LP) is associated with higher eosinophil counts compared to LN, that microfilaremic individuals have greater eosinophilia than eye-worm-only individuals, and that eosinophil level increases with microfilarial density. Analyses were adjusted for Mansonella PCR, indicating these eosinophilia findings are independent of Mansonella co-infection. Therefore the claim that eosinophilia is primarily driven by M. perstans co-infection and that there are no significant differences after adjustment is contradicted by the presented data.

135.Option: Intradermal immunization of the OSP:rTTHc conjugate vaccine induced OSP-specific IgA antibody secreting cells in intestinal lamina proprial tissue, suggesting that this route may elicit mucosal immune responses.
Evidence: We found significant IgA secreting cell responses (P<0.05) in the intestinal lamina propria of mice immunized intradermally with OSP:rTTHc in the presence or absence of alum compared with responses in mice receiving alum only (Fig 7).
We did not find this response in animals receiving OSP:rTTHc intramuscularly.
135.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports significant IgA-secreting cell responses in the intestinal lamina propria after intradermal immunization with OSP:rTTHc (with or without alum) and notes that this response was absent after intramuscular immunization. Induction of intestinal IgA-secreting cells is evidence of a mucosal immune response; therefore it is epidemiologically and immunologically reasonable to infer that intradermal administration may elicit mucosal immunity.

135.Option: The use of alum as an adjuvant significantly enhanced anti-OSP and anti-TT serum IgG responses in mice immunized with the OSP:rTTHc conjugate vaccine, regardless of the administration route.
Evidence: These responses were comparable at different doses and molar ratios of OSP to TThc, with no major increase afforded when the vaccine was administered in the presence of alum.
No antigen-specific responses were detected in mice immunized with alum alone.
135.Rationale: The passage states that serum IgG responses were comparable across doses and molar ratios and explicitly notes that there was "no major increase" when the vaccine was given with alum. Thus the data do not support the claim that alum significantly enhanced anti-OSP and anti-TT serum IgG responses regardless of route. The quoted sentences directly contradict the option's assertion.

135.Option: Intradermal immunization with the OSP:rTTHc conjugate vaccine resulted in higher systemic IgG responses compared to intramuscular immunization, indicating superior overall immunogenicity.
Evidence: We found a significant increase in OSP (Fig 3A) and tetanus (Fig 3B)-specific IgG responses in serum of mice who received Inaba OSP-rTTHc vaccine intramuscularly or intradermally.
These responses were comparable at different doses and molar ratios of OSP to TThc, with no major increase afforded when the vaccine was administered in the presence of alum.
Anti-TT responses were highest in mice immunized intradermally, with a significant boosting increase following the third immunization (Fig 3B).
135.Rationale: The passage indicates that systemic (serum) OSP- and TT-specific IgG responses increased after either intramuscular or intradermal immunization and that responses were generally comparable across cohorts. While anti-TT responses were noted to be highest with intradermal immunization, the overall data show comparable systemic IgG responses for OSP between routes. Therefore the blanket statement that intradermal immunization produced higher systemic IgG and superior overall immunogenicity is not supported by the reported results.

135.Option: The conjugation method using squaric acid chemistry enabled the induction of both systemic and mucosal immune responses regardless of the administration route.
Evidence: Conjugation of OSP to rTTHc was performed via squaric acid chemistry as previously described [16,19].
We found a significant increase in OSP (Fig 3A) and tetanus (Fig 3B)-specific IgG responses in serum of mice who received Inaba OSP-rTTHc vaccine intramuscularly or intradermally.

We found significant IgA secreting cell responses (P<0.05) in the intestinal lamina propria of mice immunized intradermally with OSP:rTTHc in the presence or absence of alum compared with responses in mice receiving alum only (Fig 7).
We did not find this response in animals receiving OSP:rTTHc intramuscularly.
135.Rationale: Although the vaccine was made using squaric acid chemistry and systemic IgG responses were induced, mucosal IgA responses were induced only after intradermal immunization and not after intramuscular immunization. The phrase "regardless of the administration route" is therefore not supported: mucosal responses were route-dependent in this study.

135.Option: The absence of IgA-secreting cell responses in the intestinal lamina propria after intramuscular immunization suggests that the vaccine is ineffective at inducing mucosal immunity regardless of route.
Evidence: We found significant IgA secreting cell responses (P<0.05) in the intestinal lamina propria of mice immunized intradermally with OSP:rTTHc in the presence or absence of alum compared with responses in mice receiving alum only (Fig 7).
We did not find this response in animals receiving OSP:rTTHc intramuscularly.
135.Rationale: The passage shows that while intramuscular immunization did not elicit intestinal IgA-secreting cells, intradermal immunization did. Therefore the absence of mucosal IgA after intramuscular dosing does not imply the vaccine is ineffective at inducing mucosal immunity regardless of route. The data indicate route-specific differences in mucosal induction rather than universal ineffectiveness.

136.Option: The greater allelic diversity observed in the Australian B. pseudomallei population compared to the Southeast Asian population suggests that the Australian strain is more virulent and responsible for higher mortality rates in clinical cases.
Evidence: Differences in mortality rates also differ greatly between the two endemic regions, with mortality rates of approximately 50% in Thailand compared with <20% in Australia [5].
The difference in mortality rates could reflect differences in virulence but is probably more likely to be due to differences in intensive care provisions between the two regions [6].
Since infections are most commonly acquired from the environment, genetic differentiation is expected to occur, leading to geographic substructure within the bacterial population.
136.Rationale: The passage does not state that Australian isolates have greater allelic diversity, nor does it attribute higher mortality to Australian strains. In fact, mortality is reported as higher in Thailand (~50%) than in Australia (<20%), and the authors explicitly state that mortality differences could reflect virulence but are "probably more likely to be due to differences in intensive care provisions." Thus the causal inference that Australian strains are more virulent and responsible for higher mortality is not supported by the passage.

136.Option: The use of Bayesian clustering algorithms like Structure and BAPS is justified because they can detect population structure even when recombination rates are low and genetic diversity is high, making them ideal for species with strong clonal inheritance.
Evidence: Due to relatively low levels of sequence diversity and high levels of lateral gene transfer among B. pseudomallei isolates [8], [11], sequence data from only seven genes are insufficient for robust phylogenetic discrimination [11], [12].
The program Structure [13] is a Bayesian-based clustering algorithm that has been used to infer population structure within genetically diverse bacteria such as Helicobacter pylori [14].
We used both BAPS and Structure results to assess population assignments [18].
136.Rationale: This option states the opposite of what the passage describes. The authors emphasize high levels of lateral gene transfer (i.e., high recombination) and relatively low sequence diversity as the reasons why traditional phylogenetic approaches (and limited MLST loci) are insufficient. They therefore apply Bayesian clustering methods (Structure, BAPS) to infer population structure in the face of recombination. The option's claim that these methods are justified when recombination is low and diversity high (and for species with strong clonal inheritance) is not supported by the passage.

136.Option: The high rate of recombination relative to mutation in B. pseudomallei necessitates the use of allele frequency-based methods, such as Structure and BAPS, for reliable population assignment, as traditional phylogenetic approaches are insufficient due to genetic mixing.
Evidence: Due to relatively low levels of sequence diversity and high levels of lateral gene transfer among B. pseudomallei isolates [8], [11], sequence data from only seven genes are insufficient for robust phylogenetic discrimination [11], [12].

Pearson et al. therefore used a population genetics approach to determine that B. pseudomallei STs form two distinct populations, conforming to the geographic regions of Southeast Asia and Australia [11].
The program Structure [13] is a Bayesian-based clustering algorithm that has been used to infer population structure within genetically diverse bacteria such as Helicobacter pylori [14].
We used both BAPS and Structure results to assess population assignments [18].
136.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that high levels of lateral gene transfer (recombination) and low sequence diversity make MLST-based phylogenetic discrimination unreliable. Consequently, the authors apply allele frequency / Bayesian clustering methods (Structure, BAPS) and population genetics approaches to assign isolates to geographic populations. This reasoning directly supports the option's fundamental population-genetic principle.

136.Option: The identification of a fourth population by BAPS, consisting of three STs including a novel human isolate from the USA, suggests that B. pseudomallei has undergone recent and widespread global dispersal beyond its endemic regions.
Evidence: The number of populations supported by Structure and BAPS are two and three respectively.

The data used to define populations and evaluate the robustness of population assignments were downloaded from the B. pseudomallei MLST database (http://bpseudomallei.mlst.net/) on January 15th, 2009. The database consisted of 641 B. pseudomallei STs from 1802 isolates collected over 89 years from 35 countries.
136.Rationale: The passage reports that Structure supported two populations and BAPS supported three; there is no mention of a fourth population or a novel human USA isolate. While isolates originate from many countries, the authors do not claim identification of an additional population implying recent widespread global dispersal. Therefore this specific inference is not supported by the passage.

136.Option: The high rate of recombination in B. pseudomallei leads to frequent misclassification of isolates into incorrect populations, which undermines the reliability of both Structure and BAPS for epidemiological attribution.
Evidence: Due to relatively low levels of sequence diversity and high levels of lateral gene transfer among B. pseudomallei isolates [8], [11], sequence data from only seven genes are insufficient for robust phylogenetic discrimination [11], [12].

As there is no empirical measure of determining the accuracy of population assignments, we further assessed Structure and BAPS assignments of B. pseudomallei using MLST data, by comparing individual ST assignments made by Structure and BAPS to the geographic information listed in the MLST database and to the likelihood of assignment into each population as calculated by Genetic Analysis in Excel (GenAlEx) v.6 [23].

To be conservative in our assignment of STs to a population, we suggest that a ST only be considered to be from a given population if Structure or BAPS assigned it to that population ≥95% of the time.
136.Rationale: Although the passage documents high recombination and acknowledges limits of phylogenetic approaches, it does not claim that recombination causes frequent misclassification that undermines the reliability of Structure and BAPS. Instead, the authors explicitly evaluate and compare these methods, use conservative thresholds (≥95%) for assignments, and present them as useful tools. The passage does not support the strong claim that recombination leads to frequent misclassification that invalidates these methods.

137.Option: The presence of multiple PvDBP copies in Ethiopian P. vivax isolates is a direct result of intense transmission and high rates of polyclonal infections, which promote gene duplication through genetic drift.
Evidence: P. vivax infections are also much more likely to contain multiple parasite strains in areas where transmission is intense and/or relapse is common [10, 15–18].

The moimix analyses showed that 24 of the 44 isolates from Ethiopia had Fws values of more than or equal to 0.90 with the highest values in 10 samples from Jimma, five samples from Badowacho, five samples from Arbaminch, two samples from Halaba, and the remaining two samples from Hawassa (S3 Table).

Eight of the isolates from Ethiopia were shown to have low Fws values ranging from 0.4 to 0.7. They included isolates from Arbaminch (Fws value = 0.423) followed by a isolates from Jimma (Fws value = 0.512), indicative of the presence of more than one clone within the sample (S3 Table).

Though fewer erythrocyte binding genes were detected with copy number variation in the isolates from Ethiopia, the range of copy number e.g., in the DBP1 gene was higher in isolates from Ethiopia than in isolates from Southeast Asia (S5 Table).
137.Rationale: The option claims DBP1 multiplicity in Ethiopia is a direct result of intense transmission and high polyclonality. The passage links high polyclonality to intense transmission in general, but the empirical data from Ethiopia show many samples are monoclonal (Fws >= 0.90) with only a minority showing low Fws. The passage also explicitly notes that fewer erythrocyte binding genes had CNV in Ethiopia (although DBP1 copy range was larger), which does not support a conclusion that intense transmission and high polyclonality are the proximate causes of DBP1 duplication in this dataset. Thus the causal claim (direct result of intense transmission/high polyclonality via genetic drift) is not supported by the passage.

137.Option: The phylogenetic clustering of P. vivax isolates with multiple PvDBP copies in Ethiopia suggests that gene duplication events occurred only once in the evolutionary history of the parasite population.
Evidence: A maximum likelihood tree constructed based on the whole genome sequences showed an admixture of P. vivax isolates with single and multiple PvDBP copy number (Fig 6A).

No distinct clusters were observed between isolates with single and multiple PvDBP and PvMSP3 genes. These patterns suggest that these gene regions could have expanded multiply among samples at different locations.
137.Rationale: The passage describes an admixture of isolates with single and multiple PvDBP copies and explicitly states that no distinct clustering by copy number was observed and that these gene regions "could have expanded multiply among samples at different locations." This directly contradicts the claim that duplication events occurred only once. Therefore the single-origin inference is not supported; the passage points toward multiple independent expansion events.

137.Option: The expansion of the DBP1 gene in P. vivax isolates from Ethiopia is likely due to selection pressure from antimalarial drugs, as evidenced by the high copy number of resistance genes in the same population.
Evidence: In Ethiopia, the chloroquine resistance transporter gene (Pvcrt) of P. vivax on chromosome 14 had been shown with significant selection in a region upstream of the promotor, highlighting the ability of P. vivax to rapidly evolve in response to control measures [20].

Among the 43 erythrocyte binding genes, duplications were observed in DBP1 on chromosome 6 and MSP3.11 on chromosome 10. DBP1 ranged from one to as high as five copies, and MSP3.11 ranged from one to as high as three copies among our samples (Fig 5), consistent with previous findings [19, 20, 79].
The remaining erythrocyte binding genes were detected with a single copy across our samples.

Annotation of these genes can be found in S4 Table. Among them, PvDBP1 (PVP01_0623800) and PvMSP3.11 (PVP01_1030900) were associated with erythrocyte invasion. Other genes that were found to have high-order copy number were yet to be described exported plasmodium proteins.
137.Rationale: While the passage notes prior evidence of selection in a chloroquine resistance gene (Pvcrt) in Ethiopia, the present study's CNV findings identify duplications primarily in erythrocyte-invasion genes (DBP1 and MSP3.11). The passage does not report high copy number of known antimalarial resistance genes in these samples, nor does it present evidence linking DBP1 expansion to drug selection. Therefore the specific causal claim that DBP1 expansion is due to antimalarial drug selection (supported by co-occurring resistance gene CNV) is not supported by the data in the passage.

137.Option: The high level of genetic diversity in erythrocyte binding genes among P. vivax isolates from Ethiopia is primarily driven by frequent recombination and gene conversion events between paralogous genes.
Evidence: Recent genomic studies have indicated that some highly polymorphic genes in the P. vivax genome are associated with red blood cell invasion and immune evasion [10, 12, 19, 23].
Based on PAML, 17 out of the 43 erythrocyte binding genes showed evidence of positive selection (Table 2; S4 Table).
These patterns suggest that these gene regions could have expanded multiply among samples at different locations.

Though fewer erythrocyte binding genes were detected with copy number variation in the isolates from Ethiopia, the range of copy number e.g., in the DBP1 gene was higher in isolates from Ethiopia than in isolates from Southeast Asia (S5 Table).
137.Rationale: The passage documents polymorphism and reports signals of positive selection in many erythrocyte binding genes, as well as gene duplications. However, it does not present empirical evidence that frequent recombination or gene conversion between paralogs is the primary driver of diversity in Ethiopia. Because the passage emphasizes selection signals and multiple independent expansions, but does not measure recombination/gene conversion rates or attribute diversity primarily to them, the specific causal statement that recombination/gene conversion is the primary driver cannot be derived from the passage.

137.Option: Despite lower overall transmission intensity in Ethiopia compared to Southeast Asia, P. vivax isolates from Ethiopia exhibit higher copy number variation in the DBP1 gene, suggesting that host-parasite interactions, particularly related to Duffy phenotype diversity, may drive gene expansion rather than transmission intensity.
Evidence: Compared to Southeast Asia and South America where P. vivax is highly endemic, data on polymorphisms in erythrocyte binding gene candidates of P. vivax from Africa is limited.

The moimix analyses showed that 24 of the 44 isolates from Ethiopia had Fws values of more than or equal to 0.90 ... Eight of the isolates from Ethiopia were shown to have low Fws values ranging from 0.4 to 0.7.

Though fewer erythrocyte binding genes were detected with copy number variation in the isolates from Ethiopia, the range of copy number e.g., in the DBP1 gene was higher in isolates from Ethiopia than in isolates from Southeast Asia (S5 Table).

It was previously thought that most African populations were immune to P. vivax infections due to the absence of DARC gene expression required for erythrocyte invasion. However, several recent reports have indicated the emergence and potential spread of P. vivax across Africa [32, 48–50].
Annotation of these genes can be found in S4 Table. Among them, PvDBP1 (PVP01_0623800) ... were associated with erythrocyte invasion.
137.Rationale: The passage establishes that Southeast Asia is a region of higher endemicity/genetic diversity, while many Ethiopian samples show high Fws (indicative of lower polyclonality), consistent with lower transmission intensity. Despite this, the DBP1 gene shows a larger range of copy number in Ethiopian isolates than in Southeast Asian isolates. The DBP1 gene encodes a Duffy-binding protein involved in erythrocyte invasion, and the text discusses Duffy-related host factors and emergence of P. vivax in Africa. Together these observations support the epidemiologic inference that host-parasite interactions (for example selection related to Duffy phenotype diversity) could be a plausible driver of DBP1 expansion in Ethiopia rather than transmission intensity alone. The option is worded cautiously ('may drive'), which aligns with the suggestive (but not definitive) evidence presented in the passage.

138.Option: The variation in drug efficacy estimates across diagnostic methods indicates that the WHO-recommended single Kato-Katz method is no longer valid for monitoring drug efficacy in STH control programs.
Evidence: To monitor the efficacy of anthelmintic drugs against STHs, WHO currently recommends measuring the reduction in number of STH eggs excreted in stool after drug administration (egg reduction rate, ERR) using either a single Kato-Katz thick smear or the McMaster method [13].
In summary, a duplicate Kato-Katz provided drug efficacy significantly or marginally equivalent to a single Kato-Katz for the three STH species in all study sites.
For T. trichiura infections, ALB efficacy estimations varied significantly depending on the study site and the diagnostic method that was used (Table 4).
138.Rationale: The passage states that WHO currently recommends the single Kato-Katz and uses single Kato-Katz as the reference comparator. It also reports that a duplicate Kato-Katz provided drug efficacy estimates that were significantly or marginally equivalent to single Kato-Katz across species and sites. Although the passage documents variation in estimates for some species (notably T. trichiura) and across methods, it does not present evidence that single Kato-Katz is invalid. Therefore the strong conclusion that single Kato-Katz is 'no longer valid' is not supported by the passage.

138.Option: FECPAKG2 is the most reliable method for assessing drug efficacy due to its high agreement with single Kato-Katz in classifying individual responses, despite its lower sensitivity.
Evidence: FECPAKG2 was previously evaluated as being less sensitive than Kato-Katz, while qPCR was found to have superior sensitivity for all STH [16, 26, 39].

When applying FECPAKG2, drug efficacy could not be measured in 438 (32.3%) of the 1,354 individuals with STH infection, because of false negative results at baseline (A. lumbricoides: 148/441, T. trichiura: 135/456 and hookworms: 155/457).
For FECPAKG2 (κFleiss = 0.65, p = 0.01) there was a substantial agreement (0.61 ≤ κFleiss <0.81), for MiniFLOTAC (κFleiss = 0.60, p = 0.03) there was a moderate agreement (0.41 ≤ κFleiss <0.61).
For FECPAKG2, there was moderate agreement for A. lumbricoides (κFleiss = 0.55, p <0.001) and a fair agreement for the remaining STH (T. trichiura: 0.31; hookworm: 0.39, p <0.001).
138.Rationale: The passage documents that FECPAKG2 has substantially lower sensitivity with many false negatives (drug efficacy could not be measured in ~32.3% of infected individuals). Although FECPAKG2 shows at best 'substantial' overall agreement (κ=0.65) and lower agreement by species (moderate/fair), other methods (duplicate Kato-Katz and qPCR) show higher agreement (κ≥0.81). Thus the claim that FECPAKG2 is the most reliable is not supported by the data in the passage.

138.Option: The use of qPCR for monitoring drug efficacy is not recommended due to its high sensitivity, which leads to false positive results at follow-up and inflates the estimation of drug failure.
Evidence: qPCR was found to have superior sensitivity for all STH [16, 26, 39].

When applying qPCR, individual drug efficacy could not be measured in only 56 (4.1%) of the 1,354 individuals because of false negative results at baseline (top graphs of Figs 2–4: A. lumbricoides: 22/441, T. trichiura: 11/456 and hookworm: 23/456).

Overall, these types of cases were observed by at least one diagnostic method in 8% of the total number of cases (n = 1,354), but differences across diagnostic methods and STH were observed. They were more prevalent when the FECPAKG2 method was used (5.5% = 75/1,354). For the other diagnostic methods, the proportion of samples that were positive at follow-up but negative at baseline did not exceed 1.3% (single Kato-Katz: 1.2%; duplicate Kato-Katz: 1.3%; Mini-FLOTAC: 1.0%; qPCR: 1.2%),
For both duplicate Kato-Katz (κFleiss = 0.81, p <0.001) and qPCR (κFleiss = 0.84, p <0.001) there was almost a perfect agreement (κFleiss ≥0.8).
138.Rationale: The passage reports that qPCR has superior sensitivity and far fewer false negatives than FECPAKG2. It does not provide evidence that qPCR produces a problematic number of false positives at follow-up; in fact the proportion of cases positive at follow-up but negative at baseline for qPCR is reported as low (1.2%), similar to other methods. Additionally, qPCR showed almost perfect agreement with single Kato-Katz overall. Therefore the blanket recommendation against qPCR on the basis claimed is not supported by the passage.

138.Option: The efficacy of single ALB against STHs was lower in sites where drug pressure has been high, suggesting that anthelmintic resistance is likely emerging in Pemba (Tanzania) and other high-exposure areas.
Evidence: Based on the reported national coverage of drug administration to both PSAC and SAC for the last 5 years (2009–2014; Preventive Chemotherapy Database of the WHO), the site in Ethiopia was considered to have experienced a low drug exposure, the site in Lao PDR a medium drug exposure and the site in Pemba (Tanzania) a high drug exposure prior to the start of the trials [35] (Table 1).

For T. trichiura infections, ALB efficacy estimations varied significantly depending on the study site and the diagnostic method that was used (Table 4). Estimations obtained by single Kato-Katz were 52.9% in Ethiopia, 36.7% in Lao PDR and -11.2% in Pemba (Tanzania).
For hookworm infections (Table 5), the drug efficacy measured by single Kato-Katz was high (>95%) in both Ethiopia (96.3%) and Lao PDR (96.1%), but moderate in Pemba (Tanzania) (84.2%).
The downside of these increased control efforts is that resistance to anthelmintic drugs, such as ALB and MEB, is likely to develop.
138.Rationale: The passage documents that Pemba was a high drug-exposure site and that measured efficacy (by single Kato-Katz) was lower there for some species (T. trichiura and to a lesser extent hookworm). However, the passage does not present direct evidence that anthelmintic resistance has emerged there; lower observed efficacy can have multiple explanations (species-specific drug response, diagnostic method differences, local epidemiology). Although the authors note resistance is a concern in general, the data as presented do not suffice to conclude that resistance is 'likely emerging' in Pemba or other high-exposure areas. Thus the causal claim of likely emerging resistance is not directly supported by the passage.

138.Option: The results suggest that despite differences in diagnostic sensitivity and fecal egg counts across methods, there was good agreement in classifying drug efficacy according to WHO guidelines, indicating that each method holds promise for assessing drug efficacy in STH control programs.
Evidence: FECPAKG2 was previously evaluated as being less sensitive than Kato-Katz, while qPCR was found to have superior sensitivity for all STH [16, 26, 39].

In summary, a duplicate Kato-Katz provided drug efficacy significantly or marginally equivalent to a single Kato-Katz for the three STH species in all study sites. For the other methods, the equivalence of drug efficacy varied by STH species and study site.

For both duplicate Kato-Katz (κFleiss = 0.81, p <0.001) and qPCR (κFleiss = 0.84, p <0.001) there was almost a perfect agreement (κFleiss ≥0.8). For FECPAKG2 (κFleiss = 0.65, p = 0.01) there was a substantial agreement (0.61 ≤ κFleiss <0.81), for MiniFLOTAC (κFleiss = 0.60, p = 0.03) there was a moderate agreement (0.41 ≤ κFleiss <0.61).
Third, they indicate that variation in individual drug response across STH and countries is similar across diagnostic methods.
138.Rationale: The passage documents clear differences in diagnostic sensitivity (qPCR high, FECPAKG2 low) and differences in absolute (G)ERR estimates by species and site. Nevertheless, agreement statistics show that duplicate Kato-Katz and qPCR had almost perfect agreement with single Kato-Katz in classifying drug efficacy according to WHO thresholds, while FECPAKG2 and Mini-FLOTAC showed substantial-to-moderate agreement. The authors also note that patterns of individual drug response were similar across methods. Taken together, these findings support the inference that, despite sensitivity differences, multiple diagnostic methods can reliably classify drug efficacy and therefore have potential utility ('hold promise') for monitoring drug efficacy in STH control programs, with appropriate consideration of their strengths and limitations.

139.Option: The functional conservation of cathepsin-like cysteine proteases between B. malayi and C. elegans is complete, as demonstrated by the ability of O. volvulus CPL-1 to rescue the C. elegans cpl-1 mutant.
Evidence: RNAi targeting the cathepsin L- and Z-like cysteine proteases (CPL & CPZ respectively) has clearly validated their essential role during O. volvulus L3 molting [33].
detailed studies of the CPLs in C. elegans [40],[41] have provided more direct proof of their function in the free living nematodes.

However, their predicted essential function during development may not always be conserved in both filarial nematodes and C. elegans, as has been shown for the roles of CPL and CPZ in the molting of O. volvulus and C. elegans [33].
139.Rationale: The passage does not present any data showing a rescue experiment (e.g., O. volvulus CPL-1 rescuing a C. elegans mutant). In fact the text explicitly cautions that predicted functions are not always conserved between filarial nematodes and C. elegans. Therefore the strong claim of complete functional conservation and a specific rescue demonstration cannot be derived from the Passage Body.

139.Option: The use of siRNA in filarial nematodes has been shown to be effective in reducing microfilariae release and inducing embryonic abnormalities, suggesting that siRNA may be a more efficient RNAi tool than dsRNA for targeting filarial genes.
Evidence: In comparison to the control worms, adult worms treated with dsRNA or siRNA corresponding to the Bm-cpl genes showed a persistent and significant reduction in the release of Mf into the culture medium after 1, 3 and 5 days (P<0.05 for all) (Fig. 3).
Reduction in Mf release were also seen after treatment with siRNA corresponding to the Bm-cpl-5 and Bm-cpl Pro genes (Fig. 3).
the siRNA appeared in these regions earlier and was clearly already seen after 24 h (Fig. 2B).

Bm-cpl transcript levels were reduced by 51.0% in Bm-cpl-5 dsRNA treated worms and by 48.9% in the siRNA treated worms, these reductions were significantly different from both the medium control (dsRNA; P = 0.0006, siRNA; P<0.0001) and the negative control (dsRNA; P = 0.0033, siRNA; P<0.0001).

The Bm-cpl transcript levels were also reduced in Bm-cpl Pro treated worms; 66.5% and 37.4% in dsRNA and siRNA treated worms respectively, with significant differences from both the medium control (dsRNA; P = 0.0003, siRNA; P = 0.002) and the negative control (dsRNA; P = 0.0016, siRNA; P = 0.0082).
139.Rationale: The Passage shows that siRNA is effective in reducing Mf release and causing embryonic abnormalities, and that siRNA is taken up faster in some tissues. The passage documents neutrophil recruitment and chemokine up-regulation but does not discuss literature-based associations in other liver diseases nor explicitly state that the role of neutrophils in fibrotic response is ambiguous. 145.Option: The co-incidence of peak TLR expression with that of the pro-fibrogenic cytokines IL-13 and TGF-β1 in our study implies that TLR signalling may be involved in the development of S. japonicum-induced fibrosis.

145.Option: The up-regulation of CCL7 and CCL12 during fibrosis is consistent with the role of the related chemokine CCL2 in other liver diseases [44]. The passage also documents up-regulation of CCL21 and strong up-regulation of CXCL1, which correlates with procollagen genes and TGF-β1. However, the option's comparisons to CCL2 roles in other liver diseases and the assertion that CCL2 showed no significant change in S. japonicum infection are not stated in the Passage Body. The claim that these chemokines 'directly induce fibrogenic signalling pathways' is a mechanistic assertion not demonstrated by the correlative gene-expression and histological data presented. 145.Option: The temporal expression of distinct chemokine subsets regulates the recruitment of specific immune cell types during different phases of S. japonicum-induced granuloma formation and fibrosis.

147.Option: The high degree of genetic similarity between human and animal Salmonella serovars, despite the absence of genotypically similar strains in the same household, suggests that transmission may occur through indirect environmental routes or across broader community networks rather than direct animal-to-human contact.

All Salmonella genotypes had at least 80% similarity and the majority varied between 99% and 100%.

Together these observations support an inference that transmission is unlikely to be predominantly direct, within-household animal-to-human contact; instead, transmission via shared environmental sources or wider community networks (which would produce genetic similarity across different households) is a parsimonious epidemiological explanation. The phrasing in the option uses "suggests," which is appropriate given the observational data presented.

147.Option: The high proportion of Salmonella serovars in the chicken population compared to sheep and goats suggests that chickens are the primary reservoir for NTS transmission to humans in this rural Gambian community.
Evidence: The proportion of Salmonella isolated was higher in the chicken population than in other species: 66.6%, 28.6% and 4.8% in chickens, sheep and goats, respectively.

In the same compound, a diversity of Salmonella serovars were circulating at animal level especially in chickens; the number varying from 2 to 3 different serovars: in compound 1 (C1), S. Moualine, S. Tornow and S. Poona; in compound 6 (C6), S. Schwarzengrund and S. Stanleyville and in compound 7 (C7), S. Offa, S. Give and S. Poona (table 1).

Additionally, human diarrhoeal genotypes cluster with animal genotypes and one serovar (S. Moualine) was found in both a child and a chicken (albeit in different compounds). These elements together support the inference that chickens are a likely primary reservoir for NTS in this community. The conclusion is presented as a suggestion based on observed proportions and genetic relatedness rather than a definitive proof of direct transmission, which aligns with the evidence provided.

147.Option: The presence of multiple Salmonella serovars in the same household, especially in chickens, indicates that mixed infections and intraspecific recombination are common and may drive the emergence of new pathogenic clones in this community.
Evidence: In the same compound, a diversity of Salmonella serovars were circulating at animal level especially in chickens; the number varying from 2 to 3 different serovars: in compound 1 (C1), S. Moualine, S. Tornow and S. Poona; in compound 6 (C6), S. Schwarzengrund and S. Stanleyville and in compound 7 (C7), S. Offa, S. Give and S. Poona (table 1).

147.Option: The significant association between salmonellosis and co-diagnosis with malaria suggests that malaria is a primary driver of NTS transmission in this community, likely through immunosuppression that increases susceptibility to infection.

147.Option: The fact that all Salmonella genotypes causing diarrhoea in children were clustered with animal genotypes in the phylogenetic tree indicates that direct animal-to-human transmission occurs within the same household.

148.Option: The high concordance of ompA types in re-infections implies that the MOMP antigen is highly conserved across C. trachomatis strains, making it a reliable target for universal vaccine development.
Evidence: A total of 15 different ompA types were identified, ranging from A1 to A11 and from B1 to B4.

Of these 21 children, 14 (67%, 95%CI 43–85%) had the same strain at both timepoints, which was significantly more than would be expected by chance (permutation P<0.001; Fig 3B).

The observed same-type re-infections are better explained by repeated exposure to the same local strain and lack of protective immunity rather than by high conservation of MOMP. Therefore the claim that MOMP is highly conserved and hence a reliable universal vaccine target is not supported by the passage.

We hypothesized a lack of genotype-specific protective immunity, which would result in a relatively high proportion of re-infections with the same ompA type as the original infection.

148.Option: Chlamydia vaccines targeting only the MOMP may be less likely to confer protective immunity than vaccines containing multiple antigenic epitopes.

animal studies suggest that natural protective immunity mediated through MOMP may be serovar-specific, since experimental re-infection with the same serovar causes less clinical inflammation than the primary infection, whereas re-infection with a different serovar produces a similar degree of inflammation as the first infection. [2]
We hypothesized a lack of genotype-specific protective immunity, which would result in a relatively high proportion of re-infections with the same ompA type as the original infection.
Of these 21 children, 14 (67%, 95%CI 43–85%) had the same strain at both timepoints, which was significantly more than would be expected by chance (permutation P<0.001; Fig 3B).

However, the study observed a high proportion of re-infections with the identical ompA type (67%), contrary to what would be expected if natural immunity to MOMP were broadly protective against homologous strains. From an epidemiological and vaccine-development perspective, these findings imply that focusing a vaccine solely on MOMP may not elicit the breadth or durability of protection needed; vaccines incorporating multiple antigenic epitopes (to elicit broader immune responses) are a plausible implication derived from the reported lack of genotype-specific protective immunity.

149.Option: The inability of Shuni virus to replicate in Culex pipiens pipiens after thoracic injection indicates that the species lacks the cellular machinery necessary for arbovirus replication, rendering it incapable of supporting any stage of the virus life cycle.
Evidence: Direct injection of SHUV into the thorax resulted in high infection rates of 68% for Cx. p. pipiens (N = 50), and 100% for Ae. aegypti (N = 50).

Titers of virus-infected Cx. p. pipiens body samples were almost all below the detection limit of 103 TCID50/ml of the endpoint dilution assay (Fig 3C). This indicates that even when SHUV is injected into the thorax, there is no productive virus replication.

No SHUV infection was observed in the Cx. p. pipiens mosquitoes (N = 211) following oral exposure, whereas infection rates of 2% were found for orally exposed Ae. aegypti mosquitoes (N = 149; Fig 2B).
149.Rationale: The passage shows that intrathoracic injection produced substantial infection and measurable transmission in Cx. p. pipiens (high infection rate, nonzero transmission rate), so the species can support at least some stages of the virus life cycle. The text does state that body titers were almost all below the detection limit, indicating little productive replication after injection, but that observation does not justify the sweeping conclusion that the species "lacks the cellular machinery necessary for arbovirus replication" or is "incapable of supporting any stage". The evidence therefore contradicts the absolute claim in the option.

149.Option: Shuni virus transmission by Culex pipiens pipiens is limited by a strong salivary gland barrier, as evidenced by the low transmission success despite high infection rates after thoracic injection.
Evidence: Direct injection of SHUV into the thorax resulted in high infection rates of 68% for Cx. p. pipiens (N = 50), and 100% for Ae. aegypti (N = 50).

Transmission rates of 32% (16/50) were found for Cx. p. pipiens and 8% (4/50) for Ae.aegypti. This corresponds to transmission success of 47.1% (16/34) for Cx. p. pipiens and 8% (4/50) for Ae. aegypti.
This indicates a relatively weaker salivary gland barrier in Cx. p. pipiens compared to Ae. aegypti mosquitoes which had 100% infection rate, but relatively low transmission success.
149.Rationale: The passage reports a transmission success of 47.1% for Cx. p. pipiens after thoracic injection, and explicitly interprets this as a relatively weaker salivary gland barrier in Cx. p. pipiens compared to Ae. aegypti. The option claims a "strong salivary gland barrier" limiting transmission; this contradicts the authors' data and interpretation that the salivary gland barrier in Cx. p. pipiens is relatively weaker (i.e., less limiting) compared to Ae. aegypti. Therefore the option is not supported by the passage.

149.Option: The midgut barrier is the main barrier preventing infection of Culex pipiens pipiens with Shuni virus, as evidenced by the absence of infection following oral exposure despite successful virus replication and transmission after direct thoracic injection.
Evidence: No SHUV infection was observed in the Cx. p. pipiens mosquitoes (N = 211) following oral exposure, whereas infection rates of 2% were found for orally exposed Ae. aegypti mosquitoes (N = 149; Fig 2B).
Direct injection of SHUV into the thorax resulted in high infection rates of 68% for Cx. p. pipiens (N = 50)... Transmission rates of 32% (16/50) were found for Cx. p. pipiens...
Taken together, SHUV is able to disseminate in mosquitoes, but both the midgut and salivary glands form a barrier for SHUV.
149.Rationale: The passage documents that oral exposure produced no detectable infection in Cx. p. pipiens, whereas bypassing the midgut by intrathoracic injection produced substantial infection and measurable transmission. These findings support the inference that the midgut barrier is a key impediment to infection after natural (oral) exposure in Cx. p. pipiens. The passage also explicitly states that the midgut and salivary glands form barriers, and the contrast between oral failure and injection success points to the midgut as the primary barrier to initial infection.

149.Option: The high transmission success of Shuni virus in Culex pipiens pipiens after intrathoracic injection suggests that this mosquito species is a competent vector capable of sustaining and spreading the virus in natural settings.
Evidence: No SHUV infection was observed in the Cx. p. pipiens mosquitoes (N = 211) following oral exposure...
Direct injection of SHUV into the thorax resulted in high infection rates of 68% for Cx. p. pipiens (N = 50)... Transmission rates of 32% (16/50) were found for Cx. p. pipiens...

Titers of virus-infected Cx. p. pipiens body samples were almost all below the detection limit of 103 TCID50/ml of the endpoint dilution assay (Fig 3C). This indicates that even when SHUV is injected into the thorax, there is no productive virus replication.
Thus, SHUV was able to successfully infect a small proportion of Ae. aegypti mosquitoes but not Cx. p. pipiens, and no evidence was found for transmission of SHUV by mosquitoes.
149.Rationale: Intrathoracic injection bypasses the midgut and is an artificial route that does not mimic natural feeding. The passage shows no infection after oral exposure for Cx. p. pipiens and notes lack of productive replication after injection (body titers below detection), and explicitly states there was no evidence for transmission of SHUV by mosquitoes in the oral exposure experiments. Therefore the laboratory observation of transmission after injection cannot be extrapolated to competence for natural transmission and spreading in the field; the option overstates what can be inferred from the experimental results.

149.Option: The low infection rate of Shuni virus in Culex pipiens pipiens following oral exposure is primarily due to insufficient viral load in the blood meal, rather than biological barriers within the mosquito.
Evidence: SHUV P3 stock with a mean titer of 3.0 x 106 TCID50/ml was mixed 1:1 with cow blood.

153.Rationale: The passage provides an observed overall PCR prevalence of 2.0% in this Malaysian sample and states this is the first confirmed molecular detection in Malaysia. The passage does not present or cite any data about high (e.g., ~30%) prevalence rates from other regions, nor does it provide evidence that the low observed prevalence is due to underreporting or inadequate surveillance.
