We have not operated on noma patients for the past two years due to COVID 19 pandemic movement restriction.

Many cases remain undetected and unreported due to various factors, including limited diagnostic skills among health care providers, the rapid disease progression and high acute-phase mortality, a lack of routine surveillance and inclusion health information systems, and family stigma that leads to hiding affected children
Most of the report (67 patients) were extracted from the NGO data base who are working on noma care.
154.Rationale: While some respondents and records note fewer cases seen since 2020, the passage provides multiple reasons why reported case counts may decline without reflecting true incidence: service disruptions due to COVID-19, limited surveillance, underreporting, reliance on NGO databases, and other detection limitations. The passage does not provide evidence that public health interventions or increased awareness caused a true decline in incidence. Thus the inference of a genuine reduction is not supported.

154.Option: The high number of noma cases reported from Amhara and Somali regions indicates that these regions have the most advanced health systems and best access to specialized care for noma patients.
Evidence: The recorded patient address indicated that 31 patients are from Amhara region, 9 from Somalie, 8 from Oromia, 5 from Afar, 5 from SNNPR, 4 from Gambella and 3 from Addis Ababa (Fig 4).
The selection of hospitals was done based on convenience, with focus on institutions that provide plastic surgery, oral care, and dermatology services in each region.
The retrospective identified 69 reported case of noma from January 2015 to December 2020, with 97% of cases documented by two NGOs supporting surgical mission (Fig 3).

Many cases remain undetected and unreported due to various factors, including limited diagnostic skills among health care providers, the rapid disease progression and high acute-phase mortality, a lack of routine surveillance and inclusion health information systems, and family stigma that leads to hiding affected children
154.Rationale: Although more cases in the dataset came from certain regions, the study used convenience sampling (selecting hospitals with surgical/oral services) and most cases were documented by NGOs, creating strong detection and reporting biases. The passage also emphasizes under-detection and lack of routine surveillance. Therefore, higher reported counts from particular regions are more likely due to case-finding, referral patterns, or NGO activities rather than indicating those regions have the best health system capacity; the option is not supported by the passage.

154.Option: The integration of noma into existing non-communicable disease and nutrition programs would be sufficient to ensure timely detection and management of acute cases across Ethiopia’s primary health care system.
Evidence: Sixty percent of respondents said noma can be integrated into NTD programs, while 40% preferred integration into Non Communicable Disease, nutrition and medical services.
Eighty-five percent of the participant stated that there is no oral health program in the country’s health system.

All participant agreed on the limitation of on the availability of human resource for oral health and poor capacity of the health institution for integrating of oral health program in to the primary health care unit.
Currently there is no pre-service training or continuous professional development for oral/dental care in the country.

The Ministry of Health has also put in place HMIS to gather data from all health facilities in the country. The system is in its scale-up phase and need to be expanded to address diseases of the poor. Currently the system does not include the indicators to acute cases and sequel of noma.
154.Rationale: The passage notes opinions that noma could be integrated into other programs but also documents major gaps: no national oral health program, limited human resources, lack of training, and HMIS not capturing noma indicators. These systemic deficiencies indicate that mere integration into existing NCD/nutrition programs, without addressing workforce, training, surveillance, and program capacity, would not be sufficient to ensure timely detection and management. The option's claim of sufficiency is therefore unsupported.

154.Option: The absence of acute noma case records in the national health information system underscores the urgent need for routine surveillance programs and improved data collection at the primary health care level.
Evidence: The Ministry of Health has also put in place HMIS to gather data from all health facilities in the country. The system is in its scale-up phase and need to be expanded to address diseases of the poor. Currently the system does not include the indicators to acute cases and sequel of noma.

Many cases remain undetected and unreported due to various factors, including limited diagnostic skills among health care providers, the rapid disease progression and high acute-phase mortality, a lack of routine surveillance and inclusion health information systems, and family stigma that leads to hiding affected children

Comprehensive data on noma surgical management was gathered from the service record books of the two NGOs. The record ware validated by tracing back the patient files at each hospital where procedure had been performed. All confirmed noma cases, were included in the study.
154.Rationale: The passage directly states that the national HMIS does not include indicators for acute noma and that many cases go undetected or unreported because of lack of routine surveillance and information system inclusion. It also shows that current case data rely heavily on NGO records rather than routine primary care reporting. Therefore it is epidemiologically justified to infer an urgent need for routine surveillance and improved primary care data collection to monitor and respond to noma.

155.Option: The limited reactivity of antivenom with disintegrins and the DC fragments of SVMPs, which are recognized as inhibitors of platelet aggregation, suggests that these components are not critical in the pathophysiology of envenoming.
Evidence: The MAJar-3 antibodies are IgG1 isotypes and recognize conformational epitopes located on the jararhagin disintegrin-like domain.

SVMPs are multifunctional enzymes involved in the local and systemic symptoms of bites, such as the induction of local hemorrhage, inflammatory reaction, activation of coagulation factors and inhibition of platelet aggregation [35].

the antivenoms reacted equally with the toxins from the same protein family, regardless of snake phylogeny or the presence of the venom in the immunization pool used for antivenom production, highlighting new priorities when considering the selection of venoms to be used in the production of antivenoms.
155.Rationale: The passage provides no evidence that antivenom reactivity with disintegrins or DC fragments of SVMPs is limited; in fact it states that a monoclonal antibody (MAJar-3) recognizes the jararhagin disintegrin-like domain, indicating reactivity to such regions. The passage also explicitly describes SVMPs as involved in hemorrhage and inhibition of platelet aggregation, indicating these components are relevant to pathophysiology. Finally, the authors report that antivenoms reacted equally with toxins from the same protein family regardless of phylogeny or inclusion in the immunization pool, which contradicts the claim of limited reactivity. Therefore the conclusion that these components are not critical is not supported by the passage.

155.Option: The antivenom's efficacy in neutralizing B. jararacussu venom is lower than that for B. jararaca venom due to the absence of B. jararacussu venom in the immunization pool, despite similar toxin profiles.
Evidence: The antibothropic serum (SAB) was produced at the Instituto Butantan, São Paulo, Brazil in horses immunized with a mixture of the following venoms: B. jararaca (50%), B. neuwiedi (12.5%), R. alternatus (12.5%), B. moojeni (12.5%) and B. jararacussu (12.5%).

The efficacy of the antivenom was then assessed for the neutralization of relevant symptoms of experimental envenomings by (a) B. jararaca, which accounts for 50% of venom composition in the immunization pool and is prevalent in the southeastern Brazil, and (b) B. atrox, which is not present in the immunization pool although representing a common cause of snakebite in the Amazon.

the antivenoms reacted equally with the toxins from the same protein family, regardless of snake phylogeny or the presence of the venom in the immunization pool used for antivenom production, highlighting new priorities when considering the selection of venoms to be used in the production of antivenoms.
155.Rationale: The option's premise is factually incorrect: B. jararacussu venom is explicitly listed as part of the immunization mixture (12.5%). Thus the statement that lower efficacy is due to absence from the immunization pool is invalid. The passage also provides no data showing lower efficacy of SAB against B. jararacussu relative to B. jararaca; indeed the authors state that antivenoms reacted equally with toxins from the same protein family regardless of inclusion in the immunization pool. Therefore this inference cannot be derived from the passage.

155.Option: Despite the phylogenetic distance of the snake and the fact that the venom is not included in the immunization pool used to produce SAB, the antivenom is efficient in neutralizing the most important effects of B. atrox venom.
Evidence: The efficacy of the antivenom was then assessed for the neutralization of relevant symptoms of experimental envenomings by (a) B. jararaca, which accounts for 50% of venom composition in the immunization pool and is prevalent in the southeastern Brazil, and (b) B. atrox, which is not present in the immunization pool although representing a common cause of snakebite in the Amazon.

the antivenoms reacted equally with the toxins from the same protein family, regardless of snake phylogeny or the presence of the venom in the immunization pool used for antivenom production, highlighting new priorities when considering the selection of venoms to be used in the production of antivenoms.

For the neutralization of hemorrhagic activity, doses of 10 µg B. jararaca or B. atrox venom were incubated with SAB at ratios of 1, 2 or 4 times the SAB volume required to neutralize 10 µg of reference venom, according to the manufacturer.
155.Rationale: The passage states that SAB was tested against B. atrox venom even though B. atrox is not included in the immunization pool. Crucially, the authors report that antivenoms 'reacted equally with the toxins from the same protein family, regardless of snake phylogeny or the presence of the venom in the immunization pool', which supports the inference that SAB cross-reacts and can neutralize key toxin families in B. atrox. The described neutralization assays (hemorrhagic, lethal, coagulant) further show that efficacy against B. atrox was experimentally assessed. From these statements, it is reasonable to infer that the antivenom is effective in neutralizing the most important effects of B. atrox despite its absence from the immunization mixture.

155.Option: The cross-reactivity of the antivenom with P-III SVMPs across species is due to the high sequence homology of these toxins, which is confirmed by the structural similarity of their catalytic domains.
Evidence: the antivenoms reacted equally with the toxins from the same protein family, regardless of snake phylogeny or the presence of the venom in the immunization pool used for antivenom production, highlighting new priorities when considering the selection of venoms to be used in the production of antivenoms.

The variability in venom composition is notable and can be correlated with phylogeny [36], [37], age [38], [39], sex [40], geographical distribution [13], [40], [41] and diet [42]–[44] of the snake. However, venom variability is mostly related to the expression level of each group of toxin rather than to the presence or absence of major families of venom proteins.

within the same protein family, variability in the toxic properties may also occur when distinct functional motifs are introduced in structurally related toxins, increasing the diversity of targets that can be affected by venom toxins [45], [46].
155.Rationale: While the passage documents cross-reactivity of antivenom with toxins from the same protein family, it does not provide molecular evidence (sequence homology data or structural analyses of catalytic domains) to attribute this cross-reactivity to high sequence homology or catalytic-domain similarity of P-III SVMPs. In fact, the text emphasizes variability within protein families and that differences can arise from expression levels or distinct functional motifs. Therefore the specific mechanistic claim about sequence homology and catalytic-domain confirmation is not supported by the passage.

155.Option: The antivenom's neutralization of hemorrhagic activity is less effective in B. atrox venom than in B. jararaca venom due to differences in the expression levels of P-III SVMPs.
Evidence: For the neutralization of hemorrhagic activity, doses of 10 µg B. jararaca or B. atrox venom were incubated with SAB at ratios of 1, 2 or 4 times the SAB volume required to neutralize 10 µg of reference venom, according to the manufacturer.

the antivenoms reacted equally with the toxins from the same protein family, regardless of snake phylogeny or the presence of the venom in the immunization pool used for antivenom production, highlighting new priorities when considering the selection of venoms to be used in the production of antivenoms.
However, venom variability is mostly related to the expression level of each group of toxin rather than to the presence or absence of major families of venom proteins.
155.Rationale: The passage describes that hemorrhagic-neutralization assays were performed for both B. jararaca and B. atrox, and it reports that antivenoms reacted equally with toxins from the same protein family regardless of inclusion in the immunization pool. The authors also note that venom variability is often due to expression-level differences. However, the passage contains no data or statement showing that SAB neutralization of hemorrhage is less effective for B. atrox than for B. jararaca, nor does it link any measured difference in neutralization to P-III SVMP expression levels. Thus the specific causal inference in this option is not supported by the provided text.

156.Option: The experimental model demonstrates that ST313 infection in chickens leads to prolonged gastrointestinal inflammation, mirroring the chronic enteropathy seen in human iNTS patients.
Evidence: Infection with ST313 D23580 elicited a mean 11- fold increase in CXCLi1 gene expression and 20- fold increase CXCLi2 gene expression in the ileum at 3 days post-infection (Figure 2B).

At 7 days post-infection, little intestinal inflammation was seen in the D23580 infected group and the inflammation-associated architectural disturbance had largely resolved, whereas the 4/74 infected group continued to show inflammation, flattening of the villi and crypt hyperplasia at significantly higher levels than in the D23580-infected group (P = 0.014) (Table 2).

These findings suggest both isolates elicit an inflammatory CXC chemokine response in the intestine and that the resulting inflammation is perhaps slower in onset but more prolonged following infection with the ST19 isolate 4/74.
156.Rationale: The passage describes a rapid, pronounced intestinal inflammatory response to ST313 (D23580) at day 3 but explicitly states that by day 7 the intestinal inflammation and architectural disturbance in the D23580 group had largely resolved. Thus the experimental findings demonstrate transient, not prolonged, gastrointestinal inflammation for ST313 in chickens. There is no evidence in the passage that ST313 causes chronic enteropathy in chickens or that it mirrors chronic human iNTS enteropathy. Therefore the statement asserting prolonged gastrointestinal inflammation and mirroring chronic human disease is not supported by the data.

156.Option: Chickens are the primary natural host of ST313, as evidenced by the high levels of systemic invasion and intestinal colonization observed in the experimental model.
Evidence: Both ST313 isolates were found to colonize the ceca (Figure 1A) and invade the spleen (Figure 1B) and liver (data not shown) at 3 days post-infection with Salmonella detected in all birds in these groups.

Both ST313 isolates colonized at significantly lower levels than either F98 (P = 0.033 for Q456, P = 0.002 for D23580 at 7 days post infection) or 4/74 (P = 0.036 for Q456, P = 0.015 for D23580), though both ST19 isolates gave similar levels of colonization.
Although there is currently no epidemiological evidence that birds are a source of ST313 infection, they are perhaps the most likely zoonotic source of Salmonella.
Consequently, it has been suggested that ST313 is restricted to human infection, or at least adapted to a niche of immunosuppressed HIV or malaria-infected patients.
156.Rationale: While the experimental model shows that ST313 can colonize the chicken gut and invade systemic sites, the passage explicitly notes there is no epidemiological evidence that birds are a source of ST313 and that ST313 has been suggested to be human-restricted or adapted to immunosuppressed humans. Experimental infectivity in a laboratory model does not by itself establish that chickens are the primary natural host. The claim that chickens are the primary natural host is a stronger ecological and epidemiological conclusion than the data support; the passage contains statements that contradict such a definitive inference.

156.Option: The absence of epidemiological evidence for a zoonotic source of ST313 in Africa confirms that the pathotype is strictly human-adapted and incapable of sustained transmission through animal reservoirs.
Evidence: Epidemiological investigation has been unable to determine an environmental or zoonotic source of iNTS, with transmission being considered likely to be through direct or indirect human-to-human routes and with asymptomatic carriage potentially playing a role [1], [3], [6].
Although there is currently no epidemiological evidence that birds are a source of ST313 infection, they are perhaps the most likely zoonotic source of Salmonella.

Both ST313 isolates were found to colonize the ceca (Figure 1A) and invade the spleen (Figure 1B) and liver (data not shown) at 3 days post-infection with Salmonella detected in all birds in these groups.
156.Rationale: The passage states there is currently no epidemiological evidence identifying an animal or environmental reservoir for ST313 and that transmission is considered likely human-to-human. However, the experimental findings show that ST313 can colonize and invade chickens under laboratory conditions. The absence of epidemiological evidence does not confirm inability to be transmitted via animal reservoirs; it indicates lack of demonstrated reservoir in field studies. Therefore the strong claim that ST313 is strictly human-adapted and incapable of sustained animal transmission is not supported by the provided evidence.

156.Option: Although ST313 isolates show a reduced ability to thrive in the intestinal tract, the level of colonization is sufficient for chickens to act as a potential source of zoonotic infection.
Evidence: All ST19 and ST313 isolates colonized the ceca of the chicken by 3 days post-infection and remained colonized at 7 days post-infection (Figure 1A).
Both ST313 isolates colonized at significantly lower levels than either F98 (P = 0.033 for Q456, P = 0.002 for D23580 at 7 days post infection) or 4/74 (P = 0.036 for Q456, P = 0.015 for D23580),
In Africa, domestic chickens live in close contact with humans in both urban and village communities, often being housed overnight in the family home [7].

Infection of the chicken with pathovars of S. Typhimurium like ST19 can persist within the gastrointestinal tract leading to fecal shedding into the environment, contamination of meat and fecal contamination of eggs [8].
However, potential domestic or wild animal sources of infection have been incompletely investigated.
156.Rationale: The experiments demonstrate that ST313 can colonize the chicken ceca and persist at least through 7 days post-infection, even if at lower levels than ST19. The background text documents close contact between domestic chickens and humans and the established potential for chickens to shed Salmonella into the environment and contaminate food. Given the demonstrated colonization plus the ecological context and the acknowledged incomplete investigation of animal sources, it is epidemiologically reasonable to infer that chickens could act as a potential zoonotic source for ST313. This is a cautious, evidence-based inference (potential source), not a firm assertion of confirmed zoonotic transmission.

156.Option: The rapid systemic invasion and intense inflammatory response observed in chickens infected with ST313 suggest that this pathotype is evolving toward a 'stealth infection' strategy similar to S. Gallinarum and S. Pullorum.
Evidence: Genomic analysis of D23580, an MDR ST313 isolated in Blantyre, Malawi, revealed that ST313 has undergone genomic degradation including pseudogene formation and chromosomal deletion that is a feature of host-adapted serovars such as S. Typhi and S. Gallinarum, that cause systemic typhoidal disease in their hosts [4], [5].
ST313 S. Typhimurium shows a phenotype of rapid invasion in the chicken
Infection with ST313 D23580 elicited a mean 11- fold increase in CXCLi1 gene expression and 20- fold increase CXCLi2 gene expression in the ileum at 3 days post-infection (Figure 2B).

At 7 days post-infection, little intestinal inflammation was seen in the D23580 infected group and the inflammation-associated architectural disturbance had largely resolved, whereas the 4/74 infected group continued to show inflammation...
156.Rationale: While the passage notes genomic degradation in ST313 that is a feature of some host-adapted serovars and documents rapid systemic invasion plus an early intense inflammatory response that subsequently resolves, the leap to conclude ST313 is evolving toward a 'stealth infection' strategy (as typified by S. Gallinarum and S. Pullorum) is speculative and not directly supported by the experimental data. 'Stealth infection' implies specific evolutionary direction and host-adapted phenotypes (e.g., reduced inflammation with systemic persistence), which are not demonstrated or proven by the short-term experimental findings. The passage provides suggestive genomic parallels and some phenotypic overlap, but does not provide longitudinal evolutionary or epidemiological evidence needed to assert that ST313 is evolving toward that specific strategy.

157.Option: PORT is more likely to occur in patients with systemic immunosuppression, suggesting that immunosuppressed individuals should be prioritized for routine screening with multimodal imaging during posterior uveitis evaluations.
Evidence: Atypical presentations are more frequent in patients with systemic immunosuppression. [3]
PORT is characterized by multifocal grey-white lesions at the outer retinal layers and retinal pigment epithelium associated with minimal vitreous inflammation. [1,3,4,9–11]
Patients were evaluated using available images including OCT, fundus photography, and fundus autofluorescence (FAF).
157.Rationale: The passage states atypical presentations (of which PORT is one) are more frequent in immunosuppressed patients and describes that PORT is diagnosed and characterized using multimodal imaging (OCT, fundus photography, FAF). From these facts, it is reasonable to infer that immunosuppressed individuals represent a higher-risk group for atypical forms like PORT and that prioritizing multimodal imaging in their posterior uveitis evaluations is supported by the study's diagnostic approach.

157.Option: Since PORT lesions are often located in the macula and can lead to legal blindness, all patients with posterior uveitis should receive immediate intravitreal anti-VEGF therapy to prevent choroidal neovascularization.
Evidence: Most lesions have been reported in the macula, but this may be because macular lesions are visually the most symptomatic. [1,4]
Complications encountered include choroidal neovascularization, serous retinal detachment, [4] and macular cysts[7] that may convert to what is known as huge outer retinal cystoid space (HORC). [12]
Due to this, an anti-vascular endothelial growth factor (aflibercept) intravitreal injection was prescribed for her OD.

The aim of the study is to describe clinical findings, serology, multimodal imaging, and visual prognosis in patients with PORT to facilitate further recognition of this pathology and thereby decrease the frequency of irreversible complications leading to legal blindness.
157.Rationale: While the passage notes PORT lesions are often macular and that choroidal neovascularization can occur (and gives a single example where anti-VEGF was used), it does not provide evidence that immediate intravitreal anti-VEGF therapy is indicated for all patients with posterior uveitis. The text documents targeted use in a specific case of CNV rather than recommending universal prophylactic anti-VEGF, so the blanket recommendation is not derivable from the passage.

157.Option: PORT represents a diagnostic challenge; therefore, clinical suspicion and multimodal imaging are crucial for recognizing PORT lesions.
Evidence: PORT is characterized by multifocal grey-white lesions at the outer retinal layers and retinal pigment epithelium associated with minimal vitreous inflammation. [1,3,4,9–11]
Patients were evaluated using available images including OCT, fundus photography, and fundus autofluorescence (FAF).

PORT diagnosis was made by a uveitis specialist based on clinical findings, positive serology, and adequate treatment response (800 mg sulfamethoxazole and 160 mg trimethoprim every 12 hours, clindamycin 300 mg every 6 hours and tapering steroids initiating with 0.5 mg/kg/day of prednisone).
The aim of the study is to describe clinical findings, serology, multimodal imaging, and visual prognosis in patients with PORT to facilitate further recognition of this pathology...
157.Rationale: The passage describes PORT as an atypical lesion with minimal vitritis (making it less obvious clinically) and emphasizes use of multimodal imaging and specialist clinical judgment for diagnosis. The authors also state the study's aim is to improve recognition using these tools. Thus the inference that PORT is diagnostically challenging and requires clinical suspicion plus multimodal imaging is directly supported.

157.Option: The high rate of recurrence in PORT (25%) compared to classical OT (up to 79%) indicates that patients with PORT require more aggressive long-term immunosuppressive therapy to prevent reactivation.
Evidence: Recurrences when they appear, usually involve the adjacent retina. [2,5–7]
Treatment with pyrimethamine, sulfadiazine, and corticosteroids often results in full visual recovery, yet retinochoroidal scarring is often seen with optical coherence tomography (OCT). [3]
From 498 medical records with OT, eight patients with PORT were recovered.
157.Rationale: The passage does not provide any numerical recurrence rates for PORT or classical OT, nor does it compare recurrence frequencies (no 25% or 79% figures appear). It also does not present evidence that more aggressive long-term immunosuppression is required for PORT; treatment described involves antimicrobial therapy plus tapering steroids. Therefore the specific comparative recurrence claim and the treatment recommendation are not supported by the passage.

157.Option: The similarity between PORT and occult macular dystrophy (OMD) on OCT imaging suggests that patients with suspected posterior uveitis should undergo full-field and multifocal electroretinography to differentiate between the two conditions.
Evidence: PORT is characterized by multifocal grey-white lesions at the outer retinal layers and retinal pigment epithelium associated with minimal vitreous inflammation. [1,3,4,9–11]
Patients were evaluated using available images including OCT, fundus photography, and fundus autofluorescence (FAF).
PORT diagnosis was made by a uveitis specialist based on clinical findings, positive serology, and adequate treatment response...
157.Rationale: The passage describes OCT and other imaging modalities used to evaluate PORT but does not mention occult macular dystrophy (OMD), similarities to OMD, nor the use of full-field or multifocal electroretinography for differential diagnosis. Because these specific comparisons and testing recommendations are not discussed in the text, the inference is not derived from the passage.

158.Option: The induction of autophagy promotes T. cruzi metacyclogenesis, as evidenced by increased metacyclic trypomastigote formation and higher infection rates in Vero cells when autophagy is stimulated by rapamycin or spermidine.
Evidence: Rap was able to increase the generation of MT when added to control medium at the first period of differentiation (8.3 +/- 0.2% vs. 3.9 +/- 0.6%).
In concordance to the above result, the addition of Rap at the first period of time (Rap-F) induced a significant increase of infectivity (Fig 6C).

Our results showed that the presence of either Spd or Spm significantly increased the detection of Atg8.1 (Fig 5A). Quantitative data showed a significant increase of parasites with autophagic vesicles after PA treatment, as compared to control parasites (≈ 80% vs. ≈ 20%).
Taken together, these results demonstrated that autophagy has a key role during T. cruzi differentiation.
158.Rationale: The passage supports the general inference that induction of autophagy promotes metacyclogenesis: rapamycin (an autophagy inducer) increased MT generation and infection rates, and the authors conclude autophagy has a key role in differentiation. However, the option asserts that spermidine also produced the demonstrated increases in MT formation and Vero cell infection. The passage shows spermidine induces autophagy (increased Atg8.1 and processed TcAtg8.1) but does not present direct data that spermidine (or spermine) increased metacyclic trypomastigote counts or Vero cell infection. Because the statement attributes specific evidence to spermidine that is not shown in the Passage Body, the option overstates what can be derived and is therefore incorrect.

158.Option: Spermidine and spermine exert their proautophagic effects in T. cruzi by directly inhibiting the activity of histone acetyl transferase, leading to transcriptional upregulation of ATG genes.
Evidence: The polyamine spermidine (Spd) has been recently described as a new modulator of autophagy since Spd inhibits the activity of histone acetyl transferase, leading to the upregulation of several ATG genes including ATG7, ATG11 and ATG15 [9].

When added to culture media, Spd is also able to directly induce autophagy in a transcription-independent manner. The mechanism has not been fully elucidated yet; however, this phenomenon could be due to the enhanced deacetylation of essential autophagy-related proteins such as ATG5 and ATG7 [10].
Our results showed that the presence of either Spd or Spm significantly increased the detection of Atg8.1 (Fig 5A).
158.Rationale: The passage describes proposed mechanisms for spermidine's proautophagic effects (HAT inhibition and also a transcription-independent direct induction) in the general/autophagy literature, but it explicitly states the mechanism is not fully elucidated and that spermidine can act in a transcription-independent way. For T. cruzi, the passage only reports that spermidine/spermine increase autophagy markers; it does not demonstrate the mechanistic pathway (HAT inhibition → transcriptional upregulation of ATG genes) in T. cruzi. Therefore the specific causal mechanism asserted by the option is not supported by the Passage Body.

158.Option: The increased infection rate of Vero cells by T. cruzi parasites subjected to autophagy induction is primarily due to enhanced parasite motility rather than increased metacyclic trypomastigote formation.
Evidence: Our results showed that the percentage of MT differentiated up to 48 h was higher in both TAU medium at the first (TAU-F) and the total (TAU-T) period of metacyclogenesis than in controls (12.1 +/- 0.9% and 16.4 +/- 1.7% vs. 3.9 +/- 0,6%, Fig 6A).

In another set of experiments, samples were allowed to infect Vero cells monolayers during 24 h ... The degree of cell infection was considered an indirect measure of the number of MT present in the samples at different conditions ... Results showed that around 45% of cells were infected by TAU differentiated parasites, whereas 10% of the cells were infected by control parasites.
Rap was able to increase the generation of MT when added to control medium at the first period of differentiation (8.3 +/- 0.2% vs. 3.9 +/- 0.6%).
158.Rationale: The passage links higher Vero cell infection rates to increased numbers of metacyclic trypomastigotes (MT) generated under autophagy-inducing conditions and uses infection rate as an indirect measure of MT abundance. There is no data or discussion in the Passage Body about parasite motility or evidence attributing increased infection to motility rather than increased MT formation. Thus the claim that enhanced motility is the primary cause is not supported by the presented evidence.

158.Option: The presence of wortmannin during the first or total period of differentiation in TAU medium significantly reduced the number of metacyclic trypomastigotes, indicating that autophagy inhibition leads to a complete block in parasite differentiation.
Evidence: the addition of Wort to TAU during the first (Wort-F) or the total (Wort-T) period significantly reduced the number of MT in the parasite mixture, as compared to TAU-T condition.
The presence of Wort at either the first (Wort-F) or the whole (Wort-T) period of differentiation in TAU significantly reduced the infection rate.
158.Rationale: The Passage Body clearly shows that wortmannin (an autophagy inhibitor) significantly reduced MT counts and infection rates when present during differentiation. However, it does not state or present evidence that wortmannin caused a complete block of differentiation; the data show a reduction, not elimination. Therefore the stronger assertion of a 'complete block' is not supported by the passage and the option is incorrect.

158.Option: Bafilomycin abrogates autophagosome formation in T. cruzi due to its inhibition of the acidocalcisome acidification process, which is essential for PI3P synthesis and autophagosome biogenesis.
Evidence: Unexpectedly, a significant reduction in the autophagosome number was observed when parasites were incubated in TAU in the presence of Baf, as compared to TAU medium alone (Fig 4A and 4B).

The number of autophagosomes was lower in parasites subjected to Baf in control medium than in non-treated parasites, indicating that Baf inhibits both basal and induced autophagy. Unlike the effect observed in mammalian cells, Baf abrogated autophagosome formation in T. cruzi, resulting in a complete inhibition of the autophagic response.
158.Rationale: The passage documents that bafilomycin (Baf) abrogates autophagosome formation in T. cruzi and that this is an unexpected, species-specific effect. However, the proposed mechanistic explanation in the option (inhibition of acidocalcisome acidification leading to impaired PI3P synthesis and autophagosome biogenesis) is not described or supported in the Passage Body. No text links Baf to acidocalcisome acidification or PI3P synthesis in this report. Therefore the mechanistic claim cannot be derived from the passage and the option is incorrect.

159.Option: The induction of both IFN-γ and IL-10 production in vaccinated mice suggests that the immune response is balanced and may be sufficient for long-term protection against visceral leishmaniasis.
Evidence: Immunization with Live Recombinant L. tarentolae-A2-CPA-CPB-CTE Induces a Mixed Production of IFN-γ and IL-10
production of IFN-γ is considered an important requirement for protection against L. infantum and that the presence of a small amount of IL-10 could promote the induction of type-1 immunity [64],

stimulation of splenocytes isolated from vaccinated groups G1 and G2 prior and after challenge with all three recall antigens (e.g. rA2-rCPA-rCPB, L. tar A2-CPA-CPB-CTE-EGFP lysate and L. infantum lysate) elicited a significantly higher IFN-γ production than control groups G3, G4 and G5.
The production of IL-10 upon antigen stimulation at 4-week and especially at 8 weeks after challenge was lower in G1 and G2 vaccinated groups in comparison to the control groups (G3 to G5) (Fig. 3B).

Indeed, the Leishmania-specific IFN-γ/IL-10 ratios upon stimulation with rA2-rCPA-rCPB and L. tar A2-CPA-CPB-CTE-GFP recall antigens were higher in G1 and G2 compared to the control G3, G4 and G5 mice both at 4 and 8 weeks after challenge.
only the vaccinated groups G1 and especially G2... were able to control the infection as illustrated by the significant differences observed at 12 weeks after challenge in comparison to the controls
159.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports a "mixed" (both IFN-γ and IL-10) cytokine production after vaccination, explains that IFN-γ is required for protection and that a small amount of IL-10 can promote type-1 immunity, and shows higher IFN-γ/IL-10 ratios in vaccinated groups that correlate with lower parasite burdens. Vaccinated mice (G1 and G2) maintained control of parasite burden up to 12 weeks in the spleen, supporting the inference that this balanced response is compatible with sustained protection. The cautious phrasing "may be sufficient for long-term protection" is consistent with data showing durability of protection in the study period (up to 12 weeks) but does not overstate absolute long-term efficacy beyond the observed timeframe.

159.Option: The Live/Live prime-boost regimen using recombinant L. tarentolae A2-CPA-CPB-CTE provides superior protection against visceral leishmaniasis compared to the DNA/Live regimen and to control groups, as evidenced by significantly lower parasite burden in the spleen and improved preservation of splenic architecture.
Evidence: only the vaccinated groups G1 and especially G2, which was immunized with Live L. tar A2-CPA-CPB-CTE/Live L. tar A2-CPA-CPB-CTE vaccine regimen, were able to control the infection as illustrated by the significant differences observed at 12 weeks after challenge in comparison to the controls (G1 with G4 and G2 with G3 and G5). Thus, the G2 vaccination regimen protects against infection better than G5 (Live L. tar/Live L. tar).
the spleen AUC of DNA/Live (G1) and Live/Live (G2) parasites indicate significant differences with the controls G4 and G5 (Fig. 2F).
We could not find parasites in the spleen in vaccinated groups (G1 and G2) but they could be seen in nearly all non-vaccinated groups (G3, G4 and G5, Fig. 5B).
Normal follicle formation of the spleen was noted in G1 and G2 in 4th and 8th week but the normal follicles were disappeared in the other groups (G3, G4 and G5) (Fig. 5A).
159.Rationale: The passage presents direct experimental comparisons showing that both vaccinated groups (DNA/Live G1 and Live/Live G2) reduced parasite burdens, with G2 described as "especially" able to control infection and protecting better than the related control. Quantitative measures (spleen AUC differences) and histopathology (preserved splenic follicles/architecture in G1 and G2 versus disrupted architecture in controls) support the inference that the Live/Live regimen is superior (and that it provides improved preservation of splenic architecture).

159.Option: The use of cationic solid-lipid nanoparticles (cSLN) as a delivery system for the DNA vaccine enhances antigen expression and is critical for the observed protective immunity in the DNA/Live regimen.
Evidence: For DNA vaccination with rA2-CPA-CPB-CTE, the pcDNA-A2-CPA-CPB-CTE was formulated into cationic lipid particles with nanometer range (∼240–250 nm). It was shown previously that this formulation facilitates the uptake by dendritic cells and macrophages, thereby enhancing antigen expression, processing and presentation and resulting in stronger immune effects [59].

immunization using a DNA A2-CPA-CPB-CTE/Live L. tar A2-CPA-CPB-CTE (G1) and L. tar A2-CPA-CPB-CTE/L. tar A2-CPA-CPB-CTE (G2) prime-boosting regiments drastically (p<0.01) reduced the infection levels in both the liver (Fig. 2A and 2B) and the spleen (Fig. 2D and 2E) at 4 weeks after L. infantum challenge in contrast to the control groups (G3 to G5, n = 4 at each point/organ).
159.Rationale: While the passage states that cSLN formulation has been shown previously to facilitate uptake and enhance antigen expression (a rationale for using it), the study provides no direct experimental comparison of DNA vaccine with versus without cSLN, nor direct evidence that cSLN was "critical" for the observed protection in G1. The observed protection with the DNA/Live regimen is reported, but causally attributing it specifically to cSLN (and asserting it was critical) is not directly supported by the presented data.

159.Option: The persistence of a small number of live parasites is essential for maintaining durable immunity, and this is achieved by the recombinant L. tarentolae vaccine through controlled infection without disease progression.
Evidence: Furthermore, it has been reported that persistence of a small number of live parasites is essential for maintaining durable immunity [24], [25]. The only way to meet this requirement is by using attenuated live vaccines.
we could not find parasites in the spleen in vaccinated groups (G1 and G2) but they could be seen in nearly all non-vaccinated groups (G3, G4 and G5, Fig. 5B).
159.Rationale: Although the authors cite the general notion that persistence of a small number of live parasites can maintain durable immunity and motivate live-attenuated vaccine approaches, the experimental data in this study do not demonstrate that the recombinant L. tarentolae vaccine establishes persistence of vaccine parasites. In fact, the investigators report they "could not find parasites in the spleen in vaccinated groups," so there is no direct evidence in the passage that vaccine-induced controlled persistence occurred. Therefore the specific claim that persistence was achieved by this vaccine and is the mechanism for durable immunity is not supported by the presented data.

159.Option: The combination of CPA/CPB and CPA/CPB-CTE antigens is more protective than individual forms, indicating that the C-terminal extension enhances immunogenicity and is essential for vaccine efficacy.
Evidence: Furthermore, we have demonstrated that the combination of CPA/CPB and CPA/CPB-CTE is more protective against visceral and cutaneous leishmanial infections than the individual forms [50], [51], [52], [53], [54].
Immunization with CTE also displayed both type 1 and 2 immune signatures in experimental murine model of L. infantum infection and therefore is not protective as a vaccine candidate [49].
159.Rationale: The passage does state that the combination of CPA/CPB and CPA/CPB-CTE was previously shown to be more protective than individual forms, which supports the first part of the option. However, the conclusion that the C-terminal extension (CTE) "enhances immunogenicity and is essential for vaccine efficacy" is not supported: the passage explicitly notes that immunization with CTE alone induced mixed type-1/type-2 responses and "therefore is not protective as a vaccine candidate." Thus, while combination vaccines were reported more protective, the text does not provide evidence that the CTE itself enhances immunogenicity or is essential for efficacy; indeed, it indicates CTE alone is not protective.

160.Option: The fact that both P. perniciosus and P. perfiliewi were more attracted to infected dogs in the field suggests that these sand fly species have evolved a specific preference for L. infantum-infected hosts as part of their natural feeding behavior.
Evidence: The numbers of P. perniciosus attracted to the infected dog, uninfected dog, and blank control were 20.44 ± 4.71, 46.78 ± 7.62, and 1.89 ± 0.63, respectively (Fig 7).
Infected dog attracted more P. perniciosus than uninfected dog (MCP: z = 3.666, P<0.001).
The numbers of P. perfiliewi attracted towards the infected dog, uninfected dog, and empty control cage were 27.22 ± 8.79, 9.39 ± 2.59 and 0.17 ± 0.09, respectively (Fig 9).
Infected dog attracted more P. perfiliewi than uninfected dog (EMMEANS: z = 7.931, p<0.001).

We hypothesized that physiological changes in dogs parasitized by L. infantum change the dog’s odour making them more attractive to P. perniciosus and therefore enhancing the parasite’ s transmission success.
160.Rationale: The passage documents that both sand fly species were more attracted to infected dogs in the field (quotes above). However, the option asserts an evolutionary explanation—that the sand flies "have evolved a specific preference" for infected hosts. The passage does not provide evidence of evolutionary adaptation of the vectors; instead it raises the hypothesis that infection changes host odour (a parasite-driven change) which could increase attractiveness. Inferring an evolved, innate vector preference from the observed short-term behavioral responses goes beyond the data presented and requires evolutionary evidence not contained in the passage. Therefore this causal/evolutionary claim is not supported by the passage.

160.Option: The significant attraction of both P. perniciosus and P. perfiliewi to infected dogs in the field suggests that the presence of infected dogs leads to a dilution of parasite transmission by increasing vector exposure to non-competent hosts.
Evidence: The numbers of P. perniciosus attracted to the infected dog, uninfected dog, and blank control were 20.44 ± 4.71, 46.78 ± 7.62, and 1.89 ± 0.63, respectively (Fig 7).
Infected dog attracted more P. perniciosus than uninfected dog (MCP: z = 3.666, P<0.001).
The numbers of P. perfiliewi attracted towards the infected dog, uninfected dog, and empty control cage were 27.22 ± 8.79, 9.39 ± 2.59 and 0.17 ± 0.09, respectively (Fig 9).
Infected dog attracted more P. perfiliewi than uninfected dog (EMMEANS: z = 7.931, p<0.001).

A minimum of 36% to 100% of the lab-colonised P. perniciosus that were fed on infected dogs developed L. infantum infections when dissected and examined under dissecting microscope as described by Chelbi and Zhioua (2019) [28], whereas none of the sand flies fed on uninfected dogs were found to be infected with parasites.
160.Rationale: The passage shows infected dogs attract more vectors (quotes above) and that sand flies feeding on infected dogs became infected (xenodiagnosis). The option claims a "dilution" effect (increased exposure to non-competent hosts) resulting from attraction to infected dogs. That is inconsistent with the evidence: infected dogs are competent reservoir hosts (they infect sand flies), so attracting more vectors to infected dogs would be expected to amplify, not dilute, transmission. The passage contains no support for a dilution effect, so this inference is not supported.

160.Option: The higher number of male P. perniciosus attracted to the infected dog compared to the uninfected dog indicates that male sand flies are more likely to transmit L. infantum to humans due to increased contact with infected reservoirs.
Evidence: Zoonotic visceral leishmaniasis (ZVL) is a vector-borne zoonotic disease caused by the parasite Leishmania infantum, which is transmitted by the bite of female phlebotomine sand flies.

The number of males P. perniciosus trapped in cages baited with the infected dog (34.11±8.18) was significantly greater than the mean number of males trapped in the cage baited with the uninfected dog (11.78 + 4.70) (EMMEANS: z = 3.666, P = 0.0034) (Fig 8).

The number of female P. perniciosus trapped in the cage baited with the infected dog (59.44 ± 11.82) was significantly greater than the number of males trapped (34.11 ± 8.18) (EMMEANS: z = 2.933, P = 0.0393).
160.Rationale: While the passage documents that more male P. perniciosus were trapped near infected dogs (quote above), transmission of L. infantum is explicitly described as occurring via the bite of female sand flies. The passage does not state that male sand flies transmit the parasite; in fact the transmission mechanism is limited to females. Therefore increased male contact with infected reservoirs does not imply increased transmission by males to humans. The inference that males are more likely to transmit is not supported by the passage.

160.Option: The significantly higher number of female P. perniciosus trapped in the cage baited with the infected dog compared to the uninfected dog indicates that female sand flies are more likely to feed on infected dogs due to increased blood meal size or nutritional quality.
Evidence: The number of female P. perniciosus trapped in the cage baited with the infected dog (59.44 ± 11.82) was significantly greater than the mean number of females trapped in cages baited with the uninfected dog (29.11 ± 7.31) (EMMEANS: z = 3.666, P = 0.0034).
The numbers of female P. perniciosus that fed on the infected and uninfected dogs were (186.16 ± 16.98), and (66.83 ± 7.58), respectively.
The proportion of P. perniciosus females that fed on infected and uninfected dogs were 0.90 ± 0.02 and 0.81 ± 0.03 respectively (Fig 4).
The number of eggs laid by each female P. perniciosus fed on infected dogs and uninfected dogs were 36.46 ± 0.16, and 43.35 ± 0.15, respectively (Fig 5).
160.Rationale: The passage supports that more female sand flies were attracted to and fed on infected dogs (quotes above). However, the option attributes this behavior to increased blood meal size or improved nutritional quality from infected dogs. The study did not measure blood meal size or nutritional quality; moreover, fecundity (number of eggs laid) was lower in females fed on infected dogs, which argues against improved nutritional quality (quote above). Therefore the causal claim about blood meal size or nutritional quality is not supported by the passage.

160.Option: The enhanced attractiveness of infected dogs to both female and male Phlebotomus perniciosus and Phlebotomus perfiliewi under natural conditions suggests a zoopotentiation effect that increases the transmission success of Leishmania infantum.
Evidence: Infected dog attracted more P. perniciosus than uninfected dog (MCP: z = 3.666, P<0.001).

The number of female P. perniciosus trapped in the cage baited with the infected dog (59.44 ± 11.82) was significantly greater than the mean number of females trapped in cages baited with the uninfected dog (29.11 ± 7.31) (EMMEANS: z = 3.666, P = 0.0034).

The number of males P. perniciosus trapped in cages baited with the infected dog (34.11±8.18) was significantly greater than the mean number of males trapped in the cage baited with the uninfected dog (11.78 + 4.70) (EMMEANS: z = 3.666, P = 0.0034).
Infected dog attracted more P. perfiliewi than uninfected dog (EMMEANS: z = 7.931, p<0.001).

A minimum of 36% to 100% of the lab-colonised P. perniciosus that were fed on infected dogs developed L. infantum infections when dissected and examined under dissecting microscope as described by Chelbi and Zhioua (2019) [28], whereas none of the sand flies fed on uninfected dogs were found to be infected with parasites.

We hypothesized that physiological changes in dogs parasitized by L. infantum change the dog’s odour making them more attractive to P. perniciosus and therefore enhancing the parasite’ s transmission success.
160.Rationale: The passage shows that infected dogs attracted significantly more P. perniciosus (both females and males) and P. perfiliewi in the field (quotes above). It also documents via xenodiagnosis that sand flies feeding on infected dogs become infected with L. infantum, whereas those feeding on uninfected dogs do not. Together, these findings indicate that infected dogs both attract vectors and serve as sources of infection, which is consistent with a zoopotentiation effect (infected hosts enhancing vector contact and hence parasite transmission). The authors explicitly hypothesized that infection-induced changes in host odour could enhance transmission success. Therefore the inference that infected dogs increase transmission success via enhanced attractiveness is supported by the passage.

161.Option: The use of reagent strips alone for diagnosing urinary schistosomiasis could reduce survey costs by 2%, but this approach would result in considerable misclassification of IUs for treatment in Southern Sudan due to low specificity, making it unsuitable for implementation.
Evidence: Survey costs could be lowered if the standard operating procedure were modified. Testing for urinary schistosomiasis infection could, for example, be conducted only with Hemastix reagent sticks (Bayer Diagnostics, Basingstoke, UK), instead of the current procedure whereby all samples with blood in urine and a sub-sample of negatives were cross checked using urine filtration.
By cutting out the cost of these and other supplies for urine filtration, the cost per implementation unit surveyed would have decreased by 2% (Table 3).

The rationale for cutting out urine filtration was that the use of reagent strips alone is a reliable diagnostic procedure in some African countries [35] and may, after validation, be applicable in high schistosomiasis transmission settings in Southern Sudan.
161.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that using Hemastix reagent sticks alone could have reduced the cost per implementation unit by 2% and that reagent strips alone have been reliable in some African countries and may be applicable after validation. However, the passage provides no evidence that reagent-strip-only testing would cause "considerable misclassification of IUs for treatment in Southern Sudan due to low specificity" or that it would be unsuitable. The claim of substantial misclassification and unsuitability is not supported by the text; in fact the text suggests validation could make reagent-only use acceptable. Therefore the option's negative assertion is not derivable from the passage.

161.Option: Survey costs in Southern Sudan are likely to be higher than in other countries due to the need for international staff and post-conflict reconstruction challenges, which increase both personnel and operational expenses.
Evidence: To capture the indirect costs of project management and administration we applied an overhead of 25% to the financial cost estimate for all budget lines. This rate is higher than those applicable to operations in more stable settings of eastern Africa [e.g. [33], but in our experience provides an accurate reflection of the cost involved in implementing programmes in a land-locked country undergoing post-conflict reconstruction.

Due to severe human resource constraints affecting Southern Sudan, only two national laboratory technicians per team could be recruited, with additional technicians recruited as short-term consultants from the Vector Control Division, MoH, Uganda.

The total financial costs for the survey amounted to USD 182,067, the majority of which was spent on recurrent items. The principal cost drivers were personnel (34.4%) followed by survey consumables (30.4%) (Table 2).
161.Rationale: The passage documents higher overheads applied because the country is land-locked and undergoing post-conflict reconstruction, explicitly noting that the 25% overhead rate is higher than rates in more stable eastern African settings. It also describes severe local human resource constraints necessitating recruitment of additional technicians from Uganda. Personnel are identified as the principal cost driver. Together, these statements support the inference that survey costs in Southern Sudan are likely elevated relative to more stable countries due to post-conflict operational challenges and the need for externally recruited staff.

161.Option: Capital costs accounted for only 4.3% of total survey costs, indicating that infrastructure investment is not a major concern in integrated NTD surveys, and therefore should be deprioritized in future planning.
Evidence: Because all assets above USD 100 in value and with a lifespan >1 year were annualised and only their time in use during the survey was included, capital cost amounted to only 4.3% of the total survey costs and were largely comprised of vehicle costs.
Owing to the poor infrastructure in Northern Bahr-el-Ghazal State and fluctuating security, teams established camps in locations that were centrally located between study communities.
Three Toyota Land Cruisers were used during the surveys.

In many cases, space to pitch tents was provided by non-governmental or faith-based organizations in their compounds, but a charge was levied. Otherwise teams stayed in local guesthouses. For camping, sleeping and cooking equipment, food, fuel and small generators were procured.
161.Rationale: While the passage states that capital costs comprised only 4.3% of total costs (largely vehicle costs), it also describes challenging operational conditions—poor infrastructure, security issues, the need to camp, and use of vehicles—which imply that infrastructure and capital items are operationally important. The low proportion of capital cost in this accounting reflects accounting choices (annualisation and including only time in use), not that infrastructure is unimportant. Therefore it is not supported to conclude infrastructure investment is not a major concern or should be deprioritized in planning.

161.Option: The major cost drivers of an integrated NTD survey in Southern Sudan were personnel and consumables, and this observation is likely applicable to most other settings, warranting detailed budgeting for these inputs during planning.
Evidence: The total financial costs for the survey amounted to USD 182,067, the majority of which was spent on recurrent items. The principal cost drivers were personnel (34.4%) followed by survey consumables (30.4%) (Table 2).
To improve on current designs it is important to understand the main drivers of survey costs and investigate potential effects of modifying standard operating procedures.

Such cost analyses should be undertaken using an approach that is 'generalisable', hence allowing comparison between settings and use of results to plan and budget for similar undertakings elsewhere [20], [21].
161.Rationale: The passage explicitly identifies personnel and survey consumables as the principal cost drivers in this integrated NTD survey. It also argues that cost analyses should be generalisable to allow planning and budgeting in other settings. From these statements it is reasonable and directly supported to infer that careful budgeting for personnel and consumables is essential when planning similar surveys elsewhere; the passage frames these findings as informative for comparable contexts.

161.Option: The cost of survey consumables is likely to be similar in other African countries, as most of them are not produced in the region, while personnel costs might differ considerably between settings, particularly when comparing Southern Sudan to countries that have not been affected by decades of conflict.
Evidence: quantities of ICT tests, safety lancets and other supplies required to safely take blood samples would be reduced by approximately 80%, incurring substantial savings on survey consumables, particularly ICTs that cost >USD 4.0 per test.

To capture the indirect costs of project management and administration we applied an overhead of 25% to the financial cost estimate for all budget lines. This rate is higher than those applicable to operations in more stable settings of eastern Africa [e.g. [33], but in our experience provides an accurate reflection of the cost involved in implementing programmes in a land-locked country undergoing post-conflict reconstruction.

Due to severe human resource constraints affecting Southern Sudan, only two national laboratory technicians per team could be recruited, with additional technicians recruited as short-term consultants from the Vector Control Division, MoH, Uganda.
161.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that consumables (e.g., ICT tests at >USD4/test) are an important cost component and that personnel costs vary because of post-conflict constraints and the need to recruit external technicians. However, the passage does not state that most consumables are not produced in the region or that their costs would therefore be similar across other African countries. The assertion about consumables' production origin and uniformity across countries is not supported by the text. The part of the option about personnel costs differing between settings, especially versus non-conflict countries, is supported, but the combined statement about consumables is not derivable from the passage.

162.Option: The seasonal variation in HFRS incidence is primarily driven by changes in air temperature, with outbreaks occurring during periods of high temperature and low wind conditions from late July to late September.
Evidence: Correlation between HFRS activity and air temperature varied widely
The associations observed at one scale were not present at another one.

Fifteen studies indicated the negative correlation between air temperature and HFRS activity, while seven studies found the positive correlation between air temperature and HFRS activity. There were also two studies that defined a certain temperature as the dividing point between HFRS activity and air temperature, the correlation curve was inverted ‘U-shaped’ [23,29]. Also, three studies did not find statistically significant associations between HFRS activity and air temperature.

The study from Changchun City, Jilin Province [18] and Changsha City [33] found that HFRS was prone to outbreaks and epidemics during relatively dry, high temperature and low wind conditions from late July to late September.
162.Rationale: Although some studies (e.g., Changchun and Changsha) reported outbreaks during relatively dry, high temperature and low wind conditions from late July to late September, the passage also explicitly states that correlations between air temperature and HFRS incidence varied widely (negative, positive, inverted U-shaped, or non-significant) and that associations observed at one scale were not present at another. The claim that seasonal variation is primarily driven by air temperature is too strong and not supported by the heterogeneous findings across studies and scales reported in the passage.

162.Option: The inconsistent findings across studies regarding the correlation between air temperature and HFRS incidence are primarily due to differences in statistical methods used, rather than variations in geographical scale or population characteristics.
Evidence: The choice of appropriate statistical methods according to the type of HFRS epidemic area, the availability, fineness and data distribution characteristics of historical data such as epidemic situation or meteorological data in various regions is being another challenge.

With regards to the statistical methods adopted by the researchers to explore the correlation between air temperature and HFRS incidence, Spearman correlation analysis, Pearson correlation analysis, generalized additive model, seasonal differential autoregressive moving average model, negative binomial multivariate regression analysis, distributed lag nonlinear model conditions, conditional logistic regression analysis and wavelet analysis have been indicated in the included studies, as shown in Table 1.
The associations observed at one scale were not present at another one.
Effect of air temperature on the HFRS activity varied among different populations
162.Rationale: The passage acknowledges that choice of statistical methods is a challenge and lists many methods used, but it also explicitly notes that associations differ by spatial scale and by population groups. The passage does not provide evidence that differences in statistical methods are the primary cause of inconsistent findings; instead it highlights multiple plausible sources of heterogeneity (geographical scale, population differences, seasonal effects). Therefore the statement that inconsistencies are primarily due to statistical methods is not supported by the passage.

162.Option: The correlation between air temperature and HFRS incidence is strongest in populations with high occupational exposure to rodents, such as agricultural workers, indicating that behavioral factors are more influential than meteorological factors in disease transmission.
Evidence: Effect of air temperature on the HFRS activity varied among different populations

The study in Huludao City, Liaoning province found that HFRS activity in the population aged 35–59 years was significantly affected by air temperature, but this phenomenon could not be found in the population of other age groups [25]; the authors of this Huludao study attributed the phenomenon to the increased virus exposure in agricultural labor work in the middle-aged population.

Fifteen studies indicated the negative correlation between air temperature and HFRS activity, while seven studies found the positive correlation between air temperature and HFRS activity. ... Also, three studies did not find statistically significant associations between HFRS activity and air temperature.
162.Rationale: The passage reports a study (Huludao) where the 35–59 age group showed significant temperature effects and the authors attributed this to increased agricultural exposure, which supports that occupational exposure can modify the temperature–HFRS relationship. However, the passage does not provide evidence that the correlation is universally strongest in occupationally exposed groups, nor does it support the stronger claim that behavioral factors are more influential than meteorological factors. Given the documented heterogeneity in temperature associations (positive, negative, null), one cannot conclude from the passage that behavioral factors outweigh meteorological factors overall.

162.Option: The effect of air temperature on HFRS activity varies by population group, suggesting that demographic characteristics should be considered when estimating or predicting the relationship between meteorological factors and HFRS incidence.
Evidence: Effect of air temperature on the HFRS activity varied among different populations

The study in Huludao City, Liaoning province found that HFRS activity in the population aged 35–59 years was significantly affected by air temperature, but this phenomenon could not be found in the population of other age groups [25]; the authors of this Huludao study attributed the phenomenon to the increased virus exposure in agricultural labor work in the middle-aged population.

This study in Huludao also indicated that the correlations between HFRS activity and air temperature were statistically significant in both males and females, while with different length of period, two months in the male and no lag in the female population [25].
162.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the effect of air temperature varied among different populations and provides concrete examples (age-specific effects, sex-specific differences in lag). These findings support the epidemiological inference that demographic characteristics (age, sex, occupation-linked behavior) modify the temperature–HFRS relationship and therefore should be accounted for when estimating or predicting disease risk from meteorological factors.

163.Option: The low incidence of visceral leishmaniasis in this cohort of HIV-infected individuals on long-term antiretroviral therapy with relatively high CD4 counts suggests that the risk of progression to overt disease is low in this subgroup, supporting the prioritization of interventions for patients diagnosed with both HIV and VL simultaneously.
Evidence: The majority of patients (95.5%) were on ART, with a median of 52.2 months on treatment (IQR 27.3–79.5).
At study enrolment, the median CD4 count was 377 cells/mm3 (IQR 250–518)
In January 2017, the accumulating evidence of the pilot study suggested the incidence of VL to be very low in the study population.
Amongst all individuals enrolled in the study, one was clinically diagnosed with VL at month nine of follow-up.
Given the poor prognosis once Leishmania infection has progressed to VL, tackling L. donovani infection before disease onset looks appealing.
163.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that most participants were on long-term ART with moderate median CD4 counts and that the observed incidence of clinical VL during follow-up was very low (one case), which supports the first clause that progression risk was low in this subgroup. However, the second clause — that this finding supports prioritizing interventions for patients diagnosed with both HIV and VL simultaneously — is not supported by the passage. On the contrary, the authors state that because prognosis is poor once VL develops, ‘‘tackling L. donovani infection before disease onset looks appealing,’’ indicating an argument for preventive/screen-and-treat strategies rather than prioritizing only those already coinfected. Thus the full statement (including the policy implication) cannot be derived from the presented evidence.

163.Option: The relatively high incidence of asymptomatic Leishmania infection in this cohort indicates that current screening strategies for HIV patients in VL-endemic areas are insufficient and should be expanded to include routine testing for Leishmania markers.
Evidence: Of the 462 individuals testing negative on all Leishmania markers at enrolment (Leishmania-negative individuals), 426 had follow-up samples available, with a total follow-up time of 353 person-years. Out of these, 36 developed asymptomatic infection, yielding an incidence rate of 10.2/100 person-years.
The estimated cumulative incidence was 5.0% by six months, and 9.5% by 12 months.
While we have recently developed the theoretical basis for such a strategy [12], the evidence base for this approach is currently completely lacking.
163.Rationale: The passage documents a measurable incidence of asymptomatic infection, but it does not provide evidence about the adequacy of existing routine screening programs or demonstrate that current strategies are failing in practice. Moreover, the authors explicitly state that the evidence base for a screen-and-treat strategy is lacking. From the provided data alone one cannot conclude that existing screening is insufficient nor that routine Leishmania testing should be implemented—such policy recommendations would require additional evidence on benefits, harms, and feasibility not presented in the passage.

163.Option: The observed reversion of Leishmania markers in nearly half of the patients with prevalent infection suggests that the immune system in HIV-infected individuals on ART can effectively clear Leishmania parasites without clinical intervention.
Evidence: Of the 49 cases with prevalent infection at baseline, 46 had follow-up samples available, with a total patient follow-up time of 33 person-years. Of these, 16 reverted to negative Leishmania markers during follow-up, with a reversion rate of 48.5/100 person years.
The estimated probability of reversion was 26.3% by six months, and 40.1% by 12 months (Table 5).
The majority of patients (95.5%) were on ART, with a median of 52.2 months on treatment (IQR 27.3–79.5).
In immunocompetent individuals, Leishmania infection will usually be controlled by the immune system and treatment of asymptomatic cases is currently not recommended [9].
163.Rationale: The passage reports that a substantial proportion of prevalent infections reverted to negative markers over follow-up (reversion rate ~48.5/100 person-years; ~40% by 12 months). These reversions occurred in a cohort in which nearly all patients were on long-term ART with reasonable median CD4 counts, and no treatment for asymptomatic infection was described. Coupled with the statement that immunocompetent hosts usually control Leishmania without treatment, the observed marker reversion in ART-treated HIV patients supports the inference that restored/maintained immunity on ART can lead to clearance (marker reversion) without specific clinical intervention in a subset of patients. This inference follows directly from the reported data.

163.Option: The association between concurrent malaria infection and asymptomatic Leishmania infection suggests that malaria may act as a cofactor that enhances immune suppression, thereby increasing the risk of Leishmania progression to clinical disease.
Evidence: Risk factors identified in univariate analysis were being male, sleeping outside and having malaria (Table 3).

Multivariate analysis showed the risk of asymptomatic Leishmania infection to be increased for male patients (OR 3.2, 95% CI 1.4–7.0, p = 0.002), and for patients with malaria infection (OR 6.1, 95% CI 1.9–18.9, p = 0.004) (Table 4).
Amongst all individuals enrolled in the study, one was clinically diagnosed with VL at month nine of follow-up.
163.Rationale: The passage demonstrates an association between malaria infection and increased odds of having asymptomatic Leishmania infection. However, it does not provide evidence on mechanism (i.e. malaria causing immune suppression) nor on whether malaria increases the risk of progression from asymptomatic infection to clinical VL. Only one clinical VL occurred, and no analysis linking malaria to progression to overt disease is presented. Therefore the causal claim that malaria acts as a cofactor enhancing immune suppression and increasing risk of progression is not supported by the data in the passage.

163.Option: The high prevalence of asymptomatic Leishmania infection in this cohort, combined with the detection of parasite DNA in blood and urine, suggests that asymptomatic individuals are likely to be infectious and could contribute to ongoing transmission of the disease in the community.
Evidence: At the time of recruitment, 7.4% of patients tested positive on the rK39 RDT, 4.3% on DAT, 0.2% on PCR and 0.2% on KAtex.
Of the 36 incident cases, five tested positive on rK39 only (rK39 seroconversion), 10 on DAT only, one on both DAT and rK39 and 20 on PCR only (Table 6).
There have also been concerns that some HIV patients infected with Leishmania might be more likely to transmit, also at the asymptomatic stage [3–5]. This would hamper VL control efforts.
163.Rationale: While the study documents asymptomatic infection prevalence and detection of parasite DNA/antigen in some participants, detection of markers in blood or urine does not by itself demonstrate infectiousness to sandfly vectors or transmission capability. The passage only notes that there have been concerns about possible transmission from HIV-infected individuals, but provides no empirical data on infectivity or transmission from asymptomatic persons in this cohort. Thus the stronger inference that asymptomatic individuals are likely infectious and contributing to community transmission cannot be derived from the presented results.

164.Option: Because plasma troponin T is elevated in infants with clinical beriberi and correlates with low basal ETK, it can be used as a definitive diagnostic test for infantile beriberi in resource-limited settings.

The odds of beriberi in infants with detectable plasma troponin T were 3.4 times higher when compared with infants without detectable troponin T (OR 3.4, 95%CI 1.22 to 9.73, P=0.019, n=28 matched sets providing 69 observations).

164.Rationale: The passage shows troponin T is elevated in cases and correlates with low basal ETK, but the diagnostic performance is modest (sensitivity ~78.6% and specificity ~56.1%). The study does not claim troponin T is a definitive diagnostic test, nor does it evaluate feasibility or validity as a standalone test in resource-limited settings. Therefore the assertion that it can be used as a definitive diagnostic test is not supported by the presented evidence.

The inability to compare maternal troponin T means no inference about the influence of maternal nutritional factors on infant troponin T can be drawn from this study.

164.Option: The study's findings indicate that plasma troponin T is a highly specific and sensitive biomarker for diagnosing infantile beriberi, with a specificity of 94.1% and sensitivity of 78.6% in this population.

The 94.1% specificity in the passage refers to ntBNP, not troponin T. Thus the numerical claim in the option is incorrect and not supported by the study data.

The odds of beriberi in infants with detectable plasma troponin T were 3.4 times higher when compared with infants without detectable troponin T (OR 3.4, 95%CI 1.22 to 9.73, P=0.019, n=28 matched sets providing 69 observations).

Furthermore, the passage explicitly notes cardiac markers could suggest the diagnosis 'in the absence of confounding diseases,' implying they can be elevated by other cardiac conditions, so the statement that troponin T is 'unaffected by other cardiac conditions' is unsupported.

Evidence: As heart failure is a consistent feature of ‘wet’ beriberi, recently described circulating markers of cardiac dysfunction, such as brain natriuretic peptide (BNP), N-terminal pro-brain natriuretic peptide (ntBNP) and troponin T [33], are likely to be elevated in infantile beriberi and could, in the absence of confounding diseases, suggest a diagnosis of beriberi.
infant basal ETK, activated ETK and plasma troponin T were the only infant biochemical markers that predicted infantile beriberi consistently (Table 1).

The odds of beriberi in infants with detectable plasma troponin T were 3.4 times higher when compared with infants without detectable troponin T (OR 3.4, 95%CI 1.22 to 9.73, P=0.019, n=28 matched sets providing 69 observations).
Detectable troponin T had a sensitivity (95%CI) of 78.6 (59.0 to 91.7) % and specificity (95%CI) of 56.1 (39.7 to 71.5) % for predicting infantile beriberi.

The authors also explicitly proposed that cardiac markers could suggest beriberi (noting the caveat of confounding cardiac disease). Therefore it is reasonable to infer that cardiac markers like troponin T could be useful surrogate markers for diagnosing infantile beriberi in this population.

165.Option: The projected FOI estimates from LF case data and reporting probabilities appear to be more accurate than the FOI estimates from our ensemble model that used spatial covariates to improve model predictions in areas lacking data, particularly in areas that are projected to have the highest incidence.
