Only 20% of the IUs identified as suitable had mean predictions that exceeded the threshold across all locations within IU boundaries.
The majority of IUs with high environmental suitability are located in Angola (81 IUs), the DRC (191 IUs), Ethiopia (94 IUs), Kenya (89 IUs), and Nigeria (79 IUs).

The model results show higher environmental suitability across most of the southern half of West Africa, the DRC, South Sudan, and western Ethiopia, as well as in areas of Tanzania and Mozambique. ... in other regions, we predict high suitability in the area bordering the Republic of the Congo, the DRC, and Angola, as well as in eastern and southern Malawi, northern Nigeria, western Kenya, and eastern Central African Republic;
195.Rationale: The passage documents substantial within-IU heterogeneity in predicted suitability (range up to 0.95 and only 20% of suitable IUs uniformly above the threshold). It also identifies the DRC, Nigeria, and Malawi as areas with high predicted suitability. From an epidemiological and programmatic standpoint, when an IU contains localized high-suitability pockets (foci), survey efforts that target those high-prediction sites will be more likely to detect transmission than randomly or uniformly distributed sampling across the IU. Thus prioritizing baseline survey site selection toward the highest predicted-suitability locations within heterogeneous IUs is a logical strategic implication directly supported by the passage.

195.Option: The model results suggest that a large proportion of IUs currently considered for elimination mapping surveys may be of lower priority for survey implementation, particularly in countries such as Ethiopia that may need to survey as many as 461 IUs.
Evidence: Of the IUs considered for elimination mapping, a total of 828 IUs (50·2%) had environmental suitability predictions that exceeded the 0·71 threshold in at least one grid cell.
The majority of IUs with high environmental suitability are located in Angola (81 IUs), the DRC (191 IUs), Ethiopia (94 IUs), Kenya (89 IUs), and Nigeria (79 IUs).
195.Rationale: While the passage states that 828 of the IUs considered for elimination mapping (50.2%) exceeded the suitability threshold—implying roughly half might be lower priority—it does not support the specific claim about countries such as Ethiopia 'needing to survey as many as 461 IUs.' The passage explicitly states Ethiopia had 94 IUs predicted to be suitable, not 461, and provides no figure or analysis indicating the precise number of lower-priority IUs per country. Therefore the specific numeric claim and the strong assertion about large proportions in particular countries cannot be derived from the passage.

195.Option: Because the model predicts high environmental suitability in areas with no known Simulium breeding sites, national programs should prioritize the construction of new breeding site monitoring systems in these regions.
Evidence: Ten covariates were included in the analysis based on known evidence of an association with presence of the vector. These included variables that represent climatic factors (aridity, precipitation, and daytime temperature), vegetation (enhanced vegetation index, tasseled cap brightness, and tasseled cap wetness), breeding sites near fast-flowing rivers (distance to rivers, slope, and elevation) and transmission occurring in rural areas (urbanicity).
The model results show higher environmental suitability across most of the southern half of West Africa, the DRC, South Sudan, and western Ethiopia, as well as in areas of Tanzania and Mozambique.
195.Rationale: The passage describes that the model used covariates related to breeding sites and presents areas with high predicted suitability. However, it does not state that these high-suitability areas lack known Simulium breeding sites, nor does it make any recommendation about constructing new breeding site monitoring systems. The leap from model-predicted suitability to prioritizing construction of monitoring systems in areas 'with no known breeding sites' is not supported by the text; there is no evidence provided about the presence or absence of known breeding sites in the specific predicted areas or about the need to build monitoring infrastructure. Thus the prescriptive action cannot be derived from the passage.

195.Option: The model results indicate that over half of the IUs currently under ivermectin MDA for the elimination of lymphatic filariasis may also be suitable for onchocerciasis transmission, suggesting that MDA for onchocerciasis should be extended in these areas to prevent loss of progress.
Evidence: Among IUs currently under MDA with ivermectin for the purpose of LF elimination, 495 (63%) were predicted to have at least one grid cell exceeding 0·71.
The results of this analysis could be used by national programmes and implementing partners to identify priority areas for mapping surveys.
195.Rationale: The passage supports the first clause: 63% (495) of IUs under LF MDA were predicted to have at least one grid cell exceeding the suitability threshold, so over half are potentially suitable for onchocerciasis. However, the second clause recommending extension of MDA for onchocerciasis is an intervention decision that the passage does not make or directly justify. The authors frame the model as a tool to identify priority areas for mapping surveys, not as direct evidence to extend MDA without primary mapping or programmatic deliberation. Therefore the policy action ('MDA should be extended') is not derivable solely from the passage.

195.Option: The model's high AUC of 0.90 and low RMSE of 0.38 indicate that it can reliably predict the exact magnitude of onchocerciasis transmission in any given implementation unit, allowing for precise resource allocation.
Evidence: Model fit statistics for AUC were 0·90 and RMSE was 0·38.

The BRT method models environmental suitability of onchocerciasis transmission as a suitability index (from 0 to 1) based on the values of environmental covariates at the locations corresponding to occurrence inputs.
We then implemented 100 BRT models to generate the mean, lower 2·5th percentile, and 97·5th percentile predictions for every 5 × 5-km location.
Only 20% of the IUs identified as suitable had mean predictions that exceeded the threshold across all locations within IU boundaries.
195.Rationale: Although the model shows good discrimination (AUC 0.90) and reports RMSE, the passage clarifies the model predicts an environmental suitability index (not direct measured prevalence or the exact magnitude of transmission) and explicitly reports uncertainty (bootstrap percentiles) and substantial within-IU heterogeneity. The authors also convert suitability to a binary classification using a threshold rather than claiming precise numeric prevalence estimates per IU. Therefore the claim that the model can reliably predict the exact magnitude of transmission in any IU for precise resource allocation is not supported by the passage.

196.Option: The use of platelet transfusions without meeting WHO criteria is associated with improved clinical outcomes, despite higher costs.
Evidence: Platelet transfusion occurred in 17.7% (51/288) of the dengue cases, 35.2% of which (18/51) did not meet the WHO criteria.

The total costs were higher (p = 0.040) in the group using platelets that did not meet the WHO criteria (18/51), or US $1,090.6 (IQR, US $1,454. 3 to US $506.6), compared with the total costs in the group that was administered platelets and met the WHO criteria (n = 33), or US $595.6 (IQR, US $1,185.7 to US $344.8).
However, we observed no differences in SD hospitalizations or in clinical outcomes between the public and the private health care systems.
196.Rationale: The passage documents that platelet transfusions given without meeting WHO criteria occurred and were associated with higher costs, but it provides no data showing improved clinical outcomes for those patients. In fact, the passage explicitly states no differences in clinical outcomes between public and private systems, and no outcome data are linked to non-guideline transfusions. Therefore the claim of improved outcomes is not supported by the presented evidence.

196.Option: The use of WHO recommendations for dengue management may result in cost savings by reducing unnecessary hospitalizations and the use of blood products.
Evidence: In this study, 51 individuals were AWC, which represented 18.1% of all dengue cases (n = 288). These cases represented 11.1% (US $23,343.7) of the total hospital health care costs. 74.5% of the AWC patients (n = 38) were hospitalized in a private institution.
Platelet transfusion occurred in 17.7% (51/288) of the dengue cases, 35.2% of which (18/51) did not meet the WHO criteria.

The total costs were higher (p = 0.040) in the group using platelets that did not meet the WHO criteria (18/51), or US $1,090.6 (IQR, US $1,454. 3 to US $506.6), compared with the total costs in the group that was administered platelets and met the WHO criteria (n = 33), or US $595.6 (IQR, US $1,185.7 to US $344.8).

The criteria for hospitalization based on the WHO recommendations are as follows: i) patients with co-existing conditions that may make dengue or its management more complicated (such as pregnancy, infancy, old age, obesity, diabetes mellitus, renal failure or chronic hemolytic disease); ii) patients with certain social circumstances (such as living alone or living far from a health facility, without reliable means of transport); and iii) patients who require emergency treatment and urgent referral due to SD [2].
196.Rationale: The passage shows a measurable proportion of admissions (AWC) and platelet transfusions occurred without meeting WHO criteria and that these non-guideline uses contribute substantially to costs (AWC = 11.1% of total hospital costs; non-criteria platelet transfusions have higher median costs). From an epidemiological and health economics perspective, adherence to WHO recommendations that aim to limit hospitalization and transfusion to indicated cases would be expected to reduce unnecessary resource use and therefore save costs. This inference is directly supported by the presented cost data.

196.Option: The underfunding of the SUS, as evidenced by the fixed payment of US $135.4 per dengue hospitalization, directly causes higher rates of severe dengue cases in the public health care system.
Evidence: Therefore, the SUS transfers a fixed amount to accredited hospitals using Hospital Admission Authorization (Autorização de Internação Hospitalar - AIH), or US $135.4 per hospitalization for dengue cases [26], [27].

This amount includes the costs of daily hospitalization, medications, supplies, laboratory tests and X-rays and remains constant, regardless of the number of hospitalization days or tests required. However, additional fee is paid when computed tomography (CT); magnetic resonance imaging (MRI); or other special procedures, such as a transfusion or ICU use, are required.
However, we observed no differences in SD hospitalizations or in clinical outcomes between the public and the private health care systems.
196.Rationale: While the passage documents the fixed SUS payment per hospitalization, it does not present any evidence that this funding level causes higher rates of severe dengue in the public system. In fact, the study reports no differences in severe dengue hospitalizations or clinical outcomes between public and private systems. Causation ('directly causes') is not supported by the data provided.

196.Option: The lack of significant difference in hospitalization costs between DWWS and DWS cases suggests that the revised WHO guidelines are ineffective in guiding clinical decision-making.
Evidence: A comparison of the length of the hospital stay and costs stratified by classification (DWWS, DWS and SD) and the type of health care system showed significant differences (Table 6), including between SD and DWS (p<.0001) and between SD and DWWS (p=0.0002). However, when we compared DWS and DWWS, there was no statistically significant difference (p=0.6447).

The new classification was based on a list of clinical warning signs suggestive of a severe disease outcome and classified dengue into dengue without warning signs (DWWS); dengue with warning signs (DWS)... and severe dengue (SD).
196.Rationale: The passage does report no statistically significant difference in costs between DWS and DWWS. However, concluding that the revised WHO guidelines are ineffective is not supported by the data: cost similarity alone does not demonstrate ineffectiveness in guiding clinical management (many factors could explain similar costs, including practice patterns, case mix, or reimbursement structures). The passage provides no direct evidence that the guidelines fail to guide clinical decision-making.

196.Option: The higher costs in the private sector are primarily due to the greater use of advanced imaging and laboratory tests, which are not covered by the SUS.
Evidence: When we compared the length of hospitalization and the costs of the hospitalized cases according to illness categories (WHO criteria) and the health care systems, private had significantly higher cost than public, except for SD cases (p = 0.3307) (Table 4).

This amount includes the costs of daily hospitalization, medications, supplies, laboratory tests and X-rays and remains constant, regardless of the number of hospitalization days or tests required. However, additional fee is paid when computed tomography (CT); magnetic resonance imaging (MRI); or other special procedures, such as a transfusion or ICU use, are required. These additional costs may contribute to the variations in the total amount paid by the SUS to the hospitals for each case [26], [27].
196.Rationale: The passage establishes that private-sector hospitalizations had higher costs and that the SUS payment is fixed and excludes separate payment for complex procedures (CT, MRI, transfusion, ICU). However, the study does not provide direct data attributing the higher private costs primarily to greater use of advanced imaging or laboratory tests. The statement asserts a primary cause that is not demonstrated in the presented data, so it cannot be concluded from the passage alone.

197.Option: The survival of the African green monkey in this study was associated with an earlier transition from innate to adaptive immunity, as indicated by the timing of B cell recovery and the early increase in cytokines linked to adaptive immune activation.
Evidence: no antibody response until 10 dpi at which time one of the animals, the survivor (8197), had evidence of IgM and neutralizing antibody titers (Fig 1A and 1B).

In all six animals the B cell populations decreased over the course of the acute disease (defined as 0–12 dpi) with populations starting to recover in four of the six animals beginning at 10 dpi (Fig 4).

This animal had marked increases in several cytokines and chemokines between days 10 and 16 post-infection that correlate with increases in IgG production and improved clinical health. These cytokines included IL-1β, IL-1RA, IL-2, IL-4, IL-5, IL-8, IL-15, TNFα, MIP-1α (CCL3), MIP-1β (CCL4), TGFα and GM-CSF (Figs 2 and 3).
The increases in IL-2, IL-4, IL-5, GM-CSF and MIP-1β are all potentially associated with the transition from innate to adaptive immunity.
197.Rationale: The passage documents that the surviving animal (8197) showed the first detectable antibody (IgM and neutralizing) at 10 dpi and that B cell populations began to recover beginning at 10 dpi in multiple animals. It also explicitly states the survivor had marked increases in cytokines between days 10–16, and identifies several of these (IL-2, IL-4, IL-5, GM-CSF, MIP-1β) as potentially associated with the transition from innate to adaptive immunity. Together these temporally concordant findings support the epidemiologic inference that survival was associated with an earlier transition from innate to adaptive immunity as evidenced by B cell recovery and adaptive-associated cytokine increases.

197.Option: The high dose of NiV-M exposure led to a more consistent and robust systemic inflammatory response, which was associated with improved survival outcomes in the African green monkey model.
Evidence: There were also some apparent dose specific responses with peak IFN-γ levels generally higher in high dose group animals (Fig 2A, blue squares).
The IL-1RA levels were markedly higher in all three high dose group animals at 12 days post-infection while levels in the low dose group remained much lower (Fig 2B).
Similarly, peak levels of IL-1β, IL-2, and MIP-1β were higher in all three high dose group animals than any of the low dose group animals (Figs 2C, 2D and 3E).
All animals developed acute disease with no clear observational differences between the low and high dose groups.
One of the six inoculated animals survived the infection and recovered with no evident sequelae. This animal received a dose of approximately 400 pfu of NiV.
197.Rationale: While the passage does describe dose-specific differences in cytokine responses (higher peak IFN-γ, IL-1RA, IL-1β, IL-2, MIP-1β in high dose animals), it does not provide evidence that a more robust systemic inflammatory response conferred improved survival. Only one animal survived overall, and although that animal received ~400 pfu (near the low end of the reported high-dose range), the text also states there were "no clear observational differences between the low and high dose groups." The passage contains no direct evidence linking high-dose–associated inflammation to better survival; therefore the causal claim that high dose led to improved survival is not supported by the presented data.

197.Option: The animal that survived the infection had a more robust and persistent increase in NiV antigen-positive NK cells compared to the other animals, suggesting that NK cell infection may have contributed to viral clearance and survival.
Evidence: Interestingly, the surviving animal had the most robust and persistent increase in NiV antigen positive NK cells (Fig 11E).

In the surviving animal, the NK cell population was essentially unchanged through day 10 post-infection (Fig 10A and 10B), but then increased on day 12 and remained elevated through study termination.
The proliferating NK cell population in the surviving animal peaked at day 12 with over 40% of the cells Ki67+.

While the presence of viral antigen is intriguing, this does not mean these cell populations are infected and that virus is replicating. However, the persistent presence of NiV in some peripheral cell populations, and NK cells in particular, is suggestive of viral replication. Specific analyses to determine if NiV was replicating in these cell types are a component of on-going studies.
197.Rationale: The factual component — that the survivor showed the most robust and persistent increase in NiV antigen-positive NK cells and had marked NK proliferation — is directly supported by the passage. However, the interpretation that 'NK cell infection may have contributed to viral clearance and survival' is a causal inference not supported by the data presented. The authors explicitly caution that detection of viral antigen in cell populations does not prove productive infection or replication, and specific analyses to determine replication are pending. Therefore while NK antigen positivity and proliferation are observed in the survivor, the causal claim that NK cell infection contributed to clearance and survival cannot be derived from the passage alone.

197.Option: The survivor's immune system demonstrated a delayed but sustained adaptive immune response, as evidenced by the late appearance of neutralizing antibodies and a gradual increase in CD8+ T cell proliferation, which allowed for eventual viral control.
Evidence: no antibody response until 10 dpi at which time one of the animals, the survivor (8197), had evidence of IgM and neutralizing antibody titers (Fig 1A and 1B).

The IgM titers of 8197 peaked at 14 dpi when a NiV-specific IgG response became apparent with evidence of NiV-specific IgG1 antibodies, but not IgG2 antibodies (Fig 1C). ... The neutralizing antibody titer of 8197 plateaued at 23 dpi where it remained until the end of the study.

the proliferation of CD8+ T cells, however, showed a marked increase in the percentage of Ki67+ CD8+ T cells in the survivor late in the acute phase of disease that was sustained during convalescence (Fig 8C and 8D).
the survivor developed a classic antibody response and T cell response indicative of protective adaptive immunity.
197.Rationale: The passage documents that the surviving animal first showed neutralizing antibodies at 10 dpi, with IgG appearing by 14 dpi and neutralizing titers plateauing by 23 dpi, indicating an adaptive antibody response that arose after the initial infection and was maintained. It also reports a marked increase in proliferating CD8+ T cells in the survivor late in the acute phase that was sustained into convalescence. The authors explicitly note the survivor developed an antibody and T cell response indicative of protective adaptive immunity. These observations support the inference that the survivor mounted a delayed (post–acute onset) but sustained adaptive immune response (humoral and cellular) that corresponded with eventual control of infection.

197.Option: The survival of the African green monkey was likely due to a sustained increase in CD4+ T cell populations during the acute phase, which is consistent with a protective adaptive immune response observed in other Nipah virus models.
Evidence: In five of six animals the overall population of CD4+ T cells increased over the course of the disease while in the survivor (8197), the CD4+ T cell population decreased, even when evaluated over only the acute phase of disease (Fig 5A–5C, S1 Table).
the survivor developed a classic antibody response and T cell response indicative of protective adaptive immunity.
197.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports that, unlike most animals, the survivor's CD4+ T cell population decreased over the course of disease, not increased. Therefore the claim that survival was due to a sustained increase in CD4+ T cells during the acute phase is contradicted by the data. The passage instead attributes the survivor's protection to a combination of humoral and cellular adaptive responses (neutralizing antibody, CD8+ T cell proliferation, cytokine changes), not to a sustained CD4+ T cell increase.

198.Option: The MP-12 vaccine candidate is safer than scMP-12 because it does not replicate in the central nervous system of immunocompromised hosts.
Evidence: However, intraperitoneal (i.p.) inoculation of young mice with MP-12 can result in efficient virus replication in the central nervous system (CNS) (J. Morrill et al, unpublished data).
Furthermore, i.p. inoculation of SCID mice with MP-12 results in the development of neurological signs and death of all mice [29].
The scMP-12 did not show any sign of neurovirulence after intracranial inoculation into suckling mice, demonstrating its safety.

All MP-12 infected mice died by 3 days p.i., whereas all mice inoculated with scMP-12 or HBSS survived and did not show any clinical signs of disease (Fig. 6), demonstrating the absence of detectable levels of neurovirulence in scMP-12.
198.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that MP-12 can replicate in the CNS of young and immunocompromised (SCID) mice and caused neurological signs and death, while scMP-12 showed no neurovirulence in intracranially inoculated suckling mice and all scMP-12–inoculated mice survived. Therefore the statement that MP-12 is safer because it does not replicate in the CNS of immunocompromised hosts contradicts the reported data and cannot be derived from the passage.

198.Option: The scMP-12 vaccine candidate is more immunogenic than MP-12 because it elicits higher neutralizing antibody titers in mice following a single immunization.
Evidence: The mean PRNT80 titers of the mice immunized with 105 PFU of scMP-12 and 105 PFU of VRP were 1∶238 and 1∶38, respectively;
mice inoculated with 105 PFU of MP-12 ... had a mean PRNT80 titer of 1∶1,477 and 1∶310, respectively.
198.Rationale: At the same dose (105 PFU) MP-12 elicited a substantially higher mean PRNT80 (1:1,477) than scMP-12 (1:238). Thus the passage shows scMP-12 is not more immunogenic than MP-12 following a single immunization; in fact MP-12 at 105 PFU produced higher neutralizing titers. (scMP-12 titers were comparable to MP-12 given at the lower 104 PFU dose, but that does not support the claim that scMP-12 is more immunogenic than MP-12.)

198.Option: The scMP-12 vaccine candidate is superior to MP-12 in safety due to its lack of neurovirulence, despite being less immunogenic.
Evidence: The scMP-12 did not show any sign of neurovirulence after intracranial inoculation into suckling mice, demonstrating its safety.

All MP-12 infected mice died by 3 days p.i., whereas all mice inoculated with scMP-12 or HBSS survived and did not show any clinical signs of disease (Fig. 6), demonstrating the absence of detectable levels of neurovirulence in scMP-12.

The mean PRNT80 titers of the mice immunized with 105 PFU of scMP-12 and 105 PFU of VRP were 1∶238 and 1∶38, respectively; ... mice inoculated with 105 PFU of MP-12 ... had a mean PRNT80 titer of 1∶1,477 ...
198.Rationale: The passage provides direct experimental evidence that scMP-12 lacks detectable neurovirulence in neonatally intracranially inoculated mice while MP-12 caused fatal neurovirulence under similar conditions. The immunogenicity data show scMP-12 (mean PRNT80 1:238 at 1e5 PFU) elicits lower neutralizing titers than MP-12 at 1e5 PFU (1:1,477), consistent with being less immunogenic. Combining these findings, it is epidemiologically reasonable to infer that scMP-12 has a superior safety profile with the trade-off of somewhat lower immunogenicity.

198.Option: The scMP-12 vaccine candidate is less effective than MP-12 in preventing viremia and viral replication in target organs after wild-type challenge, as demonstrated by higher viral titers in immunized mice.
Evidence: In contrast, mice immunized with VRP or MP-12 showed neither viremia nor virus replication in the liver, spleen or brain.

scMP-12 immunization also prevented viremia and virus replication in the liver and spleen, while two mice, one having no detectable PRNT80 titer and the other having a PRNT80 titer of 1∶20, showed virus replication in the brain at day 9 p.i.
198.Rationale: The data show scMP-12 prevented viremia and replication in liver and spleen (same as MP-12), but in two vaccinated mice there was detectable brain replication at day 9. Thus scMP-12 was not generally less effective in preventing viremia or peripheral organ replication, although it was slightly less effective than MP-12 at completely preventing brain replication in a small number of animals. The option overstates the findings by claiming broadly higher viral titers and failure to prevent viremia/organ replication, which is not supported by the quoted data.

198.Option: The neurovirulence of MP-12 in neonatal mice indicates that scMP-12 may also be neurovirulent in immunocompromised humans, despite its safety in mice.
Evidence: However, intraperitoneal (i.p.) inoculation of young mice with MP-12 can result in efficient virus replication in the central nervous system (CNS) (J. Morrill et al, unpublished data). Furthermore, i.p. inoculation of SCID mice with MP-12 results in the development of neurological signs and death of all mice [29].
The scMP-12 did not show any sign of neurovirulence after intracranial inoculation into suckling mice, demonstrating its safety.

All MP-12 infected mice died by 3 days p.i., whereas all mice inoculated with scMP-12 or HBSS survived and did not show any clinical signs of disease (Fig. 6), demonstrating the absence of detectable levels of neurovirulence in scMP-12.
198.Rationale: The passage provides experimental animal data showing MP-12 can be neurovirulent in young or immunocompromised mice, while scMP-12 showed no detectable neurovirulence in the same neonatal mouse model. Inferring that scMP-12 may still be neurovirulent in immunocompromised humans is speculative and not supported by the presented data. There is no human data or direct evidence in the passage to justify extrapolation of mouse neonatal neurovirulence findings to immunocompromised humans for scMP-12.

199.Option: The seasonal patterns of Shigella in Tanzania mirror the seasonality of malnutrition, previously described, suggesting that Shigella infections may be a direct cause of malnutrition in this population.
Evidence: Peak prevalence was observed in May/June in Bangladesh, June in Nepal, July/August in India, November in South Africa, and February in Tanzania.
even in the absence of diarrheal symptoms, Shigella has been associated with impaired linear growth [6,8,9].
199.Rationale: The passage documents the timing of peak Shigella prevalence in Tanzania (February) and notes associations between Shigella and impaired linear growth. However, it does not present any data linking the seasonality of Shigella to the seasonality of malnutrition, nor does it establish temporality or causation that would support the claim that seasonal Shigella infections directly cause malnutrition in Tanzania. The inference that seasonality mirrors malnutrition patterns and that Shigella is a direct cause of malnutrition is not supported by the provided data.

199.Option: Because Shigella culture has low sensitivity, especially in young children, and because recent antibiotic use was not associated with reduced detection, the use of culture-based diagnostics should be abandoned in favor of molecular methods in all low-resource settings.
Evidence: Considering qPCR the gold standard, the overall sensitivity of culture was 6.6%.
In the subset of Shigella-attributable diarrheal stools (n = 736)... sensitivity improved to 17.5%, and ranged from 0% in South Africa and Tanzania to 24.0% in Peru.
Culture positivity was strongly associated with age, such that attributable diarrhea stools among younger children were less likely to be culture positive (Table 4).
Recent macrolide treatment was also associated with reduced detection by culture (aRR: 0.57, 95% CI: 0.30, 1.08; Table 4).
199.Rationale: While the passage clearly shows very low sensitivity of culture (supporting the superior sensitivity of qPCR) and lower culture positivity in younger children, it also reports that recent macrolide treatment was associated with reduced culture detection. Thus the statement that recent antibiotic use was not associated with reduced detection is contradicted by the passage. Moreover, recommending that culture-based diagnostics be abandoned 'in all low-resource settings' is a policy prescription that goes beyond the evidence presented. The passage supports the superiority of molecular methods in sensitivity but does not provide operational, cost-effectiveness, or generalizability data required to mandate abandoning culture everywhere.

199.Option: The presence of Shigella in non-diarrheal stools with no clinical symptoms indicates that most Shigella infections are subclinical and therefore pose no public health concern.
Evidence: The prevalence of Shigella was 11.5% (n = 4744) overall, and was higher in diarrheal stools than non-diarrheal stools (18.4% vs. 10.1%; p<0.0001).

Almost half (611/1407, 43.4%) of children with Shigella detected in one or more non-diarrheal stools never had a diarrhea episode in which Shigella was detected, and almost two-thirds (900/1407, 64.0%) never experienced a Shigella-attributable diarrhea episode.
even in the absence of diarrheal symptoms, Shigella has been associated with impaired linear growth [6,8,9].

Myeloperoxidase levels were 0.33 log(ng/mL) (95% CI: 0.27, 0.40) higher in stools with Shigella, and the association was greater in diarrheal stools (mean difference: 0.65...) than in non-diarrheal stools (mean difference: 0.32...).
199.Rationale: The passage documents many detections of Shigella in non-diarrheal stools, indicating a substantial proportion of subclinical infections. However, it also presents evidence that subclinical Shigella is associated with biomarkers of intestinal inflammation (e.g., elevated myeloperoxidase) and cites associations with impaired linear growth. Therefore it is not supported to conclude that these infections 'pose no public health concern.' The evidence indicates subclinical infections are common and potentially consequential.

199.Option: The high burden of Shigella disease documented in MAL-ED highlights the potential utility of Shigella vaccines, as almost all children were exposed to Shigella by two years of age in most sites, suggesting a pathogen-specific population-based prevention strategy is warranted.
Evidence: By two years of age, 82.0% (n = 1407) of children had been infected with Shigella (Fig 1A), and 29.6% (n = 507) had at least one episode of Shigella-attributable diarrhea (Fig 1B).

Increasing rates of fluoroquinolone and macrolide resistance have highlighted the need for novel interventions, and particularly increased the urgency of the development of a Shigella vaccine [10], which may offer a more sustainable solution.
199.Rationale: The passage documents a very high cumulative incidence of Shigella infection by age two (82%), significant attributable diarrhea incidence, and notes rising antimicrobial resistance and explicit urgency for vaccine development. From an epidemiologic and public health perspective, a high, early-life exposure burden and limited treatment options reasonably support the inference that pathogen-specific, population-based prevention (e.g., vaccines) could be useful. This conclusion is directly supported by the provided data and commentary.

199.Option: The high incidence of Shigella-attributable diarrhea in older children suggests that interventions should focus on improving hygiene practices in school-aged children rather than infants.
Evidence: Incidence of Shigella-attributable diarrhea was higher in older age groups (incidence rate ratio: 1.75, 95% CI: 1.70, 1.81 per 6-month increase in age).
By two years of age, 82.0% (n = 1407) of children had been infected with Shigella (Fig 1A), and 29.6% (n = 507) had at least one episode of Shigella-attributable diarrhea (Fig 1B).
199.Rationale: The passage shows that within the first two years of life, incidence increases with age (i.e., older infants/toddlers have higher incidence). However, this study only covers children up to two years, not school-aged children. The recommendation to prioritize school-aged children over infants is not supported by the data presented. Moreover, the passage also highlights vulnerability and clinically important outcomes in infants (e.g., first episodes with dehydration), which argues against deprioritizing interventions for infants. Therefore the specific programmatic shift to school-aged children cannot be derived from the presented results.

200.Option: The mating rhythm of Cx. quinquefasciatus is more sensitive to clk gene disruption than that of Ae. albopictus, suggesting that the molecular clock in nocturnal mosquitoes is more dependent on clk for rhythmic output.
Evidence: The total mating rate of AalclkΔ293 at night (45.8%) was significantly higher than that of WT group (30.8%; GLM, χ2 = 5.65, d.f. = 1, P = 0.017), but there was no significant difference between AalclkΔ293 and WT group in daytime mating rates (GLM, χ2 = 1.61, d.f. = 1, P = 0.205) (Fig 2E).

Comparisons of the total mating rate during LD conditions revealed that the total mating rate of CxqclkΔ98 at night (0.0%) was significantly lower than that of WT group (13.3%; GLM, χ2 = 18.46, d.f. = 1, P < 0.0001). The total mating rate of CxqclkΔ98 during daytime (9.2%) was significantly higher than that of WT group (1.7%; χ2 = 5.23, d.f. = 1, P = 0.022) (Fig 2J).

clk mutation led to a phase disruption in the diel mating rhythm under LD cycle, resulting in Ae. albopictus showing a nighttime mating peak, and Cx. quinquefasciatus showing mating during the daytime but not at night.

These results indicated that the relative abundance of desat1 in Ae. albopictus and Cx. quinquefasciatus had contrasting and opposite trends of daily oscillation.
These results support the conclusion that the clk gene orthologs are involved in regulating the oscillatory expression of their respective desat1 genes.

201.Option: The increase in CFA positivity from 2 to 8 cases in TAS 2, despite passing the threshold, indicates that the WHO criteria for stopping MDA are too lenient and should be revised to prevent resurgence.
Evidence: The current World Health Organization (WHO) criteria states that TAS can be initiated once pre-TAS sentinel site and spot checks show <2% LF antigenemia among the population over 5 years old in a given evaluation unit (EU), which is typically a district or a combination of districts [4].

WHO guidelines then state that MDA can be stopped in the EU if prevalence of LF antigenemia among children this age in the first TAS (TAS 1) is below 2% in regions where Anopheles or Culex mosquitoes are the main vectors or below 1% where Aedes mosquito species are the main vectors [4].

TAS 1 was conducted in 2015 using a cluster survey of 30 primary schools, and two children were identified as antigen-positive using the rapid filariasis test strip (FTS) (Alere, Scarborough, ME), which detects circulating filarial antigenemia (CFA) for adult worms. This was below the critical cutoff of 2% for regions with Culex vectors, and MDA in the department was stopped.
Nippes underwent TAS 2 in 2017 and passed again, however the number of CFA-positive children had increased to eight.

201.Rationale: The passage documents the numerical increase from 2 to 8 CFA-positive children and describes the WHO thresholds and stopping criteria, but it does not provide evidence that the WHO criteria are intrinsically 'too lenient' or that they should be revised. The authors explicitly call for additional research and guidance rather than concluding that the criteria are invalid. Concluding that WHO criteria must be revised is a policy judgment that cannot be directly derived from the single-EU observations and concerns presented; it would require broader evidence on performance of the criteria across settings. Therefore the statement oversteps what can be inferred from the passage.

201.Option: The fact that random sampling in neighboring EAs identified only a low number of CFA-positive cases suggests that transmission in Miragoane is isolated and unlikely to spread to surrounding communes.

201.Rationale: While the data show fewer CFA-positives in neighboring EAs at the time of sampling and higher concentration in Miragoane, the passage does not provide sufficient evidence to conclude that transmission is truly isolated or unlikely to spread. Surveillance was limited in scope and time; the authors note concerns about cluster-based surveys missing hotspots and recommend follow-up in communities with antigen-positive children. Thus the low neighbor counts cannot be taken as definitive evidence that transmission will not spread to surrounding communes.

202.Option: The higher abundance of haemoglobin subunit beta-2 in CBA/Ca mice is directly responsible for their resistance to Ascaris by enhancing oxygen delivery to the liver.

consistent, a fatal B. lanceolatus envenomation case with diffuse thrombotic microangiopathy (TMA) causing multiple cerebral, myocardial, and mesenteric infarctions on autopsy supported the possible onset of venom-induced endotheliopathy [47].

203.Option: An initial step of venom-induced hypercoagulability with increased thrombin generation could be considered followed by a secondary step of hypocoagulability with B. atrox venom or persistent hypercoagulability with B. lanceolatus venom, despite their similar proteomic composition and in vitro procoagulant activity.

Similarly, the time to peak significantly decreased with both venoms at H3 (p = 0.001 for B. atrox and p = 0.006 for B. lanceolatus) (Fig 3B).
In parallel, the peak height significantly increased with the two venoms at H3 (p = 0.04) (Fig 3C).
The endogenous thrombin potential only significantly increased with B. atrox venom at H3 (p = 0.02) (Fig 3D).
Significant thrombocytopenia was observed with B. atrox venom at H3 (p = 0.04), H6 (p = 0.02), and H24 (p < 0.001) while B. lanceolatus venom had no effect on platelet count (Fig 2C).
MCF of B. atrox venom-treated rats was significantly lower than controls (p = 0.004; Figs 2B and S2) [at H24].

B. lanceolatus venom significantly increased the start tail and the peak height at H24 (p = 0.03 and p = 0.002, respectively) (Figs 4C and 4D and S3).
203.Rationale: The passage shows that both venoms induce an early (H3) rise in thrombin generation parameters (shorter lag time and time-to-peak, higher peak height), consistent with an initial hypercoagulable phase. Subsequent ex vivo measures diverge by species: B. atrox causes significant thrombocytopenia and reduced MCF by H24 (consistent with evolving hypocoagulability/consumptive coagulopathy), whereas B. lanceolatus shows increased fibrinogen at H24 and increased fibrinography parameters, consistent with persistent or later hypercoagulability. These temporal dynamics occur despite the venoms' similar composition and in vitro procoagulant activities, which the authors also note. Thus the proposed two-step pattern (initial hypercoagulability then species-specific divergence) is directly supported by the presented time-course data.

203.Option: The early increase in plasma fibrinogen at H24 in B. lanceolatus envenomation is primarily due to direct activation of hepatic fibrinogen synthesis by venom-induced thrombin-like enzymes.
Evidence: At the opposite, plasma fibrinogen was significantly increased at H24 with B. lanceolatus venom (p < 0.001; Fig 2D).

Therefore the specific mechanistic claim (primary cause = direct hepatic activation by TLEs) cannot be derived from the presented data.

Evidence: Surprisingly, venom composition of these two Bothrops species are relatively similar with comparable activities on coagulation in vitro in the whole blood and plasma [48].
However, in vitro assays have limitations as they only offer a view of the immediate venom-induced effects whereas envenomation is a complex dynamic process.

MCF of B. atrox venom-treated rats was significantly lower than controls (p = 0.004; Figs 2B and S2).
At the opposite, plasma fibrinogen was significantly increased at H24 with B. lanceolatus venom (p < 0.001; Fig 2D).

203.Option: The lack of significant platelet count reduction with B. lanceolatus venom indicates that thrombocytopenia is not a relevant factor in the pathogenesis of thrombosis in this envenomation.
Evidence: Significant thrombocytopenia was observed with B. atrox venom at H3 (p = 0.04), H6 (p = 0.02), and H24 (p < 0.001) while B. lanceolatus venom had no effect on platelet count (Fig 2C).
Thrombosis occur in cerebral, myocardial or pulmonary vessels and may be fatal or lead to major functional sequelae in the absence of antivenom administration [39,40].

consistent, a fatal B. lanceolatus envenomation case with diffuse thrombotic microangiopathy (TMA) causing multiple cerebral, myocardial, and mesenteric infarctions on autopsy supported the possible onset of venom-induced endotheliopathy [47].
203.Rationale: The passage shows that B. lanceolatus venom did not reduce platelet counts in the rat model, and that thrombosis has been reported clinically with this species. However, the absence of thrombocytopenia in this model does not prove that thrombocytopenia is irrelevant to thrombosis pathogenesis generally. The data do not measure thrombosis directly in the model, nor do they exclude other contexts (species, severity, timing) in which platelet consumption might contribute. Moreover, thrombosis can occur via multiple mechanisms (endotheliopathy, ADAMTS13 changes, coagulation activation) independent of platelet count. Therefore concluding that thrombocytopenia is not a relevant factor is not supported by the presented evidence.

204.Option: The LLMF72 qPCR assay demonstrated superior sensitivity compared to microscopy, enabling detection of Loa loa infection in individuals with subclinical disease who would otherwise be missed in population-based screening.
Evidence: Sixteen of the blood spots in this set were collected from individuals who were apparently amicrofilaremic by microscopy. Five of these samples, however, contained detectable L. loa genomic DNA by qPCR (predicted organism burden 1–7 mf/mL).
Overall, our qPCR assay was positive for 65 of 68 samples with parasitologically proven L. loa microfilaremia (sensitivity 96%) following various methods of DNA extraction.

Using the spiked blood samples as a standard curve to estimate the concentration of L. loa organisms present in each blood spot, there was a significant positive correlation between the extent of microfilaremia predicted by qPCR and the level confirmed previously by microscopy (figure 3a, Spearman r = 0.74; P<0.0001).

The positive correlation with microscopy further supports that qPCR is measuring true organism burden. Together these data support the epidemiologic inference that the LLMF72 qPCR can detect low-level infections that microscopy misses, i.e., it has superior sensitivity for identifying infections that would be overlooked in population screening.

Evidence: Sixteen of the blood spots in this set were collected from individuals who were apparently amicrofilaremic by microscopy. Five of these samples, however, contained detectable L. loa genomic DNA by qPCR (predicted organism burden 1–7 mf/mL).
Quality of template was confirmed for all samples using a control primer/probe set targeting a conserved region of the eukaryotic 18S ribosomal RNA gene (Applied Biosystems, Carlsbad, CA, USA).

Neither qPCR assay detected genomic DNA from B. malayi, O. volvulus, W. bancrofti, or M. perstans (10-fold dilutions from 10 ng to 0.1 pg), although all samples were detected in a linear fashion using the 18S rRNA primer/probe set (data not shown).

It also states that template quality was checked with an 18S control and that the assays were specific (did not detect other filarial DNAs). The claim that these positives are 'likely due to cross-contamination' is speculative and not supported by the presented data; therefore this inference cannot be derived solely from the passage.

204.Option: The high concordance between qPCR and microscopy in samples with detectable microfilaremia suggests that both methods are equally reliable for population-based screening of Loa loa infection.
Evidence: Using the spiked blood samples as a standard curve to estimate the concentration of L. loa organisms present in each blood spot, there was a significant positive correlation between the extent of microfilaremia predicted by qPCR and the level confirmed previously by microscopy (figure 3a, Spearman r = 0.74; P<0.0001).

A second set of 36 blood spots was subjected to automated DNA extraction by easyMAG, and concentration of mf was again estimated by LLMF72 qPCR. Among this set, there was a strong linear correlation between predicted and observed microfilaremia (figure 3b, r2 = 0.88; P<0.0001).
There were two samples positive by microscopy (20 mf/mL and 200 mf/mL) but negative by qPCR.

Sixteen of the blood spots in this set were collected from individuals who were apparently amicrofilaremic by microscopy. Five of these samples, however, contained detectable L. loa genomic DNA by qPCR (predicted organism burden 1–7 mf/mL).
204.Rationale: Although the passage reports strong correlations between qPCR and microscopy for many samples, it also documents discordant results in both directions (microscopy-positive/qPCR-negative and microscopy-negative/qPCR-positive). These discrepancies indicate the methods are not equivalently reliable across all samples, so the claim that they are 'equally reliable' for population screening is not supported by the data presented.

204.Option: The LLMF72 qPCR assay is not suitable for population-based studies because it requires fresh whole blood and specialized laboratory equipment, limiting its use in endemic areas.
Evidence: Blood spots were partitioned into 6 mm circular sections (10 µL dried blood each) using disposable sterile biopsy punch tools (Acuderm, Inc., Ft. Lauderdale, FL, USA).
Boiling resulted in detection of L. loa DNA by LLMF72 qPCR in all 12 samples.
A second set of 36 blood spots was subjected to automated DNA extraction by easyMAG, and concentration of mf was again estimated by LLMF72 qPCR.
qPCR was performed in an ABI 7900 sequence detection system using Taqman fast chemistry reagents (Applied Biosystems, Carlsbad, CA, USA) and primer/probe sets described in table 1.
204.Rationale: The passage demonstrates that the assay can be performed on dried blood spots and that a simple boiling extraction yielded detectable DNA, which supports applicability to field-collected samples. While the assay does use real-time PCR instrumentation (specialized equipment), the passage provides direct evidence of methods compatible with population-based sampling (dried blood spots, boiling). The categorical statement that the assay is not suitable for population-based studies is not supported by the data in the passage.

204.Option: The LLMF72 qPCR assay detected low-level microfilaremia in 5 of 16 samples that were classified as amicrofilaremic by microscopy, suggesting that the molecular assay may identify infections missed by conventional methods due to sampling error.
Evidence: Sixteen of the blood spots in this set were collected from individuals who were apparently amicrofilaremic by microscopy. Five of these samples, however, contained detectable L. loa genomic DNA by qPCR (predicted organism burden 1–7 mf/mL).
Microscopic quantitation of microfilaremia was performed on 50 µL fresh aliquots at the time of collection.

The corrected estimates were then divided by the volume of blood processed for DNA extraction (50 µL for boiled samples vs. 20 µL for all other samples) to determine the final estimates of mf concentration for each blood spot sample.
204.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports that qPCR detected low-level L. loa DNA (predicted 1–7 mf/mL) in five samples that microscopy called amicrofilaremic. The text also documents the small sample volumes used for microscopy and for DNA extraction/analysis, which creates a plausible mechanism (sampling-related detection limits) for why microscopy might miss low-density infections. Therefore the epidemiologic inference that the molecular assay can identify infections missed by conventional microscopy, plausibly because of sampling error in low-burden infections, is supported by the passage.

205.Option: The comparable fructose prevalence observed in Ae. albopictus in the Torres Strait Islands and in Mali suggests that attractive targeted sugar baits (ATSBs) trials would be warranted to determine their potential to reduce the risk of Ae. albopictus spreading to mainland Australia.
Evidence: An initial study on Yorke Island found fructose prevalences of 36% and 28% in Ae. albopictus males and females, respectively [20].
Recently, attractive targeted sugar baits (ATSBs) were proposed to control outdoor mosquitoes, including Ae. albopictus [14–18].
Trials of ATSBs on Ae. albopictus in Israel and the United States demonstrated over 80% reductions in Ae. albopictus populations [15–18].

The aim of this study was to quantify Ae. albopictus abundance and sugar feeding over time on different islands within the Torres Strait. This information is fundamental for understanding the potential of ATSBs to control Ae. albopictus across the islands beyond the cordon sanitaire.
205.Rationale: The passage provides data on fructose prevalence in the Torres Strait (Yorke Island) and discusses ATSBs and their demonstrated effectiveness elsewhere, and it states that quantifying sugar feeding is fundamental to assessing ATSB potential. However, the option claims a specific comparison to Mali (‘comparable fructose prevalence in Mali’), but the passage contains no information about Mali or fructose prevalences outside the Torres Strait other than trials in Israel and the United States. Because the Mali comparison is not present in the passage, the specific inference as worded cannot be derived solely from the provided text. The passage does support that ATSB trials in the Torres Strait would be reasonable to evaluate (based on local sugar-feeding data and prior successful trials elsewhere), but the stated Mali comparison is unsupported; therefore the option is incorrect as written.

205.Option: The higher fructose content in female Ae. albopictus compared to males suggests that females are more dependent on natural sugar sources, making them more vulnerable to ATSBs.
Evidence: Overall, females took larger fructose meals than males (31.2 µg ± 0.9 in females; 29.4 µg ± 0.8 in males; GLMM, P < 0.001).
Fructose positive male Ae. albopictus were more prevalent overall than female fructose positives (31.6% ± 1.4 in males and 30.5% ± 1.2 in females; GLMM, P = 0.002, Figs 1(II) and 2(II)).
It has been proposed that attractive targeted sugar baits have the potential to be effective where vectors frequently feed on sugar or where natural sugar sources are limited [19].
205.Rationale: The passage does show that individual females had larger fructose meal sizes than males, which is evidence of greater meal size per feeding. However, it also reports that males had a slightly higher prevalence of fructose-positivity (i.e., were more often sugar-positive). The option interprets larger fructose content as indicating greater dependence on natural sugar sources and therefore greater vulnerability to ATSBs. That leap is not fully supported by the passage: dependence would require evidence of frequency and necessity beyond a larger single-meal size, and the passage provides mixed sex-specific findings (higher prevalence in males but larger meal size in females). While ATSBs could target sugar-feeding behavior (and the passage notes ATSBs may be effective where vectors feed on sugar), the specific claim that females are more dependent and therefore more vulnerable is not directly derivable from the presented data. Thus the option overstates what can be concluded from the passage.

205.Option: The presence of flowers and/or fruit in vegetation is the most reliable predictor of Ae. albopictus abundance and sugar feeding behavior across the Torres Strait Islands.
Evidence: Overall, 40% of plant occurrences had some form of flowers (even at a low flowering density) and 19% had fruits, and the presence of any potential sugar source (flowers and/or fruit) was analysed as a single category.

The results from the GLMM showed that Ae. albopictus abundance was significantly associated by island, sex, collection time, canopy, PC2 (variation in vegetation gradients), habitat and the presence of flowers and/or fruits (GLMM Table 1 and Figs 1(I) and 2(I)).
The prevalence of fructose was significantly associated by collection time, sex and PC2 (Table 1 and Fig 1(II)).

Principal Component 2, derived from the PCA of plant composition data, exerted a notable influence on fructose prevalence, suggesting that specific combinations of plant species were correlated with variations in fructose positivity.
205.Rationale: The passage indicates that presence of flowers and/or fruits is one factor associated with Ae. albopictus abundance, but abundance and sugar-feeding behavior are also associated with multiple other variables (island, sex, collection time, canopy, habitat, and vegetation PC2). For fructose prevalence specifically, the passage states it was significantly associated with collection time, sex and PC2 rather than simply presence of flowers/fruits. The role of plant community composition (PC2, Phyllanthaceae) is emphasized for sugar positivity. Because multiple predictors influence abundance and sugar feeding and the passage does not identify flowers/fruits presence as the single or most reliable predictor, the statement that it is the most reliable predictor is not supported by the evidence.

205.Option: The higher fructose prevalence in male Ae. albopictus compared to females suggests that male mosquitoes are more likely to be involved in the transmission of arboviruses due to increased sugar feeding activity.
Evidence: Fructose positive male Ae. albopictus were more prevalent overall than female fructose positives (31.6% ± 1.4 in males and 30.5% ± 1.2 in females; GLMM, P = 0.002, Figs 1(II) and 2(II)).
Aedes albopictus and Ae. aegypti are both vectors of dengue, Zika and chikungunya viruses [3,4].
Sugar feeding is essential to the survival of both male and female mosquitoes.
205.Rationale: While the passage documents that male Ae. albopictus had a slightly higher fructose prevalence than females and notes that these species are vectors of arboviruses, it does not provide any evidence linking male sugar feeding activity to arbovirus transmission. Transmission of dengue, Zika and chikungunya is a function of infectious blood feeding, which the passage does not attribute to males. The passage does not state that males transmit arboviruses or that increased sugar feeding increases transmission risk. Therefore, the claim that higher male fructose prevalence implies greater likelihood of involvement in transmission is not supported by the passage and cannot be derived from the provided evidence.

205.Option: The seasonal timing of sugar feeding in Ae. albopictus, peaking in the morning, indicates that vector control efforts should be timed to coincide with peak mosquito activity to maximize effectiveness.
Evidence: Fructose positive Ae. albopictus were almost two times more prevalent in the morning than in the afternoon (42.0% ± 1.9 in morning and 22.0% ± 1.9 in afternoon; GLMM, P < 0.001).

The average fructose amount in Ae. albopictus collected in the morning was almost 60% more than that found in the afternoon (38.48 µg ± 1.1 in the morning and 24 µg ± 0.7 in the afternoon; GLMM, P < 0.001).
Recently, attractive targeted sugar baits (ATSBs) were proposed to control outdoor mosquitoes, including Ae. albopictus [14–18].
Sugar feeding is essential to the survival of both male and female mosquitoes.
205.Rationale: The passage provides clear, statistically significant evidence that both the prevalence of sugar feeding and the amount of fructose per mosquito are substantially higher in the morning than in the afternoon. It also describes ATSBs as an intervention targeting sugar feeding and notes the essential role of sugar meals for survival. From an epidemiological and operational perspective, timing vector control measures (including deployment of ATSBs or other interventions aimed at sugar-feeding mosquitoes) to coincide with peak sugar-feeding activity (morning) is a logical inference supported by the data presented. Therefore, the recommendation to time control efforts to peak activity is derivable from the passage.

206.Option: The higher prevalence of active T. solium infections in African communities, despite similar exposure levels to the parasite compared to Latin America and Asia, suggests that factors in Africa may enhance the establishment of viable cysticerci in humans after exposure.
Evidence: Similar sero-epidemiological studies conducted in two endemic communities in Africa and Latin America, presented comparable serological results for exposure to T. solium eggs with exposure levels of 34.55% and 31.22%, respectively but presented significant differences when reporting active infections with almost 12 times more infections in the African than in the Latin American community [16;18], suggesting that there were significant variations in the conditions for transmission and in the establishment of infection in each community.

The random effects model used in the meta-analysis gave an overall estimated prevalence for circulating T. solium antigens in Africa of 7.30% (95% CI [4.23–12.31]) from 9 studies. The overall estimated prevalence for circulating T. solium antigens in Latin America was 4.08% (95% CI [2.77–5.95]) from 5 studies and 3.98% (95% CI [2.81–5.61]) for Asia from 5 studies.
206.Rationale: The passage explicitly documents instances where antibody-based measures of exposure were similar between an African and a Latin American community while antigen-based measures of active infection were much higher in the African community ("almost 12 times more infections"). The authors state this pattern "suggesting that there were significant variations in the conditions for transmission and in the establishment of infection in each community." Therefore it is epidemiologically reasonable to infer that, in at least some African communities, post-exposure processes (environmental, behavioral, host or parasite factors) may favor establishment of viable cysticerci more than in the compared Latin American/Asian settings. This inference follows directly from the presented comparative serological data and the authors' interpretation.

206.Option: The lack of association between pig ownership and pork consumption with T. solium infection in African communities suggests that human-to-human transmission is the dominant route of infection in Africa, unlike in Latin America and Asia.
Evidence: The identified risk factors for the presence of T. solium circulating antigens in Africa were: insufficient latrines or sanitary toilets [35], washing hands by dipping method [67], history of taeniasis or proximity to tapeworm carriers [37;39], being male [36] and increased risk with age [18;32;35–37;39], while the identified risk factors for presence of anti T. solium antibodies were: insufficient latrines or sanitary toilets, and increased risk with age [35].

In Latin America the only identified risk factor for presence of T. solium circulating antigens was associated with increased age [60], while the identified risk factors for presence of T. solium antibodies were: insufficient latrines or sanitary toilets [45;53;59], lack of potable water [45], poor personal hygiene [41], deficient house hygiene [41], earthen floor [45], presence of infected pigs [48;52], pig owning [45;52;53;59], pork consumption [41;59], history of taeniasis/proximity to tapeworms carriers [41;43;51;52;58], being female [43;46;59], age (risk increased with age) [16;40;42;57;60], increased risk at younger age [54], low education level [45;54], presence of a sewage system [57].

Pigs contract porcine cysticercosis (PCC) by ingestion of viable T. solium eggs contained in feces from human tapeworm carriers. HCC occurs when humans accidentally ingest T. solium eggs and develop the larval stage of T. solium in different tissues.
206.Rationale: While the passage lists risk factors and shows that pig-related factors (pig owning, presence of infected pigs, pork consumption) were identified in Latin America (and pig ownership in Asia), those pig-related factors are not listed among the reported significant risk factors for African studies. The passage does report "history of taeniasis or proximity to tapeworm carriers" as a risk factor in Africa, which is consistent with human fecal-oral exposure. However, concluding that human-to-human transmission is the dominant route in Africa (and that this differs from other regions) is stronger than the data support. The absence of pig-related risk factors in the African risk-factor list could reflect differences in the studied populations, study power, reporting, or context-specific ecology rather than definitive dominance of one transmission route. The passage does not present direct comparative analyses proving dominance of human-to-human transmission in Africa, so the option overstates what can be inferred.

206.Option: The higher prevalence of circulating antigens in African communities compared to Latin America and Asia indicates that the T. solium parasite is more virulent in African populations, leading to more severe and persistent infections.
Evidence: The random effects model used in the meta-analysis gave an overall estimated prevalence for circulating T. solium antigens in Africa of 7.30% (95% CI [4.23–12.31]) from 9 studies. The overall estimated prevalence for circulating T. solium antigens in Latin America was 4.08% (95% CI [2.77–5.95]) from 5 studies and 3.98% (95% CI [2.81–5.61]) for Asia from 5 studies.

Similar sero-epidemiological studies ... presented comparable serological results for exposure to T. solium eggs with exposure levels of 34.55% and 31.22%, respectively but presented significant differences when reporting active infections with almost 12 times more infections in the African than in the Latin American community [16;18], suggesting that there were significant variations in the conditions for transmission and in the establishment of infection in each community.
206.Rationale: Although antigen prevalence estimates are higher in Africa in the cited studies, the passage contains no data on parasite virulence, severity, or persistence of infection by region. Higher antigen prevalence can result from many factors (greater exposure-to-infection conversion, environmental/behavioral conditions, host susceptibility, diagnostic differences), but virulence (intrinsic severity of the parasite strain) is not measured or described. The passage itself interprets regional differences as variations in transmission conditions and establishment of infection, not as evidence of greater parasite virulence. Therefore the claim about increased virulence and more severe/persistent infections is not supported by the provided data.

206.Option: The significantly higher seroprevalence of T. solium antibodies in Africa compared to Latin America and Asia indicates that African populations have a stronger immune response to the parasite, leading to more persistent antibody production.
Evidence: For the anti T. solium antibodies seroprevalence, the overall estimated seroprevalence in Africa was 17.37% (95%CI [3.33–56.20]) from 2 studies, while in Latin America it was 13.03% (95% CI [9.95–16.88]) from 20 studies and in Asia 15.68% (95% CI [10.25–23.24]) from 2 studies.
The prevalence of circulating T. solium antigens was not significantly different between continents and neither was the anti T. solium antibodies seroprevalence.
206.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that antibody seroprevalence was not significantly different between continents, so it does not support the premise of a "significantly higher" seroprevalence in Africa. Even if point estimates differ, inferring a stronger or more persistent immune response in African populations requires immunological data (e.g., antibody kinetics, titers, duration) which are not provided. Differences in seroprevalence are more parsimoniously attributed to exposure or epidemiologic factors rather than intrinsic differences in immune response; the passage does not provide evidence to make the immunological claim, so this option is not supported.

206.Option: The presence of sewage systems in households was associated with higher positivity to EITB in a village in northern Peru, suggesting that improved sanitation infrastructure may inadvertently increase the risk of T. solium transmission in some settings.
Evidence: In Latin America the ... identified risk factors for presence of T. solium antibodies were: insufficient latrines or sanitary toilets [45;53;59], lack of potable water [45], poor personal hygiene [41], deficient house hygiene [41], earthen floor [45], presence of infected pigs [48;52], pig owning [45;52;53;59], pork consumption [41;59], history of taeniasis/proximity to tapeworms carriers [41;43;51;52;58], being female [43;46;59], age (risk increased with age) [16;40;42;57;60], increased risk at younger age [54], low education level [45;54], presence of a sewage system [57].
The EITB has a sensitivity ranging from 97 to 98% and a specificity ranging from 97 to 100% to detect circulating antibodies to T. solium in human serum.
206.Rationale: The passage notes that "presence of a sewage system [57]" was identified as a risk factor for antibody presence in Latin America, but it provides no contextual details (for example, that this finding came from "a village in northern Peru") within the quoted text, nor does it describe mechanisms why improved sanitation infrastructure would increase transmission. An observed association between presence of a sewage system and seropositivity may reflect confounding, reverse causation, poorly constructed sanitation leading to contamination, or other contextual factors; the passage does not provide causal interpretation. Therefore the specific causal suggestion that improved sanitation "inadvertently increases the risk" is not supported by the passage, and the geographic detail in the option (northern Peru) is not present in the provided text. Hence this inference is not derivable from the passage alone.

207.Option: The use of in-house immunofluorescence assays in this study limits the validity of the Q fever seropositivity findings, making it impossible to draw conclusions about disease burden.
Evidence: There were enough kits to test 200 samples by the commercial assay, while the other 404 samples needed to be tested with material produced in-house by a national reference laboratory.
positive and negative controls were from patients previously tested in the laboratory, with positive and negative titers of 1:16.
Seropositivity of antibodies against C. burnetii was observed in 129 samples (21.36%) (95% CI 18.15–24.85), revealing past exposure to the pathogen.
207.Rationale: The passage documents the use of both commercial and in-house IFA assays, the use of positive and negative controls, and reports a concrete seropositivity estimate (21.36%). Nothing in the passage states that use of the in-house assay invalidates the findings or makes it impossible to draw any conclusions about disease burden in the studied sample. Therefore the strong claim that conclusions are impossible due solely to use of an in-house assay is not supported by the passage.

207.Option: The absence of seropositivity for Brucella spp. antibodies in this study suggests that brucellosis is not endemic in the state of São Paulo, despite previous reports of animal infections.
Evidence: All samples tested were negative for brucellosis. One patient was reactive to the disease through AAT. However, the individual was classified as negative in the confirmatory test, discarding the disease status.
Brucella spp. is the causative agent of brucellosis, an endemic disease in animals in Brazil, and lacks investigations in human populations.
human brucellosis may be underestimated in São Paulo, considering the status of animal brucellosis in the region with a prevalence of 2.4%
207.Rationale: While the study found no confirmed human seropositivity for Brucella among these sampled patients, the passage also notes animal brucellosis prevalence in the region and that human brucellosis may be underestimated. A zero result in this convenience sample of dengue-negative febrile patients does not logically support the broad conclusion that brucellosis is not endemic in the entire state. The passage provides no population-representative data or negative surveillance evidence to justify declaring brucellosis non-endemic.

207.Option: The lack of association between Q fever seropositivity and municipality size suggests that urban populations are equally at risk of infection as rural populations, regardless of animal exposure.
Evidence: Most patients were from large cities, but in contrast, populations from rural municipalities showed higher positivity than the other populations.
For the other variables analyzed, there were no significant associations between the groups.
207.Rationale: The passage reports that most patients came from large cities yet rural municipalities showed higher positivity, and that no statistically significant associations were found for variables other than symptom duration. It does not, however, state that urban and rural populations are equally at risk 'regardless of animal exposure.' The statement adds an inference about equivalence of risk and the role of animal exposure that is not supported by the data presented. Thus the specific claim about equal risk irrespective of animal exposure cannot be derived from the passage.

207.Option: The higher seropositivity rate for Q fever compared to brucellosis among patients with dengue-like symptoms suggests that Q fever may play a more critical role in febrile illness in the state of São Paulo than brucellosis.
Evidence: All samples tested were negative for brucellosis. One patient was reactive to the disease through AAT. However, the individual was classified as negative in the confirmatory test, discarding the disease status.
Seropositivity of antibodies against C. burnetii was observed in 129 samples (21.36%) (95% CI 18.15–24.85), revealing past exposure to the pathogen.

The aim of this study was to investigate the presence of antibodies against C. burnetii and Brucella spp. infection in patients with systemic clinical signs and negative for dengue, attended by the public health service of the state of São Paulo, Brazil, and therefore contribute to the elucidation of possible differential diagnosis of fever of unknown origin in the state.
207.Rationale: In the studied sample of dengue-negative febrile patients the authors found a substantial Q fever seropositivity (21.36%) while no confirmed brucellosis seropositivity was detected. For this specific studied population (patients with dengue-like illness seeking public health services), these findings support the inference that Q fever is a more important contributor to febrile illness than brucellosis. The conclusion is limited to the studied population, which matches the option's wording.

207.Option: The high seropositivity rate for Q fever in patients with prolonged symptoms indicates that Q fever is more likely to cause chronic illness than acute illness in this population.
Evidence: patients with 14–20 days of symptoms had 2.12 (95% CI 1.34–3.35) times more chances of being seropositive for Q fever than patients with 7–13 days, and patients with 21–27 days of fever had 2.62 (95% CI 1.27–5.41) times more chances of being seropositive for Q fever than patients with 7–13 days.

In the first chart, we see that the median (Min-Max) of positives regarding symptom duration was 11 (7–70) days, while the median of negatives was 9 (7–300) days. A significant difference was observed between the medians, indicating an association between symptom duration and Q fever seropositivity.

Of the 46 seropositive patients by commercial immunofluorescence test, 29 (63%) had phase II antibody titers higher than 128 and were therefore defined as having acute Q fever. Regarding chronic Q fever, three patients (6.5%) had phase I antibody titers higher than 1024, suggesting that these patients had a persistent infection.
207.Rationale: Although seropositivity was associated with longer symptom duration in this sample, serological titration results indicate that most seropositive cases met criteria for acute Q fever (phase II high titers) while only a small minority had titers consistent with chronic Q fever (phase I titers >1024). Therefore the data do not support the inference that Q fever is more likely to cause chronic rather than acute illness in this population.

208.Option: The presence of bacteria in the submucosa at 22 days post-infection is primarily due to the increased permeability of the intestinal barrier caused by L4 larvae, without active contribution from parasite movement.
Evidence: At 22 d.p.i., we observed fractures in the cecum, revealing the mucosa, submucosa and muscle layers, with L4 larvae causing several ruptures in the mucosa (Fig 2C) and penetrating into the syncytial tunnels (Fig 2D).
In Fig 1C, the larva is located in the mucosa, inserted, close to a region highly populated with bacteria.

At this stage, we observed the nematode larvae inside the syncytial tunnel within the epithelial cells at the surface of the mucosa adjacent to the lumen (Fig 1B) and the presence of bacterial structures inside the tissue in the submucosal layer (Fig 1D; Inset).
Together, these results strongly suggest a dynamic association between the attachment and movement of the parasite and bacterial translocation.
revealing how nematode movement promotes bacterial translocation from the intestinal lumen to underlying tissues, such as the lamina propria and submucosa (S1 Movie).
208.Rationale: The passage explicitly links bacterial translocation to parasite attachment and movement. It reports bacteria adhered to the nematode cuticle, larvae inside syncytial tunnels, and the authors conclude there is a "dynamic association between the attachment and movement of the parasite and bacterial translocation," and that 3D models reveal "how nematode movement promotes bacterial translocation." Therefore it is not supported to assert that submucosal bacteria at 22 d.p.i. are primarily due to passive increased permeability alone and that parasite movement had no active contribution. The passage provides direct evidence that parasite movement/attachment actively facilitates translocation.

208.Option: Bacterial translocation into the intestinal submucosa occurs during the acute phase of infection, beginning at 17 days post-infection, and is facilitated by the mechanical action of nematode movement and attachment, not solely by tissue rupture.
Evidence: The invasive process was confirmed to start at 17 days after infection.

At 17 d.p.i., larvae from the L3 larval stage were penetrating into the intestinal mucosa, and a large number of bacilliform bacteria were present on the mucosa surface and adhered to the nematode cuticle (Fig 1B and 1C).

we observed the nematode larvae inside the syncytial tunnel within the epithelial cells at the surface of the mucosa adjacent to the lumen (Fig 1B) and the presence of bacterial structures inside the tissue in the submucosal layer (Fig 1D; Inset).
Together, these results strongly suggest a dynamic association between the attachment and movement of the parasite and bacterial translocation.
revealing how nematode movement promotes bacterial translocation from the intestinal lumen to underlying tissues, such as the lamina propria and submucosa (S1 Movie).
208.Rationale: The passage states that the invasive process begins at 17 days post-infection and documents bacteria in the submucosa at that time. The authors observed bacteria adhered to the nematode cuticle, larvae within syncytial tunnels, and explicitly state a "dynamic association between the attachment and movement of the parasite and bacterial translocation," with models "revealing how nematode movement promotes bacterial translocation." These statements support the inference that translocation begins at ~17 d.p.i. during the acute phase and is facilitated by mechanical action of the parasite rather than being attributable solely to passive tissue rupture.

208.Option: The increase in body temperature observed at 22 days post-infection is a direct result of bacterial translocation and systemic inflammation, indicating the onset of sepsis.
