The text also reiterates known mechanical transmission routes for Anaplasma and cites examples of ked incompetence for other pathogens. Thus the stronger claim that keds support a biological transmission cycle (sustaining/amplifying the pathogen) is not supported by the presented evidence.

243.Option: The low prevalence of 'Ca. Anaplasma camelii' in camel keds, despite high infection rates in camels, suggests that transmission by keds is mechanical rather than biological.
Evidence: The prevalence of Anaplasma infection in camels was generally high throughout the year. The rate of infection during dry, wet, and late wet seasons was 70.3% (n = 175/249), 63.9% (n = 179/280), and 77.9% (n = 348/447), respectively (Table D in S1 Text).
The prevalence of ‘Ca. Anaplasma camelii’ in keds varied with season, with the percentage prevalence being: 9.9% (dry), 20.8% (wet), and 28.9% (late wet) (Table D in S1 Text).

Anaplasma is transmitted by different species of hard ticks [6] as well as mechanically via contaminated mouthparts of Stomoxys calcitrans and Tabanidae, among other biting flies, albeit with reduced efficiency [7,8].

Ked-feeding bite exposure frequencies influenced transmission of ‘Ca. Anaplasma camelii’ The success of ‘Ca. Anaplasma camelii’ transmission to the experimental mice and rabbits by camel keds during blood feeding was influenced by the exposure frequencies to fly bites (OR = 0.6504 (95% CI = 0.4714–0.8373), p = 0.0004, n = 115) (Table A-C in S1 Text).
243.Rationale: The Passage Body shows a marked mismatch: very high Anaplasma prevalence in camels but much lower proportions of keds testing positive. It also documents known mechanical transmission pathways for Anaplasma by biting flies and reports experimental transmission events that could be consistent with mechanical transfer (e.g., variable transmission with single or repeated exposures). There is no direct evidence of replication of the pathogen within keds presented. From an epidemiological standpoint, this pattern (high host prevalence but relatively low vector infection prevalence and lack of evidence for internal amplification) is consistent with mechanical rather than biological transmission by keds, so this inference can be reasonably drawn from the passage.

243.Option: The consistent detection of 'Ca. Anaplasma camelii' in both camels and keds across all seasons indicates that camel keds play a central role in the long-term maintenance and spread of the pathogen within camel herds.
Evidence: Hippobosca camelina was present: (i) in all of the camel herds surveyed, (ii) throughout the year, and (iii) nearly all camels in the herds were infested by keds.

The prevalence of Anaplasma infection in camels was generally high throughout the year. The rate of infection during dry, wet, and late wet seasons was 70.3% (n = 175/249), 63.9% (n = 179/280), and 77.9% (n = 348/447), respectively (Table D in S1 Text).
The prevalence of ‘Ca. Anaplasma camelii’ in keds varied with season, with the percentage prevalence being: 9.9% (dry), 20.8% (wet), and 28.9% (late wet) (Table D in S1 Text).

Anaplasma is transmitted by different species of hard ticks [6] as well as mechanically via contaminated mouthparts of Stomoxys calcitrans and Tabanidae, among other biting flies, albeit with reduced efficiency [7,8].
243.Rationale: Although keds are present year-round and the pathogen was detected in both camels and keds, the Passage Body does not provide direct evidence that keds are the central maintenance mechanism. Keds show much lower pathogen prevalence than camels, and the text explicitly recognizes ticks and mechanical routes as transmission mechanisms. Without demonstration of biological maintenance/amplification in keds or exclusion of other vectors (e.g., ticks) as maintenance hosts, one cannot conclude keds play the central long-term role in maintenance and spread from the data presented.

243.Option: The seasonal variation in ked infestation levels, with peak numbers during the dry season, suggests that 'Ca. Anaplasma camelii' transmission is most efficient during this period due to increased host-vector contact.
Evidence: The highest number of keds were present on camels in the middle of dry season, reaching up to 100 keds/camel. In contrast, during the wet season, the number of keds/camel reduced to an average of seven keds/camel (n = 14 camel herds)., Camel keds numbers increased gradually to an average of 10 keds/camel towards the late wet season in June-July 2018. This rising trend in the number infesting camel keds continued until reaching peak numbers of 80–100 keds/camel in the dry season (Fig 3C).
The prevalence of ‘Ca. Anaplasma camelii’ in keds varied with season, with the percentage prevalence being: 9.9% (dry), 20.8% (wet), and 28.9% (late wet) (Table D in S1 Text).

The prevalence of Anaplasma infection in camels was generally high throughout the year. The rate of infection during dry, wet, and late wet seasons was 70.3% (n = 175/249), 63.9% (n = 179/280), and 77.9% (n = 348/447), respectively (Table D in S1 Text).
243.Rationale: While ked counts peak in the dry season, the Passage Body shows that ked infection prevalence and camel infection prevalence do not peak necessarily in the dry season (ked prevalence was lowest in dry season, and camel prevalence was highest in the late wet season). There is no direct evidence presented that transmission efficiency is greatest during the dry season; in fact, the lower ked infection prevalence in the dry season argues against higher transmission efficiency at that time. Therefore the specific inference that transmission is most efficient in the dry season cannot be drawn from the provided data.

244.Option: Epidemiological studies involving nomadic populations need to factor in the fluidity in geographic localisation and household definition that are characteristic of mobile pastoralist populations.
Evidence: These assumptions do not hold for mobile populations such as nomadic pastoralists who inhabit the arid and semi-arid parts of West and Eastern Africa.

Their nomadic lifestyles involve regular migration, seeking grazing land and water for their livestock in lands dominated by patchy seasonal rainfall [7]. Such lifestyles increase the risk of settling in snake-abundant locations and thus increase the risk of snakebites.
This can have consequences for defining sampling frames and sampling units for population health research.

The homes or settlements are referred to as nkang or manyatta, and they consist of a single circular hut, or multiple huts arranged in a circular manner with a space in the middle, surrounded by an acacia thorn fence. The houses are inexpensively constructed with muds and sticks that can readily be abandoned (midjoni [abandoned homestead where there are still some structures standing]) in case of conflict or to look for grazing land for the remaining livestock.
244.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that common epidemiological assumptions (fixed residence/household) do not hold for nomadic pastoralists, and describes regular migration, split families, and readily abandoned homesteads. It also states that these features "have consequences for defining sampling frames and sampling units for population health research." Therefore it is directly supported that studies must account for fluid geographic localisation and household definition when designing research in such mobile populations.

244.Option: The use of satellite imagery and GIS tools to create a sampling frame can reduce bureaucratic delays and improve the timeliness of research planning in mobile populations.
Evidence: We created a spatial sampling frame by directly identifying and creating data points for all features that looked like a household within the 2018 level 1 global administrative unit boundary for Samburu County, using high-resolution satellite images.
Images were downloaded from the Copernicus Sentinel-2 Earth observation mission of the European Space Agency [54].
We identified 50,707 homesteads from the whole of Samburu, and these were all manually digitised and converted into datapoints using ArcGIS.
We used a modified grid-based GIS sampling method to select our primary sampling units [55].
244.Rationale: The passage documents the use of satellite imagery and GIS to build a spatial sampling frame and select grids and households. However, it does not state or provide evidence that these methods "reduce bureaucratic delays" or "improve the timeliness of research planning." Those specific claims about bureaucratic processes and timeliness are not supported by the text and would require additional information outside the passage. Thus the asserted causal/operational benefit cannot be derived from the passage.

244.Option: The use of a fixed household definition based on shared meals or sleeping under a roof is sufficient for accurately estimating snakebite burden in mobile populations.
Evidence: These assumptions do not hold for mobile populations such as nomadic pastoralists who inhabit the arid and semi-arid parts of West and Eastern Africa.

Their mobility limits their access to healthcare workers and engagement in research studies, and has resulted in nomadic populations being historically under-represented in population censuses, surveys, and epidemiological research activities, with some authors labelling them as being “statistically invisible” [8].
We adopted the definition of a household from the Kenyan Bureau of National Statistics which considers everyone who spent the previous night in the household as a member of the household [61].
However, there were significant population movements in some areas, and in extreme cases, all of the inhabitants of some grids had moved to another location.
In instances where the inhabitants had moved beyond a radius of 10 km, we replaced the original household with a new household in the same grid.
244.Rationale: The passage shows that fixed-residence assumptions and standard household definitions do not hold well for nomadic populations, and documents substantial household movement requiring relocation or replacement of sampled households. Although the study adopted a standard household definition for operational purposes, the passage provides evidence that mobility complicates its application and that additional methods (e.g., GIS mapping, replacements) were required. Therefore the claim that a fixed household definition is sufficient is not supported by the passage.

244.Option: Snakebite risk is strongly associated with proximity to water sources, and therefore, research in mobile populations should prioritize waterpoint-based sampling strategies.
Evidence: Their nomadic lifestyles involve regular migration, seeking grazing land and water for their livestock in lands dominated by patchy seasonal rainfall [7]. Such lifestyles increase the risk of settling in snake-abundant locations and thus increase the risk of snakebites.
244.Rationale: The passage notes that nomadic movements are driven by searching for grazing land and water and that such lifestyles can increase the risk of settling in snake-abundant locations, thereby increasing snakebite risk. However, it does not state that proximity to water sources is a primary or strong determinant of snakebite risk, nor does it recommend waterpoint-based sampling strategies. The specific inference that water proximity is strongly associated with risk and that sampling should prioritize waterpoints is not directly supported by the provided text and requires additional evidence.

244.Option: The high rate of household movement during the dry season necessitates conducting data collection during the rainy season to ensure accurate representation of population distribution.
Evidence: Rainfall patterns are highly variable, ranging between 250 to 700mm in the plains and 750mm– 1250mm in the highlands, and temperatures range between 24°C in July and 33°C in December meaning that Samburu county has an arid or semi-arid environment [14].
Their nomadic lifestyles involve regular migration, seeking grazing land and water for their livestock in lands dominated by patchy seasonal rainfall [7].
However, there were significant population movements in some areas, and in extreme cases, all of the inhabitants of some grids had moved to another location.
Data collection was carried out between December 2019 and March 2020, three months after completing the geospatial cluster and household mapping.
244.Rationale: While the passage documents variable rainfall, nomadic movement related to seeking grazing and water, and reports substantial household movement during fieldwork, it does not state that movement is specifically higher in the dry season nor that conducting surveys in the rainy season would ensure more accurate representation. The timing of data collection is reported but no evidence or recommendation is provided linking seasonality (dry vs rainy) to optimal survey timing. Therefore the specific necessity claimed in the option is not supported by the passage.

245.Option: The observed biological activity of AgCNPs is due to the synergistic interaction between silver and cerium oxide, where silver acts as a co-catalyst that enhances the redox activity of cerium oxide through surface defect engineering.
Evidence: Incorporation of the known larvicidal agent, silver, into the CNP structure may allow for an increased total larvicidal response.

The general bioactivity observed for CNPs is often ascribed to their chemical interaction with the local, biological environment and their catalytic response to (radical) oxygen species [28–30]. In particular, catalysis occurs at surface defects, such as oxygen vacancies, and is highly dependent on physicochemical conditions [31–35].
XPS analysis showed 0.72at% silver incorporation for AgCNPs (Table 2).

Peak breadth and position for Ag3d signals suggest presence of both zero and non-zero valence state silver; with this distinction possibly related to the position of silver species relative to the cerium oxide lattice.
245.Rationale: The passage explicitly hypothesizes and provides material-characterization evidence consistent with synergistic action: authors state that incorporation of silver into ceria "may allow for an increased total larvicidal response" and explicitly propose that "ceria and silver could function as co-catalysts." Supporting physicochemical data (XPS showing silver incorporation and Ag3d features indicating mixed valence/interaction with the ceria lattice) plus the discussion that CNP bioactivity is driven by catalytic redox activity at surface defects provide a coherent chain linking silver incorporation, surface-defect–mediated redox activity, and enhanced biological activity. These statements and data within the Passage support the inference of a synergistic, co-catalytic interaction rather than solely independent effects of the components.

245.Option: The larvicidal activity of AgCNPs is primarily due to the presence of oxygen vacancy-related (point defect) highly redox-active Ce3+ species, which are responsible for the observed biological effects.
Evidence: The general bioactivity observed for CNPs is often ascribed to their chemical interaction with the local, biological environment and their catalytic response to (radical) oxygen species [28–30]. In particular, catalysis occurs at surface defects, such as oxygen vacancies, and is highly dependent on physicochemical conditions [31–35].
The ratio of Ce3+ to Ce4+ redox states within the material was calculated as 34.7% Ce3+ for AgCNPs.
245.Rationale: While the Passage links CNP bioactivity to redox catalysis at surface defects and reports Ce3+ fraction for AgCNPs, it does not provide direct evidence that oxygen-vacancy–associated Ce3+ species are the primary mechanism for AgCNP larvicidal activity. The experimental results show markedly different biological effects for AgCNP versus CNP (e.g., AgCNP has greater larvicidal and fecundity impacts), and the authors explicitly discuss possible co-catalytic roles of silver. The data do not demonstrate that Ce3+ alone (or oxygen vacancies alone) are primarily responsible; thus the strong causal statement that Ce3+ is the primary mechanism is not supported by the provided evidence.

245.Option: The observed biological activity of AgCNPs against Ae. aegypti does not result from free Ag+.
Evidence: This data suggests the presence of negligible amounts of silver contaminants/dissociation products (i.e., free silver ion or silver nanoclusters) in AgCNP solution.

245.Rationale: ICP-MS and related characterization explicitly demonstrated negligible free silver in the AgCNP preparations, and the authors conclude that observed mosquito effects are attributable to incorporated silver rather than free Ag+. The presence of a free-silver positive control (AgNO3) that has known high larvicidal effect further supports that the AgCNP effects are not due to dissolved free Ag+ in solution. Therefore the inference that activity does not result from free Ag+ is directly supported by the Passage.

245.Rationale: The experimental data show the opposite of the option's claim: larvicidal activity of AgCNPs was lower when larval food (organic matter) was present (≈25% dead) and substantially higher when food was withheld (≈58–66% dead). The authors even state they believed organic matter may blunt activity and then confirmed greater killing when food was withheld. There is no evidence in the Passage for formation of an organic corona that stabilizes and increases bioavailability; the data directly contradict the proposed enhancement by larval food.

245.Option: The reduction in egg clutch observed in adult mosquitoes fed AgCNPs via blood meal is due to the physical obstruction of oocytes by undissolved nanoparticles in the reproductive tract.
Evidence: The maximum intensity z-projections of ovarioles/ovaries from mosquitoes which failed to oviposit showed a very interesting phenomenon occurring specifically in the ovarioles of AgCNP-exposed mosquitoes ... it was revealed that oocytes in the AgCNP-exposed mosquitoes appeared to have suffered a growth arrest by or before 16h PBM.

245.Rationale: While the Passage documents reduced egg clutch and provides confocal evidence of oocyte growth arrest in AgCNP-exposed mosquitoes, it does not report any observations of undissolved particles physically obstructing the reproductive tract or oocytes. The Passage offers no data (e.g., microscopy showing particulate masses blocking oviducts or ovarioles) to support the specific mechanism of physical obstruction, so this causal claim is not supported by the provided evidence.

247.Option: The identification of SNPs at TNFSF pathways associated with T1R outcomes in populations from Vietnam and Brazil suggests that a universal genetic test for leprosy reactions can be developed with minimal validation across different populations.
Evidence: SNPs at NINJ1, TLR1, IL6, and TNFSF8 are clearly associated with reactional phenotypes (TR1 or TR2) [28–32].
However, so far few of these genes have been replicated or functionally characterized to support the epidemiological findings.

Here, we confirmed the association between IL6 and NOD2 SNPs and leprosy reactions among Brazilians, and demonstrated that the NOD2 genotype associated with a decreased risk for developing a reaction is also related to lower levels of IL-6 in the sera of non-reactional patients.
247.Rationale: The passage notes prior associations (including TNFSF8) but explicitly states that few of these genes have been replicated or functionally characterized. Although this study replicated some associations (IL6, NOD2) in Brazilians, the authors emphasize limited replication overall. Therefore the passage does not support the strong inference that a universal genetic test can be developed with minimal cross-population validation; rather it implies further replication/characterization across populations is needed.

247.Option: The failure to detect associations with TLR1 SNPs in this study, despite prior reports, indicates that genetic markers for leprosy reactions are unreliable and should not be pursued further in future research.
Evidence: TLR1 SNPs rs5743592 and rs4833095 were included in this study in order to replicate previous findings regarding their association to leprosy per se and reactional episodes [29,30,42–44].
From the 15 candidate SNPs (total counts and frequencies detailed at S3 Table), 2 showed different Kaplan–Meier curves between genotype groups (log-rank p-value < 0.05).
Here, we confirmed the association between IL6 and NOD2 SNPs and leprosy reactions among Brazilians...
247.Rationale: While the study included TLR1 SNPs and reports that only two of the 15 candidate SNPs showed significant Kaplan–Meier differences (IL6 and NOD2), the passage does not conclude that genetic markers in general are unreliable. In fact, the study confirmed associations for other genes. The passage also highlights limited replication of some genes, implying continued study rather than abandonment. Thus the provided statement is not supported by the passage.

247.Option: The presence of risk alleles for leprosy reactions does not have additive or synergistic effects, suggesting that combining multiple genetic markers may not be feasible for developing a predictive genetic test.
Evidence: We estimated that our sample was sufficient in detecting a genetic risk effect of 1.8 with a statistical power of 79%, considering single nucleotide variants with a minor allele frequency of 0.10 under the additive model.
From the 15 candidate SNPs (total counts and frequencies detailed at S3 Table), 2 showed different Kaplan–Meier curves between genotype groups (log-rank p-value < 0.05).
To estimate the SNP effects on reaction occurrence, the Cox proportional-hazards regression model was applied including adjusted analyses for gender and clinical forms (strata).
247.Rationale: The passage describes analyses of individual SNP effects (Kaplan–Meier curves and Cox models) and notes power considerations for single-variant effects. It does not present any analysis of additive or synergistic effects among multiple risk alleles. Because the study did not assess combinations of markers, the passage provides no evidence to support the negative claim that combining markers is not feasible; therefore this inference cannot be derived from the data presented.

247.Option: The lack of association between IL6 rs2069845 and serum IL-6 levels in this study suggests that genetic markers are not useful for predicting leprosy reactions, as they do not correlate with measurable biological outcomes.
Evidence: Patients with the AG genotype for IL6 rs2069845 developed reactions faster (MST = 116 weeks) than other genotypes (MSTAA = 195, MSTGG = 295 weeks; p = 0.023)...
AG genotype and AG/GG carriers of IL6 rs2069845 were associated with having an increased risk of developing a reaction (aHR = 1.88, p = 0.0008 and aHR = 1.73, p = 0.002, respectively).
Neither was IL-6 production associated with IL6 rs2069845 SNP (Fig 3A).
individuals carrying NOD2 rs751271-GT showed a borderline difference (p = 0.05), suggesting lower IL-6 production (GT median: 0.008) as compared to GG individuals (GG median: 0.53) (Fig 3B).
Here, we confirmed the association between IL6 and NOD2 SNPs and leprosy reactions among Brazilians...
247.Rationale: Although IL6 rs2069845 showed no association with measured serum IL-6 levels in this dataset, the same IL6 SNP was associated with increased risk of reaction, and the NOD2 SNP both associated with reaction risk and showed a borderline correlation with lower IL-6. Thus the passage contains mixed results and demonstrates that some genetic markers do associate with outcomes and/or biological measures. The single negative genotype–protein correlation does not justify the broad conclusion that genetic markers are not useful for prediction; the passage does not support that sweeping claim.

247.Option: Since the study confirmed gender and clinical form as risk factors for leprosy reactions, a genetic test should be developed to complement these non-genetic variables in predicting reaction outcomes.
Evidence: As observed, the length of time before a leprosy reaction, as described by survival curves, shows a significant difference when considering the variables of gender (p = 0.002) and clinical form (p < 0.001).

Here, we confirmed the association between IL6 and NOD2 SNPs and leprosy reactions among Brazilians, and demonstrated that the NOD2 genotype associated with a decreased risk for developing a reaction is also related to lower levels of IL-6 in the sera of non-reactional patients.
To estimate the SNP effects on reaction occurrence, the Cox proportional-hazards regression model was applied including adjusted analyses for gender and clinical forms (strata).

The NOD2 rs751271-GT genotype and G-allele carriers (GT/GG) were associated with protection against a reaction’s occurrence, even after adjustment (aHR = 0.45, p = 0.002 and aHR = 0.56, p = 0.01, respectively),
AG genotype and AG/GG carriers of IL6 rs2069845 were associated with having an increased risk of developing a reaction (aHR = 1.88, p = 0.0008 and aHR = 1.73, p = 0.002, respectively).
247.Rationale: The passage documents that gender and clinical form are significant non-genetic risk factors and separately demonstrates reproducible genetic associations (IL6, NOD2) with reaction risk, with genetic effects estimated after adjustment for gender and clinical form. Epidemiologically, when both non-genetic and genetic variables independently associate with an outcome and can be jointly modeled (as done here with adjusted Cox models), it is reasonable to infer that a predictive approach combining genetic markers with known non-genetic risk factors could improve prediction of reaction outcomes. The passage therefore supports the inference that developing a genetic test to complement these variables is justified.

248.Option: The high antibody titers observed in geese following DENV2 vaccination, reaching up to 1:6,400,000, suggest that goose-derived IgY could be used as a prophylactic vaccine alternative in humans due to its strong immunogenicity and long-lasting response.
Evidence: Of the 104 eggs that were measured, the average serum endpoint titer across all weeks post vaccination was 1:924,807 with the highest titer being 1:6,400,000 at 7 weeks post vaccination (Fig 1B).
We suggest that IgY, the avian homolog to mammalian IgG, which naturally fails to bind FcγR, could be used as alternative therapeutic antibodies to treat DENV-induced disease.
In this study, we demonstrate the efficacy of goose-derived, purified anti-DENV2 IgY in vitro and in vivo.
248.Rationale: The passage documents very high antibody titers in vaccinated geese, but the authors frame IgY as a therapeutic antibody source and demonstrate therapeutic efficacy in mouse models. There are no data presented on using goose-derived IgY as a human prophylactic vaccine, on immunogenicity in humans, or on duration of protective effect in humans. Therefore the leap to claiming IgY could serve as a prophylactic vaccine alternative in humans (strong immunogenicity and long-lasting response) is not supported by the passage.

248.Option: The inability of anti-DENV IgY to bind mammalian Fcγ receptors means that it does not induce antibody-dependent enhancement (ADE) of dengue virus infection, making it a promising therapeutic candidate for treating dengue without increasing the risk of severe disease.
Evidence: Another key advantage of IgY is its inability to bind mammalian FcγR; therefore, it is possible that anti-DENV IgY will be able to neutralize a viral infection without inducing ADE [18].

In the enhancement experiment, whereas the positive control anti-DENV Mab E60 generated ~15% infection at its peak enhancement titer, neither the anti-DENV IgY nor control IgY sera were enhancing at any dilution tested (Fig 3).
Specifically, we demonstrate that anti-DENV2 IgY is able to neutralize DENV2 in vitro and provide protection in the AG129 mouse model when administered therapeutically following lethal challenge.
248.Rationale: The passage states that IgY does not bind mammalian Fcγ receptors and explicitly links that property to the potential to avoid ADE. Experimental data in the passage show anti-DENV2 IgY did not enhance infection in in vitro ADE assays and provided therapeutic protection in vivo. From these pieces of evidence, it is epidemiologically reasonable to infer that IgY's lack of FcγR binding prevents ADE and supports its promise as a therapeutic that does not increase ADE-associated severe disease risk.

248.Option: The recognition of novel epitopes on the DENV2 E protein by anti-DENV2 IgY suggests that IgY-based therapies may be effective across all dengue serotypes due to conserved structural motifs.
Evidence: we confirmed that anti-DENV2 IgY binds to previously uncharacterized epitopes, and furthermore full-length IgY and alternatively spliced IgY also bind epitopes that are unique from one another.
Specifically, we demonstrate that anti-DENV2 IgY is able to neutralize DENV2 in vitro and provide protection in the AG129 mouse model when administered therapeutically following lethal challenge.
There are four distinct serotypes (DENV1, DENV2, DENV3, DENV4) that differ at the amino acid level by 25–40% [5].
248.Rationale: While the passage shows anti-DENV2 IgY recognizes novel epitopes on DENV2 E protein and is effective against DENV2, it provides no experimental data on cross-serotype neutralization. The passage also highlights substantial amino acid differences among serotypes (25–40%), which argues against assuming cross-serotype efficacy. Thus inferring broad effectiveness across all serotypes based on DENV2 epitope recognition is not supported by the provided evidence.

248.Option: The moderate protection observed with control IgY in vivo suggests that IgY may exert non-specific immune activation in mice, potentially enhancing innate immune responses and reducing viral load independently of neutralizing antibodies.
Evidence: Similar to the positive control E60 N297Q mAb, 100% therapeutic efficacy was also observed with 1 mg anti-DENV2 IgY (n = 4) administered 24 hours post-infection (p = 0.0019), while 1 mg of control IgY yielded 25% protection (n = 4, p = 0.04 comparing equal amounts of anti-DENV2 IgY and control IgY) (Fig 4A).
IgY does not interact with other Fc-binding receptors, which limits its ability to elicit an inflammatory reaction in humans [19–24].
248.Rationale: The passage reports that control (naïve) IgY provided 25% protection in the mouse model at one dose, which is an observation of moderate protection. However, the authors do not provide evidence of the mechanism (e.g., non-specific innate immune activation) responsible for that protection. Moreover, the passage notes IgY's limited interaction with Fc-binding receptors and does not present data showing innate immune stimulation by control IgY in mice. Therefore concluding a specific mechanism of non-specific immune activation is not supported by the text.

248.Option: Because IgY does not bind human complement, it is unlikely to contribute to immune enhancement during secondary dengue infections, thereby reducing the risk of vascular leakage and severe disease.
Evidence: Although IgY is able to activate complement in the avian system, its unique structure prevents it from being able to activate human complement [23].
it has also been proposed that complement activation during secondary heterotypic DENV infection by non-neutralizing antibodies contributes to immune enhancement.
Therefore, the inability of anti-DENV IgY to induce complement activation could prove to be detrimental or beneficial depending on the individual disease state.
248.Rationale: The passage confirms IgY cannot activate human complement and notes that complement activation has been proposed to contribute to immune enhancement in secondary DENV infection. However, the authors explicitly state that the inability to induce complement activation "could prove to be detrimental or beneficial depending on the individual disease state," indicating uncertainty. The option's definitive claim that IgY is unlikely to contribute to immune enhancement and thereby reduces risk of severe disease overstates the evidence; the passage does not settle the net clinical effect of lacking complement activation.

249.Option: Despite repeated rounds of school-based deworming, hookworm remains prevalent in adult men in Kwale County, suggesting that school-based deworming alone is insufficient to control community-wide hookworm transmission.
Evidence: annual school-based deworming with albendazole has been implemented successfully since 2012 (with a prior round in 2009) with programmatic coverage of 82% of pre-school and school-age children (2–14 years) in 2014 [19, 20].

Additionally, there have been four rounds of community-based mass drug administration (MDA) for lymphatic filariasis (LF), in which albendazole and diethylcarbamazine (DEC) were delivered to individuals aged 2 years and above. These LF MDA rounds were implemented in Kwale County in 2003, 2005, 2008 and 2011. For the two most recent MDA rounds prior to this survey, 2008 and 2011, programmatic coverage levels of 62.7% and 58.3% were reported [11, 20, 21].
Prevalence of hookworm infection was 19.1% (95% confidence interval [CI]: 16.4–21.7%); 12.5% in under 5s, 17.4% in 5–14 years and 20.7% in adults;
Hookworm prevalence increased with age across both sexes, and was higher in males (Fig 3a).
A higher proportion of the MHI infections were found in adults (ρ = 0.77, p<0.001) (Fig 4b), consistent with patterns shown in Fig 3a.
249.Rationale: The passage documents sustained school-based deworming with high coverage among children but shows that hookworm prevalence and a higher proportion of moderate-to-high intensity (MHI) infections are found in adults, and that prevalence increases with age and is higher in males. These points together support the epidemiological inference that focusing treatment on schoolchildren alone has not eliminated hookworm among adults and therefore is unlikely to be sufficient to control community-wide transmission, since untreated adults can serve as a reservoir.

249.Option: The observed age-related increase in hookworm infection intensity among adults suggests that the current deworming strategy is ineffective because it does not account for the biological differences in susceptibility between age groups.
Evidence: Prevalence of hookworm infection was 19.1% (95% confidence interval [CI]: 16.4–21.7%); 12.5% in under 5s, 17.4% in 5–14 years and 20.7% in adults;
Hookworm prevalence increased with age across both sexes, and was higher in males (Fig 3a).
In relation to environmental factors, only high levels of vegetation was associated with infection intensity.

annual school-based deworming with albendazole has been implemented successfully since 2012 (with a prior round in 2009) with programmatic coverage of 82% of pre-school and school-age children (2–14 years) in 2014 [19, 20].
249.Rationale: While the passage documents an age-related increase in hookworm prevalence and intensity among adults and describes the current deworming strategy (school-based albendazole), it does not present any evidence about biological differences in susceptibility between age groups. The claim that the strategy is ineffective because it fails to account for biological susceptibility is a causal interpretation not supported by the data presented. The observed pattern can be explained by other factors contained in the passage (for example, adults not being routinely targeted by school-based programs), so attributing ineffectiveness to unaccounted biological susceptibility is not derivable from the passage.

249.Option: The high prevalence of hookworm in adults, particularly men, suggests that the current deworming strategy is ineffective in preventing reinfection due to poor compliance among adult populations.
Evidence: Prevalence of hookworm infection was 19.1% (95% confidence interval [CI]: 16.4–21.7%); 12.5% in under 5s, 17.4% in 5–14 years and 20.7% in adults;
Hookworm prevalence increased with age across both sexes, and was higher in males (Fig 3a).

Additionally, there have been four rounds of community-based mass drug administration (MDA) for lymphatic filariasis (LF), in which albendazole and diethylcarbamazine (DEC) were delivered to individuals aged 2 years and above. These LF MDA rounds were implemented in Kwale County in 2003, 2005, 2008 and 2011. For the two most recent MDA rounds prior to this survey, 2008 and 2011, programmatic coverage levels of 62.7% and 58.3% were reported [11, 20, 21].
Wearing shoes (Adjusted odds ratio [AOR] 0.71 (95% CI: 0.64–0.78), attending school and being dewormed in the last 12 months were all found to be protective behaviours for hookworm infection.
249.Rationale: The passage shows high adult prevalence (especially in males) and documents past community MDAs with specified coverage levels and that recent deworming is protective. However, it contains no data on adult compliance (i.e., whether adults refuse or fail to take treatment), nor does it quantify reinfection rates or compliance behaviors among adults. The assertion that the problem is 'poor compliance among adult populations' is a specific causal claim that is not supported by the evidence provided; the more directly supported inference is that adults are less likely to be covered by routine school-based programmes and so remain infected, not that they are non-compliant.

249.Option: The high prevalence of hookworm in adults, despite school-based deworming, indicates that the treatment is ineffective against adult worms, and therefore, alternative drugs should be used in future interventions.
Evidence: annual school-based deworming with albendazole has been implemented successfully since 2012 (with a prior round in 2009) with programmatic coverage of 82% of pre-school and school-age children (2–14 years) in 2014 [19, 20].
Prevalence of hookworm infection was 19.1% (95% confidence interval [CI]: 16.4–21.7%); 12.5% in under 5s, 17.4% in 5–14 years and 20.7% in adults;
Wearing shoes (Adjusted odds ratio [AOR] 0.71 (95% CI: 0.64–0.78), attending school and being dewormed in the last 12 months were all found to be protective behaviours for hookworm infection.
249.Rationale: The passage documents ongoing albendazole use in school programmes and shows protective associations with having been dewormed in the last 12 months, which argues that treatment can reduce infection when delivered. There is no data provided on drug efficacy against adult worms or evidence of drug failure/resistance. The observed adult burden is more plausibly explained by adults not being the focus of routine school-based deworming (i.e., untreated reservoir) rather than by intrinsic ineffectiveness of the drug against adult worms. Therefore, the specific conclusion that the treatment is ineffective against adult worms and that alternative drugs are required is not supported by the passage.

249.Option: The persistence of moderate-to-heavy hookworm infections in adults indicates that the current deworming strategy has failed to reduce transmission, and therefore, the treatment regimen should be changed to target only the most vulnerable age groups.
Evidence: A higher proportion of the MHI infections were found in adults (ρ = 0.77, p<0.001) (Fig 4b), consistent with patterns shown in Fig 3a.
Prevalence of hookworm infection was 19.1% (95% confidence interval [CI]: 16.4–21.7%); 12.5% in under 5s, 17.4% in 5–14 years and 20.7% in adults;

annual school-based deworming with albendazole has been implemented successfully since 2012 (with a prior round in 2009) with programmatic coverage of 82% of pre-school and school-age children (2–14 years) in 2014 [19, 20].
249.Rationale: While the persistence of MHI infections in adults indicates that transmission has not been fully controlled, the proposed response—to change the regimen to target only the most vulnerable age groups—is inconsistent with the evidence. The data show adults (not just children) harbor a substantial portion of infections and MHI infections, meaning that narrowing the target to only vulnerable groups (e.g., children) would likely leave the adult reservoir untreated and could perpetuate transmission. Thus the recommended policy change is not supported by the passage; if anything, the findings argue for broader community-level targeting rather than focusing only on the vulnerable groups.

250.Option: The combination of CPA, CPB and CPC resulted in enhanced protection in liver (>93%) and spleen (>98%) which was even higher than all other CPs based vaccines tested against Leishmania so far.
Evidence: the mean hepatic and splenic parasite burden (LDU) were lowered by ∼93% and ∼98% respectively, in animals immunized with cocktail antigens as compared to the adjuvant controls at 3 months post infection.

Highest level of protection against L. donovani was however achieved in hamsters immunized with the liposomal triple antigen cocktail which showed 13 log-fold and 16 log-fold reductions in the parasite burden in liver and spleen, respectively, compared to adjuvant controls (p<0.0001) after 3 months of infectious challenge.

However, these research efforts have been largely restricted to cutaneous and zoonotic VL with variable success rate but have never been investigated against L. donovani, the causative agent of kala-azar.
250.Rationale: The first part of the option (that the cocktail reduced liver and spleen parasite burden by ~93% and ~98%) is directly supported by the passage quotes. However, the latter clause claiming this protection is 'higher than all other CPs based vaccines tested against Leishmania so far' is not supported by the Passage Body. The passage notes prior CP vaccine work was largely on cutaneous/zoonotic VL and that comparisons across those studies or to other CP-based vaccines against L. donovani are not provided. Therefore the comparative claim beyond the study's own results cannot be derived from the passage.

250.Option: Although direct comparison of our work with previous reports is difficult, it is noteworthy that rCPC individually induced better protection than promising vaccine candidates like gp63, ORFF, LelF-2 tested in murine VL against L. donovani.
Evidence: rCPC induced greater protection than both rCPA and rCPB, when injected alone.
Compared to an array of antigens that have been tested, very few are sufficiently promising to be carried out to Phase I clinical trials or advanced preclinical work against VL.
250.Rationale: The passage supports that rCPC provided greater protection than the other two cysteine proteases tested in this study. However, the passage contains no data or statements comparing rCPC to other named vaccine candidates (gp63, ORFF, LelF-2) or to results from murine VL studies of those antigens. Thus the specific comparative claim to gp63, ORFF, LelF-2 cannot be derived from the Passage Body.

250.Option: The cocktail vaccine induced a synergistic enhancement of protection due to the cumulative increase in T-cell epitopes in a non-antagonistic manner.
Evidence: Taken together, these finding suggest that all three CPs present in the cocktail vaccine act synergistically to counteract the impaired T cell response after challenge for improved protection.

Our aim in this study was therefore towards a comparative evaluation of prophylactic potential of CPA, CPB and CPC, individually, as well as a multi-antigen cocktail vaccine, maximizing the exposed antigenic epitopes to protect against VL.

Highest percent lymphoproliferation was observed in hamsters vaccinated with triple antigen cocktail which was significantly higher than rCPA (p<0.0001), rCPB (p<0.0001) and rCPC (p<0.0001) as single antigen.
Highest level of protection against L. donovani was however achieved in hamsters immunized with the liposomal triple antigen cocktail...
250.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the three CPs in the cocktail 'act synergistically' to improve protection and earlier describes the rationale of a multi-antigen cocktail to 'maximize the exposed antigenic epitopes.' Experimental data support greater lymphoproliferation and the highest protection with the cocktail versus single antigens. Together these statements and results justify the inference that the cocktail induced a synergistic enhancement of protection likely via addition of antigenic/T-cell epitopes in a non-antagonistic fashion; this conclusion is directly supported by the passage.

250.Option: The use of TLR agonists with cationic liposomes allows concurrent antigen presentation along with targeting pattern recognition receptor (PRR) pathways, for effective expansion of effector T cells.
Evidence: Monophosphoryl lipid A (MPLA) is a Toll-like receptor 4 (TLR4) agonist with more than 100,000 human doses safely administered as a part of licensed hepatitis B and Human papillomavirus vaccines [6].
When used together, both the adjuvants i.e. MPL and TDM non-specifically activate the immune system, allowing a better response to the associated immunogen [10].

CLSM of MΦ cells incubated for 2 hrs with both Rh123-labeled liposomes (green), LysoTracker Red (red) and DAPI(blue) simultaneously showed successful co-localization (yellow) of labeled liposomes in acidic compartments such as lysosomes (Fig. S3, B) which confirms antigen presentation by MΦ.
These data indicated that combining MPL-TDM with cationic liposomes result in increased the adjuvant activity of the formulation on the APCs in vitro, serving our rationale for its utilization.
Significant enhancement of antigen-specific splenocyte proliferation was observed for liposomal rCPC (p<0.001) and liposomal cocktail (p<0.0001) vaccinated animals compared to adjuvant controls.
All immunized groups receiving either of liposomal rCPA, rCPB and rCPC singly or as cocktail produced significant upregulation of IL-2 and IFN-γ mRNAs in comparison to controls...
250.Rationale: The passage identifies MPL as a TLR4 agonist and TDM as an immunostimulant acting via macrophage receptors/TLR2/Mincle, and documents that combining MPL-TDM with cationic liposomes increases APC activation and leads to antigen delivery to lysosomes (antigen presentation). The study shows downstream consequences consistent with effector T cell expansion (increased splenocyte proliferation, upregulation of IL-2 and IFN-γ). Thus the epidemiological/immunological inference that using TLR agonists with cationic liposomes enables concurrent antigen presentation and PRR targeting to expand effector T cells is supported by the Passage Body.

250.Option: The liposomal formulation of rCPC alone provided the highest level of protection among all tested antigen formulations, surpassing even the cocktail vaccine.
Evidence: rCPC induced greater protection than both rCPA and rCPB, when injected alone.

Highest level of protection against L. donovani was however achieved in hamsters immunized with the liposomal triple antigen cocktail which showed 13 log-fold and 16 log-fold reductions in the parasite burden in liver and spleen, respectively, compared to adjuvant controls (p<0.0001) after 3 months of infectious challenge.
Significantly higher protection was obtained using the cocktail vaccine compared to the single antigens: rCPA (73%), rCPB (76%) but not rCPC (91%) in spleen.
250.Rationale: The passage clearly states that rCPC provided better protection than the other single CP antigens (rCPA, rCPB). However, it also explicitly states that the triple-antigen cocktail achieved the highest level of protection overall (larger log-fold reductions and higher percent reductions than single antigens). Therefore the claim that rCPC alone surpassed the cocktail is contradicted by the Passage Body and cannot be derived from it.

251.Option: The absence of differences in life cycle duration between infected and non-infected ticks indicates that R. conorii conorii infection does not affect the overall fitness of Rh. sanguineus in laboratory conditions.
Evidence: No difference was observed between the duration of life cycle of R. conorii conorii-infected and non-infected ticks held at 25°C, but the average weight of engorged females and eggs was found to be significantly lower in infected ticks.
The average weight of 31 infected engorged females was 0.38111 g (range, 0.2518–0.5389 g) compared to 0.4749 g (range, 0.2333–0.6203 g) for six non-infected engorged females (p = 0.0136).
The average weight of eggs from one female infected tick was 0.2099 g (range, 0.0998–0.2976 g, 31 samples) compared to 0.2919 g (range, 0.1321–0.4271 g, 6 samples) for non-infected eggs (p = 0.0021).
251.Rationale: The passage does state there was no difference in life cycle duration at 25°C, but it also reports significantly lower engorged female and egg weights in infected ticks. 'Overall fitness' in epidemiology includes reproductive capacity (fecundity) and not only developmental timing. The reduced female and egg weights are evidence of a negative effect on at least one component of fitness, so the claim that infection does not affect overall fitness is not supported by the passage.

251.Option: The lower fecundity of R. conorii conorii-infected female ticks, indicated by reduced engorged female and egg weights, suggests that the prevalence of infection in a tick population would gradually decline and disappear without periodic re-infection.
Evidence: the average weight of engorged females and eggs was found to be significantly lower in infected ticks.
Twelve successive generations were obtained, and the infected colony is still growing in our laboratory as of this writing (Figure 2).
These data suggest 100% transstadial and transovarial transmission of R. conorii conorii in Rh. sanguineus ticks.
251.Rationale: While reduced female and egg weights indicate a fecundity cost, the passage also provides strong counter-evidence: the investigators maintained an infected colony for twelve successive generations and report data 'suggest[ing] 100% transstadial and transovarial transmission.' The observed ability to sustain infection across many laboratory generations argues against the inference that prevalence would necessarily 'gradually decline and disappear' without re-infection. Therefore, the conclusion that infection would disappear solely because of reduced fecundity is not supported by the presented data.

251.Option: The 100% transovarial transmission of R. conorii conorii in Rh. sanguineus over twelve generations implies that the infection can be maintained indefinitely in tick populations without the need for vertebrate reservoirs.
Evidence: Twelve successive generations were obtained, and the infected colony is still growing in our laboratory as of this writing (Figure 2).
The PCR assay was positive for specimens of all stages of these generations for rickettsial detection.
These data suggest 100% transstadial and transovarial transmission of R. conorii conorii in Rh. sanguineus ticks.

These data suggested that transstadial transmission (transfer of bacteria from stage to stage) but also transovarial transmission (TOT, the transfer of bacteria from adult female ticks to the subsequent generation of ticks via the eggs) of R. conorii conorii occurs in ticks and consequently that Rh. sanguineus (Figure 1) could act not only as a vector but also as a reservoir of R. conorii conorii [1].
251.Rationale: The passage documents sustained infection across twelve laboratory generations with molecular detection in all life stages and explicitly states the data 'suggest 100% transstadial and transovarial transmission' and that Rh. sanguineus 'could act ... as a reservoir.' From these observations, it is epidemiologically reasonable to infer that the pathogen can be maintained within a tick population via vertical transmission without requiring vertebrate hosts to re-seed infection. The wording 'indefinitely' is an extrapolation, but the demonstrated multi-generation maintenance directly supports the inference that vertebrate reservoirs are not strictly required for persistence.

251.Option: The high mortality rate of R. conorii conorii-infected engorged nymphs and adults at 37°C compared to non-infected ticks suggests that temperature extremes may prevent the establishment of new MSF foci in warmer regions.
Evidence: Significantly more engorged, infected nymphs maintained at 37°C died than non-infected nymphs maintained at the same temperature (17–67%, 101/300 and 4–7%, 15/300, respectively, p = 0.0001) (Table 1).
In the third batch, which was maintained at 37°C for one month, 37.5–85% of infected ticks and 15–40% of non-infected ticks died (75/120 vs. 29/120, p = 0.0001).
In conclusion, infected ticks (nymphs and adults) had a comparatively higher rate of mortality at 37°C than at 25°C, and after a 4°C cooling when compared to non-infected ticks.
251.Rationale: The passage provides clear experimental evidence that infected nymphs and adults suffer substantially higher mortality at 37°C compared with non-infected counterparts. Epidemiologically, higher mortality of infected vectors under high-temperature conditions would reduce the likelihood that infected tick populations establish or persist in very warm environments, so it is reasonable to infer that temperature extremes could hinder the establishment of new MSF foci in warmer regions. The passage does not prove this in the field, but the experimental mortality data support the stated inference.

251.Option: The reactivation phenomenon observed in R. conorii conorii-infected ticks at elevated temperatures explains why infected ticks are more likely to survive winter conditions in temperate climates.
Evidence: None of these stages, either infected or non-infected, could tolerate low (4°C) or high (37°C) temperatures for one month (data not shown).

In the 3 experiments with engorged nymphs, 88–100% of the R. conorii conorii-infected nymphs that had been previously maintained at 4°C died after being transferred to 25°C (53–80% died without molting) compared to 41% (62/150) of non-infected nymphs after one month (288/300 vs. 62/150, p = 0.0001).
In conclusion, infected ticks (nymphs and adults) had a comparatively higher rate of mortality at 37°C than at 25°C, and after a 4°C cooling when compared to non-infected ticks.
251.Rationale: The passage contains no description of a 'reactivation phenomenon' that would increase winter survival. Instead, the experimental data show that infected ticks had higher mortality after exposure to low temperatures (4°C) and did not tolerate 4°C or 37°C for a month. These findings contradict the claim that elevated-temperature reactivation explains greater winter survival of infected ticks; the data indicate the opposite (reduced survival after cold exposure). Therefore the option is not supported by the passage.

252.Option: The addition of Quil A significantly (P<0.05) increased IgG2c but not significantly IgG1 titers by ∼5–10 fold (Fig. 5B–C). In summary, a single 1 µg dose of insect-cell derived CHIKV VLPs induced potent CHIKV-specific neutralizing antibody and IgG titers.
Evidence: The addition of Quil A significantly (P<0.05) increased IgG2c but not significantly IgG1 titers by ∼5–10 fold (Fig. 5B–C).
In summary, a single 1 µg dose of insect-cell derived CHIKV VLPs induced potent CHIKV-specific neutralizing antibody and IgG titers.
252.Rationale: The option restates findings that are explicitly reported in the passage. The passage directly states that Quil A significantly increased IgG2c but not IgG1 by approximately 5–10-fold, and it explicitly summarizes that a single 1 µg dose of insect-cell derived VLPs induced potent CHIKV-specific neutralizing antibody and IgG titers. These statements are directly derivable from the Passage Body.

252.Option: The complete CHIKV structural cassette was cloned downstream the strong polyhedrin promoter of the AcMNPV baculovirus (Ac-S27). Western analysis on cell and medium fractions of Sf21-cells that were infected with Ac-S27 indicated that glycoprotein processing (glycosylation, furin cleavage and surface localization) is efficient and that the functionality of E1 as fusion protein is retained.
Evidence: The coding sequence of the structural polyprotein was cloned downstream the polyhedrin promoter in an AcMNPV backbone, after which Sf21 cells were infected with a MOI = 10 TCID50/ml.

PNGase F treatment resulted in an expected reduction in molecular mass of CHIKV-E1 in both the cell fraction and purified VLPs (Fig. 1C, lane 2–5), indicating that E1 was efficiently N-glycosylated by the insect cells.
The size difference between non-treated and treated samples was significantly larger for E2 than that of E1, which may suggest that E2 is N-glycosylated at the two predicted loci (Fig. 1C, lane 2–4).
Treated cells displayed ring-like structures (Fig. 2, middle and bottom), indicating that the glycoproteins are indeed exposed at the surface of the infected cells.
Therefore, it can be concluded that E1 retains its fusogenic properties when expressed in Sf-cells.
252.Rationale: The passage describes cloning the complete structural polyprotein downstream of the polyhedrin promoter in an AcMNPV backbone (Ac-S27). Western blots and PNGase F treatment demonstrate N-glycosylation of E1 and E2, and immunofluorescence shows surface exposure. The syncytia assay and concluding sentence demonstrate that E1 retains fusogenic function. Together these statements support the option's inference about efficient processing, surface localization, and retained E1 functionality.

252.Option: The 1 µg non-adjuvanted VLP vaccine elicited a similar neutralizing antibody response as compared to 10 µg of inactivated virus (Fig. 5A), yet induced lower IgG2c titers (Fig. 5C). This might suggest that chemical inactivation results in generation of antibodies with reduced neutralizing activity, although further experimentation would be required to confirm this.
Evidence: The 1 µg VLP dose induced neutralizing antibody titers comparable with those observed after vaccination with 10 µg of inactivated CHIKV (Fig. 5A).
At the 1 µg dose, VLPs produced ∼10–20 fold, significantly lower antibody titers (IgG1 P<0.05, IgG2c P<0.01) than 10 µg inactivated CHIKV.
252.Rationale: The first part of the option accurately restates passage data: 1 µg VLPs gave neutralizing titers comparable to 10 µg inactivated CHIKV, and the VLPs had significantly lower IgG1/IgG2c ELISA titers. However, the subsequent suggestion that chemical inactivation might result in generation of antibodies with reduced neutralizing activity is a speculative causal inference not supported by the passage. The passage shows comparable neutralizing antibody titers between the two vaccines despite higher ELISA IgG titers in the inactivated-virus group; it does not provide evidence that chemical inactivation reduces neutralizing activity (and in fact the comparable neutralization argues against that). Therefore the speculative interpretation cannot be derived from the Passage Body alone.

252.Option: A single low-dose immunization with 1 µg of non-adjuvanted CHIKV VLPs provided complete protection against viraemia and foot swelling caused by CHIKV infection.
Evidence: All animals vaccinated with 1 µg VLPs were completely protected against viraemia (Fig. 6A).
The 1 µg dose of VLPs (with or without Quil A) provided complete protection against foot swelling, while the 0.1 µg dose reduced the peak foot swelling from 60–70% to 20–30% (Fig. 6B).
252.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports that all animals given 1 µg VLPs were completely protected against viraemia and that the 1 µg dose (with or without adjuvant) provided complete protection against foot swelling. Thus the statement is directly supported by the reported animal challenge outcomes.

252.Option: The VLPs induced a balanced IgG1/IgG2c response, in contrast with CHIKV infection where IgG2c responses dominate [12]. Whether this would have an important effect on protection is unclear.
Evidence: CHIKV-specific IgG1 and IgG2c titers were determined by ELISA, and broadly similar titers were seen for IgG1 and IgG2c (Fig. 5B, C), which contrasts with natural CHIKV infection where IgG2c dominates [12].
All animals vaccinated with 1 µg VLPs were completely protected against viraemia (Fig. 6A).
252.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that VLP vaccination produced broadly similar IgG1 and IgG2c titers, and notes that this contrasts with natural infection where IgG2c dominates. The passage does not provide data that isolate the effect of isotype balance on protection: despite the balanced isotype response, vaccinated animals were protected, but the text does not attribute protection specifically to the isotype pattern nor assess whether the balance alters protection. Therefore the statement that the effect on protection is unclear is consistent with the information provided and is a reasonable inference from the absence of direct evidence on isotype-driven protection.

253.Option: The persistence of H. ducreyi after MDA is due to the high rate of reinfection following clinical cure, which is more common in children than in adults.
Evidence: The infectivity of H. ducreyi is high [7], and reinfections following clinical and microbiological cure of H. ducreyi genital ulcers are common [8].
253.Rationale: The passage does state that reinfections following clinical and microbiological cure of H. ducreyi genital ulcers are common, which supports reinfection as a possible contributor to persistence. However, the passage does not provide any evidence that reinfection is 'more common in children than in adults.' That age-specific claim is not supported by the text, so the full option cannot be derived from the passage.

253.Option: The presence of H. ducreyi DNA in flies and bed linens indicates that these vectors are the primary source of new infections in the community.
Evidence: Flies (n = 10) were caught outside 10 houses of patients with ulcers; 9 (90%) of 10 flies had H. ducreyi DNA and 5 (50%) had amplifiable T. p. pertenue DNA (Table 3).
Environmental contamination was observed in 3 (50%) of 6 bed sheets sampled from 6 households with index cases; 2 sheets had detectable H. ducreyi DNA and 1 had detectable T. p. pertenue DNA.
Nevertheless, there is no clear evidence of H. ducreyi or yaws transmission by insects or fomites.
253.Rationale: While the study found H. ducreyi DNA on flies and bed linens, the passage explicitly states that 'there is no clear evidence of H. ducreyi or yaws transmission by insects or fomites.' Detecting DNA on potential environmental sources does not establish that they are the primary source of new infections. Therefore the strong causal statement that they are the primary source is not supported by the passage.

253.Option: The persistence of H. ducreyi after mass azithromycin treatment may be explained by asymptomatic skin colonization, environmental reservoirs such as flies and bed linens, and incomplete penetration of azithromycin to the skin surface.
Evidence: However, the persistence of H. ducreyi skin ulcers after mass azithromycin treatment [12,13] raises the possibility that the bacteria may exist in a natural reservoir.

If H. ducreyi adheres and survives on the healthy skin of asymptomatic carriers, this could enhance its persistence in the community and its transmission, because skin-colonizing bacteria might escape systemic azithromycin treatment and could infect the skin after a minor abrasion.

Asymptomatic carriage of H. ducreyi was demonstrated by PCR in the genital mucosa of about 2% of sex workers in The Gambia [14], but no studies address whether either H. ducreyi or T. p. pertenue potentially colonize non-genital skin.
Overall, 16 (21%, 95%CI 12–31) of 78 asymptomatic participants had H. ducreyi bacterial DNA on the skin, and 1 (1%) had T. p. pertenue DNA.
Flies (n = 10) were caught outside 10 houses of patients with ulcers; 9 (90%) of 10 flies had H. ducreyi DNA and 5 (50%) had amplifiable T. p. pertenue DNA (Table 3).
Environmental contamination was observed in 3 (50%) of 6 bed sheets sampled from 6 households with index cases; 2 sheets had detectable H. ducreyi DNA and 1 had detectable T. p. pertenue DNA.
253.Rationale: The passage explicitly raises the hypothesis that H. ducreyi may persist in a natural reservoir, and states that skin-colonizing bacteria could 'escape systemic azithromycin treatment,' explicitly suggesting incomplete eradication from skin. The study documents H. ducreyi DNA on asymptomatic skin (21%), detects H. ducreyi DNA on flies and in bed linens, and even recovered viable H. ducreyi from some asymptomatic skin cultures. These lines of evidence together directly support the multi-factor explanation in the option and are presented as the plausible explanations in the passage.

253.Option: The high prevalence of H. ducreyi in asymptomatic children suggests that the organism is more transmissible through sexual contact than through non-intact skin contact.
Evidence: Both infections are thought to be exclusively transmitted through non-intact skin contact with infectious lesions.

Asymptomatic carriage of H. ducreyi was demonstrated by PCR in the genital mucosa of about 2% of sex workers in The Gambia [14], but no studies address whether either H. ducreyi or T. p. pertenue potentially colonize non-genital skin.
Overall, 16 (21%, 95%CI 12–31) of 78 asymptomatic participants had H. ducreyi bacterial DNA on the skin...
253.Rationale: The passage states that transmission is thought to occur via non-intact skin contact and provides evidence of asymptomatic skin carriage. Although genital mucosal carriage in sex workers is mentioned, there is no evidence presented that sexual transmission is the dominant or more transmissible route for the cutaneous infections studied in children. The option's assertion that the organism is more transmissible through sexual contact contradicts the passage and is not derivable from the provided evidence.

253.Option: The limited impact of azithromycin on H. ducreyi is likely due to widespread resistance to macrolides in the H. ducreyi population.
Evidence: A single oral dose of 30 mg/kg azithromycin is highly effective for treatment of yaws [9], while 15 mg/kg generally provides effective treatment and prophylaxis against H. ducreyi genital ulcer strains [10].
Cutaneous strains of H. ducreyi are also susceptible to macrolides in vitro [11].
However, in rural villages of Lihir Island, MDA drastically decreased the proportion of ulcers containing T. p. pertenue DNA, but had limited impact on H. ducreyi [12].
253.Rationale: The passage reports that H. ducreyi strains are susceptible to macrolides in vitro and that azithromycin doses effective against yaws and genital H. ducreyi strains are known. There is no evidence in the passage of macrolide resistance in the H. ducreyi population; instead the authors propose reservoirs and carriage as explanations for persistence. Therefore the claim that persistence is 'likely due to widespread resistance' is not supported by the passage.

254.Option: The frequent and prolonged interactions between humans and bats in buildings, combined with common attempts to remove bats, create repeated opportunities for pathogen exposure through both direct and indirect contact, as well as through domestic animals that may act as intermediate hosts.
Evidence: Over 70% of people reported bat use of their buildings for >5 years (n = 72), with bat presence for 5–10 years most commonly reported (χ2 = 36.52, P < 0.01, Fig 1).
Most properties (88%) had bat presence year-round (n = 90).
Close to half of participants (39%) reported direct contact with bats, including people touching bats (n = 40) and one report of being bitten.
Over 90% of respondents reported indirect contact with bats, mostly through interactions with their feces and urine (n = 98).

Daily occurrences of indirect contact were reported by most participants (78%, χ2 = 285.06, P < 0.01, Fig 2B) and children, spouses, house guests, and custodians were also involved in these interactions.
Attempts to remove bats from buildings were reported by almost 80% of participants (n = 81).
Of those reporting removal efforts, almost half reported direct contact with bats (n = 34).
Domestic animals can also be exposed to these pathogens after contact with bats and their excreta or fluids [25].
Some also reported burning (n = 6), feeding domestic cats (n = 2), and burying bat carcasses (n = 1).
Interestingly, 13 respondents reported seeing domestic animals (dogs, cats, and chickens) consume dead bats on their property, most often their own animals.
254.Rationale: The passage documents long-term and year-round bat presence in buildings, high frequencies of indirect contact (feces/urine) and substantial direct contact, and frequent bat-removal attempts that often involve direct handling. It also explicitly notes that domestic animals can be exposed and provides observations of domestic animals consuming dead bats. Combining these facts, it is epidemiologically reasonable to infer repeated opportunities for pathogen exposure via direct and indirect human-bat contacts and via domestic animals that could serve as intermediate hosts. This inference follows directly from the documented contact patterns and reported behaviors.

254.Option: The high frequency of indirect contact with bat excreta and the common practice of disposing of dead bats by throwing them over property lines suggest that bat-borne pathogens are likely to spread widely within communities through environmental contamination.
Evidence: Over 90% of respondents reported indirect contact with bats, mostly through interactions with their feces and urine (n = 98).

Daily occurrences of indirect contact were reported by most participants (78%, χ2 = 285.06, P < 0.01, Fig 2B) and children, spouses, house guests, and custodians were also involved in these interactions.
Nearly 65% reported dead bats on properties (n = 51). Most removed dead bats, usually by throwing them over property lines (n = 30) or swept them outside (n = 7).
Few options exist for people to safely manage bat use of their buildings, and this provides numerous opportunities for human-bat contact and conflict.
254.Rationale: The passage documents behaviors that create opportunities for environmental contamination (high frequency of indirect contact with excreta and disposal of carcasses off-property). However, it does not provide evidence that bat-borne pathogens are present, transmitted, or established in the community, nor does it present data on infection incidence or transmission chains. The leap from observed behaviors that could facilitate contamination to the conclusion that pathogens are 'likely to spread widely within communities' is not directly supported by the presented data. Therefore this stronger claim about likely wide community spread cannot be derived solely from the passage.

255.Option: The use of household water treatment methods such as boiling significantly reduces the risk of infection from Cryptosporidium and Giardia in drinking water, as demonstrated by the 6 log10 reduction in viable parasites.
Evidence: To account for the effects of boiling water (the only point of use treatment method used by the study population) on parasite viability, we used WHO reported [39] 6 log10 reduction values for Cryptosporidium and Giardia from boiling (i.e. 6 log10 = 99.9999% reduction) and used household survey data collected from study households (n = 355) to estimate the fraction of households that boiled their drinking water (9% and 16% of deep and shallow tubewell users, respectively, reported boiling; see Table 1, B, boiling rates) (Appendix B.5 in Supporting Information).

For both parasites, diarrhea estimates were least sensitive to the volume of water ingested, the fraction of the population that treated water by boiling, the morbidity ratio, and recall bias as these parameters had sensitivity indices generally below 0.2.
255.Rationale: While the passage explicitly states that boiling yields a 6 log10 reduction in viable parasites (WHO value) and reports the modeled household boiling rates, the modeling sensitivity analysis shows that the fraction of the population that boiled water had low influence on modeled diarrhea estimates (sensitivity indices generally below 0.2). Thus, although boiling reduces organism counts in theory, the study's modeling indicates that household boiling was not a key driver of variation in estimated disease burden. Therefore the option's claim that boiling is a significant influence on variation in modeled burden (as implied by the question) is not supported by the results presented.

255.Option: The spatial proximity of tubewells to household latrines is the main determinant of whether detected parasites are from human or non-human sources, directly influencing disease burden estimates.
Evidence: Scenario two (ENV) assumed the probability of a parasite shed by a human host (H) was directly proportional to the ratio of the estimated environmental load of parasites shed by the population of humans to the total estimated environmental load shed by the population of humans, livestock, and domestic animals in Puri District [15].

To calculate the parasite environmental loading rates for humans and each animal species, we used four primary pieces of information as described previously [15]: parasite prevalence, parasite shedding rate, host population demographics, and host fecal production rates (see Appendix B.6.2 in S1 Supporting Information for further details).
255.Rationale: The passage describes how the model apportions human versus non-human sources using environmental loading estimates and microbial source tracking (MST) data, based on parasite prevalence, shedding rates, host demographics, and fecal production. There is no mention of spatial proximity of tubewells to household latrines being used as the primary determinant in the model. Because the model explicitly uses ENV and MST approaches rather than latrine distance, and no evidence links proximity to the key attribution, this option is not supported by the passage.

255.Option: The concentration of Cryptosporidium and Giardia in tubewell water varies significantly between years, which is the primary driver of differences in estimated disease burden between 2012 and 2013.
Evidence: A two-sample Kolmogorov-Smirnov test indicated concentrations were significantly different between years (P-value = < 0.001).
Comparing years, 2012 had a much higher estimated prevalence of diarrhea compared to 2013 under all scenarios.

We developed independent probability distributions for Cryptosporidium and Giardia concentrations by fitting a statistical distribution to the concentration data, which were non-negative, continuous, and typically skewed, using maximum likelihood methods. ... To account for concentration differences between parasites (Cryptosporidium and Giardia), sample year (2012–2013), and tubewell type (deep vs. shallow), separate gamma distributions were fitted for each parasite, year, and tubewell type (see Appendix B.2 in S1 Supporting Information).
255.Rationale: The passage shows that measured parasite concentrations in tubewells differed significantly between 2012 and 2013 (KS test p < 0.001), and the modeled disease burden was notably higher in 2012 than in 2013 across scenarios. The modeling explicitly used year-specific concentration distributions as an input, linking year-to-year concentration variation to differences in estimated diarrhea prevalence. Thus the statement that interannual variation in tubewell concentrations is a primary driver of the observed differences in modeled burden is directly supported by the passage.

255.Option: The morbidity ratio of symptomatic infections for Cryptosporidium and Giardia is the most sensitive parameter in the model, leading to the greatest variation in estimated disease burden.
Evidence: Of the parameters examined, Cryptosporidium 7-day recall diarrhea period prevalence estimates were most sensitive to the rate parameter used in the dose-response model, the fraction of detected parasites assumed to be viable, and the method recovery of parasites from tubewell samples.
For Giardia, diarrhea estimates were most sensitive to model parameters representing viability, method recovery, and the concentration of Giardia observed in tubewell samples.

For both parasites, diarrhea estimates were least sensitive to the volume of water ingested, the fraction of the population that treated water by boiling, the morbidity ratio, and recall bias as these parameters had sensitivity indices generally below 0.2.
255.Rationale: The sensitivity analysis presented in the passage identifies the dose-response rate parameter, viability, method recovery, and (for Giardia) concentration as the most influential parameters. It explicitly reports that the morbidity ratio had low sensitivity (indices generally below 0.2). Therefore the claim that the morbidity ratio is the most sensitive parameter is contradicted by the evidence and is incorrect.

255.Option: The fraction of parasites in drinking water that are from humans versus non-human animal hosts and that are species infectious-to-humans has important implications for infection risk and diarrhea disease burden estimates.
Evidence: We simulated three different scenarios to estimate the fraction of parasites detected in a tubewell sample that was a species infectious-to-humans (Sp)... Scenario one (HUM) assumed humans shed all of the detected parasites and 100% of both parasites were species infectious-to-humans. ... Scenario two (ENV) assumed the probability of a parasite shed by a human host (H) was directly proportional to the ratio of the estimated environmental load of parasites shed by the population of humans to the total estimated environmental load shed by the population of humans, livestock, and domestic animals in Puri District [15]. ... Scenario three (MST) used microbial fecal source tracking (MST) results by year and tubewell type for the same protozoa-tested tubewell water samples [55].

Across all tubewell types and years, assuming all parasites were shed from humans (HUM) increased diarrhea prevalence estimates by two-fold or more, compared to using fractions based on environmental parasite loading estimates (ENV) for Puri District (see Table H in S1 Supporting Information).

The results indicate that as much as 65.8% or as little as 2.9% of the all-cause diarrhea burden in < 5 children can be attributed to waterborne infections from Cryptosporidium and Giardia in tubewell water.
255.Rationale: The passage describes three attribution scenarios (HUM, ENV, MST) for the fraction of detected parasites that are infectious to humans and shows that these assumptions substantially change estimated diarrhea prevalence (e.g., HUM increases estimates two-fold or more versus ENV). The wide range of estimated attributable fractions (2.9% to 65.8%) across scenarios demonstrates that whether detected parasites are human-derived and of human-infectious species materially affects modeled infection risk and disease burden. Therefore this option is directly supported by the modeling results presented.

256.Option: The fact that 74.9% of patients reported trismus indicates that surgical release of ankylosis is ineffective and should be avoided in Noma survivors.
