The text discusses metabolic and microbiome interactions and helminth-related modulation of metabolic responses but does not address allergic inflammation or provide evidence linking the observed Lactobacillus increase to modulation of allergy. Thus the causal immunological claim about allergic inflammation cannot be derived from the provided material.

314.Option: The development of a placental injury score based on histological parameters not dependent on the presence of Plasmodium parasites or hemozoin allows for the detection of P. vivax-associated placental pathology in a setting where such evidence is scarce.
Evidence: By developing a histology score based on the most significant placental histology parameters associated with P. vivax, we show that women from an endemic region of the Brazilian Amazon, who were diagnosed with P. vivax during pregnancy, had higher levels of placental lesions than non-infected pregnant women and that these lesions increase in frequency with increasing exposure to the parasite.

Both Plasmodium parasites and malarial pigment (hemozoin) were only observed in the placental samples from women diagnosed with malaria during pregnancy. Only one of the 59 “vivax” group samples was found to have parasites in the intervillous space and whilst six of the 19 “falciparum” group samples had parasites present (χ2 test, P<0.001).
four of the 59 “vivax” group placentas exhibited evidence of hemozoin when compared with 11 of the 19 “falciparum” group placentas (χ2 test, P<0.001).
Placentas belonging to the “vivax” group exhibited higher scores than those from the “no plasmodium” group (Mann-Whitney, P=0.027).
the vivax-score increased significantly (** Cuzick's trend test: z=2.76, P=0.006) with increased exposure to P. vivax during pregnancy.
314.Rationale: The passage describes that parasites/hemozoin were rarely observed in P. vivax placentas (only 1/59 with parasites, 4/59 with hemozoin) but that the authors developed a histology-based score (vivax-score) from parameters associated with P. vivax exposure and that this score was higher in vivax-exposed placentas and increased with number of infections. Therefore it is supported that a placental injury score based on histological parameters (not reliant on finding parasites/hemozoin) can detect P. vivax-associated placental pathology where direct evidence of parasites is scarce.

314.Option: The increased syncytial knotting and placental barrier thickness observed in P. vivax-exposed placentas are likely due to systemic hypoxia caused by malaria-induced cytokine storms, as supported by previous studies.
Evidence: In cases of PM, accumulation of maternal immune cells (predominantly monocytes/macrophages and neutrophils) in the placenta and increased levels of inflammatory cytokines are common [10]–[13].
little is known regarding the potential of P. vivax to induce PM or even placental pathology.
Both syncytial knots (z=2.35; P=0.019) and placental barrier thickness (z=2.63; P=0.008) were elevated with increased frequency of P. vivax infections.
314.Rationale: While the passage documents increased syncytial knotting and placental barrier thickness associated with P. vivax exposure, it does not provide any data or citations that link these histological changes to systemic hypoxia or 'malaria-induced cytokine storms' in P. vivax infections. The background notes that inflammatory cytokines are common in placental malaria generally (mainly describing P. falciparum), but the authors explicitly state that 'little is known' about P. vivax placental pathology and suggest different mechanisms. Therefore the specific causal claim (systemic hypoxia from cytokine storms) is not supported by the passage and cannot be derived from the presented data.

314.Option: The presence of Plasmodium vivax in the placenta is associated with a significant increase in inflammatory cytokines and immune cell infiltration, similar to that observed in P. falciparum infections.
Evidence: In cases of PM, accumulation of maternal immune cells (predominantly monocytes/macrophages and neutrophils) in the placenta and increased levels of inflammatory cytokines are common [10]–[13].

Numbers of mononuclear cells were increased in the groups exposed to Plasmodium during pregnancy (Fig. 3C). Once again, the “vivax” group had intermediate values between the “no plasmodium” and the “falciparum” group which exhibited the highest values (ANOVA, P=0.039).
Only one of the 59 “vivax” group samples was found to have parasites in the intervillous space and whilst six of the 19 “falciparum” group samples had parasites present (χ2 test, P<0.001).
four of the 59 “vivax” group placentas exhibited evidence of hemozoin when compared with 11 of the 19 “falciparum” group placentas (χ2 test, P<0.001).
314.Rationale: The passage shows that mononuclear cells (a component of immune cell infiltration) were increased in Plasmodium-exposed placentas and that vivax-exposed placentas had intermediate values compared with falciparum. However, the statement asserts (1) the presence of P. vivax in the placenta is associated with significant increases and (2) there is similarity to P. falciparum including increased inflammatory cytokines. The passage reports that actual placental presence of P. vivax parasites/hemozoin was rare (only 1/59 with parasites; 4/59 with hemozoin), and it does not present any measurements of inflammatory cytokines for P. vivax. Thus the full claim (including cytokine increases and equivalence to P. falciparum) is not supported by the data in the passage.

314.Option: Because P. vivax infections are primarily detected in the first and third trimesters, placental damage is most severe in women infected during those periods, regardless of the number of infections.
Evidence: All P. vivax infections were equally distributed along the trimesters of gestation while P. falciparum infections occurred primarily in the first and third trimesters.
314.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that P. vivax infections were 'equally distributed along the trimesters of gestation,' so the premise that P. vivax infections are primarily detected in the first and third trimesters is false. Furthermore, the passage presents evidence that placental lesion scores increased with increasing numbers of P. vivax infections (trend tests), but it does not claim or present data that placental damage is 'most severe' in first/third trimester infections regardless of infection number. Therefore this option cannot be derived from the passage.

314.Option: The low number of P. falciparum-infected women in the study prevented the development of a falciparum-specific placental score, but this limitation does not affect the validity of the vivax-score for assessing P. vivax-associated pathology.
Evidence: A total of 123 placentas from singleton deliveries were available for study. ... consequently, 119 placentas were studied.

Forty-one (35%) of the placentas were obtained from women without documented evidence of any Plasmodium infection during pregnancy, 59 (49%) were taken from women with microscopically documented P. vivax infection during pregnancy and 19 (16%) were obtained from women with microscopically documented P. falciparum during pregnancy.

Multivariate logistic regression analyses were conducted to identify the placental histology parameters that were associated with P. vivax exposure during pregnancy when compared to no Plasmodium exposure. This was performed first in a random selection of women from the “no plasmodium” and the “vivax” groups, which were used to develop a placental score, which was subsequently applied to the entire cohort to confirm the results obtained (Table 5).
Placentas belonging to the “vivax” group exhibited higher scores than those from the “no plasmodium” group (Mann-Whitney, P=0.027).
314.Rationale: The passage documents that there were 19 falciparum placentas and that the authors developed a vivax-specific score using the 'no plasmodium' and 'vivax' groups and then applied it to the cohort, finding significant differences. However, the passage does not state that the low number of falciparum cases 'prevented' development of a falciparum-specific score—no such claim or attempt is described. The second clause (that this limitation does not affect validity of the vivax-score) is partially supported because the vivax-score showed statistically significant differences and trends in the data, but the assertion that the falciparum sample size limitation had no effect on validity is not explicitly addressed or proven in the passage. Because the first causal claim (prevention of falciparum score development) is not supported and the statement about lack of effect on validity is not established in the text, the option as a whole cannot be derived from the passage.

315.Option: The protective effect observed in S100A8/A9-/- mice is primarily due to a shift toward a type 2 immune response, as evidenced by elevated levels of IL-4 and IL-13 in the thoracic cavity.
Evidence: splenocytes of S100A8/A9-/- mice cultured in the presence of LsAg exhibited an increased production of IFN-γ (S3C Fig) and TNF (S3D Fig) compared to splenocytes of WTmice.
Concentrations of CXCL-1 (Fig 1E), CXCL-2 (Fig 1F), CXCL-5 (Fig 1G), and elastase (Fig 1H) were significantly increased in the thoracic cavity lavage of S100A8/A9-/- mice compared to WT controls.
315.Rationale: The passage presents evidence of increased type 1-associated cytokine production (IFN-γ and TNF) and elevated neutrophil-associated chemokines in S100A8/A9-/- mice, not elevated IL-4 or IL-13. The manuscript does not report increased IL-4 or IL-13 in the thoracic cavity; therefore a conclusion that the protective effect is primarily due to a shift toward a type 2 response (with elevated IL-4/IL-13) is not supported by the provided data.

315.Option: S100A8/A9 acts as a direct chemoattractant for L. sigmodontis L3 larvae, promoting their migration through the lung and into the thoracic cavity.
Evidence: Our data indicates an anti-inflammatory role of S100A8/S100A9 on neutrophil activation, which reduced inflammatory responses and facilitated the migration of infective L3 larvae into the thoracic cavity.
L3 larvae cultured with S100A8/A9-/- neutrophils showed an earlier inhibition of L3 motility compared to L3 larvae cultured with neutrophils of WT mice.
315.Rationale: The passage attributes S100A8/A9's effect to modulation of host inflammation/neutrophil activation (an anti-inflammatory role that facilitates larval migration), not to a direct chemoattractant effect on the larvae. There is no experimental evidence showing S100A8/A9 directly attracts larvae or acts on larvae as a chemoattractant; instead the data indicate an indirect effect via suppression of inflammatory/neutrophil responses.

315.Option: S100A8/A9 deficiency leads to a systemic increase in vascular permeability, which enhances the migration of L. sigmodontis L3 larvae into the thoracic cavity.
Evidence: However no statistically significant differences were observed between LsAg-treated WT and S100A8/A9-/- mice, suggesting that the absence of S100A8/A9 does not impact vascular permeability.
315.Rationale: The passage directly reports that the absence of S100A8/A9 does not impact vascular permeability in the experimental vascular permeability assay. Therefore the claim that deficiency leads to increased vascular permeability (which would enhance larval migration) is contradicted by the provided data.

315.Option: The increased neutrophil activation in S100A8/A9-/- mice is a consequence of enhanced apoptosis, which directly contributes to the clearance of L. sigmodontis L3 larvae.
Evidence: This increased activation of S100A8/A9-/- neutrophils was further associated with an increased apoptosis, resulting in a significantly higher frequency of apoptotic and dead neutrophils (Fig 4D).

L3 larvae cultured with S100A8/A9-/- neutrophils showed an earlier inhibition of L3 motility compared to L3 larvae cultured with neutrophils of WT mice (Fig 4E). Addition of DNase abrogated the inhibitory effect of S100A8/A9-/- neutrophils on L3 motility, suggesting that the effect is DNA trap-dependent.
315.Rationale: While the passage reports both increased neutrophil activation and increased apoptosis in S100A8/A9-/- neutrophils, it frames apoptosis as associated with activation rather than establishing that apoptosis causes the activation. Furthermore, the mechanism shown to impair larval motility is DNA trap-dependent (DNase-sensitive), implicating extracellular DNA/NETs rather than apoptosis per se as the direct mediator of larval inhibition. Thus the specific causal claim that enhanced apoptosis is the consequence producing increased activation and that apoptosis directly contributes to clearance is not supported by the data.

315.Option: S100A8/A9 inhibits L3-induced inflammation by decreasing chemokine production and neutrophil activation, thereby facilitating larval migration during L. sigmodontis infection.
Evidence: Our data indicates an anti-inflammatory role of S100A8/S100A9 on neutrophil activation, which reduced inflammatory responses and facilitated the migration of infective L3 larvae into the thoracic cavity.
Concentrations of CXCL-1 (Fig 1E), CXCL-2 (Fig 1F), CXCL-5 (Fig 1G), and elastase (Fig 1H) were significantly increased in the thoracic cavity lavage of S100A8/A9-/- mice compared to WT controls.

Myeloperoxidase (MPO) as well as elastase, enzymes which are abundantly expressed in neutrophils, were increasingly released (MPO: p<0.05; elastase: p = 0.11) from bone marrow-derived neutrophils of S100A8/A9-/- mice compared to WT neutrophils at baseline conditions in vitro (Fig 4A and 4B).
315.Rationale: The passage explicitly concludes an anti-inflammatory role for S100A8/A9 that down-modulates neutrophil activation and inflammatory chemokine production. Experimental data show that knockout mice (lacking S100A8/A9) have higher chemokine and neutrophil enzyme levels and more activated neutrophils, correlate with decreased worm burden (i.e., reduced larval migration). These observations support the inference that S100A8/A9 normally inhibits L3-induced inflammation and neutrophil activation, thereby facilitating larval migration during infection.

316.Option: Despite the delay in disease progression and the reduction in virus load in blood and organs as evidenced by virus titration and IHC, ribavirin was not able to prevent the lethal pathophysiological cascade.
Evidence: Although one animal survived after treatment, ribavirin did not significantly increase the survival rate (p = 0.4).

However, the drug prolonged the time to death (median 3 vs. 6 days for placebo vs. ribavirin, p = 0.0007), reduced the levels of AST (p = 0.001) and ALT (p = 0.006) at day 2, reduced the virus titer in blood at day 2 (p = 0.0007), increased the weight at day 2 and 3 (p = 0.03 and p = 0.002, respectively), and reduced the terminal virus concentration in all organs when compared to placebo at day 3 (p < 0.001 separately for each organ).

Histopathological analysis of organs collected at day 3 from ribavirin-treated mice revealed only small disseminated foci of necrosis; most of the liver parenchyma resembled naïve mice. Markedly reduced hepatocellular necrosis correlated with low numbers of apoptotic hepatocytes (cleaved caspase-3), T-cells (CD3), B-cells (B220), and activated monocyte-derived cells (iNOS). Virus antigen-positive cells (NP) were significantly reduced in liver and spleen compared to untreated or placebo-treated mice (data not shown).

However, ribavirin-treated mice that succumbed to infection on days 4–9 showed extensive bridging hepatocellular necrosis at the time of death (Fig. 2). Like in untreated mice, the necrosis was accompanied by presence of numerous Iba-1-positive macrophages (Kupffer cells), showing enlarged cell bodies and focal clustering, and iNOS-expressing activated monocyte-derived cells (Fig. 3). Both alterations are suggestive for strong monocyte/macrophage activation.
Thus, ribavirin reduces CCHFV load and delays disease progression, but it does not prevent terminal liver necrosis, monocyte/macrophage activation, and lethal outcome in the IFNAR−/− mouse model.
316.Rationale: The passage provides direct experimental findings showing that ribavirin reduced viral titers in blood and organs, lowered liver enzyme elevations early, and delayed time to death (evidence lines 2–3). Histology and IHC demonstrate reduced virus antigen and less necrosis at day 3 in treated animals (evidence line 3). Crucially, treated animals that later died still exhibited extensive terminal hepatocellular necrosis and markers of monocyte/macrophage activation despite low detectable virus in liver (evidence lines 4–5). The authors explicitly conclude that ribavirin "reduces CCHFV load and delays disease progression, but it does not prevent terminal liver necrosis, monocyte/macrophage activation, and lethal outcome," which directly supports the inference that ribavirin cannot prevent the lethal pathophysiological cascade even though it lowers virus load and delays disease. Therefore the statement is correctly derived from the Passage Body.

318.Option: The 24-bp insertion in the chitotriosidase gene, which results in impaired enzyme activity, is associated with an increased risk for the development of Madurella mycetomatis mycetoma.
Evidence: In the gene encoding for chitotriosidase, a 24-bp insertion in exon 10 was described previously [12, 14, 19, 20].
It was described elsewhere that chitotriosidase activity is reduced when patients are heterozygous for this insertion and completely absent when they were homozygous for this allele [12, 14].

Among the patients, 1/112 (0.9%) was homozygous for the 24-bp insertion and 27/112 (24.1%) were heterozygous. Among the controls, 1/103 (1.0%) was homozygous for the insertion and 8/103 (7.8%) were heterozygous.
The insertion containing allele was found significantly more frequently in the patients than in the control group (p = 0.004).
Based on these data, the 24-bp insertion in the chitotriosidase gene does increase the risk for invasive mycetoma disease caused by M. mycetomatis (odds ratio 2.9; 95% CI 1.4–6.1).
318.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the 24-bp insertion reduces or abolishes chitotriosidase activity and presents case-control data showing the insertion allele is significantly more frequent among patients, with an OR of 2.9 and p = 0.004. Therefore the genetic variant that impairs enzyme activity is associated with increased risk of M. mycetomatis mycetoma in this population.

318.Option: The high prevalence of M. mycetomatis in Sudanese soil and the presence of antibodies in the population indicate that the 24-bp insertion in chitotriosidase is a primary determinant of disease susceptibility in this population.
Evidence: In endemic areas in Sudan, M. mycetomatis DNA was found in the soil [3] and antibodies against mycetoma causative agents have been detected in the majority of the inhabitants in these areas [4].
However, it is not clear why only a minority of the exposed humans develop overt clinical infection.
Several environmental and patient-related factors have been described to influence the risk for the development of mycetoma, including concurrent schistosomiasis [5].
Based on these data, the 24-bp insertion in the chitotriosidase gene does increase the risk for invasive mycetoma disease caused by M. mycetomatis (odds ratio 2.9; 95% CI 1.4–6.1).
318.Rationale: While the passage documents environmental exposure and an association between the 24-bp insertion and disease risk, it also notes that only a minority of exposed people develop disease and lists multiple environmental and patient-related risk factors. The data show the insertion increases risk but do not establish it as the primary determinant of susceptibility in the population. The claim that it is the primary determinant is stronger than what the presented evidence supports.

318.Option: The lack of association between AMCase polymorphisms and mycetoma suggests that AMCase is not involved in the host immune response to M. mycetomatis in endemic populations.
Evidence: Thus, tissue sections were stained for chitotriosidase and for AMCase (Fig 2). The presence of chitinases is shown by a red color, which was mainly located in the grain on the fungal hyphae and not in the cement component of the grain.
We also found that both chitotriosidase and AMCase were found in the tissue surrounding the fungal grain, however both chitinases concentrated in the grain.

In contrast, binding of the AMCase specific antibodies was noted on some in vitro grown, fixated M. mycetomatis hyphae, although the staining was less intense than that inside the grain. Therefore it is plausible that not only chitotriosidase but also AMCase are indeed binding to the hyphae inside the mycetoma grain.
Neither the A50G and the A290G SNP, nor the 10-bp insertion in the 5’UTR region of AMCase is associated with invasive mycetoma

Allele frequencies of A50G and A290G in the patients were similar to those in the controls (p = 0.65; odds ratio 1.2; 95% CI 0.6–2.1; and p = 0.72; odds ratio 1.1; 95% CI 0.7–1.8, respectively). Furthermore, no difference was found in the presence of the 10-bp insertion between patients and controls (p = 0.72; odds ratio 1.1; 95% CI 0.7–1.8).
318.Rationale: The passage provides direct immunohistochemical evidence that AMCase is present and concentrated in and around the fungal grain and may bind hyphae, indicating involvement in the local host response. Although the specific AMCase polymorphisms studied were not associated with disease, absence of association for these genetic variants does not prove that AMCase is uninvolved in the immune response. Therefore the stronger statement that AMCase is not involved is not supported by the evidence provided.

318.Option: The 24-bp insertion in the chitotriosidase gene is associated with increased chitinase activity, which may contribute to a stronger immune response against M. mycetomatis in exposed individuals.
Evidence: It was described elsewhere that chitotriosidase activity is reduced when patients are heterozygous for this insertion and completely absent when they were homozygous for this allele [12, 14].
The insertion containing allele was found significantly more frequently in the patients than in the control group (p = 0.004).
Based on these data, the 24-bp insertion in the chitotriosidase gene does increase the risk for invasive mycetoma disease caused by M. mycetomatis (odds ratio 2.9; 95% CI 1.4–6.1).
318.Rationale: This option asserts the insertion increases chitinase activity and strengthens immunity, but the passage states the insertion reduces or abolishes chitotriosidase activity. Furthermore, the insertion is associated with increased disease risk, which is inconsistent with the idea that it enhances protective immune response. Thus the option contradicts the passage's data.

319.Option: The high rate of positive environmental swabs on household furniture and children’s hands indicates that flies are the dominant vector for trachoma transmission in hyperendemic communities.
Evidence: Samples of the household environment showed that C. trachomatis DNA was present on household objects, children, and adults (Fig 1).
Locations with the highest proportion of positive swabs were household furniture (18%) and children’s hands (8%).
Maintaining facial hygiene and environmental improvements, largely focused on reduction of flies through latrine construction, have been promoted to reduce access to infected secretions [9–15].
However, other possible routes of transmission of infected secretions, such as on hands of caretakers or clothing and bedding, may be present and could serve as targets for interventions.
319.Rationale: The passage documents C. trachomatis DNA on furniture and hands and notes fly-reduction interventions are promoted, but it does not provide evidence that flies are the dominant vector. In fact the authors explicitly point to other possible routes (hands, clothing, bedding). Therefore the assertion that flies are the dominant vector is not supported by the Passage Body.

319.Option: In high prevalence villages, C. trachomatis DNA was detected on household objects, children's hands, and clothing, suggesting that environmental contamination may contribute to transmission, particularly in settings where hygiene practices such as frequent clothing washing are less common.
Evidence: Samples of the household environment showed that C. trachomatis DNA was present on household objects, children, and adults (Fig 1).
Locations with the highest proportion of positive swabs were household furniture (18%) and children’s hands (8%).
There were 23 positive environmental swabs out of the 633 taken (3.6%).
The 23 positive environmental swabs were obtained from 13 households... Twenty-one of the positive swabs, collected from 11 households, were from the same village, Njoge.
other possible routes of transmission of infected secretions, such as on hands of caretakers or clothing and bedding, may be present and could serve as targets for interventions.

In this model, presence of an infant <1 year in the household was associated with increased odds of obtaining a positive environmental swab (OR 4.56; 95% CI 1.19–17.5) and washing clothes <3 days prior to the study visit was protective against obtaining a positive environmental swab (OR 0.17; 95% CI 0.03–0.88).
319.Rationale: The Passage Body directly shows detection of C. trachomatis DNA on household objects, hands, and clothing, concentrated in a high-positivity village. It also identifies hands and clothing as plausible routes of contamination and provides analytic evidence that recent clothes washing was protective (OR 0.17). Together these statements support the inference that environmental contamination may contribute to transmission and that less frequent hygiene (e.g., infrequent clothes washing) is associated with more detectable contamination.

319.Option: The lack of positive environmental swabs in two villages with low trachoma prevalence indicates that environmental contamination is not a significant factor in disease transmission.
Evidence: The 23 positive environmental swabs were obtained from 13 households (Table 3). Twenty-one of the positive swabs, collected from 11 households, were from the same village, Njoge. The villages of Majawanga and Makawa each contributed one positive environmental sample and the remaining two villages yielded none.

Obtaining at least one positive environmental swab from the household was associated with village of residence (ranging from 0% in Mkutani and Banyi Banyi to 34% in Njoge, p = 0.011) and presence of ocular infection in the index child (percent positive swabs 34% vs. 3%, p = 0.001).
However, other possible routes of transmission of infected secretions, such as on hands of caretakers or clothing and bedding, may be present and could serve as targets for interventions.
319.Rationale: While some villages had no positive environmental swabs, the Passage Body does not support concluding that environmental contamination is not a significant factor overall. The data show variation by village and a strong association between presence of ocular infection in the index child and environmental positives, which is compatible with environmental contamination playing a role in transmission. Absence of positives in some villages could reflect lower prevalence, sampling variability, or other factors; the passage does not justify the categorical conclusion that environmental contamination is not significant.

Significantly higher mf productions were observed in gerbils, which were 5-fold and 20-fold higher than those of CB.17 SCID or BALB/c IL-4Rα-/-/IL-5-/- mice; gerbil median = 7.4x104 mf/female ... vs = 1.5x104 mf/female ... or 3.5 x103 mf/female, (CB.17 SCID or BALB/c IL-4Rα-/-/IL-5-/- respectively, P<0.0001, Kruskal-Wallis with Dunn’s post-hoc test, Fig 1D).
324.Rationale: The passage states the opposite of the option's claim: although CB.17 SCID and BALB/c IL-4Rα-/-IL-5-/- mice produce higher adult worm yields, gerbils produce significantly higher microfilariae per female. Thus it is incorrect to infer that those mouse strains result in higher mf production compared to gerbils, and the option's conclusion is not supported by the passage.

324.Option: The decline in Wolbachia content in cultured female B. malayi is due to the absence of male mating partners, indicating that the culture system cannot support reproductive biology.
Evidence: As mf production was not sustained beyond 7 days in vitro (Fig 4A), the cessation of embryogenesis may have impacted on measures of overall metabolic activity and Wolbachia content, compared with freshly isolated, gravid female worms.

To more accurately interpret whether these measures reflected a real decline in viability or cessation of mf production, a sample of 62 adult female worms were isolated from CB.17 SCID mouse infections, and approximately equal batches were either processed immediately for metabolic activity and Wolbachia molecular assays, set up into culture within LEC bilayers, or implanted into naïve CB.17 SCID recipients (Fig 4C). After removal of male worms and 2-week in vivo implantation, female worm mf production had ceased.

Reductions in metabolic activity and Wolbachia production in female-only explants after two weeks were similar to levels in B. malayi cultured on LEC bilayers, which displayed an average 24% decline in metabolic activity (median 0.5 O.D.), and a 45% reduction in Wolbachia content (1.8x107 vs 3.3x107). Levels of metabolic activity and Wolbachia were adjudged not to vary significantly between female ex-implanted worms and cultured parasites.
324.Rationale: The authors observed that mf release ceased in vitro and that Wolbachia titres declined with time in culture. Crucially, an experimental control in which female worms were implanted alone (after removal of male worms) in vivo showed cessation of mf production and similar reductions in metabolic activity and Wolbachia content as seen in cultured females. This supports the inference that absence of male mating partners (female-only condition) leads to cessation of embryogenesis and reduced Wolbachia, and that the culture system, as implemented, does not sustain reproductive biology (mf production) in the absence of males. Therefore the option is supported by the passage evidence.

325.Option: The increase in mosquito abundance in oil palm plantations is likely due to higher mean temperatures, which enhance larval development and adult survival.
Evidence: Overall, human landing catches conducted in plantations yielded an average 1.5 times more mosquitoes per session than those at logged forest sites (GLM, RR = 0.42, df = 64, 95% CI: 1.17, 2.0, p = 0.002).

Temperature data indicated that although mean daily temperatures did not differ significantly between land-use types during the sampling period, daily temperature fluctuations were greater in oil palm plantations than in logged forest [55].
As neither gonotrophic cycle length nor parity rate differed significantly between land-use types, the median value across all sites was used to estimate adult survival (S) in both land-use types.
325.Rationale: The passage reports higher mosquito abundance in plantations but explicitly states mean daily temperatures did not differ significantly between land-use types, while daily fluctuations were greater in plantations. It also states measures linked to survival/reproduction (gonotrophic cycle length and parity rate) did not differ between land-use types. Therefore the specific causal claim that higher mean temperatures drove increased abundance (via enhanced larval development and adult survival) is not supported by the passage.

325.Option: The observed increase in vectorial capacity in oil palm plantations is consistent with predictions from published thermal performance curves, as both mean temperature and microclimatic variation were higher in these sites.
Evidence: Temperature data indicated that although mean daily temperatures did not differ significantly between land-use types during the sampling period, daily temperature fluctuations were greater in oil palm plantations than in logged forest [55].

Despite warmer temperatures in plantation sites, none of the parameters estimated using the temperature-dependent responses from Mordecai et al. [22] differed significantly between the land-use types for either sampling year (Wilcox, Z = 0.43, p = 0.663; Fig 1).
This key difference between land-use types was not predicted by commonly used TPCs.
325.Rationale: The passage indicates that published thermal performance curves (TPCs) did not predict the observed land-use difference in vectorial capacity, and that mean temperatures did not differ significantly (though fluctuations were greater in plantations). Thus the assertion that the increase in vectorial capacity is consistent with TPC predictions and driven by higher mean temperature and microclimatic variation is contradicted by the passage.

325.Option: The similarity in vectorial capacity between land-use types, despite differences in temperature fluctuations, suggests that microclimatic variation has no significant effect on mosquito transmission dynamics.
Evidence: Vectorial capacity was found to be 1.5 times greater in oil palm plantations (1.51 ± 0.03, mean ± SE) than in logged forest (0.99 ± 0.01, mean ± SE; F(1,4) = 61, β = 0.50, p < 0.001).

Temperature data indicated that although mean daily temperatures did not differ significantly between land-use types during the sampling period, daily temperature fluctuations were greater in oil palm plantations than in logged forest [55].

The higher vectorial capacity in oil palm plantations was driven by greater mosquito density (m) at plantation sites, as rVC estimates did not differ significantly between land-use types (Wilcox, W = 6.5, Z = 0.06, p = 0.77).
325.Rationale: The passage actually reports a substantial difference in vectorial capacity (higher in plantations). It does note greater temperature fluctuations in plantations, but it attributes the VC difference to higher mosquito density, not to an absence of microclimatic effects. Hence the claim of similarity in VC is false, and the stronger claim that microclimatic variation has no significant effect is not supported by the data presented.

325.Option: The higher vectorial capacity in oil palm plantations is primarily due to increased biting rates resulting from greater daily temperature fluctuations in these habitats.
Evidence: The higher vectorial capacity in oil palm plantations was driven by greater mosquito density (m) at plantation sites, as rVC estimates did not differ significantly between land-use types (Wilcox, W = 6.5, Z = 0.06, p = 0.77).

Temperature data indicated that although mean daily temperatures did not differ significantly between land-use types during the sampling period, daily temperature fluctuations were greater in oil palm plantations than in logged forest [55].

When calculated from thermal performance curves, biting rates were estimated to increase by 50% compared to estimates derived from laboratory data on gonotrophic cycle length, and did not differ significantly between land-use types (Wilcox, Z = -1.55, p = 0.13).
325.Rationale: The passage identifies greater mosquito density as the primary driver of higher vectorial capacity in plantations. It further reports that biting rates did not differ significantly between land-use types, and thus increased biting rate driven by temperature fluctuations is not supported as the primary cause.

325.Option: The increase in vectorial capacity in oil palm plantations, driven by greater mosquito abundance, was not predicted by commonly used thermal performance curves, despite similar mean temperatures between land-use types.
Evidence: vectorial capacity was estimated to be greater in oil palm plantation than in forest, largely driven by greater mosquito abundance in oil palm.
Vectorial capacity was found to be 1.5 times greater in oil palm plantations (1.51 ± 0.03, mean ± SE) than in logged forest (0.99 ± 0.01, mean ± SE; F(1,4) = 61, β = 0.50, p < 0.001).

Temperature data indicated that although mean daily temperatures did not differ significantly between land-use types during the sampling period, daily temperature fluctuations were greater in oil palm plantations than in logged forest [55].

Despite warmer temperatures in plantation sites, none of the parameters estimated using the temperature-dependent responses from Mordecai et al. [22] differed significantly between the land-use types for either sampling year (Wilcox, Z = 0.43, p = 0.663; Fig 1).
This key difference between land-use types was not predicted by commonly used TPCs.
325.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that vectorial capacity was higher in oil palm plantations and that this was largely driven by greater mosquito density. It also states mean daily temperatures did not differ significantly between land-use types while daily fluctuations were greater in plantations, and that commonly used thermal performance curves did not predict the observed land-use difference. Together these assertions support the inference in this option.

326.Option: Pyriproxyfen exposure through autodissemination does not affect the survivorship of adult A. mellifera workers, indicating that the method poses no significant risk to pollinator populations.
Evidence: Results suggest the direct and indirect transfer of PPF to artificial nectar sources from Ae. albopictus males. Furthermore, A. mellifera when in the same cages with PPF treated Ae. albopictus were also contaminated with PPF. The results suggest a potential risk of using autodissemination approaches to non-target species, particularly important insect pollinators.

To quantify the amount of PPF on individual and pooled Ae. albopictus and A. mellifera, and artificial nectar sources, we performed liquid-chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) analyses. Insects and nectar source materials and oviposition water were collected from cage types 3 and 4. Results suggest the presence of PPF on A. mellifera, Ae. albopictus females, and artificial nectar source materials from cages where PPF treated males were released (Fig 6). The most abundant amounts of PPF were observed on PPF treated Ae. albopictus males (0.12 ± 0.05 ng/mL) and A. mellifera workers (0.49 ± 0.26 ng/mL) (Fig 6).

No difference in A. mellifera survivorship was observed in cage types 1, 3, and 4 (Log-rank, Chi-square = 0.59, DF = 2, P = 0.75). A. mellifera mortality was ~25–30% in all cage types at day five (Fig 5D).

PPF is not mosquito specific and has been shown to inhibit the development of other insects [13–15] and to impact the physiology of non-target invertebrates [16, 17]. The effects of PPF on pollinators such as honey bees (Apis mellifera) are of particular concern [18–20].
326.Rationale: The option asserts there is no effect on adult A. mellifera survivorship and therefore no significant risk. The passage, however, documents direct and indirect transfer of PPF to A. mellifera, detectable PPF loads on bees by LC-MS (0.49 ± 0.26 ng/mL), and explicitly states the results suggest a potential risk to pollinators. Although the short-term cage survivorship analysis did not show a statistical difference between some cage types (mortality ~25–30% across cages), that finding alone does not justify the broad conclusion that autodissemination poses no significant risk. The study provides evidence of contamination and established non-specific toxic effects of PPF on other insects, so the blanket statement that there is no significant risk to pollinators is not supported by the passage.

326.Option: The indirect transfer of pyriproxyfen to honey bees via artificial nectar sources is unlikely in natural settings because A. mellifera foraging behavior in laboratory cages does not reflect real-world foraging patterns.
Evidence: Ae. albopictus males were demonstrated to deposit PPF on artificial nectar sources (Fig 1C, 1D, and 1E) in laboratory cages by placing PPF treated males in cages with an artificial nectar source and examining for PPF using bioassays.

A. mellifera placed in laboratory cages were also observed to forage on the artificial nectar sources. After acclimation to the laboratory cage conditions over a 2 h period, 12 of 15 bees were observed on the artificial nectar source (Fig 1G and 1H).
Images of A. mellifera collected from cage type 3 show the indirect transfer of PPF from treated Ae. albopictus males and artificial nectar sources (Fig 4A, 4B, 4E and 4F).
The results suggest a potential risk of using autodissemination approaches to non-target species, particularly important insect pollinators.
326.Rationale: The option makes an inferential claim about natural settings (that indirect transfer is unlikely) based on laboratory foraging behavior not reflecting reality. The passage does not provide any data or analysis comparing laboratory foraging to field foraging or concluding that laboratory results are not generalizable. Instead, the study demonstrates that bees will forage on nectar sources in cages and that indirect transfer to bees and nectar sources occurs under experimental conditions; the authors interpret these results as suggesting potential risk to pollinators. The passage provides no evidence to support the claim that such transfer is unlikely in natural settings, so this inference cannot be derived from the provided text.

326.Option: The persistence of pyriproxyfen on treated male Ae. albopictus males decreases over time due to foraging, grooming, mating, and contact with substrates, suggesting that the risk to non-target organisms diminishes as the treatment ages.
Evidence: PPF has been shown to persist up to four months in the environment and is incredibly potent [8, 9].

Ae. albopictus males were demonstrated to deposit PPF on artificial nectar sources (Fig 1C, 1D, and 1E) in laboratory cages by placing PPF treated males in cages with an artificial nectar source and examining for PPF using bioassays.

To quantify the amount of PPF on individual and pooled Ae. albopictus and A. mellifera, and artificial nectar sources, we performed liquid-chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) analyses. ... The most abundant amounts of PPF were observed on PPF treated Ae. albopictus males (0.12 ± 0.05 ng/mL) and A. mellifera workers (0.49 ± 0.26 ng/mL) (Fig 6).
326.Rationale: The option posits a temporal decline in PPF on treated males due to behaviors like foraging, grooming, mating, and contact, implying diminishing non-target risk over time. The passage does not provide experimental data on temporal decline of PPF loads on treated males or on how behavioral processes change PPF quantities over time. In fact, the Introduction notes that PPF can persist up to four months in the environment, and the study documents transfer from males to substrates and non-targets within the multi-day experiments, but no data are presented to support the specific claim that PPF on males decreases over time due to those behaviors or that risk diminishes as treatment ages. Therefore this inference is not supported by the passage.

326.Option: The use of autodissemination approaches with pyriproxyfen may pose a significant risk to non-target insect pollinators, such as honey bees, through indirect contamination via artificial nectar sources, despite the method's targeted delivery design.
Evidence: Results suggest the direct and indirect transfer of PPF to artificial nectar sources from Ae. albopictus males. Furthermore, A. mellifera when in the same cages with PPF treated Ae. albopictus were also contaminated with PPF. The results suggest a potential risk of using autodissemination approaches to non-target species, particularly important insect pollinators.

Ae. albopictus males were demonstrated to deposit PPF on artificial nectar sources ... Bioassays with cotton wicks from treatment cages showed a significant reduction in larval survivorship ... suggesting cross-contamination of the nectar source by PPF treated Ae. albopictus males (Fig 1F).

A. mellifera placed in laboratory cages were also observed to forage on the artificial nectar sources. After acclimation ... 12 of 15 bees were observed on the artificial nectar source (Fig 1G and 1H).
Images of A. mellifera collected from cage type 3 show the indirect transfer of PPF from treated Ae. albopictus males and artificial nectar sources (Fig 4A, 4B, 4E and 4F).

A significant lethal effect was observed in bioassays containing materials from an artificial nectar source, A. mellifera, and PPF treated Ae. albopictus males (ANOVA, F = 34.4, DF = 6, P < 0.0001) (Fig 5A).

Results suggest the presence of PPF on A. mellifera, Ae. albopictus females, and artificial nectar source materials from cages where PPF treated males were released (Fig 6). The most abundant amounts of PPF were observed on PPF treated Ae. albopictus males (0.12 ± 0.05 ng/mL) and A. mellifera workers (0.49 ± 0.26 ng/mL) (Fig 6).
326.Rationale: The passage provides multiple lines of experimental evidence that autodissemination using PPF can result in PPF deposition on artificial nectar sources, bee foraging on those sources, contamination of bees (demonstrated by imaging and LC-MS), and lethal effects in related bioassays. The authors explicitly state these findings suggest a potential risk to non-target pollinators. Thus, the inference that autodissemination approaches with PPF may pose significant risk to pollinators through indirect contamination via nectar sources is directly supported by the study's results.

326.Option: The primary route of pyriproxyfen transfer to non-target insects is direct contact during copulation between treated male and female mosquitoes, not through shared nectar sources.
Evidence: Ae. albopictus males were demonstrated to deposit PPF on artificial nectar sources (Fig 1C, 1D, and 1E) in laboratory cages by placing PPF treated males in cages with an artificial nectar source and examining for PPF using bioassays.

Bioassays with cotton wicks from treatment cages showed a significant reduction in larval survivorship (Kruskal-Wallis, Chi-sq = 11.4, DF = 3, P ≤ 0.01), suggesting cross-contamination of the nectar source by PPF treated Ae. albopictus males (Fig 1F).
Images of A. mellifera collected from cage type 3 show the indirect transfer of PPF from treated Ae. albopictus males and artificial nectar sources (Fig 4A, 4B, 4E and 4F).
PPF transfer to Ae. albopictus females was also observed in cage type 2 and 3 as suggested by the presence of fluorescent powder on untreated females placed in the cages (Fig 4I and 4J).
326.Rationale: The option claims the primary route is direct transfer via copulation rather than via shared nectar sources. The passage documents and emphasizes transfer from treated males to nectar sources and then to bees (imaging, bioassays, and LC-MS), and demonstrates that artificial nectar sources were contaminated and that A. mellifera foraged and acquired PPF. While the passage also documents transfer to untreated female mosquitoes (presence of fluorescent powder), it does not state that copulation is the primary route of transfer to non-target insects, nor does it present data showing mating/contact is the dominant pathway over nectar-mediated transfer. The experimental results explicitly demonstrate nectar-mediated indirect transfer to pollinators, so claiming copulation as the primary route is not supported by the provided evidence.

327.Option: The presence of two melting peaks in the cow sample indicates that the HRM-Cyt b technique is prone to false positives due to nuclear mitochondrial DNA (Numts) contamination, limiting its reliability in field studies.
Evidence: On the other hand, although the cow melt curve showed two peaks, the genotype was recognized with a high confidence percentage (Table 2).
These two peaks were found in different types of samples (muscle and blood).
327.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports that the cow sample showed two peaks but that the genotype was still recognized with a high confidence percentage; it attributes the two peaks to different sample types (muscle and blood). The passage contains no mention of nuclear mitochondrial DNA (Numts) contamination or that the two peaks represented false positives. Therefore the specific inference that two peaks indicate Numts-driven false positives and that HRM-Cyt b is thereby limited in reliability is not supported by the provided text.

327.Option: The HRM-Cyt b technique is the only method capable of detecting mixed blood-meal sources in triatomine insects, as confirmed by the successful identification of both mouse and chicken DNA in a single sample.
Evidence: In this case, the melt curve showed two peaks belonging to mouse and chicken amplicons, with their respective Tm.
So far, the methods used for this purpose include serologic and PCR-based molecular techniques.
327.Rationale: While the passage clearly documents that HRM-Cyt b detected mixed feeding (mouse and chicken peaks in one sample), it also states that other methods (serologic and PCR-based molecular techniques) have been used for blood-meal determination. The passage does not claim HRM is the only method capable of detecting mixed blood meals. Thus the exclusivity claim cannot be derived from the passage even though HRM's capability to detect mixed feeds is supported.

327.Option: The HRM analysis of the Cyt b gene is a fast, inexpensive, and accurate technique capable of identifying mixed blood-meal sources in triatomine insects, even when standard species are not available for comparison.
Evidence: Here we present a fast, inexpensive, accurate, and sensitive technique to identify blood-meal sources of triatomines based on Cyt b gene HRM analysis of host-specific genotypes and its application in field studies.
In this case, the melt curve showed two peaks belonging to mouse and chicken amplicons, with their respective Tm.

As mentioned above, four mixed samples, including one intradomiciliary insect, showed a peak with a Tm = 84.83±0.05, which was inconsistent with any of our standards (Table 4), indicating another additional blood-meal source that was not considered in this study.
Blastn results showed significant identity values greater than 95% for all samples except for sample number 20 (Table 5).
327.Rationale: The passage explicitly describes HRM-Cyt b as fast, inexpensive, accurate, and sensitive and shows it can detect mixed blood-meal sources (mouse and chicken peaks). However, the claim that it can identify mixed sources "even when standard species are not available for comparison" is not supported. The text indicates unknown Tm values inconsistent with standards signal an unconsidered source, but identification in such cases required sequencing confirmation (Blastn, sequencing and distance tree analyses). Therefore the portion of the option asserting capability to identify without standards is not derivable from the passage.

327.Option: The HRM-Cyt b technique can be used to identify the blood-meal source in triatomine insects without requiring any prior knowledge of the local fauna, making it universally applicable across all epidemiological settings.
Evidence: Species were chosen according to their epidemiological relevance as reservoirs of T. cruzi or by their proximity to anthropic environments.
Genotypes were defined by selecting a sample from each standard species as a reference control to identify unknown samples.

As mentioned above, four mixed samples, including one intradomiciliary insect, showed a peak with a Tm = 84.83±0.05, which was inconsistent with any of our standards (Table 4), indicating another additional blood-meal source that was not considered in this study.
327.Rationale: The passage describes building HRM genotypes using reference (standard) species selected for epidemiological relevance, indicating the method relies on prior inclusion of likely local species. It also reports instances where field samples had Tm values inconsistent with the available standards, indicating unconsidered blood-meal sources. Therefore the claim that HRM-Cyt b requires no prior knowledge of local fauna and is universally applicable in all settings is not supported by the passage.

327.Option: The HRM technique is highly sensitive and can detect blood-meal sources in triatomine insects for up to 30 days post-feeding, indicating that the method is superior to PCR-based methods for long-term surveillance of vector feeding patterns.
Evidence: All samples were successfully amplified by qPCR 1, 5, 15, and 30 days post-feeding (Table 3). Intestinal content and feces samples showed successful amplification and detection of the blood-meal source after 30 days post-feeding, indicating the high sensitivity of Cyt b-HRM at this time.

Unlike serological tools, PCR-based molecular methods are more specific and usually easier to perform, although the design of specific primers for each species involved in transmission scenarios makes it difficult to identify the vector and mammals from the wild cycle where a wide variety of species exist.
327.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that HRM-Cyt b detected blood-meal sources up to 30 days post-feeding and calls this high sensitivity. However, the assertion that HRM is superior to PCR-based methods for long-term surveillance is not supported by comparative data in the passage. The text discusses advantages and limitations of PCR-based approaches but does not present a direct comparison demonstrating HRM's superiority for long-term surveillance. Thus the superiority claim cannot be derived from the passage.

328.Option: The significant increase in eosinophil counts following ivermectin treatment is primarily due to the direct stimulatory effect of ivermectin on eosinophil production in the bone marrow.
Evidence: Median eosinophil counts rose from 171/μL to 1,095/μL (p<0.001).
Median MFD significantly decreased from 3,605 mf/mL to 1,810 mf/mL.

IVM mass drug administration in onchocerciasis endemic regions may cause SAEs such as life-threatening encephalopathies in patients with high L. loa MFD above 30,000 mf/mL, due to its potent microfilaricidal effect [9].
Eosinophils, known to express tissue factor, may trigger blood coagulation [5], leading to thrombosis.
328.Rationale: The passage documents a post-IVM rise in eosinophil counts but does not state or provide evidence that ivermectin directly stimulates eosinophil production in bone marrow. Instead, the text emphasizes IVM's microfilaricidal effect (decrease in MFD) and links eosinophils to coagulation via tissue factor, implying the eosinophil rise is more plausibly a reaction to dying microfilariae and ensuing inflammation rather than a direct marrow stimulatory effect of the drug. Therefore the specific mechanistic claim about direct bone-marrow stimulation by ivermectin is not supported by the passage.

328.Option: The polyclonal elevation of gamma-globulins observed in all participants is a direct consequence of ivermectin-induced immune activation following microfilarial death.
Evidence: Gamma-globulins were also elevated in all groups, with medians from 19 to 23 g/L (normal values: < 11 g/L).
Table 1 presents the blood counts and the inflammation and hemostasis parameters according to the participants’ L. loa MFD categories.
No new clonal gamma-globulin expression on protein electrophoresis was suspected, while previously identified clonal expression in treated patients did not change after IVM.
328.Rationale: The passage reports that gamma-globulins were elevated at baseline across all MFD groups, i.e., before ivermectin treatment, and states that IVM did not produce new clonal gamma-globulin expressions. There is no statement that ivermectin induced the polyclonal gamma-globulin elevation; instead the elevation appears to be a pre-existing feature of loiasis in this population. Thus attributing the polyclonal gamma-globulin elevation directly to ivermectin-induced immune activation after microfilarial death is not supported by the provided data.

328.Option: The increase in D-dimers observed after ivermectin treatment suggests both enhanced fibrinolysis and coagulation activation, which may be driven by intravascular dying microfilariae and inflammation-induced hypercoagulability, potentially contributing to the pathogenesis of post-ivermectin serious adverse events.
Evidence: Among hemostatic parameters, median D-dimers significantly increased, from 582 ng/mL to 745 ng/mL after IVM (p = 0.024) with a median relative variation of +34.7% (interquartile range: 1.44–72.5%).
Median eosinophil counts rose from 171/μL to 1,095/μL (p<0.001).

Various inflammation markers changed significantly: CRP, ESR, fibrinogen and alpha-1-globulin levels increased (p = 0.038, p = 0.010, p = 0.002 and p = 0.044, respectively), while albumin level decreased p = 0.044).

Evidence from primate models suggests an association between post-IVM encephalopathy and hemostatic dysregulation, characterized by degenerating mfs in small vessels associated with fibrin deposition, endothelial damages, and hemorrhages [10].
Eosinophils, known to express tissue factor, may trigger blood coagulation [5], leading to thrombosis.

Reports indicate that arterial and venous thrombotic events can occur in patients with parasite-related hypereosinophilia [6], with documented instances of thrombosis [7] and micro-emboli formation in capillary vessels [8], occurring both spontaneously and as part of post-ivermectin (IVM) Loa-related serious adverse events (SAEs).
328.Rationale: The passage shows a significant post-IVM rise in D-dimers alongside increases in inflammation markers (CRP, ESR, fibrinogen) and eosinophil counts, and documents primate-model evidence of degenerating microfilariae associated with fibrin deposition and endothelial damage. D-dimers are markers of fibrin degradation and their rise here, together with evidence of fibrin deposition and inflammatory activation, supports an interpretation of concurrent coagulation activation and subsequent fibrinolysis. The linkage to dying intravascular microfilariae and inflammation is directly described in the passage and is consistent with the hypothesized contribution to post-IVM serious adverse events.

328.Option: The increase in D-dimers after ivermectin treatment is primarily due to the breakdown of fibrin clots formed during the immune response to dying microfilariae, with no contribution from coagulation activation.
Evidence: Among hemostatic parameters, median D-dimers significantly increased, from 582 ng/mL to 745 ng/mL after IVM (p = 0.024) with a median relative variation of +34.7% (interquartile range: 1.44–72.5%).

Evidence from primate models suggests an association between post-IVM encephalopathy and hemostatic dysregulation, characterized by degenerating mfs in small vessels associated with fibrin deposition, endothelial damages, and hemorrhages [10].
Various inflammation markers changed significantly: CRP, ESR, fibrinogen and alpha-1-globulin levels increased (p = 0.038, p = 0.010, p = 0.002 and p = 0.044, respectively).
Eosinophils, known to express tissue factor, may trigger blood coagulation [5], leading to thrombosis.
328.Rationale: While D-dimers reflect fibrin breakdown, the passage provides direct evidence of fibrin deposition (primate models) and increases in fibrinogen and other inflammation markers after IVM, all of which indicate activation of coagulation processes. The claim that coagulation activation does not contribute is contradicted by these findings. Therefore asserting 'no contribution from coagulation activation' is not supported by the passage.

328.Option: The rise in D-dimers after ivermectin treatment is mainly attributable to the direct pro-coagulant effect of ivermectin on endothelial cells, independent of inflammation or microfilarial death.
Evidence: IVM mass drug administration in onchocerciasis endemic regions may cause SAEs such as life-threatening encephalopathies in patients with high L. loa MFD above 30,000 mf/mL, due to its potent microfilaricidal effect [9].

Evidence from primate models suggests an association between post-IVM encephalopathy and hemostatic dysregulation, characterized by degenerating mfs in small vessels associated with fibrin deposition, endothelial damages, and hemorrhages [10].
Median eosinophil counts rose from 171/μL to 1,095/μL (p<0.001).
Various inflammation markers changed significantly: CRP, ESR, fibrinogen and alpha-1-globulin levels increased (p = 0.038, p = 0.010, p = 0.002 and p = 0.044, respectively).
328.Rationale: The passage attributes post-IVM pathophysiology to the microfilaricidal effect of ivermectin (dying microfilariae) and documents associated inflammation, fibrin deposition, and endothelial damage in animal models. There is no evidence presented that ivermectin has a direct pro-coagulant action on endothelial cells independent of inflammation or microfilarial death. The data instead support mechanisms involving intravascular dying microfilariae and inflammation-driven coagulation, so the specific claim of a direct ivermectin endothelial pro-coagulant effect is unsupported by the passage.

329.Option: Because LAMP shows excellent agreement with lymph node aspirate microscopy, it can be used as a primary diagnostic tool in all suspected VL cases, even in the absence of confirmatory testing.
Evidence: Lymph node aspirate microscopy (LNA-M) was considered as the reference test of the study.
Participants who were positive by LNA-M were considered VL cases, while all other subjects were considered non-VL cases.
Loopamp Leishmania Detection Kit had high specificity (99.01%), regardless of the type of sample tested (whole blood or buffy coat) or the sample processing method.
The sensitivity was also high, with the best results obtained with whole blood processed either by the Boil & Spin method (97.62%) or by the QIAgen columns (100%).
Any of the LAMP methods as well as the RDT had a very good agreement with LNA-M (k = 0.96–0.99), while the agreement between DAT and LNA-M was inferior (k = 0.60–0.65, good agreement).
329.Rationale: While the passage documents that LAMP has very high sensitivity/specificity and excellent agreement with the reference (LNA-M), it also makes clear that LNA-M was the study reference and that cases were defined by LNA-M positivity. The passage does not claim that LAMP should be adopted universally as the sole primary diagnostic in all suspected cases without any confirmatory testing. Therefore the strong assertion that LAMP can be used as the primary diagnostic in all suspected VL cases, replacing confirmatory testing entirely, cannot be directly derived from the presented evidence.

329.Option: The Loopamp Leishmania Detection Kit has a shelf life of one year if stored between 1 and 30°C, and remains stable for 12 months at 30°C, making it suitable for use in high-temperature environments without cold chain requirements.
Evidence: A LAMP assay, Loopamp Leishmania Detection Kit (Eiken Chemical Co., Japan), based on dried down reagents immobilised in the reaction tube that can be stored at room temperature, was developed in a collaboration between Eiken Chemical Co., FIND and partners.
329.Rationale: The passage states that the Loopamp kit uses dried reagents that "can be stored at room temperature," but it gives no specific data on shelf life, stability durations, or performance at defined temperatures (e.g. 12 months at 30°C or storage between 1 and 30°C). The specific temperature and shelf-life claims in the option are not present in the passage and therefore cannot be derived from it.

329.Option: Given the high performance of the rK28 RDT and the excellent diagnostic accuracy of LAMP using peripheral blood, the Loopamp Leishmania Detection Kit can be used to replace invasive lymph node aspiration in most cases requiring parasite confirmation during VL diagnosis.
Evidence: examination of lymph node aspirates is preferred over bone marrow or spleen aspirates because it is easier and safer, and can be performed by paramedical staff.
Bone marrow and splenic aspiration, however, are rarely performed in Sudan due to technical and logistical difficulties, and are limited to reference laboratories [3,5,6].
Thus, this is another scenario where approaches to confirm Leishmania infection that are less invasive are required.

A LAMP assay, Loopamp Leishmania Detection Kit (Eiken Chemical Co., Japan), based on dried down reagents immobilised in the reaction tube that can be stored at room temperature, was developed in a collaboration between Eiken Chemical Co., FIND and partners.
we report on the evaluation of the performance of Loopamp Leishmania Detection Kit in the diagnosis of VL using peripheral blood in Sudan.
Loopamp Leishmania Detection Kit had high specificity (99.01%), regardless of the type of sample tested (whole blood or buffy coat) or the sample processing method.
The sensitivity was also high, with the best results obtained with whole blood processed either by the Boil & Spin method (97.62%) or by the QIAgen columns (100%).
Any of the LAMP methods as well as the RDT had a very good agreement with LNA-M (k = 0.96–0.99)
329.Rationale: The passage documents that tissue aspirates (bone marrow, spleen) are invasive and often impractical in Sudan, and explicitly states that less invasive approaches for parasite confirmation are needed. It then describes the Loopamp LAMP kit as a room-temperature-storable, field-amenable assay evaluated on peripheral blood and reports very high sensitivity and specificity (up to 100% with whole blood QIAgen processing, and 97.62% with Boil & Spin) and excellent agreement with the reference LNA-M. Taken together, these facts support the epidemiological inference that LAMP performed on peripheral blood can serve as a less invasive alternative to lymph node aspiration for parasite confirmation in most cases.

329.Option: The Loopamp Leishmania Detection Kit is the most sensitive and specific diagnostic tool available for VL, outperforming both serology and microscopy in all clinical contexts.
Evidence: Of the two serological test used, the rK28 RDT presented higher sensitivity (98.81%) and specificity (100%) than DAT, considering borderline results as either negative (88.10% and 78.22%) or positive (91.67% and 70.30%).
Loopamp Leishmania Detection Kit had high specificity (99.01%), regardless of the type of sample tested (whole blood or buffy coat) or the sample processing method.
The sensitivity was also high, with the best results obtained with whole blood processed either by the Boil & Spin method (97.62%) or by the QIAgen columns (100%).
Any of the LAMP methods as well as the RDT had a very good agreement with LNA-M (k = 0.96–0.99), while the agreement between DAT and LNA-M was inferior (k = 0.60–0.65, good agreement).
329.Rationale: Although LAMP shows excellent diagnostic performance, the passage does not support the absolute claim that it is the single most sensitive and specific tool in all clinical contexts. The rK28 RDT also showed very high sensitivity (98.81%) and specificity (100%) in this study. Furthermore, the passage presents performance metrics that vary by method and sample processing, and uses LNA-M as the reference. The blanket statement that LAMP outperforms both serology and microscopy in all contexts is not substantiated by the data presented.

329.Option: Since the rK28 RDT has 98.81% sensitivity and 100% specificity, it can fully replace the need for any confirmatory testing in suspected VL cases, including during test-of-cure and relapse evaluation.
Evidence: Of the two serological test used, the rK28 RDT presented higher sensitivity (98.81%) and specificity (100%) than DAT, considering borderline results as either negative (88.10% and 78.22%) or positive (91.67% and 70.30%).

Parasite confirmation by tissue aspirate microscopy is also used for test-of-cure, since serology is useless for this purpose as anti-Leishmania antibodies remain detectable up to several years after cure [1,7].
329.Rationale: While the rK28 RDT performs very well for initial diagnosis in this study, the passage explicitly states that serology is unsuitable for test-of-cure because anti-Leishmania antibodies can persist for years after cure. Therefore serological tests, including the rK28 RDT, cannot fully replace parasite-confirmatory testing (e.g., tissue microscopy or molecular methods) when evaluating cure or relapse. The passage does not support the broad claim that RDTs eliminate the need for any confirmatory testing in all clinical scenarios.

330.Option: The standard Rose Bengal Test is unreliable in endemic settings due to cross-reactivity with other gram-negative bacteria, particularly Yersinia enterocolitica serotype O:9.
Evidence: Antibodies induced by some gram-negative bacteria (mainly Yersinia enterocolitica serotype O:9) react in all brucellosis tests but, while they may pose a problem in animals, this cross-reactivity is not relevant in human brucellosis because the syndromes caused by such bacteria are clinically very different [34].
the so called acid pH agglutination tests, namely the RBT and BrucellaCapt, which detect both acute and long evolution cases [21,25–29].
RBT is a rapid agglutination assay well suited for resource limited scenarios.
Normally used with plain serum, RBT can be positive in persons from endemic areas and risk groups that do not have clinical symptoms, a problem largely solved by testing serum dilutions [21,30–32].
330.Rationale: The passage acknowledges that antibodies from some gram-negative bacteria (including Yersinia O:9) can react in brucellosis tests, but it explicitly states that this cross-reactivity "is not relevant in human brucellosis". The text further describes RBT as suited to resource-limited settings and explains that false positives in endemic areas can be mitigated by testing serum dilutions. Thus the specific claim that the standard RBT is unreliable in endemic settings primarily due to cross-reactivity with Yersinia O:9 is not supported by the passage. The passage does not conclude RBT is broadly unreliable for humans because of that cross-reactivity; instead it presents methods to address potential issues and describes RBT as a valuable tool.

330.Option: Despite the high likelihood of exposure to Brucella in slaughterhouse workers, the use of standard RBT with a titer ≥1:4 is sufficient for diagnosis under local conditions, reducing the need for additional confirmatory testing in most cases.
Evidence: Clinical examination, patient history, and epidemiological evidence largely form the basis for diagnosis of human brucellosis in resource limited settings. However, clinical diagnosis alone is inadequate because brucellosis signs and symptoms are unspecific, protean and can be confused with those of several other febrile illnesses, including malaria, tuberculosis and typhoid fever, all endemic in many African countries [6,15]. Therefore, it is essential to complement clinical examination with laboratory tests [6,16,17].
the so called acid pH agglutination tests, namely the RBT and BrucellaCapt, which detect both acute and long evolution cases [21,25–29].
Normally used with plain serum, RBT can be positive in persons from endemic areas and risk groups that do not have clinical symptoms, a problem largely solved by testing serum dilutions [21,30–32].

Out of the 31 sera positive in the standard RBT protocol, eight had a titre ≥ 1:8 in the modified RBT protocol, a very high titre in BrucellaCapt and seven self-reported symptoms (one case did not return this information). ... The remaining 21 RBT-positive sera had a titre 1:2 (i.e., only agglutinated with the plain serum) and of these only two had a 1:640 titre and 12 had a 1:320 titre with BrucellaCapt. When recorded, symptoms in these 21 slaughterhouse workers were variable.
330.Rationale: The passage emphasizes that laboratory testing must complement clinical evaluation and describes limitations of plain RBT (plain-serum positives in endemic areas) that are addressed by dilution testing. The empirical results show substantial heterogeneity: many RBT positives had low modified RBT titres (1:2) and variable BrucellaCapt results, indicating that an RBT ≥1:4 threshold alone would not consistently classify cases. The study itself used serial testing with RBT followed by BrucellaCapt to better capture acute and long-evolution cases. Therefore the claim that standard RBT with a titer ≥1:4 is sufficient and obviates need for confirmatory testing is not supported by the passage.

330.Option: The BrucellaCapt test is superior to the standard RBT for diagnosing brucellosis in Uganda because it detects both acute and long evolution cases and has higher sensitivity.
Evidence: the so called acid pH agglutination tests, namely the RBT and BrucellaCapt, which detect both acute and long evolution cases [21,25–29].
Complementary testing was done using the BrucellaCapt test, performed according to the manufacturer’s instructions (Vircell SL, Granada, Spain) on all RBT positive sera samples.

Among the ruminant slaughterhouse workers, the sero-prevalence according to results of the standard RBT protocol was 9.0% (31/344) (95% CI: 6.3–12.7). Considering the BrucellaCapt test and a titre ≥ 320 the seroprevalence was 7.3% (25/344) (95% CI: 4.8–10.7) (Table 2).

Out of the 31 sera positive in the standard RBT protocol, eight had a titre ≥ 1:8 in the modified RBT protocol, a very high titre in BrucellaCapt and seven self-reported symptoms... The remaining 21 RBT-positive sera had a titre 1:2 ... and of these only two had a 1:640 titre and 12 had a 1:320 titre with BrucellaCapt.
330.Rationale: The passage states that both RBT and BrucellaCapt detect acute and long-evolution cases, so detection scope is not unique to BrucellaCapt. The study used BrucellaCapt as complementary testing on RBT-positive sera rather than asserting outright superiority. Empirical results do not show higher sensitivity for BrucellaCapt: the RBT-based seroprevalence was 9.0% while BrucellaCapt (titre ≥320) yielded 7.3%. Individual sera showed mixed patterns (some low-RBT titres had high BrucellaCapt titres and vice versa), which supports complementary use rather than a clear claim that BrucellaCapt is uniformly superior with higher sensitivity. The passage does not provide direct evidence that BrucellaCapt has higher sensitivity overall, so the assertion of superiority is not supported by the study text.

331.Option: The identification of compounds that inhibit both TGR and Prx2 in the dual-enzyme assay suggests that the most effective therapeutic strategy for schistosomiasis may involve dual-target inhibition of the parasite's redox cascade.
Evidence: we chose to screen both enzymes in one assay as a reconstituted redox cascade.

When tested against Prx2 in a GR-coupled hydrogen peroxide reduction assay none of the selected compounds showed activity up to the 50 µM top concentration tested (and by extension, none were active against GR, an enzyme related to TGR).
Prx2 was therefore ruled out as the target of any of the actives identified in the screen.

The majority of active compounds demonstrated approximately the same, and in some instances improved (most notably with 7 and 8), potency against the isolated TGR as they did against the dual-enzyme system. These results not only confirm the initial findings from the screen, but also further support the hypothesis of TGR being the sole target of these actives.
331.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports that, after follow-up target-deconvolution assays, none of the selected compounds inhibited Prx2 and that Prx2 was ruled out as the target. It further states that the majority of actives target isolated TGR and supports TGR as the sole target. Therefore the claim that the identified compounds inhibit both TGR and Prx2 is contradicted by the passage, and a conclusion favoring dual-target inhibition is not supported by the evidence presented.

331.Option: The successful identification of potent inhibitors of TGR, combined with the lack of inhibition of human GR, suggests that these compounds may be used as broad-spectrum antiparasitic agents effective against multiple neglected tropical diseases.
Evidence: When tested against Prx2 in a GR-coupled hydrogen peroxide reduction assay none of the selected compounds showed activity up to the 50 µM top concentration tested (and by extension, none were active against GR, an enzyme related to TGR).

The majority of active compounds demonstrated approximately the same, and in some instances improved (most notably with 7 and 8), potency against the isolated TGR as they did against the dual-enzyme system.
Such improved understanding of the organisms responsible for neglected tropical diseases (NTDs) presents opportunities for new drug development.
331.Rationale: While the passage shows identification of potent TGR inhibitors and notes no activity in the Prx2/GR-coupled assay (interpreted as lack of activity against GR in that assay context), it does not provide evidence that these compounds are effective against other parasites or NTDs beyond S. mansoni. The statement claiming broad-spectrum antiparasitic utility across multiple NTDs is not supported by data in the passage (no cross-species efficacy testing, no demonstration of activity against other parasite targets). Thus the broad-spectrum implication cannot be derived from the presented evidence.

331.Option: The identification of several novel structural series of TGR inhibitors, several of which are highly potent, provides both mechanistic tools for probing the redox balance in S. mansoni and starting points for developing medicinal leads for much-needed new treatments for schistosomiasis.
Evidence: Analysis of the qHTS results revealed 39 actives characterized by full concentration-response curves and IC50 values of better than 10 µM.

the following series and singletons were selected for further studies after SAR analysis (Figure 4): oxadiazole 2-oxides (5 actives out of 29 analogues in collection, IC50 potency range between 8 µM and inactive), phosphinic amides (two compounds in the collection, one inactive and one active at 37 nM), phosphoramidite (singleton active, IC50 of 560 nM), and isoxazolone (singleton active, IC50 of 530 nM).

Gratifyingly, the previously-untested analogues of the phosphinic amides (compounds 1–4), phosphoramidite (13–16), and isoxazolone (10–12) actives all showed activity with various degrees of potency, supporting and expanding the qHTS findings.

The majority of active compounds demonstrated approximately the same, and in some instances improved (most notably with 7 and 8), potency against the isolated TGR as they did against the dual-enzyme system. These results not only confirm the initial findings from the screen, but also further support the hypothesis of TGR being the sole target of these actives.
Since inhibiting either TGR or Prx can potentially lead to schistosome death [22], we chose to screen both enzymes in one assay as a reconstituted redox cascade.
Such improved understanding of the organisms responsible for neglected tropical diseases (NTDs) presents opportunities for new drug development.
