There are great phylogenetic distances separating the genera that contain this gene family, but paralogs are restricted to select animal-infecting species within each lineage; suggesting that these proteins may be uniquely related to host adaptation.
389.Rationale: The passage explicitly states there are great phylogenetic distances separating the genera that contain this family. This contradicts the idea of a recent common ancestor shared by these bacteria with a fully developed virulence mechanism. The observed distribution is better explained by other evolutionary scenarios (e.g., independent gain, retention in certain lineages, or horizontal transfer), so a recent common-ancestor explanation is not supported by the provided data.

389.Option: The identification of a paralogous gene family shared by pathogenic Leptospira and the distantly related pathogens B. bacilliformis and B. australis suggests that horizontal gene transfer may have played a role in the evolution of virulence mechanisms in these bacteria.
Evidence: The PF07598 gene family has also been recently described in the unrelated α-proteobacteria species Bartonella bacilliformis and Bartonella australis. B. bacilliformis has 15 paralogs in its genome with B. australis having nearly the same (Fig. 5B).

There are great phylogenetic distances separating the genera that contain this gene family, but paralogs are restricted to select animal-infecting species within each lineage; suggesting that these proteins may be uniquely related to host adaptation.
389.Rationale: The passage documents an unexpected distribution of a paralogous gene family across distantly related bacterial genera (Leptospira and Bartonella) and notes restriction to certain animal-infecting species, which is an unusual phylogenetic pattern. While the authors do not demonstrate mechanism, this pattern is consistent with—and therefore allows the inference that—horizontal gene transfer may have contributed to the presence of these virulence-associated genes across distant lineages. The option's cautious phrasing ('may have played a role') matches what can be inferred from the data.

389.Option: The high degree of transcriptional up-regulation of the PF07598 gene family in blood and liver during acute infection suggests that these genes are primarily involved in immune evasion within the host's circulatory system.
Evidence: All 12 members of the leptospiral PF07598 gene family were analyzed for in vivo expression in hamsters acutely infected with virulent, wild type L. interrogans Lai 55601. All members of this gene family were up-regulated in blood and liver to varying degrees, with LA_3490 and LA_3388... being most highly up-regulated (more than ∼1000-fold); all members of this gene family were up-regulated in the circulation and liver to varying degrees.
All full-length members of this family (PF07598/DUF1561) are predicted to have secretory signal peptides, although degenerate forms do occur.

There are great phylogenetic distances separating the genera that contain this gene family, but paralogs are restricted to select animal-infecting species within each lineage; suggesting that these proteins may be uniquely related to host adaptation.
389.Rationale: The passage supports that PF07598 genes are highly up-regulated in blood and liver and are predicted to be secreted, implying host interaction. However, it does not provide experimental evidence that their primary function is immune evasion within the circulatory system. The data show tissue-specific expression and possible involvement in host adaptation, but assigning a specific mechanism (immune evasion) is not directly supported by the presented genomic or expression data.

390.Option: The high frequency of HLA-A0201 expression in Latin American populations enables the identification of a broad range of T. cruzi-specific CD8+ T cell responses, making it the most effective strategy for studying immune responses in chronic Chagas disease.
Evidence: Class I molecules can be grouped based upon structural similarities, into sets (supertypes) with similar predicted binding motifs; nine such supertypes have been defined to date [26]. The six most common HLA supertypes cover one or more HLA allele in >95 of a human population, irrespective of ethnicity [26],[27].

IFN-γ ELISPOT responses to peptide pools containing a total of ninety-six epitopes representing HLA A01 (18 epitopes), HLA A02 (20 epitopes), HLA A03 (20 epitopes), HLA A24 (14 epitopes), HLA B07 (11 epitopes) and HLA-B44 (13 epitopes) supertypes were evaluated in 25 chronic Chagas disease subjects without clinical and radiological evidence of heart disease (G0 status) regardless of their HLA type.
It is noteworthy that responses to HLA-A02 and HLA03 supertype-restricted epitopes covered 77% of the total responses detected, consistent with the high worldwide frequency of these alleles [26].
390.Rationale: The passage discusses HLA supertypes generally and notes that A02 and A03 supertypes covered a large share of responses and are frequent worldwide, but it does not state that HLA-A0201 frequency is especially high in Latin American populations nor that focusing solely on HLA-A0201 is the most effective strategy. The authors explicitly evaluated multiple supertypes (A01, A02, A03, A24, B07, B44) to extend coverage without individual HLA typing. Thus the specific claim that HLA-A0201 in Latin America makes it the single most effective strategy cannot be derived from the passage.

390.Option: The broad and multispecific CD8+ T cell response to T. cruzi in humans, compared to the focused response in mice, is primarily due to the greater genetic diversity of HLA alleles in human populations.
Evidence: Contrasting with the mouse system, a lower and more diffuse pattern of CD8+ T cell responses specific for HLA-A0201-restricted ts peptides in human subjects was found [25].

The loss of parasite-specific T cell responses during chronic infection, together with the diversity of HLA types and the diversity of parasite strains and thus epitope expression, have made it difficult to identify the specific epitopes recognized by T. cruzi-specific CD8+ T cells, and therefore to quantify the response and function of parasite-specific CD8+ T cells from chronic chagasic individuals.

A number of parameters have been implicated in determining which pathogen epitopes are primarily targeted by T cell responses, including the nature of the restricting MHC allele, efficiency of epitope processing and translocation into the endoplasmatic reticulum, the degree of sequence variability in epitopes derived from highly variable pathogens and antigen availability by either cross-presentation of exogenous antigen or processing of intracellular proteins [9]–[11].
390.Rationale: The passage notes that human CD8+ responses are lower and more diffuse than in mice and lists HLA polymorphism among several factors that complicate epitope identification. However, it does not provide evidence that HLA genetic diversity is the primary cause of the broader human response—only that it is one of multiple contributing parameters (processing, antigen availability, parasite strain diversity, etc.). The statement that HLA diversity is the primary reason is not directly supported by the presented data.

390.Option: The low prevalence of IL-2-secreting T cells specific for T. cruzi, despite the presence of functional IFN-γ-secreting T cells, suggests that chronic antigen persistence drives T cells toward a monofunctional, exhausted phenotype with impaired proliferative capacity.
Evidence: The simultaneous assessment of IFN-γ and IL-2 production after stimulation with ts-derived peptides, demonstrates that the CD8+ T cell compartment specific for T. cruzi displays a very low level of polyfunctional cytokine responses with a predominance of T cells secreting IFN-γ alone and very low prevalence of single IL-2-secreting or dual IFN-γ/IL-2 secreting T cells.

Positive IL-2 responses were very low irrespective of the clinical status of the subjects, and was essentially undetectable in the G1 group upon stimulation with ts peptides (mean net SFCs±SD/106 PBMC in subjects with positive IL-2 ELISPOT responses = 28±8, range = 15–55; mean SFCs±SD/106 PBMC in media alone = 6±5)
Less than 30% of the individuals evaluated displayed positive IL-2 and IFN-γ ELISPOT responses while IL-2 responses alone was not detected (Fig. 2A).

This functional pattern of cytokine production in response to HLA-class I-restricted ts epitopes in conjunction with the negative correlation between disease severity and IFN-γ-secreting T cells, found in the present and former studies [7], as well the increased levels of fully differentiated T cells in these patients [8] supports the notion that the long-term persistence of T. cruzi infection may lead to a degradation of parasite-specific T cell responses.

This apparent impairment in CD8+ T cell responses was associated with an increased frequency of fully differentiated memory (CD27−CD28−CD45RA−) CD8+ T cells and a high rate of apoptosis, possibly reflecting exhaustion in the CD8+ T cell compartment [8].
390.Rationale: The passage documents a pattern of predominantly IFN-γ-only secreting T cells with very low IL-2 or dual IFN-γ/IL-2 secretion (i.e., low polyfunctionality). It also links impaired CD8+ responses to increased fully differentiated memory phenotypes and higher apoptosis, and states that long-term persistence may lead to degradation of parasite-specific T cell responses. Low IL-2 is consistent with impaired proliferative capacity. Taken together, these statements support the inference that chronic antigen persistence drives T cells toward a monofunctional, exhausted phenotype with reduced IL-2-associated functions.

390.Option: The impaired IL-2 production in T. cruzi-specific T cells is a direct result of heart failure in advanced Chagas disease, rather than a consequence of chronic immune activation.
Evidence: Positive IL-2 responses were very low irrespective of the clinical status of the subjects, and was essentially undetectable in the G1 group upon stimulation with ts peptides (mean net SFCs±SD/106 PBMC in subjects with positive IL-2 ELISPOT responses = 28±8, range = 15–55; mean SFCs±SD/106 PBMC in media alone = 6±5)
Less than 30% of the individuals evaluated displayed positive IL-2 and IFN-γ ELISPOT responses while IL-2 responses alone was not detected (Fig. 2A).
This functional pattern of cytokine production ... supports the notion that the long-term persistence of T. cruzi infection may lead to a degradation of parasite-specific T cell responses.
390.Rationale: The passage reports that IL-2 responses were very low irrespective of clinical status, which argues against IL-2 impairment being directly caused only by heart failure in advanced disease. The authors instead link the pattern to long-term persistence and degradation/exhaustion of parasite-specific T cell responses. Therefore, the assertion that impaired IL-2 is a direct result of heart failure rather than chronic immune activation is not supported by the passage.

390.Option: The presence of IFN-γ-secreting T cells in chronic Chagas disease patients indicates that the immune system is effectively controlling parasite replication, preventing further tissue damage.
Evidence: Supporting the hypothesis that disease is less severe when immune control is more efficient we have shown that chronic chagasic subjects with no or mild clinical disease have significantly higher frequency of interferon-gamma (IFN-γ) producing T cells specific for T. cruzi than do individuals with more severe disease [7],[8].

Even in cases in which such responses are stimulated sufficiently to control the acute infection, T. cruzi is not completely cleared but instead, persists in infected hosts for decades. The inflammatory responses that such persistence induces may eventually result in the tissue damage that is associated with chronic Chagas disease [5],[6].
This functional pattern of cytokine production ... supports the notion that the long-term persistence of T. cruzi infection may lead to a degradation of parasite-specific T cell responses.
390.Rationale: While the passage shows a correlation between higher frequencies of IFN-γ-producing T cells and less severe disease, it also states that T. cruzi persists despite immune responses and that persistence can lead to inflammatory tissue damage. The data therefore do not support the stronger causal claim that presence of IFN-γ-secreting T cells indicates effective control of parasite replication that prevents tissue damage. The passage provides correlation but not definitive evidence of effective control or prevention of further damage.

391.Option: The temporal pattern of dengue in Port Sudan showed a bi-cyclic trend, with two transmission peaks: a short peak in winter (November–December) and a longer peak in summer (June–August), both preceded by peaks in mosquito density.
Evidence: The M-shaped curves have two peaks. The first peak of P/P (0.51) occured in November followed by a serological peak of IgM in December (7.89%). The second and largest peak of IgM was in July (8.14%). It was preceded by a higher peak of P/P two months earlier in May (0.66) and by the highest peak of larval ration in April (1.81).
The largest number of pupae was collected during April (n = 759), May (n = 739) and November (n = 759).
Both the very low P/P in August (0.04) and disappearance of dengue vector in September resulted in zero IgM seropositivity in September and October.
391.Rationale: The passage clearly documents a bi-modal (M-shaped) pattern with two peaks: a pupae peak in November preceding an IgM peak in December, and the largest IgM peak in July preceded by pupae peaks in April–May. Thus the existence of two peaks and that they are preceded by rises in mosquito density is supported. However, the option's specific claim that the summer peak is a "longer peak in summer (June–August)" is not supported by the text: the data show the major serological peak in July but report very low P/P in August and vector disappearance in September (leading to zero IgM in Sept/Oct). The passage does not provide evidence that the summer peak spans June–August or that it is longer in duration, so the extra temporal detail in the option is not derivable from the passage.

391.Option: The low incidence rate in lower and middle class neighborhoods suggests that these areas have higher herd immunity due to earlier exposure to dengue.
Evidence: BI, HI and P/P indices were lower in the upper class neighborhoods (65, 51 and .24), as compared to the other two strata (91, 66 and .38, respectively).

Overall, an incidence rate of 35 new cases in 10,000 (193/54,886) people was shown (Table 5). A significant difference between the high stratum and the other two strata was shown on incidence rates (F = 73.97, d.f. = 2, p<0.001), 0.0031 versus 0.0036 each, respectively.
391.Rationale: The passage shows that entomological indices (BI, HI, P/P) were lower in the upper class neighborhoods and that incidence rates were significantly lower in the high stratum (0.0031) versus the middle/lower strata (0.0036). This indicates incidence is higher, not lower, in the middle and lower class neighborhoods. The option's inference that those areas have low incidence and higher herd immunity is contradicted by the reported incidence data and invokes an unproven causal explanation (herd immunity) that is not supported by any serological/immunity evidence in the passage.

391.Option: The disappearance of the dengue vector (Ae. aegypti) in September demands further research to define whether there is a true disappearance and if so how and from where the vector is reintroduced.
Evidence: The dengue vector was found throughout the year in Port Sudan, except in September when no larvae or pupae Ae. aegypti was found in the inspected water containers.
It is possible that Ae. aegypti dwells in different breeding containers during September and/or there are desiccated eggs of the mosquito in the breeding containers.
391.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports an absence of detected larvae/pupae in September and offers possible explanations (different containers or desiccated eggs), indicating uncertainty about whether the vector truly disappears or is simply undetected. Concluding that further research is needed to clarify whether there is a true disappearance and the mechanism/source of reintroduction is a direct and reasonable epidemiological inference from the stated observations and the authors' own suggested possibilities.

391.Option: The bi-cyclic transmission pattern in Port Sudan is driven primarily by seasonal monsoon winds, which facilitate the dispersal of Ae. aegypti from neighboring countries.
Evidence: The global emergence of dengue is linked to increasing travel of viremic people as well as dispersal of its main vectors (i.e. Aedes aegypti and Ae. albopictus) into new locations [3]–[5].

Meteorological parameters (minimum and maximum temperature, relative humidity, precipitation and, evaporation rates, wind speed and direction) were recorded on daily basis in two stations in Port Sudan.
The dengue vector was found throughout the year in Port Sudan, except in September when no larvae or pupae Ae. aegypti was found in the inspected water containers.
391.Rationale: While the introduction describes broad drivers of global dengue emergence (travel and vector dispersal) and the study recorded meteorological parameters including wind, the passage provides no evidence that seasonal monsoon winds drive the local bi-cyclic pattern or that wind-mediated dispersal from neighboring countries is the primary mechanism. The study's temporal analyses focus on local mosquito indices, larval/pupal peaks, temperature and humidity correlations, and do not present data linking monsoon winds to local Ae. aegypti dispersal. Therefore the causal claim about monsoon winds is not supported by the passage.

391.Option: The high seroprevalence of IgM in July (8.14%) was likely due to secondary dengue infection in adults aged 18–39, who are more vulnerable to severe disease.
Evidence: The second and largest peak of IgM was in July (8.14%).
Among these age groups, higher dengue attack rates were shown in adults aged 18–39 and above 40 years, 2.14% (17/791) and 1.77% (14/791), respectively.
Dengue IgM high-titer serums were shown in 5.2% (41/791) specimens.
391.Rationale: The passage documents the July IgM peak and notes higher attack rates in adults 18–39 and those above 40 years, but it does not present any data on primary versus secondary infections, nor does it provide evidence that adults 18–39 are more vulnerable to severe disease in this setting. Inferring that the July IgM peak was "likely due to secondary dengue infection" and that adults 18–39 are especially vulnerable to severe disease is speculative and not derivable from the serology and age distribution data provided.

392.Option: Visiting workers in Al Ahsa exhibit a stronger immune response to sandfly saliva because they are more likely to have been previously infected with L. major.
Evidence: In Al Ahsa, we found that non-local patients (visiting labour) had significantly higher levels (P<0.001) of anti-PpSP32 compared to the local residents (Fig. 5A).
Interestingly, nearly three quarters of the non-local patients developed more than three lesions compared to only 40% in the local group (Fig. 5B).
Donor sera were classified as healthy (no history of leishmaniasis infection), ZCL (L. major) or ACL (L. tropica) patients with either active or cured CL.
392.Rationale: The passage supports that visiting workers (non-local patients) have higher anti-PpSP32 antibody levels and more lesions than local residents, but it does not provide any evidence that this stronger antibody response is explained by a higher likelihood of prior L. major infection in visiting workers. The classification statement shows the study recorded infection history (active or cured), but no data or quote in the passage indicates visiting workers were more likely to have been previously infected. Therefore the causal claim 'because they are more likely to have been previously infected with L. major' is not supported by the passage.

392.Option: The transient nature of the antibody response to sandfly bites allows for the detection of recent exposure but does not reflect long-term risk of leishmaniasis infection.
Evidence: Repeated exposure to sandfly bites produces antibodies against its salivary components in the host, providing an indirect measure of exposure to vectors [5].
The transient nature of the antibody response to sandfly bites [6–10] allows for the study of temporal changes in transmission risk and the efficacy of vector control programmes [11].
392.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that repeated exposure produces antibodies that serve as an indirect measure of exposure, and that the antibody response is transient, which 'allows for the study of temporal changes in transmission risk.' These statements support the inference that anti-saliva antibodies are indicators of recent or recent-past exposure (useful for detecting recent exposure) rather than persistent markers of long-term cumulative exposure or long-term infection risk. While the passage does not use the exact phrase 'does not reflect long-term risk,' the described transient quality logically implies the marker is not a stable indicator of long-term risk and instead reflects recent exposure.

392.Option: The higher antibody response to sandfly saliva in visiting workers compared to long-term residents in Al Ahsa suggests that the migrant population is highly exposed to sandfly bites and less immune to cutaneous leishmaniasis, while residents may have developed tolerance due to long-term exposure.
Evidence: In Al Ahsa, we found that non-local patients (visiting labour) had significantly higher levels (P<0.001) of anti-PpSP32 compared to the local residents (Fig. 5A).
Interestingly, nearly three quarters of the non-local patients developed more than three lesions compared to only 40% in the local group (Fig. 5B).
Repeated exposure to sandfly bites produces antibodies against its salivary components in the host, providing an indirect measure of exposure to vectors [5].
392.Rationale: The passage supports that visiting workers have higher anti-PpSP32 antibody levels (interpretable as higher exposure) and that they had more lesions. However, the additional claims that migrants are 'less immune to cutaneous leishmaniasis' and that residents 'may have developed tolerance due to long-term exposure' are not stated or evidenced in the passage. No data or discussion in the passage documents immunologic tolerance, differential immunity, or mechanisms explaining fewer lesions in residents. Therefore the speculative interpretation about relative immunity/tolerance cannot be derived from the provided text.

392.Option: The significantly higher number of lesions in visiting workers compared to local residents in Al Ahsa is primarily due to genetic susceptibility differences among the migrant population.
Evidence: In Al Ahsa, we found that non-local patients (visiting labour) had significantly higher levels (P<0.001) of anti-PpSP32 compared to the local residents (Fig. 5A).
Interestingly, nearly three quarters of the non-local patients developed more than three lesions compared to only 40% in the local group (Fig. 5B).
392.Rationale: While the passage documents that visiting workers had both higher antibody levels and a higher number of lesions, it contains no information about genetic factors or genetic susceptibility in the migrant population. The assertion that the difference in lesion number is 'primarily due to genetic susceptibility differences' is not supported by any data or discussion in the passage and therefore cannot be derived from it.

393.Option: Elevated levels of IL-6 in severe visceral leishmaniasis are primarily driven by concurrent bacterial infections, as indicated by high serum levels of LPS and FABP2.
Evidence: IL-6 levels were more significantly elevated in the patients with severe VL (Fig 3A; p = 0.03).
Severe VL patients that died had IL-6 serum levels of 11,443 ± 15,388 pg/ml, whereas the surviving severe VL patients had 2,848 ± 7,512 pg/ml (Fig 3B; p = 0.01).
All of the patients that died had circulating IL-6 levels ≥ 200 pg/ml, while only 2 patients that survived (1 SVL, 1 classical VL) presented with IL-6 levels above this threshold.
LPS and FABP2 levels were similar in the VL D0, DTH+ and HC groups and were not changed after treatment (VL D30) (Fig 4A and 4B).
393.Rationale: The passage shows IL-6 is elevated in severe/fatal VL, but it explicitly reports that LPS and FABP2 (serum markers indicative of bacterial translocation/infection) were similar between VL patients and controls and did not change with treatment. Therefore there is no evidence in this study that IL-6 elevation is primarily driven by concurrent bacterial infections as indicated by high LPS or FABP2; in fact the LPS/FABP2 data argue against that mechanism.

393.Option: IL-6 levels are elevated in severe visceral leishmaniasis due to a failure in the regulation of IFN-γ production, which leads to uncontrolled inflammation and tissue damage.
Evidence: classical VL patients present an impairment of IL-2, IFN-γ and IL-12 production from T cells specific to leishmania antigens in vitro, that is restored after successful treatment [6–14]. Interestingly, patients with active VL have high levels of IFN-γ and IL-12 in their serum, which theoretically could help to control the infection.
High levels of IFN-γ, IL-10, IL-6, IL-27 and TNF-α cytokines were observed in sera of VL patients before treatment when compared with healthy control and DTH+ individuals (Fig 1).
We observed a strong correlation between IL-27 and IL-6 (r = 0.69), IL-10 (r = 0.85) and TNF-α (r = 0.86).
The levels of particular circulating cytokines during the inflammatory processes observed in active VL disease could be a determinant, or arise as a consequence, of disease severity.
393.Rationale: The passage documents both impaired T-cell IFN-γ responses in vitro and elevated serum IFN-γ in active disease, and shows IL-6 is elevated in severe disease. However, it provides no data linking a failure in regulation of IFN-γ to the rise in IL-6 or demonstrating a causal pathway from dysregulated IFN-γ to uncontrolled IL-6-driven inflammation and tissue damage. The authors themselves note cytokine elevations could be cause or consequence. Thus this specific causal inference is not supported by the presented data.

393.Option: The presence of sCD14 in serum is a reliable biomarker for bacterial co-infection in visceral leishmaniasis patients, as it is elevated during active disease and decreases after treatment.
Evidence: sCD14 and MIF, molecules associated with TLR recognition, were higher in the active disease group (2,882 ng/ml and 39 ng/ml, respectively) than DTH+ group (1,348 ng/ml and 19 ng/ml, respectively). Furthermore, circulating levels of sCD14 and MIF decreased with treatment (1,820 ng/ml and 17 ng/ml, respectively) (Fig 4C and 4D).
LPS and FABP2 levels were similar in the VL D0, DTH+ and HC groups and were not changed after treatment (VL D30) (Fig 4A and 4B).
sCD14 levels were not, however, associated with disease severity/ mortality.
393.Rationale: While sCD14 is elevated during active disease and falls with treatment, the passage also shows LPS and FABP2 (other markers of bacterial translocation/infection) were not elevated. Moreover, sCD14 was not associated with disease severity or mortality. The authors suggest sCD14 could modulate immune responses, but they do not provide evidence that sCD14 is a reliable biomarker specifically for bacterial co-infection in VL patients. Therefore the strong claim of reliability for bacterial co-infection is not supported by the presented data.

393.Option: The systemic release of cytokines, particularly IL-6, is associated with disease severity and mortality in visceral leishmaniasis, independent of bacterial co-infection.
Evidence: IL-6 levels were more significantly elevated in the patients with severe VL (Fig 3A; p = 0.03).
Severe VL patients that died had IL-6 serum levels of 11,443 ± 15,388 pg/ml, whereas the surviving severe VL patients had 2,848 ± 7,512 pg/ml (Fig 3B; p = 0.01).
All of the patients that died had circulating IL-6 levels ≥ 200 pg/ml, while only 2 patients that survived (1 SVL, 1 classical VL) presented with IL-6 levels above this threshold.
LPS and FABP2 levels were similar in the VL D0, DTH+ and HC groups and were not changed after treatment (VL D30) (Fig 4A and 4B).
sCD14 levels were not, however, associated with disease severity/ mortality.
393.Rationale: The study demonstrates a clear association between higher IL-6 levels and both severe disease and death in VL patients. At the same time, direct markers of bacterial translocation/infection (LPS, FABP2) were not elevated, and sCD14—though increased—was not associated with mortality. These findings support an inference that systemic cytokine release, with IL-6 as a prominent marker, is linked to severity and mortality in VL and that this relationship is not explained by the study's measured indicators of concurrent bacterial co-infection.

393.Option: The strong correlation between IL-27, IL-10, IL-6, and TNF-α suggests that these cytokines act in a synergistic cascade to drive the progression of visceral leishmaniasis.
Evidence: We observed a strong correlation between IL-27 and IL-6 (r = 0.69), IL-10 (r = 0.85) and TNF-α (r = 0.86) (Fig 2).
High levels of IFN-γ, IL-10, IL-6, IL-27 and TNF-α cytokines were observed in sera of VL patients before treatment when compared with healthy control and DTH+ individuals (Fig 1).
The levels of particular circulating cytokines during the inflammatory processes observed in active VL disease could be a determinant, or arise as a consequence, of disease severity.
393.Rationale: The passage documents strong correlations among these cytokines and elevated levels in active VL, but correlation does not demonstrate a causal, synergistic cascade driving disease progression. The authors explicitly acknowledge that cytokine elevations could be either determinants or consequences of severity. Therefore the specific causal claim that these cytokines act in a synergistic cascade to drive progression is not supported by the data presented.

394.Option: The reproduction number in Chile fluctuated around 1.0 from early August 2020 onward, indicating that the epidemic was in a state of equilibrium with no net growth or decline in transmission.
Evidence: This was followed by a decline in the reproduction number to less than 1.0 between June 19th-August 9th, 2020. Since then, the reproduction number has fluctuated around 1.0 with the most recent estimate of R ~ 0.96 (95% CrI: 0.95, 0.98) (Fig 5).
The most recent estimate of R~ 0.96 (95% CrI: 0.95, 0.98) as of November 2nd, 2020.
Estimates of effective R indicate if the disease transmission continues (R>1) or if the active disease transmission ceases (R<1). Therefore, in order to contain an outbreak, we need to maintain R<1.
394.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that after a decline to <1.0 between June 19–Aug 9, the reproduction number "has fluctuated around 1.0" with the most recent estimate ~0.96. The passage also defines the epidemiological interpretation of R (>1 implies growth, <1 implies decline). Thus an R fluctuating around 1.0 is consistent with an epidemiological state near equilibrium with no clear sustained growth or decline in transmission and this inference is directly supported by the quoted text.

394.Option: The reopening of society in late April 2020 under the 'Safe Return' plan led to a sustained increase in case incidence, which was not mitigated by subsequent localized lockdowns in Santiago and Valparaíso.
Evidence: the government started to ease the COVID-19 restrictions in late April by reopening the economy under the “Safe Return” plan, including the televised opening of some businesses and stores, as new infections had reduced between 350–500 per day by the end of April, implying an only apparent flattening of the COVID-19 curve [33–35].

This strategy resulted in a new wave of infections; with the virus spreading from more affluent areas of Chile to more impoverished, crowded communities, forcing the government to reimpose lockdown measures in Santiago in mid-May (Fig 1) [23,37,38].
we calibrate the GLM from May 9th- July 7th, 2020, incorporating the effects of the reimplementation of localized lockdowns in Santiago, Antofagasta, and Valparaíso.
The reproduction number was estimated at 0.87 (95% CI: 0.84, 0.89) as of July 7th, 2020 (Fig 4).
394.Rationale: The passage supports that the Safe Return reopening in late April coincided with a resurgence of cases in mid-May. However, it does not support the claim that subsequent localized lockdowns in Santiago and Valparaíso failed to mitigate transmission. In fact, the GLM calibration that incorporated reimplementation of localized lockdowns produced an estimated reproduction number of 0.87 by July 7, 2020, indicating transmission was reduced (R<1). Therefore the claim that localized lockdowns did not mitigate the increase is not derivable from the passage.

394.Option: The initial sub-exponential growth pattern in Chile (p ~0.8) was primarily due to the country’s high population density and urbanization, which limited the effectiveness of early interventions.
Evidence: The incidence curve displays sub-exponential growth dynamics with the scaling of growth parameter (deceleration of growth parameter), p, estimated at 0.77 (95% CI: 0.73, 0.81) and the intrinsic growth rate, r, estimated at 0.81 (95% CI: 0.67, 1.00).
imposing and lifting lockdowns in small geographical areas (municipalities) proved unsuccessful in areas with high interdependencies such as the Greater Santiago.
394.Rationale: While the passage reports a sub-exponential growth pattern (p ~0.77–0.80) and mentions that small-area lockdowns were unsuccessful in areas with high interdependencies (Greater Santiago), it does not attribute the initial sub-exponential growth primarily to high population density or urbanization, nor does it state that these factors limited early interventions. The causal statement that population density/urbanization were the primary drivers is not supported by the passage text and therefore cannot be derived solely from the presented material.

394.Option: The implementation of lockdowns and apt social distancing interventions have indeed slowed the spread of the virus.
Evidence: The 20-day ahead forecast suggested that the early social distancing measures significantly slowed down the early spread of the virus in Chile, whose effect is noticeable about two weeks after implementing an intervention, as shown in Fig 6.

The 20-day ahead average forecast utilizing the GLM model showed that Chile could accumulate ~45,160 cases (95% CI: 27,934–67,600) between July 8th-July 27th, 2020 (Fig 7). Our forecast results approximate closely the ~46,798 cases reported between July 8th-July 27th, 2020 by the Ministry of Health, Chile.
The reproduction number was estimated at 0.87 (95% CI: 0.84, 0.89) as of July 7th, 2020 (Fig 4).
394.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that model forecasts indicated early social distancing measures "significantly slowed down the early spread" with effects evident about two weeks after interventions. Additionally, the reproduction number estimated after reimplementation of lockdowns was below 1 (0.87 by July 7), consistent with reduced transmission. These statements together support the inference that lockdowns and social distancing slowed viral spread.

394.Option: The high positivity rate at the beginning of the epidemic indicates that the government failed to cast a wide enough net to test the masses early in the pandemic, and there were probably many more active cases than those detected by epidemiological surveillance, underestimating the epidemic growth curve.
Evidence: The total number of tests performed for this time period were 4,325,617, amongst which 10.9% (47,597) had positive results.
The average number of daily tests was estimated at ~5,460 for April 2020 and ~12,959 for May 2020, a 137% increase.

The positivity rate (percentage of positive tests among the total number of tests) has fluctuated from a monthly average of ~9.07% (SD: 2.3) in April 2020 to a monthly average of ~4.87% (SD: 0.65) in October 2020.
394.Rationale: The passage provides testing counts and positivity rates (higher early positivity declining over time) but does not explicitly state that the government "failed" to test broadly early on or quantify the degree of under-ascertainment. While a high positivity rate can be consistent with insufficient testing and undetected cases, this causal conclusion is not directly asserted or quantified in the passage. Therefore the more definitive claim about government failure and probable large underestimation of cases cannot be conclusively derived from the provided text alone.

395.Option: The level of knowledge about rabies and its control measures is directly associated with the uptake of the 2012 vaccination campaign, indicating that educational campaigns should be prioritized to improve vaccination rates.
Evidence: The vaccination uptake was significantly associated with the knowledge of the dog owners about rabies (p<0.001) and its control measures (p<0.05) (Table 6).

Of the 13 independent variables that had an association (p-value less than 0.25) with the uptake of the 2012 vaccination campaign in the univariable analyses (Table 1 and Table 6), only five variables were retained in the final multivariable model.
Regency, having female dogs for breeding, economic value of dogs, income of dog owners, and accessibility of the village were significantly associated with the uptake of vaccination (Table 7).
395.Rationale: While the passage states that vaccination uptake was significantly associated with knowledge in univariable analyses, knowledge was not retained as an independent predictor in the final multivariable model. The final model identifies regency, female dogs, economic value, owner income, and village accessibility as the significant independent predictors. Therefore, it is not supported by the passage to conclude that knowledge is the primary or most influential factor (or that educational campaigns should necessarily be prioritized above other interventions) based solely on these data.

395.Option: Geographical accessibility is one of the important predictors of vaccination uptake among dog owners, and targeted interventions in villages with poor accessibility may increase vaccination uptake in the future.
Evidence: Regency, having female dogs for breeding, economic value of dogs, income of dog owners, and accessibility of the village were significantly associated with the uptake of vaccination (Table 7).

The uptake of vaccination was higher if the dog owners had a monthly income of more than 1 million Rupiah (OR = 2.39; 95%CI = 1.10–5.20) and lived in a village with a good accessibility (OR = 3.84; 95%CI = 1.92–7.67) compared with those having a yearly income less than Rp500,000 and who lived in a village with poor accessibility.

For those dog owners who had not vaccinated their dogs (216 of 450 dog owners), the most important reasons for not joining were the lack of information about the schedule of the vaccination campaign (40%) and the difficulty to catch their dogs during the vaccination campaign (37%).
395.Rationale: The multivariable analysis identifies village accessibility as an independent predictor of vaccination uptake, with substantially higher odds of uptake in villages with good accessibility (OR = 3.84). The survey also documents logistical barriers (lack of information, difficulty catching dogs) that plausibly interact with accessibility. From these data, it is epidemiologically reasonable to infer that targeted interventions addressing access and logistics in poorly accessible villages could increase vaccination uptake.

395.Option: The low vaccination coverage in Flores Island is primarily due to the lack of trust in the vaccine's efficacy, as many dog owners expressed skepticism about its effectiveness.
Evidence: For those dog owners who had not vaccinated their dogs (216 of 450 dog owners), the most important reasons for not joining were the lack of information about the schedule of the vaccination campaign (40%) and the difficulty to catch their dogs during the vaccination campaign (37%).
Other reasons, of minor importance, included the lack of belief in the vaccine efficacy (13%), and the young age of the dog at the time of the vaccination campaign (6%).

The uptake rate of the dog vaccination measure has been low, with an average vaccination coverage of around 53% of the registered dogs during the 2000–2011 vaccination campaigns ("This value is lower than the 70% coverage ... recommended").
395.Rationale: The passage lists lack of information and difficulty catching dogs as the two main reasons for non-participation (40% and 37%, respectively), while lack of belief in vaccine efficacy is reported by only 13% of non-participants. Therefore, the data do not support the claim that distrust in vaccine efficacy is the primary cause of low coverage.

395.Option: The high uptake of post-exposure treatment among dog-bite victims indicates that the public health system in Flores Island is well-equipped to manage rabies exposure and prevent fatalities.
Evidence: Of the 450 dog owners interviewed, 89 (20%) reported that at least one of their family members had been bitten by a suspected rabid dog during the last fourteen years. Of these 89 bite cases, 75 (84%) cleaned the wound, and 50 (56%) received vaccination.
The level of uptake of these measures did not differ between regencies (p>0.05).
Thousands of people bitten by dogs are looking for post-exposure treatment each year, resulting in a large economic cost for both government and local communities.
395.Rationale: Although a majority cleaned wounds (84%), only 56% received vaccination after a bite. The passage does not provide data on outcomes (fatalities prevented), the capacity of health services, or whether the vaccines were timely and complete. The statement that the system is 'well-equipped' is an inference beyond the presented data. Therefore, this conclusion cannot be directly derived from the passage.

395.Option: The culling of roaming dogs in Manggarai Regency has been effective in reducing rabies transmission and should be expanded to other regencies as a primary control strategy.
Evidence: The proportion of dog owners that had culled their dogs was significantly higher in Manggarai (45%) than in Sikka (27%) (p<0.001).
The difference in the number of dogs per regency is a result of the culling measures that were implemented in Manggarai.
However, this campaign has not yet been successful in eliminating rabies from Flores Island.
395.Rationale: While culling was more commonly reported in Manggarai and contributed to lower dog counts, the passage explicitly states that the overall campaign has not eliminated rabies on Flores Island. No data are presented linking culling to reduced human or canine rabies incidence or demonstrating its effectiveness as a primary control strategy. Therefore, one cannot conclude from these data that culling has been effective or should be expanded.

396.Option: The spatial distribution of dengue cases in Peru is primarily driven by human movement and population density, with weather patterns playing a secondary role in determining transmission intensity.
Evidence: We conclude that a coupling of temperature-humidity is an important and sensitive determinant of DENV transmission-potential regulating the location, timing and intensity of risk.

With 30 million people in Peru, the largest population centers are mostly clustered along the western coast. Highest dengue incidence rates developed in a different spatial pattern across northeastern Peru.
High dengue impact was concentrated in a small area of weather-space above 25°C mean temperature and above 75% mean humidity.
396.Rationale: The passage explicitly concludes that the coupling of temperature and humidity is an important determinant regulating the location, timing and intensity of dengue risk. It also notes that largest population centers are clustered on the western coast while highest incidence developed in northeastern Peru, demonstrating that population distribution does not primarily explain case spatial distribution. The evidence therefore does not support the claim that human movement and population density are the primary drivers with weather secondary; rather, weather (temperature-humidity coupling) is presented as the primary determinant.

396.Option: The coupling of temperature and humidity is a critical determinant of dengue transmission potential, with higher humidity levels amplifying transmission magnitude when temperature is optimal, and transmission intensity increasing exponentially with longer duration in optimal weather-space.
Evidence: We conclude that a coupling of temperature-humidity is an important and sensitive determinant of DENV transmission-potential regulating the location, timing and intensity of risk.

Temperature and humidity are emerging as weather components with greatest impact on DENV transmission, yet the separate and combined effects of this coupling across a range of ecological conditions remain ill-defined [3,4,7,11].

High dengue impact was concentrated in a small area of weather-space above 25°C mean temperature and above 75% mean humidity. Impact was highest as humidity increased above 80% when mean temperature was 25–29°C.

The natural log of cumulative incidence rate was used because the increase in incidence as temperatures rise within the optimal range and as humidity rises amidst temperatures optimal for transmission is exponential in nature.
Annual duration above 22°C was the best predictor of incidence magnitude in lower incidence districts; duration above 25°C was the best predictor in higher incidence districts.

In higher incidence districts, mean humidity was particularly important. Annual duration above mean humidity levels of 70%, 75%, and 80% when mean temperature was concurrently above 25°C were associated with the highest rate of increasing cumulative incidence (p<0.0001 in each scenario)
396.Rationale: Multiple statements in the passage directly support this inference: the authors conclude that temperature-humidity coupling is an important determinant of transmission potential; analyses show high impact is concentrated where mean temperature >25°C and mean humidity >75%, with greatest impact when humidity >80% at 25–29°C; and they model incidence on the log scale because incidence increases exponentially with time spent in optimal temperature-humidity conditions. Duration above specific temperature and humidity thresholds is linked to higher cumulative incidence, supporting the claim that longer duration in optimal weather-space amplifies transmission exponentially.

396.Option: The relationship between weather and dengue transmission is linear and predictable, with each degree increase in temperature directly proportional to a corresponding increase in case counts.
Evidence: Weather-dengue dynamics are complex, multi-factorial, and non-linear and traditional statistical methods have produced diverse and conflicting perspectives regarding the weather-disease connection [3–5,7,8,11–18].

The natural log of cumulative incidence rate was used because the increase in incidence as temperatures rise within the optimal range and as humidity rises amidst temperatures optimal for transmission is exponential in nature.
Synthesis of many weather dependent dynamics into a measure of potential for virus transmission in ecological settings that continually change in space and time is a complex undertaking.
396.Rationale: The passage explicitly characterizes weather-dengue dynamics as complex and non-linear, and states that incidence increases exponentially (not linearly) with time spent in optimal weather-space. Therefore the claim of a simple linear, directly proportional relationship between temperature and case counts is contradicted by the text and cannot be derived from the passage.

396.Option: Rainfall is a more significant predictor of dengue transmission in Peru than temperature and humidity, as it directly influences mosquito breeding sites and seasonal onset of epidemics.
Evidence: Temperature and humidity are emerging as weather components with greatest impact on DENV transmission, yet the separate and combined effects of this coupling across a range of ecological conditions remain ill-defined [3,4,7,11].
High dengue impact was concentrated in a small area of weather-space above 25°C mean temperature and above 75% mean humidity.
Impact was highest as humidity increased above 80% when mean temperature was 25–29°C.
396.Rationale: The passage focuses on temperature and humidity as the primary weather components affecting dengue transmission and presents multiple analyses linking temperature-humidity coupling to transmission potential and impact. Rainfall is not analyzed nor presented as a primary predictor in the text. Thus the specific claim that rainfall is a more significant predictor than temperature and humidity is not supported by the passage and cannot be derived from its findings.

396.Option: The largest dengue epidemics in Peru occurred in areas with the highest population density, indicating that human population size is the primary determinant of epidemic intensity regardless of weather conditions.
Evidence: High dengue impact was concentrated in a small area of weather-space above 25°C mean temperature and above 75% mean humidity.
Together these areas represent 11% of the Peruvian population and 70% of cases reported during 1994–2012.

The temperature interval in which most incidence occurred was narrow and strongly aligned across locations; 80% of cases were within: 25.5–29°C mean temperature, 21–23.5°C minimum temperature and >75% mean humidity.
We conclude that a coupling of temperature-humidity is an important and sensitive determinant of DENV transmission-potential regulating the location, timing and intensity of risk.
396.Rationale: Although the passage notes that the six elevated-risk areas account for a disproportionate share of cases relative to their population, the authors attribute epidemic intensity to specific temperature-humidity conditions concentrated in those areas. The passage emphasizes that most incidence falls within a narrow temperature-humidity range and explicitly concludes that temperature-humidity coupling regulates intensity. Therefore the assertion that population size is the primary determinant regardless of weather is not supported by the passage.

397.Option: The structural similarity of Ov-TSP-1 to Sm-TSP-2 from S. mansoni implies that Ov-tsp-1 is essential for tegument formation in both species due to conserved function.
Evidence: Sm-TSP-2 is a tetraspanin from the tegument of S. mansoni [15], [16] that is essential for proper tegument formation, and silencing of the Sm-tsp-2 gene proved lethal for schistosomula in vivo [17].

Ov-TSP-1 showed the conserved characteristics of the TSP family, notably the signature cysteine–cysteine-glycine (CCG) motif in the LEL, which is the location for the formation of three disulfide bonds within the LEL and influences interactions with other molecules (Figure 1).

Ov-TSP-1 shares 97% identity with a TSP of its close relative, Clonorchis sinensis (GAA49954.1) calculated from the alignment over 229 amino acid (amino acid position 19–247 of Ov-TSP-1 to 87–315 of C. sinensis), and 74% identity with Sm-TSP-1 of Schistosoma mansoni (XP_002580456.1) (Figure 1).

Suppression of Ov-tsp-1 in adult flukes impacts on proper tegument formation and results in increased vacuolation, implying that this protein is essential for fluke development and survival and is therefore worthy of consideration as a vaccine and/or drug target.

This RNAi procedure knocked down expression of Ov-tsp-1 by 67% (p = 0.06), 72% (p = 0.01) and 55% (p = 0.02) on days 6, 10 and 16 in vitro, respectively, compared to the negative control group that received luciferase dsRNA (Figure 6).

The tegument of Ov-tsp-1 dsRNA treated worms (Figure 7C, E and F) was more highly vacuolated than firefly luciferease dsRNA and non dsRNA controls (Figure 7B, A and D), with extensive and enlarged vacuoles throughout the surface layer.
397.Rationale: The passage provides direct experimental evidence that Sm-TSP-2 in S. mansoni is essential for tegument formation and that knockdown of Ov-tsp-1 in O. viverrini causes tegument malformation. It also documents sequence conservation and conserved TSP motifs in Ov-TSP-1. Combining the demonstrated essential role of a related tetraspanin in S. mansoni, the high sequence/conserved motif similarity, and the observed loss-of-structure phenotype after Ov-tsp-1 suppression, it is epidemiologically and biologically reasonable to infer that Ov-tsp-1 is essential for tegument formation and that this reflects a conserved function across these fluke species.

397.Option: Suppression of Ov-tsp-1 expression in adult O. viverrini results in tegument malformation, including increased vacuolation and a thinner tegument, indicating that Ov-TSP-1 is essential for proper tegument formation and structural integrity.
Evidence: Suppression of Ov-tsp-1 in adult flukes impacts on proper tegument formation and results in increased vacuolation, implying that this protein is essential for fluke development and survival and is therefore worthy of consideration as a vaccine and/or drug target.

This RNAi procedure knocked down expression of Ov-tsp-1 by 67% (p = 0.06), 72% (p = 0.01) and 55% (p = 0.02) on days 6, 10 and 16 in vitro, respectively, compared to the negative control group that received luciferase dsRNA (Figure 6).

The tegument of Ov-tsp-1 dsRNA treated worms (Figure 7C, E and F) was more highly vacuolated than firefly luciferease dsRNA and non dsRNA controls (Figure 7B, A and D), with extensive and enlarged vacuoles throughout the surface layer.
The tegument of knocked down tsp-1 worm is more highly vacuolated (indicated by arrows) and thinner compared with controls.
397.Rationale: The study directly demonstrates that RNAi-mediated suppression of Ov-tsp-1 reduces transcript levels and produces clear ultrastructural tegumental abnormalities (increased vacuolation and thinning) on TEM. The authors explicitly state this phenotype implies that Ov-TSP-1 is essential for fluke development and survival. Therefore the option is directly supported by the experimental outcomes described.

397.Option: The recognition of Ov-TSP-1 by sera from infected humans and hamsters indicates that the protein is a promising candidate for serodiagnosis of opisthorchiasis.
Evidence: Immunoblot analysis showed that sera from humans and hamsters infected with O. viverrini both reacted with rOv-TSP-1 (Figure 5).

Lanes 1, immunoblot of rOv-TSP-1 probed with serum of O. viverrini infected human; lane 2, immunoblot of rOv-TSP-1 probed with serum of O. viverrini infected hamster; lane 3, immunoblot of rOv-TSP-1 probed with normal serum of hamster and lane 4, immunoblot of rOv-TSP-1 probed with sera of mice immunized with rOv-TSP-1.
397.Rationale: While the passage shows that sera from infected humans and hamsters react with recombinant Ov-TSP-1 (demonstrating antigenicity), it provides no data on diagnostic performance (sensitivity, specificity, cross-reactivity, false positives/negatives) or comparisons with other antigens. Recognition by sera is necessary but not sufficient to conclude that a protein is a 'promising candidate for serodiagnosis.' Such a claim requires additional validation data that are not provided in the passage, so this inference cannot be derived solely from the presented evidence.

397.Option: The expression of Ov-tsp-1 in all developmental stages of O. viverrini suggests that it plays a role in the initial excystation of metacercariae from fish hosts.
Evidence: Ov-tsp-1 is expressed in all stages; expression was highest in metacercariae followed by two-week old juveniles, adults and eggs in descending order (Figure 3).
Metacercariae of O. viverrini were collected from infected fishes obtained from reservoirs in Khon Kaen province, Thailand.
397.Rationale: Although Ov-tsp-1 is expressed across developmental stages and particularly highly in metacercariae, the passage provides no experimental or observational data linking Ov-tsp-1 to excystation (the process by which metacercariae emerge from cysts). Expression alone does not establish functional involvement in a specific process such as excystation. Therefore this specific functional inference is not supported by the provided evidence.

397.Option: The high sequence identity between Ov-TSP-1 and C. sinensis TSP suggests that Ov-tsp-1 may function as a receptor for host bile components, facilitating parasite survival in the biliary tree.
Evidence: Ov-TSP-1 shares 97% identity with a TSP of its close relative, Clonorchis sinensis (GAA49954.1) calculated from the alignment over 229 amino acid (amino acid position 19–247 of Ov-TSP-1 to 87–315 of C. sinensis), and 74% identity with Sm-TSP-1 of Schistosoma mansoni (XP_002580456.1) (Figure 1).

Tetraspanins contain 4 transmembrane domains and are frequently expressed at the cell surface in association with each other and with other molecules, such as integrins, where they function to regulate cell adhesion, migration, proliferation, and differentiation [12], [13].
Tetraspanins have also been shown to act as receptors for viruses, most notably CD81 binding to hepatitis C [14].
the tegument of the adult fluke forms an intimate contact with the host biliary epithelium [20], resulting in chronic cell proliferation, immunopathology and ultimately tumorigenesis [21], [22].
397.Rationale: The passage documents very high sequence identity between Ov-TSP-1 and the C. sinensis tetraspanin and describes general tetraspanin roles and instances where tetraspanins serve as receptors for viruses. It also notes intimate contact of the fluke tegument with the biliary epithelium. However, there is no direct evidence that Ov-TSP-1 binds host bile components or functions specifically as a receptor for such molecules. The suggested receptor role for bile components is speculative and not supported by the experimental data presented, so this inference cannot be drawn from the passage.

398.Option: Since FZT infections involve multiple reservoir hosts including domestic animals and the transmission cycle includes environmental contamination, preventive chemotherapy targeting only humans may not be sufficient to reduce long-term transmission.
Evidence: FZT infects animals and humans after consumption of raw or insufficiently cooked freshwater fish, in the case of humans often as a traditional dish [2].

Studies from the same area have revealed high prevalence of different MIF-species, especially Haplorchis spp., in dogs, cats and pigs, suggesting that domestic animals could play an important role as reservoir hosts and in the transmission of FZT [9], [10].

Nissen et al. found that half of the North Vietnamese farm dogs that had been given a single dose of praziquantel were reinfected with FZT within three months [24]. Hence they could not recommend anthelmintic treatment as the only intervention to control FZT in dogs.
398.Rationale: The passage explicitly describes a multi-host transmission cycle: animals (dogs, cats, pigs) are infected and likely serve as reservoir hosts, and humans acquire infection via consumption of infected freshwater fish. The finding that treated dogs were rapidly reinfected and the authors' conclusion that anthelmintic treatment alone is not sufficient for dogs support the epidemiological inference that treating humans only is unlikely to sustainably interrupt transmission. These citations together justify concluding that human-targeted preventive chemotherapy alone will probably not reduce long-term transmission.

398.Option: The high reinfection rate observed within one year after preventive chemotherapy suggests that the current strategy of selective treatment based on self-reported raw fish consumption is ineffective and should be replaced with universal treatment in all endemic areas.
Evidence: Preventive chemotherapy is here given as selective chemotherapy to people confirming that they eat raw fish.

As seen in Figure 2, the prevalence of small trematode eggs in stool before preventive chemotherapy for the two sites combined were 40.2% (95% confidence interval (CI) of 35.3–45.0). The prevalence two, 16, 29 and 60 weeks after preventive chemotherapy were 2.3% (CI 0.8–3.7), 10.9% (CI 7.8–13.9), 18.2% (CI 14.4–22.0) and 29.8% (CI 25.3–34.3), respectively.

If the prevalence in a district is >20%, universal treatment of all individuals in the district is recommended once a year. If the prevalence is <20%, the recommendation is either a universal treatment every second year, or targeted treatment once a year of all those who report habitually eating raw fish [12].
398.Rationale: The passage documents a substantial rebound in prevalence within one year after selective (self-reported) treatment, which indicates limited sustainability of the observed strategy. However, the statement that selective treatment is 'ineffective' and that it 'should be replaced with universal treatment in all endemic areas' is not directly supported. The passage cites WHO guidance that recommends universal treatment when district prevalence exceeds 20% and alternative schedules when below 20%, implying that the choice between selective and universal strategies depends on local prevalence. The study does not provide direct comparative evidence that universal treatment in all endemic areas would be superior, so the stronger prescriptive claim cannot be derived solely from the passage.

398.Option: The fact that C. sinensis infections are less common in fish than in humans suggests that human-to-human transmission of this species is more likely than transmission via contaminated fish.
Evidence: FZT infects animals and humans after consumption of raw or insufficiently cooked freshwater fish, in the case of humans often as a traditional dish [2].

There is also variation in predominance of species within specific geographical areas [4], [5]. The morphological characteristics of eggs belonging to different species are difficult to differentiate by microscopy, and this may lead to unreliable data of their epidemiological distribution [6].
398.Rationale: The passage consistently describes transmission occurring via consumption of raw or insufficiently cooked freshwater fish and identifies animal reservoirs; it does not present any data indicating that C. sinensis is less common in fish than in humans, nor does it describe or provide evidence for human-to-human transmission. The statement therefore posits a transmission route (human-to-human) that is not supported by the passage, and it draws an inference from an unstated premise (that C. sinensis is less common in fish), so it cannot be derived from the provided text.

398.Option: Because MIF infections are less serious and not carcinogenic, preventive chemotherapy should be prioritized for liver fluke infections rather than for minute intestinal fluke infections.
Evidence: The MIF infections have been less studied, but the clinical manifestations may be less serious and MIF are not considered carcinogenic [5].
The most serious health hazard associated with liver fluke infection is the increased risk of cholangiocarcinoma.
The morbidity of the liver trematodes, especially the predisposition for cholangiocarcinoma, is the main reason for establishing control measures [11].
WHO recommends preventive chemotherapy, that is large-scale distribution of anthelminthic drugs to populations at risk, as the main intervention strategy against liver flukes [12], [13].
398.Rationale: The passage states that MIF infections are generally less severe and not associated with carcinogenesis, whereas liver flukes carry a serious risk of cholangiocarcinoma and are explicitly the focus of control measures. WHO's recommendation of preventive chemotherapy 'as the main intervention strategy against liver flukes' further supports prioritizing treatment for liver fluke infections. From these points, it is reasonable to infer that preventive chemotherapy efforts should be prioritized toward liver fluke infections rather than MIF, based on relative morbidity and existing recommendations.

398.Option: The use of praziquantel in dogs and cats has been shown to be highly effective in reducing FZT transmission, making animal treatment a viable alternative to human-targeted interventions.
Evidence: Studies from the same area have revealed high prevalence of different MIF-species, especially Haplorchis spp., in dogs, cats and pigs, suggesting that domestic animals could play an important role as reservoir hosts and in the transmission of FZT [9], [10].

Nissen et al. found that half of the North Vietnamese farm dogs that had been given a single dose of praziquantel were reinfected with FZT within three months [24]. Hence they could not recommend anthelmintic treatment as the only intervention to control FZT in dogs.
398.Rationale: The passage does not provide evidence that praziquantel use in dogs and cats is highly effective at reducing overall FZT transmission. On the contrary, it reports rapid reinfection of treated dogs (50% reinfected within three months) and the authors' conclusion that anthelmintic treatment alone could not be recommended to control FZT in dogs. Therefore, the claim that animal treatment alone is a viable alternative to human-targeted interventions is not supported by the study's findings.

399.Option: The presence of specific refractory genes in Ae. aegypti populations from northern Cameroon may explain the lower transmission rates observed in these populations, suggesting that genetic resistance could limit ZIKV spread in certain regions.
Evidence: It has been demonstrated that the level of vector competence varies according to mosquito populations and ZIKV strains [28–31].

TR varied significantly (Fisher’s exact test: P = 0.0014) according to the population with lowest TR reported in two populations from northern part of Cameroon, Bénoué (56%) and Maroua (43%) (Fig 3B).
399.Rationale: The passage documents that vector competence varies by mosquito population and specifically reports lower transmission rates in two northern Ae. aegypti populations (Bénoué and Maroua). However, it provides no data or evidence about genetic mechanisms (e.g., specific refractory genes) underlying those differences. Inferring the presence of specific genes and attributing causation to genetic resistance is speculative and not directly supported by the experimental data presented. Therefore this conclusion cannot be derived from the passage.

399.Option: The invasive species Ae. albopictus, which has replaced Ae. aegypti in many areas of Central Africa, is likely to be the primary driver of future ZIKV outbreaks due to its higher population density and broader geographic distribution.
Evidence: While Ae. albopictus originated from Asia, it was reported for the first time in Central Africa in early 2000 in Cameroon [21] and has progressively colonized almost all countries in the region where it tends to supplant the resident species Ae. aegypti [22–25].
When all populations of each species were analysed together, Ae. aegypti (56%) exhibited higher TEs than Ae. albopictus (42%) (Fisher’s exact test: P = 0.02).
Overall, ZIKV titres were significantly higher in Ae. aegypti compared to Ae. albopictus (Chi-squared test: χ2 = 6.4527, df = 1, P = 0.01).
399.Rationale: Although the passage states that Ae. albopictus is invasive and has tended to supplant Ae. aegypti in the region, the experimental findings show that Ae. aegypti has higher transmission efficiency and higher viral titres than Ae. albopictus. The option asserts that Ae. albopictus will be the primary driver of outbreaks because of higher density and distribution, but the passage provides no data on population densities or a direct link from distribution to outbreak driving; moreover, the vector competence data contradicts the claim that Ae. albopictus has greater transmission potential. Thus the conclusion is not supported by the passage.

399.Option: The higher transmission efficiency of Ae. aegypti in Central Africa is primarily due to its greater preference for human blood meals, which enhances its role in sustaining human-to-human transmission cycles.
Evidence: When all populations of each species were analysed together, Ae. aegypti (56%) exhibited higher TEs than Ae. albopictus (42%) (Fisher’s exact test: P = 0.02).
For each population, six batches of 60, 7–10 day-old females were exposed to an infectious blood meal containing 1.4 mL of washed rabbit erythrocytes and 700 μL of viral suspension.
399.Rationale: The passage demonstrates that Ae. aegypti had higher transmission efficiency than Ae. albopictus under experimental conditions. However, the study used rabbit erythrocytes for blood meals and does not present data on host-feeding preferences or the role of anthropophily in transmission. The causal claim that higher TE is primarily due to greater human blood preference is not supported by any data or analysis in the passage and therefore cannot be derived from it.

399.Option: Despite the invasive nature of Ae. albopictus and its dominance in sympatric areas, Ae. aegypti exhibits higher transmission efficiency and viral titres in saliva compared to Ae. albopictus, suggesting a greater potential for ZIKV transmission in Central Africa.
Evidence: While Ae. albopictus originated from Asia, it was reported for the first time in Central Africa in early 2000 in Cameroon [21] and has progressively colonized almost all countries in the region where it tends to supplant the resident species Ae. aegypti [22–25].
When all populations of each species were analysed together, Ae. aegypti (56%) exhibited higher TEs than Ae. albopictus (42%) (Fisher’s exact test: P = 0.02).
Overall, ZIKV titres were significantly higher in Ae. aegypti compared to Ae. albopictus (Chi-squared test: χ2 = 6.4527, df = 1, P = 0.01).
399.Rationale: The passage documents that Ae. albopictus is invasive and tends to supplant Ae. aegypti in Central Africa, but the experimental infection data show that Ae. aegypti had significantly higher transmission efficiency and higher viral titres than Ae. albopictus. From an epidemiological standpoint, higher TE and saliva viral loads indicate greater capacity to transmit ZIKV to humans, so it is reasonable to infer that Ae. aegypti has a greater potential to sustain ZIKV transmission in the region based on the provided data.

400.Option: The lack of further titer increase after a third vaccination in adult pigs suggests that mRNA vaccines are less effective than traditional vaccines in boosting long-term immunity.
Evidence: The third vaccination at day 49 did not further increase RABV-G specific neutralizing antibody levels.
These experiments, therefore, indicate comparable immunogenicity of the mRNA vaccine with licensed rabies vaccine and the general induction of specific immune response in mice.
400.Rationale: The passage documents that a third vaccination in adult pigs did not further increase neutralizing antibody levels, but it does not provide any direct comparison showing that mRNA vaccines are less effective than traditional vaccines at boosting long-term immunity. In fact, the passage explicitly states comparable immunogenicity of the mRNA vaccine with licensed rabies vaccines in mice. From the provided data one can observe a plateau after a third dose in pigs, but one cannot infer a general inferiority of mRNA vaccines versus traditional vaccines without direct comparative boosting data in the same species and schedule. Thus the stated conclusion is not supported by the passage.

400.Option: The fact that some mice with VN titers above 0.5 IU/ml still died after challenge suggests that the 0.5 IU/ml threshold is invalid for all forms of rabies infection, including natural exposure.
Evidence: After two vaccinations, all RABV-G immunized mice developed relevant virus neutralizing (VN) titers of ≥0.5 IU/ml (range: 5.9 to 70.2 IU/ml), considered as protective in humans, dogs and cats [34].
All RABV-G mRNA and HDC vaccinated mice were protected against lethal rabies virus challenge (Fig 3A).

In the CD4-depleted, mRNA immunized group VN titers were strongly reduced: 3 out of 8 mice showed VN titers below 0.5 IU/ml and the mean titer of the groups was about 3 IU/ml, i.e. more than 15-fold reduced compared to the CD4-intact, mRNA-vaccinated group.
400.Rationale: The passage does not present evidence that mice with VN titers above 0.5 IU/ml died after challenge. On the contrary, it reports that mice developing VN titers ≥0.5 IU/ml were considered to have protective titers and that all RABV-G mRNA and HDC vaccinated mice were protected in the intracerebral challenge. Where deaths occurred (e.g., in CD4-depleted groups), those mice had markedly reduced VN titers, with some below 0.5 IU/ml. Therefore, the passage does not support the claim that animals with titers above 0.5 IU/ml died, nor that the threshold is categorically invalid for all forms of rabies infection.

400.Option: The observed reduction in TNFα mRNA expression in vaccinated mice indicates that the mRNA vaccine prevents rabies virus replication by suppressing neuroinflammation rather than by direct antiviral mechanisms.
Evidence: In mRNA and Rabipur vaccinated mice TNFα mRNA transcript level was reduced compared to mock vaccinated control group at both analysis time points and different brain sections (Fig 4B).

In contrast, all but one of the analyzed brains of mice immunized with RABV-G mRNA or Rabipur were negative for N-protein mRNA at both time points, demonstrating a strong suppression of viral replication below the detection limit after vaccination (Fig 4A).
400.Rationale: The passage shows both reduced TNFα (a marker of neuroinflammation) and greatly reduced viral N-protein RNA in vaccinated animals. However, these are correlated observations and do not establish the causal mechanism by which vaccination prevents replication. One cannot deduce from these data that prevention of replication is achieved primarily by suppression of neuroinflammation rather than by induction of direct antiviral immune mechanisms (e.g., neutralizing antibodies and T cell responses). The authors also present evidence of neutralizing antibodies and T cell responses induced by vaccination, supporting direct antiviral immunity. Thus the specific mechanistic claim in the option is not supported by the passage.

400.Option: The mRNA vaccine's ability to induce strong CD4+ T cell responses suggests that cellular immunity is more critical than humoral immunity for protection against rabies virus in experimental models.
Evidence: antigen-specific CD4+ T cells were significantly elevated in mRNA immunized mice compared to Rabipur treated animals with a high proportion of IFN-γ/TNFα-double positive CD4+ T cells (mean of 0.48% for IFN-γ/TNFα expressing CD4+ T cells after RABV-G mRNA and 0.091% after Rabipur vaccination).
In the CD4-depleted, mRNA immunized group VN titers were strongly reduced: 3 out of 8 mice showed VN titers below 0.5 IU/ml and the mean titer of the groups was about 3 IU/ml...
In the CD4-depleted, mRNA vaccinated group, all mice had to be sacrificed within 11 days after infection with similar kinetics as in the negative control group...
400.Rationale: While the passage demonstrates that the mRNA vaccine induces strong CD4+ T cell responses and that CD4+ T cell depletion markedly reduces virus neutralizing (VN) antibody titers and abrogates protection, this evidence indicates that CD4+ T cells are important—in part because they are required to generate effective humoral (antibody) responses. It does not support the conclusion that cellular immunity is more critical than humoral immunity. In fact, loss of CD4+ cells led to loss of VN antibodies and loss of protection, pointing to an essential role of CD4 help for humoral immunity and protection. The data therefore support an integrated role of cellular and humoral responses rather than placing cellular immunity above humoral immunity as the primary protective mechanism.

400.Option: The titer of 0.5 IU/ml, while considered a correlate of protection for natural rabies infection, may not reliably predict protection against intracerebral challenge in experimental settings.
Evidence: After two vaccinations, all RABV-G immunized mice developed relevant virus neutralizing (VN) titers of ≥0.5 IU/ml (range: 5.9 to 70.2 IU/ml), considered as protective in humans, dogs and cats [34].
Intracerebral infection poses an extremely harsh challenge that is artificial, but accepted by the scientific community as a stringent experimental test.

In contrast, all but one of the analyzed brains of mice immunized with RABV-G mRNA or Rabipur were negative for N-protein mRNA at both time points, demonstrating a strong suppression of viral replication below the detection limit after vaccination (Fig 4A). The brain of one mRNA vaccinated mouse was positive for viral N-protein RNA at day 6 after infection (telencephalon: ct-value of 25.4) indicating a non-protective response to the mRNA vaccine in one case.
400.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that a VN titer of 0.5 IU/ml is considered a correlate of protection for natural infection in humans and animals. It also emphasizes that the intracerebral (i.c.) challenge used in these experiments is an "extremely harsh" and artificial test used as a stringent experimental model. The report that one vaccinated mouse showed viral N-protein RNA (a non-protective response) despite vaccination illustrates that even with vaccination/induced immune responses there can be failures in this stringent model. Taken together, these statements support the epidemiological inference that the 0.5 IU/ml threshold—established as a correlate for natural exposure—may not reliably predict protection in the artificial, highly stringent intracerebral challenge model. The option is appropriately cautious ('may not reliably predict') and is consistent with the data and caveats presented in the passage.

401.Option: The presence of N. mikurensis in bat-associated ticks that fed on domestic dogs, bats, and other wildlife suggests that these ticks may serve as bridge vectors in the transmission of this zoonotic pathogen between different host species.
Evidence: We detected Neoehrlichia mikurensis in both I. simplex (n = 1, EW16, Romania) associated with M. schreibersii and I. vespertilionis (n = 3, EW54, Romania; EW75, EW159, Hungary), previously fed on C. lupus familiaris, M. schreibersii, and M. myotis (Fig 5).

Although these data are limited, they suggest that bat-associated ticks may play a hitherto neglected link between bats and non-chiropteran hosts, implying their potential role in the zoonotic transmission of bat-derived vector-borne pathogens.
