The following variables L. infantum species, area (New World or Old World) where infection was acquired, mucocutaneous form, and frontline therapy with L-AmB were included in multivariable conditional logistic regression analysis.
In multivariable analysis, only infection with L. infantum was independently associated with complete healing after L-AmB therapy (OR 5.8 IC 95% [1.03–32]).
429.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that no other clinical or biological factors were associated with outcome, and although mucocutaneous form was evaluated in multivariable analysis, it was not independently associated with healing. Thus there is no evidence in the study supporting higher cure rates for mucocutaneous versus localized cutaneous disease or that greater disease severity correlates with better response.

429.Option: The use of liposomal amphotericin B as front-line therapy was independently associated with complete healing after treatment, indicating that early administration improves outcomes.
Evidence: Two third of patients (30/43; 70%) received L-AmB as front-line therapy while the others had received prior treatment for leishmaniasis with pentavalent antimony (n = 6), pentamidine (n = 4), oral azoles (n = 3) or local treatment (n = 3).

The following variables L. infantum species, area (New World or Old World) where infection was acquired, mucocutaneous form, and frontline therapy with L-AmB were included in multivariable conditional logistic regression analysis.
In multivariable analysis, only infection with L. infantum was independently associated with complete healing after L-AmB therapy (OR 5.8 IC 95% [1.03–32]).
429.Rationale: Although frontline use of L-AmB was considered and many patients received it as first-line therapy, the multivariable analysis demonstrated that frontline therapy was not independently associated with complete healing; only infection with L. infantum was. Therefore the claim that early (frontline) administration independently improves outcomes is not supported by the study data.

430.Option: The third intervention—annual mass vaccination of replacements for a decade followed by a decade of a test and slaughter strategy—is the most cost-effective approach for controlling brucellosis in India.
Evidence: For cattle, the NPV was found to be US $ 4·16 billion (95% CI: US $ 3·16; 5·39 billion) for the scenario 1, US $ 8·31 (6·40; 9·87) billion for the scenario 2 and, US $ 4·26 (3·26; 5·61) for the third scenario (Table 2).
The BCR for the first, second and the third intervention for cattle were 7·98 (6·29; 10·09), 10·62 (8·33; 12·5) and, 3·16 (2·66; 3·83), respectively.

The results indicate that first 20 years of the programme will be cost-effective for all scenarios with the second intervention (vaccination of replacements with mass vaccination at the beginning of the program) being a significantly more cost-effective choice.
430.Rationale: The passage reports that the third scenario (vaccination then test-and-slaughter) has a lower NPV (US $4.26 billion) and a substantially lower benefit-cost ratio (BCR = 3.16) compared with the second scenario (NPV US $8.31 billion; BCR = 10.62). The authors explicitly state the second intervention is the significantly more cost-effective choice. Therefore the claim that the third intervention is the most cost-effective cannot be derived from the passage.

430.Option: The second intervention—vaccinating both the adult and young populations at the beginning of the program followed by annual vaccination of replacement calves—is the most cost-effective approach for controlling brucellosis in India.
Evidence: For cattle, the NPV was found to be US $ 4·16 billion (95% CI: US $ 3·16; 5·39 billion) for the scenario 1, US $ 8·31 (6·40; 9·87) billion for the scenario 2 and, US $ 4·26 (3·26; 5·61) for the third scenario (Table 2).

The results indicate that first 20 years of the programme will be cost-effective for all scenarios with the second intervention (vaccination of replacements with mass vaccination at the beginning of the program) being a significantly more cost-effective choice.
The BCR for the first, second and the third intervention for cattle were 7·98 (6·29; 10·09), 10·62 (8·33; 12·5) and, 3·16 (2·66; 3·83), respectively.
Similar results were obtained for buffaloes (Table 3).
430.Rationale: The passage provides direct economic comparisons showing the second intervention has the highest net present value (US $8.31 billion for cattle) and the highest benefit-cost ratio (BCR = 10.62), and explicitly states the second intervention is "a significantly more cost-effective choice." The same pattern is noted for buffaloes. Thus the inference that the second intervention demonstrates the strongest overall efficiency relative to implementation costs is directly supported by the reported results.

430.Option: The first intervention—annual mass vaccination of replacement calves only—is the most cost-effective approach for controlling brucellosis in India.
Evidence: For cattle, the NPV was found to be US $ 4·16 billion (95% CI: US $ 3·16; 5·39 billion) for the scenario 1, US $ 8·31 (6·40; 9·87) billion for the scenario 2 and, US $ 4·26 (3·26; 5·61) for the third scenario (Table 2).
The BCR for the first, second and the third intervention for cattle were 7·98 (6·29; 10·09), 10·62 (8·33; 12·5) and, 3·16 (2·66; 3·83), respectively.

The results indicate that first 20 years of the programme will be cost-effective for all scenarios with the second intervention (vaccination of replacements with mass vaccination at the beginning of the program) being a significantly more cost-effective choice.
430.Rationale: Although the first intervention yields positive net benefits (NPV US $4.16 billion) and a favorable BCR (7.98), the passage shows these metrics are lower than those for the second intervention (NPV US $8.31 billion; BCR 10.62). The authors explicitly identify the second intervention as significantly more cost-effective. Therefore the claim that the first intervention is the most cost-effective is not supported by the provided results.

431.Option: The lethality rate in Rio Grande do Sul has remained stable over time, indicating consistent healthcare access and treatment quality across the state.
Evidence: From 2007 to 2019, 6,162 cases of human leptospirosis and 321 deaths due to the disease were confirmed in Rio Grande do Sul. The average incidence rate was 4 cases per 100,000 inhabitants, and the lethality rate was 5%.
These indicators were annually mapped in all municipalities in Rio Grande do Sul to confirm the existence of clusters and thus analyze the disease risk in different regions of the state.
431.Rationale: The passage provides an overall (average) lethality rate for 2007–2019 (5%) but does not present temporal trends of lethality or analyses showing stability over time. The quoted text shows lethality was calculated as an indicator, but there is no evidence in the passage that the lethality rate remained stable year-to-year or that consistent healthcare access/treatment quality across the state is the reason. Therefore the claim of stability and its attribution to healthcare consistency cannot be derived from the Passage Body.

431.Option: The seasonal pattern of leptospirosis incidence from January to April is likely due to increased outdoor activity and higher human exposure to contaminated environments during the warmer months.
Evidence: time series analysis revealed that the disease was seasonal in the months of January, February, March, and April (p<0.05) (Fig 4B).
Rio Grande do Sul experiences hotter months from November to April and colder months from May to October [19,34].

Leptospirosis is caused by Leptospira interrogans, a bacterial species that survives in varied environments for prolonged periods and affects humans and wild, domestic, and synanthropic animals, which then become carriers of this species and contribute to its spread in nature [7].
431.Rationale: The passage documents seasonality (Jan–Apr) and that these months fall within the hotter season, and it describes environmental survival and animal reservoirs. However, it does not provide direct evidence linking the observed seasonality to increased outdoor activity or higher human exposure during warmer months. The proposed causal explanation (increased outdoor activity/higher exposure) is plausible but is not stated or demonstrated in the Passage Body, so it cannot be inferred solely from the provided text.

431.Option: The SARIMA model predicted a decrease in leptospirosis incidence in Rio Grande do Sul in 2020, despite the observed upward trend in the preceding years, suggesting that predictive models can provide valuable insights for public health planning even when short-term trends deviate from long-term patterns.
Evidence: By using GAM, a leptospirosis nonlinear regression model was developed for the cases over the years (Fig 4A). An increase in the disease occurrence risk was observed in Rio Grande do Sul from 2010, with a peak in 2011, followed by a decrease in risk until 2012. Between 2013 and 2019, the disease risk annually increased, with new peaks in the years 2014 and particularly 2019.
predictive analytical methods provide the scope to predict the future burden of the disease and to early predict an epidemic [16,17].
Among the leptospirosis incidence time series models, SARIMA (1,2,1) (1,0,1) was the best model.
The model considered the 12-month seasonal pattern and predicted a decrease in incidence in 2020 (Fig 5).

Theil-U statistic value was 0.683, and the MAPE value was 50.0%, thus, indicating acceptable forecast errors; this suggested that the proposed model was adequate to predict future leptospirosis incidence.
431.Rationale: The Passage Body explicitly reports an upward trend in incidence through 2019 and states that the SARIMA model predicted a decrease in 2020. It also discusses the purpose and utility of predictive analytical methods and provides model performance metrics indicating acceptable forecasting. Therefore, the inference that the SARIMA model predicted a 2020 decrease despite recent increases, and that predictive models can inform public health planning even when short-term predictions differ from longer-term trends, is supported by the Passage Body.

431.Option: The high incidence of leptospirosis in the Metropolitan of Porto Alegre and Centro Oriental Rio Grandense mesoregions reflects the effectiveness of the current surveillance system in identifying and reporting cases in high-risk areas.
Evidence: In several regions of the state, high leptospirosis incidence rates were observed, particularly in Metropolitana de Porto Alegre, Centro Oriental Rio Grandense, and in recent years, in the Noroeste Rio-Grandense mesoregion.

Fig 3 shows annual maps of the areas with the highest concentration of high leptospirosis incidence, represented by density levels according to shade. The hotspots, i.e., central points represented in red, demonstrate a high concentration of high leptospirosis incidence rates.... The mapping shows hotspots in the Porto Alegre Metropolitan and Centro Oriental Rio Grandense mesoregions in all years. Thus, possibly in these places, there is a high risk of leptospirosis transmission.

220 municipalities did not confirm any case in the entire study period, while deaths from leptospirosis were recorded in 59 municipalities, with the highest number of deaths recorded in the capital, Porto Alegre (60 deaths, lethality rate: 8.9%).
431.Rationale: While the Passage Body documents consistently higher incidence and persistent hotspots in the specified mesoregions, it does not evaluate or provide evidence about surveillance system performance or its effectiveness in identifying/reporting cases. The presence of hotspots could reflect true higher transmission, differences in exposure, or differences in reporting; the Passage does not attribute the high incidence to surveillance effectiveness. Therefore the claim that high incidence reflects surveillance effectiveness is not derivable from the provided text.

431.Option: The SARIMA model's prediction of a decrease in incidence in 2020 is likely due to the implementation of new public health interventions in 2019, which were not accounted for in the model.
Evidence: The model considered the 12-month seasonal pattern and predicted a decrease in incidence in 2020 (Fig 5).

Several random factors, such as climate and health policy changes, also need to be considered while evaluating leptospirosis incidence through a monthly series. These factors are known as white noise, as they are random elements that occur due to probability and are not related to cyclical variations or trends.
431.Rationale: The Passage Body indicates that the SARIMA model predicted a decrease and notes that random factors including health policy changes can affect incidence, but it does not report any specific new public health interventions implemented in 2019 or attribute the predicted decrease to such interventions. The suggestion that the model's predicted decrease is likely due to unaccounted-for 2019 interventions is speculative and not supported by the Passage Body. Thus this causal assertion cannot be derived from the provided information.

432.Option: The similar mortality rates between IRIS and non-IRIS groups indicate that TSM-associated IRIS does not significantly affect patient outcomes or healthcare burden.
Evidence: Compared with non-IRIS group, IRIS group had a longer hospital stay (41.0 days vs. 33.0 days, P = 0.001).
In IRIS group, 12 patients (50.0%) experienced readmission events, compared with 41 patients (20.5%) in non-IRIS group, indicating a significantly higher readmission rate in IRIS group (P = 0.001).
No difference in mortality rates between two groups (0 vs. 6.0%, P > 0.05) (Table 4).
432.Rationale: The option focuses on mortality alone to conclude no significant effect on patient outcomes or healthcare burden. While the passage reports no statistical difference in mortality, it also reports significantly longer hospitalization and higher readmission rates in the IRIS group. Those latter findings indicate increased healthcare resource use and patient burden despite similar mortality. Therefore, concluding no significant effect on outcomes or healthcare burden based solely on mortality is not supported by the passage.

432.Option: The lack of significant differences in baseline CD4+ counts between IRIS and non-IRIS groups suggests that immunosuppression severity is not a risk factor for TSM-associated IRIS.
Evidence: No significant differences were observed between two groups regarding demographic characteristics, co-infection status, the interval between antifungal and ART initiation, CD4+ counts, and blood test results at baseline (Tables 2 and S2).
432.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that baseline CD4+ counts did not differ significantly between the IRIS and non-IRIS groups. From an epidemiological perspective, absence of a baseline difference in this cohort suggests that baseline immunosuppression severity (as measured by CD4+ count) was not associated with subsequent development of TSM-associated IRIS in this study population. Thus the inference that immunosuppression severity is not a risk factor is supported by the presented data (within the limits of this study).

432.Option: The earlier onset of TSM-associated IRIS within 2 weeks of ART initiation suggests that immune recovery occurs more rapidly in patients with TSM compared to those with other fungal infections.
Evidence: In 24 IRIS patients, the median occurrence time was 9.0 days (IQR, 5.0–16.8 days) after ART initiation. Among them, 70.9% (21/24) occurred within 2 weeks of ART initiation (Fig 2).
432.Rationale: The passage documents early onset of TSM-associated IRIS (median 9 days; ~71% within 2 weeks). However, it provides no comparative timing data on IRIS onset for other fungal infections. Therefore, claiming that immune recovery occurs more rapidly in patients with TSM than in patients with other fungal infections is not supported by the passage and requires external comparative data.

432.Option: The occurrence of TSM-associated IRIS prolonged hospitalization, increased readmission rates, and exacerbated patients’ economic and disease burdens.
Evidence: Compared with non-IRIS group, IRIS group had a longer hospital stay (41.0 days vs. 33.0 days, P = 0.001).
In IRIS group, 12 patients (50.0%) experienced readmission events, compared with 41 patients (20.5%) in non-IRIS group, indicating a significantly higher readmission rate in IRIS group (P = 0.001).
432.Rationale: The passage demonstrates that TSM-associated IRIS is associated with significantly longer hospital stays and higher readmission rates—direct indicators of greater healthcare resource utilization. From an epidemiological and health-services perspective, prolonged hospitalization and increased readmissions reasonably imply greater economic costs and increased disease burden for patients and the healthcare system. Thus the combined assertion about prolonged hospitalization, increased readmissions, and exacerbated burdens is supported by the data presented.

433.Option: The migration route of AC in nonpermissive host mice is identical to that in permissive host rats, with the same timing of organ arrival and consistent pathological outcomes across both host types.
Evidence: In permissive host rats, AC migrates from the intestinal wall to the liver within 2 hours post-infection (hpi), to the lung at 2 hpi and the brain at 8 hpi [6] and finally returns from the brain to the lung at 28 days post-infection (dpi) [7] where adult worms lay eggs [8].

we used live imaging technology to explore the migration route of AC in nonpermissive host mice, revealing that after infection, AC L3 migrated from the intestinal wall to the liver at 2 hpi, then to the lung at 4 hpi and the brain at 8 hpi.
we observed fatal pneumonia in the mouse lung during the acute and early phases of AC infection.
it is well documented that AC causes optic neuritis [9], eosinophilic meningitis [10] at 21 dpi and calcified pulmonary nodules during the late phase of infection [11].

Based on laboratory observations, nonpermissive host mice infected with 30 AC L3 exhibited severe eosinophilic meningitis at 21 dpi and died before 28 dpi. In contrast, permissive host rats infected with 100 AC L3 survived more than 42 days.
433.Rationale: The passage explicitly describes different timing of lung arrival in rats (lung at 2 hpi) versus mice (lung at 4 hpi) and documents distinct pathological outcomes between host types (e.g., fatal early pneumonia in mice, calcified pulmonary nodules and longer survival in rats). Therefore the claim of identical migration timing and consistent pathological outcomes across both host types is not supported by the text.

433.Option: The primary cause of death in nonpermissive host mice infected with high-dose AC is eosinophilic meningitis, which develops during the late infection phase and leads to irreversible neurological damage.
Evidence: The results showed that no mouse died after infection with 30, 60 and 120 AC L3, while 10% of mice infected with 300 AC L3 died at 1 dpi, 70% died at 2 dpi and all died at 3 dpi (Fig 4A).
As presented, no significant bleeding or tissue injury occurred in the mouse brain (Fig 4B left), while in the mouse lung, we observed areas of severe bleeding (Fig 4B right).
H&E staining of the mouse brain showed a normal tissue structure (Fig 4C left), but that of the mouse lung revealed very severe alveolar congestion and inflammatory cell infiltration (Fig 4C right).

Altogether, the results suggested that infection with a high dose of AC L3 could lead to fatal pneumonia in a nonpermissive host during the early infection phase before the manifestation of eosinophilic meningitis.
433.Rationale: The passage shows that deaths after high-dose infection occurred very early (1–3 dpi) and were associated with severe lung pathology while the brain showed no significant injury at the time of death. The authors explicitly state high-dose infection leads to fatal pneumonia during the early phase before eosinophilic meningitis appears. Thus the claim that eosinophilic meningitis (a late-phase phenomenon) is the primary cause of early deaths is contradicted by the reported findings.

433.Option: Pulmonary fibrosis is a common and severe outcome of AC infection in nonpermissive host mice during the late infection phase, resulting from sustained inflammation and collagen deposition.
Evidence: we performed Masson staining, RT–qPCR and immunoblot analysis and found no obvious fibrosis in the mouse lung (S9A and S9B Fig), which was further confirmed by the lack of elevated transcript and protein levels of Col1a1, Col3a1, α-SMA and TGF-β (S9C–S9E Fig).
In contrast, we observed prominently enhanced fibrosis (approximately 3-fold) in rat lungs at 35 and 42 dpi, as revealed by Masson staining (Fig 9A and 9B).
Overall, AC infection-induced sustained lung inflammation and could lead to fibrosis in rat but not mouse lungs during the late infection phase.
433.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports no obvious fibrosis in mouse lungs during the late phase and contrasts this with clear fibrosis observed in rat lungs. Therefore the assertion that pulmonary fibrosis is a common/severe late outcome in nonpermissive mice is not supported by the study's findings.

433.Option: The immune response in nonpermissive host mice during the early infection phase is dominated by a Th1-type response, which is essential for controlling AC infection and preventing fatal pneumonia.
Evidence: Overall, during the early infection phase, AC migration led to persistent pneumonia characterized by a Th2 bias in mice (mainly IL-13 and IL-6) and rats (mainly IL-6).

the levels of Th1 cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β and INOS) were significantly elevated by approximately 3-fold at 1 dpi compared with 0 dpi but gradually decreased to normal at 2–4 dpi and slightly increased again from 5–7 dpi.

For Th2 cytokines, we observed that levels of IL-6 strikingly increased by 7-fold and the levels of IL-13 increased by 20-fold at 1 dpi compared to 0 dpi and dropped to normal at 4 dpi but rose again to high levels from 5–7 dpi.
433.Rationale: The passage indicates that although some Th1 cytokines transiently increased, the early infection phase is characterized overall by a Th2 bias (not Th1 dominance), with much larger increases in IL-6 and IL-13. The passage provides no evidence that a Th1 response is dominant or that it is the essential mechanism preventing fatal pneumonia; in fact, fatal pneumonia occurred in some mice despite cytokine changes. Therefore this option is not supported by the presented data.

433.Option: AC larvae can induce fatal pneumonia in nonpermissive host mice during the acute and early infection phases, which is characterized by activation of Stat3/IL-6 signaling and enrichment of innate immune myeloid cells.
Evidence: we observed fatal pneumonia in the mouse lung during the acute and early phases of AC infection.
The results showed that no mouse died after infection with 30, 60 and 120 AC L3, while 10% of mice infected with 300 AC L3 died at 1 dpi, 70% died at 2 dpi and all died at 3 dpi (Fig 4A).
the protein level of IL-6 was significantly elevated but that of IL-13 showed only a slight increase (Fig 4D).
activated Stat3 and p65 were significantly upregulated by 4- and 2-fold, respectively (Fig 4D).
the immune response of the host to AC exhibited a Th2 bias characterized by activation of Stat3/IL-6 signaling and enrichment of innate immune myeloid cells.
the enrichment of innate immune myeloid cells caused by AC was notably counteracted (Fig 10F and 10G) by LMT-28 and C188-9.
Blockade of Stat3/IL-6 signaling attenuates AC infection-induced pulmonary inflammation
433.Rationale: The passage directly reports that high-dose AC infection causes early fatal pneumonia in mice, documents marked IL-6 upregulation and Stat3 activation in affected lungs, and links the host response to a Th2/Stat3/IL-6 program with enrichment of innate myeloid cells. Intervention targeting Stat3 or IL-6 reduced lung inflammation and the myeloid cell enrichment, supporting the characterization that fatal early pneumonia is associated with Stat3/IL-6 activation and innate myeloid cell involvement. Thus this inference is supported by the presented experimental findings.

434.Option: The persistence of lymphohistiocytic infiltrates in target organs after the peak of bacterial burden suggests a robust host immune response that contributes to infection control in sublethal scrub typhus.
Evidence: Initially, the magnitude of lymphohistiocytic cellular infiltration coincided with increased bacterial loads; however, it continued to intensify as the bacterial loads were controlled.
Intravenous inoculation resulted in dose-dependent, self-limited systemic infection.

The lobular lesions were more numerous and encompassed greater tissue area by 6 dpi, as indicated by the increased liver inflammatory index, and were unresolved at the experimental endpoint of 15 dpi (Fig 5A).
434.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that lymphohistiocytic infiltration initially coincided with increased bacterial loads but then continued to intensify as bacterial loads were controlled. This temporal pattern (inflammation persisting or increasing while bacterial burden declines) is consistent with an active host immune response that contributes to controlling infection in this sublethal model. The description that intravenous inoculation produced a dose-dependent, self-limited systemic infection supports that the host response is effective in controlling the pathogen in these non-lethal cases.

434.Option: The lack of eschar formation in the i.d. inoculation model suggests that the immune response in this route is delayed or ineffective in containing the initial infection at the inoculation site.
Evidence: Scrub typhus presents one to two weeks after exposure with a not-always-observed bite-site eschar and regional lymphadenopathy, followed by fever and rash accompanied by non-specific flu-like symptoms, requiring empirical treatment based on presumptive etiology.

After intradermal inoculation, mice were monitored through 30 dpi, during which mean percent body weight did not deviate below uninfected controls. However, decreased activity was observed from 12–16 dpi, ruffled fur during 13–18 dpi, and conjunctivitis on 13 dpi.
434.Rationale: The passage mentions that in human scrub typhus a bite-site eschar is 'not-always-observed,' but it does not report whether eschars formed or did not form in the intradermally inoculated mice. There is no direct statement in the passage that eschars were absent in the i.d. mouse model, nor any data linking eschar presence/absence to the effectiveness or timing of the immune response at the inoculation site. Therefore the specific inference that lack of eschar in the i.d. model indicates a delayed or ineffective immune response is not supported by the passage.

434.Option: The antibody response in mice infected with O. tsutsugamushi Gilliam strain is dominated by IgG2c, suggesting a Th1-driven immune response that may be insufficient to prevent persistent inflammation in target organs.
Evidence: At the final experimental time point, the reciprocal endpoint titer of IgG2c antibodies was higher than IgG1 for high dose i.v. (medians 2048 vs. 512), mid-dose i.v. (medians 4096 vs. 1024), low dose i.v. (medians 1024 vs. 0), and i.d. route (medians 16384 vs. 512) (Fig 3E and 3F).
Initially, the magnitude of lymphohistiocytic cellular infiltration coincided with increased bacterial loads; however, it continued to intensify as the bacterial loads were controlled.
434.Rationale: The passage provides clear evidence that IgG2c titers exceeded IgG1 titers at the final time points, supporting the statement that the antibody response is dominated by IgG2c. The passage also documents persistent/intensifying tissue inflammation as bacterial loads were controlled. However, the claim that IgG2c dominance 'suggests a Th1-driven immune response' is an immunologic interpretation not stated in the passage (the text does not link IgG2c to a Th1 profile). Furthermore, asserting that this Th1-dominated response 'may be insufficient to prevent persistent inflammation' requires causally linking the isotype profile to failure to resolve inflammation, which the passage does not explicitly establish. Thus, while parts of the option are supported (IgG2c > IgG1; persistent inflammation), the additional immunological interpretation and causal claim are not directly derivable from the passage alone.

434.Option: The delayed onset of peak inflammation in the liver and kidney after i.d. inoculation compared to i.v. inoculation suggests that the intradermal route leads to a less effective immune response and greater tissue damage.
Evidence: Following i.d. inoculation, the peak liver inflammatory index occurred at 12–18 dpi, and although the lesions decreased in diameter and quantity, they were still present at the experimental endpoint of 30 dpi (Fig 5D).

The kidney inflammatory ratio continued to intensify through the experimental endpoint, 15 dpi after i.v. high and mid-dose inoculation (Fig 6B). After i.v. low dose inoculation, the kidney inflammatory ratio plateaued at 6 dpi and remained elevated through 15 dpi. The peak of the kidney inflammatory ratio after i.d. inoculation occurred at 18 dpi and was unresolved at day 30 pi.
434.Rationale: The passage documents that peak inflammation in liver and kidney occurs later after intradermal inoculation than after some intravenous inoculations. However, concluding that this delay indicates a 'less effective immune response' and results in 'greater tissue damage' is not supported. The passage does not provide measures that equate delayed peak timing with reduced effectiveness of immunity, nor does it demonstrate that intradermal inoculation causes greater tissue damage than intravenous routes (it reports lesions that persisted but also notes decreases in lesion size/quantity for some endpoints). Therefore the causal claim about less effective immunity and greater damage is not derivable from the provided data.

434.Option: The sustained lung inflammation observed after the peak of bacterial load is likely due to ongoing bacterial replication in the pulmonary tissue, indicating incomplete pathogen clearance.
Evidence: The lungs had the highest bacterial load starting at 3 dpi and reached a peak at 9 dpi for the high dose i.v. group (Fig 4B), which was the day of onset of weight loss (Fig 1A).

Orientia identified by IHC on 9 dpi, the peak of bacterial burden after high dose i.v. inoculation, was located in endothelial cells of the alveolar capillaries (Fig 7A), and in splenic and hepatic mononuclear phagocytes (Fig 7B and 7C).
Initially, the magnitude of lymphohistiocytic cellular infiltration coincided with increased bacterial loads; however, it continued to intensify as the bacterial loads were controlled.
434.Rationale: The passage describes that lung bacterial loads peak (e.g., at 9 dpi for high dose i.v.) and that Orientia antigen is identified in pulmonary endothelial cells at that time. Crucially, the authors state that lymphohistiocytic infiltration 'continued to intensify as the bacterial loads were controlled,' indicating that persistent inflammation occurs despite declining bacterial burden. There is no direct evidence in the passage that sustained lung inflammation is driven by ongoing bacterial replication or incomplete clearance; in fact, the described temporal relationship argues against that simple explanation. Therefore the option's inference that sustained inflammation is 'likely due to ongoing bacterial replication' is not supported by the passage.

435.Option: The significantly lower prevalence of IPIs in Sungai Layau village is due to the absence of domestic animals and the exclusive use of treated water, which together eliminate the primary transmission routes for intestinal parasites.
Evidence: Infections were very low in Sungai Layau village (4.5%) (Table 1).
Rearing of animals such as pigs, chickens, ducks, dogs, cats and monkeys are common practices.

these facilities are either not fully utilized or evenly distributed. Even if these provisions were given, most of them could not afford to pay their monthly utility bills due to extreme poverty leading to the termination of water and electricity supplies. Therefore, rivers located adjacent to the village remained their main source of water for domestic needs (i.e., drinking, cooking, bathing and washing clothes).

Although being a female (73.3%; p = 0.935), jobless (74.1%; p = 0.088), have had no close contact with animals (80%; p = 0.193) and not taking any iron supplement (73.5%; p = 0.801) were factors which had higher infection rates, nonetheless these variables were not statistically significant (Table 4).
435.Rationale: The passage does report a very low prevalence in Sungai Layau (4.5%), but it does not state that Sungai Layau lacks domestic animals or exclusively uses treated water. On the contrary, the text describes rearing of animals as a common practice across the communities and states that rivers often remain the main water source because treated utilities are inconsistently used or unaffordable. Moreover, contact with animals was not a statistically significant risk factor. Therefore, the specific causal attribution in this option (absence of animals + exclusive treated water) is not supported by the passage.

435.Option: Restoring mass deworming programs alone is sufficient to control intestinal parasitic infections in rural West Malaysia, given the high re-infection rates observed after treatment.
Evidence: not taking any anthelminthic drugs in the last 12 months (OR = 1.29; 95% CI = 1.01–1.65; p = 0.038).

These infections are ubiquitous with high prevalence among the poor and socioeconomically deprived communities where overcrowding, poor environmental sanitation, low level of education and lack of access to safe water are prevalent [4], trapping them in a perennial cycle of poverty and destitution [5].
IPIs which are closely associated with environmental and personal hygiene practices are still causing major health problems among the poor in rural and remote communities in Malaysia [12].
The establishment of such data will be beneficial for the public health service to justify and facilitate the reassessment of control strategies and policies.
435.Rationale: Although the passage notes an association between not taking anthelminthic drugs and infection, it also clearly identifies socioeconomic factors, poor sanitation, untreated water use and indiscriminate defecation as strong predictors (some with much higher odds). The study emphasizes environmental and hygiene drivers and the need to reassess control strategies and policies. The passage provides no data on reinfection rates post-treatment, nor evidence that mass deworming alone would be sufficient; instead the evidence supports multi-faceted interventions. Therefore the claim that restoring mass deworming alone is sufficient is not supported by the passage.

435.Option: The most effective way to reduce IPIs in rural West Malaysia is to focus on treating protozoan infections with targeted antiparasitic drugs, given their high prevalence and association with severe diarrhea in children.
Evidence: The overall prevalence of IPIs among 716 participants was 73.2% with STH infections (73.2%; 524) being significantly more common compared to protozoa infections (21.4%; 153) (p<0.001).

With regards to the protozoa infections, the overall prevalence was 21.4%. The highest prevalence rate was due to G. duodenalis (10.4%), followed by E. histolytica/dispar (10.2%) and Cryptosporidium sp. (2.1%).

Cryptosporidium sp. has also emerged as an important cause of diarrhoeal illnesses worldwide particularly in young children and immunocompromised patients with a prevalence of 4% in developed countries and three to four times more frequent in developing countries [11].
435.Rationale: The data show that soil-transmitted helminths (STH) are far more prevalent (73.2%) than protozoa (21.4%) in these communities, with T. trichiura notably predominant. While Cryptosporidium is mentioned as an important cause of diarrhoeal illness globally, the study's local prevalence data do not support prioritizing protozoan treatment alone as the most effective overall strategy. The passage therefore does not justify focusing exclusively on protozoan-targeted therapy as the primary control measure.

435.Option: The high prevalence of T. trichiura infections in rural West Malaysia is primarily due to the widespread use of ineffective anthelminthic drugs and the development of drug resistance in the parasite population.
Evidence: The overall prevalence of STH infections was 73.2% with T. trichiura (66.8%) being the most predominant, followed by A. lumbricoides (38.5%) while only 12.8% had hookworm infections.
not taking any anthelminthic drugs in the last 12 months (OR = 1.29; 95% CI = 1.01–1.65; p = 0.038).
435.Rationale: While the passage documents a very high prevalence of T. trichiura, it does not provide any information on the efficacy of anthelminthic drugs in these communities or on the presence of drug resistance. The only relevant data on treatment indicate an association between not taking anthelminthic drugs in the last 12 months and infection, which does not support the claim that widespread drug ineffectiveness or resistance is the primary cause. Because the passage contains no evidence about drug resistance or drug efficacy, this causal claim cannot be derived from the study data.

435.Option: Improvement of socioeconomic status, sanitation, health education to promote awareness about health and hygiene together with periodic mass deworming are better strategies to control these infections.
Evidence: These infections are ubiquitous with high prevalence among the poor and socioeconomically deprived communities where overcrowding, poor environmental sanitation, low level of education and lack of access to safe water are prevalent [4], trapping them in a perennial cycle of poverty and destitution [5].
IPIs which are closely associated with environmental and personal hygiene practices are still causing major health problems among the poor in rural and remote communities in Malaysia [12].
not taking any anthelminthic drugs in the last 12 months (OR = 1.29; 95% CI = 1.01–1.65; p = 0.038).

participant less than 12 years old were 2.2 times (95% CI = 1.45–3.45; p = 0.001), low household income had 4.9 times (95% CI = 3.15–7.73; p = 0.001), using untreated water supply had 2.1 times (95% CI = 1.36–3.21; p = 0.001) and indiscriminate defecation were 5 times (95% CI = 3.30–7.62; p = 0.001) more likely to suffer from an IPIs, respectively.
The establishment of such data will be beneficial for the public health service to justify and facilitate the reassessment of control strategies and policies.
435.Rationale: The multivariate analysis identifies low household income, use of untreated water and indiscriminate defecation as strong independent predictors of IPIs, and the passage emphasizes the association of IPIs with poverty, poor sanitation and hygiene. There is also evidence that not taking anthelminthic drugs is associated with infection. Together these findings logically support a comprehensive strategy that combines socioeconomic improvement, sanitation and hygiene interventions, health education, and periodic deworming rather than relying on any single measure. The passage explicitly frames the data as useful for reassessing control strategies, which aligns with the multi-component approach proposed in this option.

436.Option: The improvement in passive surveillance in 2015 likely led to a higher number of detected cases, which may have masked the true impact of vector control on reducing transmission.
Evidence: In 2015, passive surveillance was expanded by equipping health facilities with rapid diagnostic tests (RDTs) and upgrading confirmation and treatment centres [17].
To account for this step-change in passive strategy in the model, it was assumed that this would speed up the detection of both stage 1 and 2 cases, and lead to less underreporting.
In 2015, the new passive strategy was predicted to result in slightly more case reporting than the basic medical strategy and so was not attributed to this particular decline.
Modelling results indicate that 29.6% (95% CI: 5–49%) of this two-year decline was through active screening, and the remaining 70.4% (95% CI: 51–95%) was due to vector control.
436.Rationale: The passage documents that passive surveillance was enhanced in 2015 and that the model accounted for this by assuming increased detection/reporting. However, the authors explicitly state that the new passive strategy was predicted to result in slightly more case reporting and "so was not attributed to this particular decline." The modelling attribution still assigns the majority of the two-year decline to vector control (70.4%). Thus the specific claim that improved passive surveillance "masked the true impact of vector control" is not supported by the passage: the modelling considered the surveillance change and still attributed the majority of the decline to vector control.

436.Option: The addition of tsetse control to the 'screen and treat' strategy was responsible for the majority of the decline in new HAT infections in the Mandoul focus, with 70.4% of the two-year decline attributed to vector control.
Evidence: Modelling results indicate that 29.6% (95% CI: 5–49%) of this two-year decline was through active screening, and the remaining 70.4% (95% CI: 51–95%) was due to vector control.

Mathematical modelling estimates that, without vector control, there would have been 129 (95% CI, 105–157) and 123 (95% CI, 97–150) cases per year in 2014 and 2015 respectively with the reduction due to active screening. In reality only 90 and 47 cases were observed (in 2014 and 2015) when a combined vector and enhanced medical strategy took place;
436.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports model-based attribution of the observed two-year decline: approximately 29.6% to active screening and 70.4% to vector control. The text also contrasts modeled expected case counts without vector control to the observed lower counts after vector control was implemented, supporting the conclusion that adding tsetse control was the major contributor to the decline.

436.Option: The rapid and drastic decrease in catch of tsetse suggests that tiny targets can control G. f. fuscipes in this setting, and this success may be due to the small size of the area, well-defined habitat, and low reinvasion pressure.
Evidence: At the first evaluation post target deployment (T1, 2 months later), only 2 tsetse were caught (0.02 tsetse/trap/day) were observed (Fig 3). The same density was again observed during the second evaluation (T2) but thereafter no tsetse was caught during the next three successive surveys (T3-T6), even with the additional traps and sticky traps deployed to increase the probability of capture.
During the sixth survey (T6, October 2015), trapping duration was extended to six consecutive days and one tsetse was caught. No tsetse were caught during the three surveys conducted in 2016 (T7-T9).
the pre-target deployment indictor (p<10−18, coeff = 5.3 i.e. a predicted 99.5% reduction due to tiny target intervention)
the actual area on which targets were deployed represents 45 km2, corresponding to the gallery surrounding the portion of the Mandoul River where tsetse were found.
Out of the 108 traps, 22 (20.4%) caught tsetse and they were all located on the two main branches of the Mandoul River
436.Rationale: The passage provides strong, direct evidence that tiny targets produced a rapid and dramatic decline in G. f. fuscipes captures (near-elimination of catches and a model-predicted ~99.5% reduction). This supports the first half of the option (tiny targets can control G. f. fuscipes in this setting). However, the latter half of the option offers a causal explanation ('may be due to the small size of the area, well-defined habitat, and low reinvasion pressure') that is not explicitly stated or demonstrated in the passage. While the text documents that tsetse were geographically concentrated (on two river branches) and that targets were deployed on a 45 km2 gallery, it does not provide direct evidence about reinvasion pressure or formally attribute the success to area size or habitat definition. Therefore the speculative causal explanation is not derivable solely from the presented data, so the full option cannot be concluded from the passage.

437.Option: The age-related increase in CCHFV IgG antibody prevalence is likely due to the use of a non-specific ELISA assay that produces false positives in older animals.
Evidence: In addition, an age-related increase in CCHFV IgG antibody prevalence was already also observed in sheep [14] and cattle [13,15,16].
A more detailed statistical analysis revealed a significant effect of age and species on the CCHFV status of the investigated animals, whereas sex had no influence.
The camel-specific in-house CCHFV IgG ELISA was validated using 42 camel sera from Australia as negative controls.

All sera collected after the 14th day post immunization were highly positive indicating that the ELISA is able to detect a specific seroconversion in camelids (Table 2) without any or only minimal reactivity to E. coli antigens itself.
In addition, 12 camel sera originating from German zoos were tested negative (S1 Table).
437.Rationale: The passage documents age-related increases in CCHFV IgG prevalence across multiple species and cites prior studies showing the same pattern; this argues the observation is real rather than an assay artifact. Moreover, the authors describe validation of the camel ELISA (negative controls, immunized alpaca showing specific seroconversion, German zoo negatives), which supports assay specificity. While two Australian control sera showed unexpected positives, the authors treated these as anomalies and validated repeatability. The passage therefore does not support the claim that the age effect is explained by a non-specific ELISA producing false positives in older animals.

437.Option: The higher seroprevalence in older animals is due to increased tick infestation rates in older livestock, which are more susceptible to vector-borne transmission.
Evidence: Nevertheless, most virus infections are caused by bites of infected Hyalomma ticks, which are the main reservoir and transmission vector of CCHFV [4].
Investigations of cattle in Sudan [13] showed that collection site, age, husbandry system and tick infestation are the biggest risk factors for CCHFV seroprevalence.
In addition, an age-related increase in CCHFV IgG antibody prevalence was already also observed in sheep [14] and cattle [13,15,16].
Moreover, we observed that older animals were more likely to be positive than younger animals (Table 3 and Fig 2A).
437.Rationale: The passage explicitly identifies Hyalomma ticks as the main reservoir and transmission vector and cites studies where tick infestation and age were major risk factors for seroprevalence. The observed age-related increase in seropositivity combined with the recognition of tick infestation as a risk factor makes cumulative or higher tick exposure/infestation among older animals a plausible and directly supported epidemiological explanation.

437.Option: The higher seroprevalence of CCHFV IgG antibodies in older animals across all species, combined with the lack of experimental data on antibody persistence, suggests that cumulative exposure to infected ticks over time is the most likely explanation for the observed age-related increase in seroprevalence.
Evidence: In addition, an age-related increase in CCHFV IgG antibody prevalence was already also observed in sheep [14] and cattle [13,15,16].
Moreover, we observed that older animals were more likely to be positive than younger animals (Table 3 and Fig 2A).
Investigations of cattle in Sudan [13] showed that collection site, age, husbandry system and tick infestation are the biggest risk factors for CCHFV seroprevalence.
Since no positive reference field sera existed, a serum of an immunized alpaca was used as reference.
437.Rationale: The passage supports the idea that cumulative exposure to ticks is a likely explanation (age effect + ticks as vector + tick infestation a risk factor). However, the option additionally asserts a 'lack of experimental data on antibody persistence.' The Passage Body does not state or provide evidence that experimental data on antibody persistence are lacking as a general observed fact; it only notes a lack of positive reference field sera for camels specifically. Because the claim about lack of antibody persistence data is not supported by the passage text, the combined statement in this option cannot be fully derived from the Passage Body.

437.Option: The higher seroprevalence of CCHFV IgG antibodies in older animals is primarily due to longer-lasting antibody persistence in cattle and camels compared to sheep and goats.
Evidence: Moreover, we observed that older animals were more likely to be positive than younger animals (Table 3 and Fig 2A).
A more detailed statistical analysis revealed a significant effect of age and species on the CCHFV status of the investigated animals, whereas sex had no influence.
When considering the species, there were no significant seroprevalence differences between goats and sheep nor between cattle and camels.
437.Rationale: The passage reports that cattle and camels had similarly high prevalences and that goats and sheep were similar to each other; it does not provide any data indicating that antibody persistence differs by species or that longer-lasting antibodies in cattle/camels explain the age pattern. The observed pattern is described across multiple species and is more parsimoniously explained by exposure-related factors (e.g., tick exposure) rather than species-specific differences in antibody longevity, which the passage does not document.

437.Option: The observed age-related increase in CCHFV IgG antibody prevalence is mainly driven by differences in slaughter age, as young animals are more likely to be slaughtered before reaching high seroprevalence levels.
Evidence: Samples from the capital region Nouakchott originated from a large abattoir that is connected with one of the most important livestock markets in this region.
Livestock from all over the country were driven up to this market/slaughter house.

For this reason, breeding of cattle less adapted to drought and hot climate is mainly found in the south of the country, while small ruminants and camels are also kept in the northern regions [17,18].
Moreover, we observed that older animals were more likely to be positive than younger animals (Table 3 and Fig 2A).
Investigations of cattle in Sudan [13] showed that collection site, age, husbandry system and tick infestation are the biggest risk factors for CCHFV seroprevalence.
437.Rationale: Although some samples came from an abattoir, the passage gives no evidence that slaughter-age practices are the primary driver of the age-seroprevalence pattern. The authors point to age itself, husbandry system and tick infestation as risk factors and present statistically significant age effects across species. There is no data or statement in the passage indicating that younger animals are preferentially slaughtered before seroconversion in a way that would explain the observed pattern.

438.Option: The political commitment demonstrated by the establishment of the National Program for Blindness Control in 1989 laid the foundation for the long-term success of trachoma elimination in Togo.
Evidence: The organization of trachoma control began within the National Program for Blindness control created in 1989 by the Ministry of Health.

This article reviews the history of trachoma control activities and summarizes the extensive interventions of the Ministry of Health, Public Hygiene and Universal Access to Health Care through the National Programme for Neglected Tropical Diseases to eliminate this neglected tropical disease (NTD) in Togo from its launch in 2006 until the validation of its elimination in 2022.

Togo thus becomes the five African country to be certified by WHO as having reached the milestone of trachoma elimination after Morocco in 2016, Ghana in 2018, Gambia in 2021 and Malawi in 2022 [4,5].
438.Rationale: While the passage states that trachoma control organization began within a National Program for Blindness created in 1989, it does not explicitly state that this political commitment was the key development that enabled meeting international elimination criteria. The passage describes many subsequent activities, surveys, and interventions over 2006–2022; it does not present direct evidence attributing the successful validation specifically to the 1989 program creation. Therefore the causal claim in the option cannot be reliably derived from the passage.

438.Option: The 2017 survey, conducted according to the WHO recommended methodology, revealed that the prevalence of key indicators was below the WHO elimination threshold for trachoma, which allowed Togo to be validated as having eliminated trachoma as a public health problem.
Evidence: The aim of this survey was to determine the extent of trachoma among children aged 1 to 9 years on the one hand, and on the other hand, to estimate the prevalence of TT in people aged 15 years and over according to the Tropical Data protocol recommended by WHO.

The survey carried out in the 7 districts according to the Tropical Data protocol recommended by the WHO revealed a prevalence of TF of less than 1% in all districts among children aged 1 to 9 years and a very low prevalence of TT among 15-year-olds (Table 3).
Togo thus becomes the five African country to be certified by WHO as having reached the milestone of trachoma elimination ... the last of which was eliminated in May 2022 [4].
438.Rationale: The passage reports that a WHO-recommended Tropical Data survey of selected districts found TF prevalence <1% in children 1–9 years and very low TT prevalence in adults — the standard indicators WHO uses for elimination validation. It also documents that Togo was certified as having eliminated trachoma in May 2022. From these statements, it is epidemiologically reasonable to infer that the survey results below WHO thresholds enabled validation. (Although the option specifies '2017', the passage links the Tropical Data survey results and the subsequent validation; the central inference — that WHO-methodology survey showing indicators below thresholds allowed validation — is supported by the text.)

438.Option: The use of the Tropical Data protocol in the 2017 survey allowed for the accurate identification of trichiasis cases, which led to the successful surgical management of all identified patients.
Evidence: The aim of this survey was to determine the extent of trachoma among children aged 1 to 9 years on the one hand, and on the other hand, to estimate the prevalence of TT in people aged 15 years and over according to the Tropical Data protocol recommended by WHO.

The survey carried out in the 7 districts according to the Tropical Data protocol recommended by the WHO revealed a prevalence of TF of less than 1% in all districts among children aged 1 to 9 years and a very low prevalence of TT among 15-year-olds (Table 3).

At the end of the survey, 27 patients were identified in need for surgery. 19 of them were surgered while 6 refused surgery and therefore benefited from epilation of eyelashes. In Tchamba district, one (01) patient diagnosed had died before the surgery period and one (01) other person was absent in Anié district during the surgery period.
438.Rationale: Although the Tropical Data protocol was used and 27 patients in need of surgery were identified, the passage explicitly states that only 19 of the 27 received surgery, 6 refused and had epilation, one died before surgery and one was absent. Therefore the claim that the protocol led to successful surgical management of all identified patients is false and not supported by the passage. The passage also does not provide independent evidence that the protocol uniquely 'allowed for accurate identification' beyond its use.

438.Option: The integration of trachoma screening into annual door-to-door mass treatment campaigns for onchocerciasis, schistosomiasis, and geo-helminthiasis enabled the identification of suspected trichiasis cases across all regions of Togo, including previously unsurveyed districts.
Evidence: Since 2015, the NPNTD has been organizing the Integrated mass treatment with Ivermectin, Albendazole and Praziquantel against onchocerciasis, urinary bilharzia and geo-helminthiases respectively every year.
As part of the mass treatment activities conducted door-to-door in all regions of the country, Community Health Workers were trained to identify suspected cases of trichiasis.
Community Health Workers reported 5665 suspected TT cases during the 2015 mass treatment.

At the end of the 2017 mass treatment, Community Health Workers had notified 588 cases of eyelashes rubbing on the eyes including 13 confirmed cases of TT by senior ophthalmic technicians in 5 health regions (Savanes, Kara, Centrale, Plateaux and Maritime) resulting in a TT prevalence of 2.2%.
438.Rationale: The passage clearly states that annual door-to-door mass treatment campaigns covered all regions and that Community Health Workers identified suspected TT cases during these campaigns, with thousands of suspected cases reported. This supports that integration enabled identification across regions. 440.Option: The higher total provider costs for P. vivax malaria are due to the greater number of outpatient consultations required for its treatment compared to P. falciparum.

440.Rationale: The passage shows that total provider costs are higher for P. vivax and documents that outpatient episodes involve multiple consultations. The higher total costs are explained in the text by the much larger number of P. vivax cases (e.g., 100 outpatient P. vivax vs 20 outpatient P. falciparum) rather than by a difference in number of consultations per case. 442.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that Chagas disease is an NTD that is 'largely underdiagnosed,' quantifies that 'less than 10%... know their infection status' and that 'less than 1%... receives etiological treatment.' From these facts it is epidemiologically reasonable to infer that failure to systematically diagnose infection is a central driver of persistent neglect and that most patients remain undiagnosed and untreated.

442.Option: The use of rapid diagnostic tests (RDTs) in combination could provide diagnostic confirmation of chronic infection at the point of care, potentially improving access to early diagnosis and treatment for vulnerable populations.

442.Rationale: The passage describes RDTs as suitable for point-of-care use and increasing access to diagnosis and treatment, notes PAHO recommendations favoring ICTs for prevalence studies, and states the study specifically modeled 'potential combinations of RDTs for screening and confirmatory diagnosis.' 442.Rationale: The passage explicitly links variable RDT performance across studies to 'genetic variation in T. cruzi' and notes that seven DTUs have different geographical distributions (with TcI most common in Colombia). From an epidemiological perspective, this supports the inference that geographic variation in circulating DTUs could affect RDT performance and therefore limit the generalizability of rapid test results across different endemic regions.

442.Option: The high rate of discordant results between rapid tests and the reference standard, particularly false negatives, suggests that current rapid tests are unreliable for confirming chronic T. cruzi infection in clinical practice.

Using the current diagnostic algorithm for chronic infection in Colombia as a reference standard, the sensitivity of the assessed RDTs ranged from 75.5% to 99.0% (95% CI 70.5–100), while specificity ranged from 70.9% to 100% (95% CI 65.3–100).

442.Rationale: While the passage documents discordance (7.4% of tests) and a predominance of false negatives among discordant results, it also reports that many RDTs demonstrated high sensitivity and specificity (several >90%). The study further aimed to identify best-performing tests and to model combinations for screening/confirmatory use. Therefore, the blanket conclusion that 'current rapid tests are unreliable for confirming chronic T. cruzi infection in clinical practice' is not fully supported by the passage: the evidence shows variability in performance and some notable limitations, but also indicates that several RDTs perform well, and that strategies (e.g., combinations) are being evaluated to address discordance. The passage does not provide sufficient basis to declare RDTs categorically unreliable for clinical confirmation.

all FST values were non-significant, suggesting lack of genetic differentiation between the studied populations (Table 5).

these two non-synonymous mutations found may have an impact on the protein folding and also influence its potential as an epitope.

451.Option: The elevated levels of factors VII, VIII, and IX are a direct result of the venom's ability to stimulate hepatic synthesis of clotting factors in response to systemic inflammation.

Evidence: Russell’s viper venom contains both factor X and factor V activators which trigger the clotting pathway early on, resulting in consumption of multiple clotting factors.

Factors VII, VIII and IX levels were very high prior to antivenom and then dropped dramatically into the normal range or to low levels (FVIII) post-antivenom (Fig 2).

451.Option: The transient elevation of factors VII, VIII, and IX is a consequence of antivenom-induced immune complex formation that temporarily increases factor levels before they decline.

453.Option: The effectiveness of Wolbachia-based arbovirus control might be compromised in ecosystems that experience periods of extreme heat, but given that Wolbachia levels partially recover after temperatures return to normal, any effects may be temporary.

455.Option: Age over 60 years is an independent predictor of platelet recovery within 14 days, as older patients tend to have more robust immune responses and faster recovery during convalescence.

455.Option: Patients with neurological signs or diarrhea at admission are more likely to achieve platelet recovery within two weeks, indicating that these symptoms may reflect a more robust immune response to SFTSV.
Evidence: Univariable Cox regression analysis of the PLT recovery within 14 days identified several factors: stratification of days from onset to admission, baseline stratification of PLT, lymphocytes, PT, age, and Ca2+, as well as phlegm, Neurological signs, and diarrhea (P < 0.1). Evidence: In addition, treatment information collected from cases included the application of antivirals (favipiravir/ribavirin), symptomatic and supportive therapies (antibacterial therapy, antifungal therapy, corticosteroid therapy, recombinant human granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (rhG-CSF) therapy, intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG), continuous renal replacement therapy (CRRT), platelet transfusions [12].
The non-recovery group had a higher application proportion of antifungal therapy (50%), IVIG (30%), and CRRT (10%) (P < 0.05).

Univariable Cox regression analysis of the PLT recovery within 14 days identified several factors: stratification of days from onset to admission, baseline stratification of PLT, lymphocytes, PT, age, and Ca2+, as well as phlegm, Neurological signs, and diarrhea (P < 0.1). These variables were subsequently included in a multivariate analysis, which determined three independent predictors of PLT recovery: stratification of days from onset to admission, baseline PT, and baseline PLT (P < 0.05).
455.Rationale: The passage describes treatments used and notes some therapies were more common in the non-recovery group, but it does not present evidence that antiviral or supportive therapies were independently associated with improved 14-day platelet recovery. Treatment variables were not reported as independent predictors in the multivariate model. Therefore the passage does not support the stated causal claim about antivirals accelerating platelet recovery.

455.Option: SFTS patients with baseline platelet count greater than 90 × 10⁹/L, days from onset to admission of less than 6 days, and baseline PT of less than 13 seconds may have their PLT recovered rapidly during convalescence.
Evidence: Univariable Cox regression analysis of the PLT recovery within 14 days identified several factors: stratification of days from onset to admission, baseline stratification of PLT, lymphocytes, PT, age, and Ca2+, as well as phlegm, Neurological signs, and diarrhea (P < 0.1). These variables were subsequently included in a multivariate analysis, which determined three independent predictors of PLT recovery: stratification of days from onset to admission, baseline PT, and baseline PLT (P < 0.05).

Days from onset to admission greater than 6 days (HR: 0.444, 95%CI: 0.259–0.763, P = 0.003) and PT greater than 13s (HR: 0.547, 95%CI: 0.373–0.800, P = 0.002) were identified as risk factors for PLT recovery, and baseline PLT greater than 90 × 109/L (HR: 7.929, 95%CI: 1.066–58.990, P = 0.043) contributed to PLT recovery (Table 2).
It can be seen that recovery was slowest during the first 6 days of illness and increased rapidly from 7 to 14 days, with most patients achieving a normal platelet count within 14 days (Fig 2A).

patients with a PLT count of less than or equal to 30 × 109/L at baseline had a 14-day probability of achieving a PLT recovery of 20%, compared with 63.0% among those with a PLT count at baseline between 30 × 109/L and 60 × 109/L, 81.0% among those with a baseline PLT count between 60 × 109/L and 90 × 109/L, and 92.0% among those with a PLT count at baseline more than 90 × 109/L (P < 0.001).

The probability of PLT recovery within 14 days, based on the stratification of PT at baseline, with 82% for patients with a baseline PT ≤ 13s and 67% for those with a baseline PT > 13s (P = 0.007) (Fig 2C).

According to the stratification by days from onset to admission, patients admitted within 3 days had a 14-day recovery probability of 80%, compared to 85% for those admitted between 3 and 6 days, and 65% for those admitted after more than 6 days (P = 0.002) (Fig 2D).
455.Rationale: The multivariate analysis identified baseline PLT, baseline PT, and days from onset to admission as independent predictors of 14-day platelet recovery. Specifically, baseline PLT > 90 × 10⁹/L strongly increased the chance of recovery (HR ≈ 7.93), PT > 13s and delay to admission > 6 days were associated with reduced recovery (HRs < 1). Kaplan–Meier estimates also show the highest 14-day recovery probability for PLT > 90 × 10⁹/L (92%), higher recovery for PT ≤ 13s (82% vs 67%), and better recovery when admitted within ≤6 days (80–85% vs 65% when >6 days). Thus the combination stated in the option aligns with the study findings as most strongly associated with rapid platelet recovery.

456.Option: The risk score performed well in discriminating case from control households with an accuracy of 0.78 and a sensitivity and specificity at a point value of 3 of 80% and 60%, respectively, suggesting that it can be used to predict individual-level infection risk with high confidence.
Evidence: Receiver operator curve (ROC) analysis yielded a very good to excellent c statistic of 0.78 (95 percent confidence interval: 0.71–0.84) (Figure 2).
Score predictive ability (C-statistics) was classified as excellent (>0.80), good (0.70–0.79), fair (0.60–0.69), and poor (0.50–0.59).
Sensitivity and specificity measured the proportion of case and control households, respectively, which were correctly identified as such by the risk score.
Table S2 presents the sensitivity, specificity and the estimated proportion of the case and cohort households for each score level.
A case household was defined by occurrence of at least one Leptospira infection event among subject residents of the household during the 3 years of follow-up.
456.Rationale: While the passage provides the AUC (0.78) indicating good discrimination at the household level, it does not provide the specific sensitivity and specificity values claimed for a score value of 3 (those numeric values are not given in the passage; only that Table S2 contains sensitivity/specificity by score). The study outcome and score are defined at the household level (a 'case household' definition), so the claim about predicting individual-level infection risk is not supported by the presented design or data. Finally, an AUC of 0.78 indicates good discrimination but does not by itself justify the phrase 'high confidence' for individual-level prediction; that is an overstatement relative to the reported performance.

456.Option: The presence of un-plastered exterior wall surfaces was significantly associated with case households, and is a direct proxy for rodent infestation, indicating that this feature should be prioritized in rodent control programs.
Evidence: The first model, including variables related to household environment, retained the following characteristics: rodent access to water, domicile built on a slope and un-plastered exterior wall surfaces.
The second model, including rodent infestation variables, retained the presence of R. norvegicus fecal droppings and rodent burrows.

The final model retained four variables: two household environmental variables and two rodent infestation factors (Table 2). R. norvegicus fecal droppings had the strongest association with case households in the final model followed by rodent burrows, rodent access to water and un-plastered exterior wall surface.
456.Rationale: The passage supports that un-plastered exterior wall surfaces were independently associated with case households (it was retained in the models). However, the study distinguishes environmental variables (like un-plastered walls) from direct rodent-infestation indicators (fecal droppings, burrows). The passage does not state that un-plastered walls are a direct proxy for rodent infestation, nor does it provide evidence that this single feature should be prioritized over direct infestation indicators for control programs. Thus the stronger claim that it is a direct proxy and should be prioritized is not supported by the presented evidence.

456.Option: The high household infestation rate, elevated Leptospira prevalence, and long-term carriage of R. norvegicus make it a major reservoir host for leptospires, suggesting that eliminating this species would significantly reduce human transmission in urban slums.
Evidence: In this setting, two rat species, the Norway rat (Rattus norvegicus) and black rat (Rattus rattus), are the main reservoirs for this bacterium and contaminate environments via urinary shedding, providing conditions for transmission to humans [2].
Prior studies in urban areas have shown that Leptospira carriage ranges between 7–82% for R. norvegicus [14], [15] and between 7–34% for R. rattus [16], [17].
However, the Norway rat is far more common within the urban slum environments: nearly 100% of rats trapped in the city of Salvador, Brazil comprised of this species [14], [15], [18].
Leptospira strains isolated from Norway rats were genotypically identical with strains obtained from human patients based on PCR-based typing methods [19].

We detected rodent infestation (presence of at least one rodent sign) in 63 (78%) of the case and 46 (42%) of control households. ... A total of 101 among the 189 households surveyed, had fecal droppings, 77% were from R. norvegicus...

Rodent control programs based on environmental application of a chemical rodenticide [20] as an strategy to reduce the incidence of leptospirosis are costly and their effectiveness has not been evaluated [20], [21].
456.Rationale: The passage provides multiple pieces of evidence that R. norvegicus is a predominant reservoir in this setting (high prevalence among trapped rats, genotypic identity with human strains, and frequent household fecal droppings attributable to R. norvegicus). However, the recommendation that eliminating this species would 'significantly reduce human transmission' is a causal and programmatic conclusion not supported by the study data. The passage explicitly states that rodent-control program effectiveness is costly and has not been evaluated. Therefore, while R. norvegicus is clearly an important reservoir, the effectiveness of elimination as a strategy to reduce human transmission cannot be inferred from the presented evidence.

456.Option: Although our study focused on a single area within Salvador, it would be useful to assess the utility of our measure to predict risk of leptospiral infection in other slum areas, but this will require some tailoring of the survey instrument to some other locations, as every slum has unique characteristics and significant socioeconomic and environmental heterogeneities.
Evidence: This study was conducted in Pau da Lima, a slum community situated in the periphery of Salvador, a city of 2.7 million inhabitants [23] in Northeast Brazil.
The CDC survey form has not been validated for application in slum areas of developing countries.

Herein, by using a community-based cohort study aimed to evaluate Leptospira infection, we describe an environmental and rodent survey instrument, adapted from CDC guidelines, for use in a tropical slum area.

The socioeconomic profile in this site was similar to other slum populations in Brazil: subjects were squatters (85%) who did not complete primary school (77%) and had a median household per capita income of $1.30 per day.
456.Rationale: The tool was developed and evaluated in a single slum community (Pau da Lima) and was an adaptation of the CDC survey form, which the authors note has not been validated for slum settings. Those facts support the inference that assessing the tool's utility in other slums would be useful. The need to adapt instruments for slum contexts is supported by the authors' adaptation of the CDC form for a tropical slum area, which implies that survey content may require tailoring to local conditions. While the passage notes some similarities between this site and other slum populations, the methodological point that further evaluation and likely local tailoring are required is directly supported by the study design and statements in the text.

456.Option: The risk score system we developed, by weighting and combining values for each of these features, accurately classified households into risk groups for Leptospira infection with high precision.
Evidence: To build a risk score, we assigned numerical scores to each of the four independent variables from the final model proportionate to the regression coefficient for each variable (Table 2).
The sum of the number was used to classify each household into ten categories ranging from 0 to 9.
The median risk score for case households was 7, statistically different from the value of 2 for control households (p<0.001).
Receiver operator curve (ROC) analysis yielded a very good to excellent c statistic of 0.78 (95 percent confidence interval: 0.71–0.84) (Figure 2).
Score predictive ability (C-statistics) was classified as excellent (>0.80), good (0.70–0.79), fair (0.60–0.69), and poor (0.50–0.59).
456.Rationale: The passage documents that the authors used standard weighting of regression coefficients to create a multi-item risk score and that the score discriminated between case and control households (median scores 7 vs 2; ROC AUC 0.78). However, characterizing that result as 'accurately... with high precision' overstates the evidence. An AUC of 0.78 indicates good discriminative ability but not necessarily 'high precision.' The authors themselves classify C-statistics >0.80 as 'excellent' and 0.70–0.79 as 'good,' placing 0.78 in the 'good' range. Thus the claim of 'high precision' is not supported by the reported performance metrics.

457.Option: The presence of the same haplotypes dominating the population in multiple fetuses and fetal brains from multiple dams suggests that these variants were likely acquired through random chance rather than selection.
Evidence: The presence of the same haplotypes dominating the population in multiple fetuses and fetal brains from multiple dams suggests these haplotypes may be the result of positive selection rather than purifying selection or genetic drift.
However, if the variants comprising the haplotypes were present in the starting population at high frequencies, it likely would be the result of random chance.

Of the four fetal brains/bodies containing the 3895 single variant haplotype (D-1, D-2, E-1, E-2), this haplotype was present in the majority of sequences from all four of their matched placentas (variant frequency 57–82%; Fig 4B and Tables 4 and 5). Therefore, this haplotype likely was present at a high frequency in the fetus at the time of transmission to the brain, and may have become the dominant variant in the brain simply due to chance.
457.Rationale: The passage explicitly presents the opposite interpretation as plausible: the authors state that the sharing of the same haplotypes across multiple fetuses and dams "suggests these haplotypes may be the result of positive selection rather than purifying selection or genetic drift." They do note a caveat that if those variants were already at high frequency in the starting (placental) population, dominance in the fetus could be due to chance. Thus the passage does not support the blanket inference that the presence of the same haplotypes "likely" reflects random chance; rather it presents both possibilities and specifically highlights positive selection as a plausible explanation. Therefore the option overstates what can be concluded from the data and is not supported as the primary inference by the passage.

457.Option: The observed reduction in ZIKV diversity in both the fetal bodies and fetal brains at the late stages of ZIKV infection could arise by the reduction in ZIKV diversity first occurring in the fetal periphery prior to neuroinvasion, or by the selection of certain ZIKV variants during entry into the brain.
Evidence: ZIKV RNA levels were consistently higher and detected earlier in fetal bodies compared to fetal brains, confirming that ZIKV first enters and replicates in the fetal periphery prior to entering the brain during ZIKV vertical transmission.

Overall, there was a small reduction in ZIKV diversity in the placentas and a sharp reduction of ZIKV diversity in most fetal bodies at this later stage of ZIKV infection. This observation suggests that after viral entry into the fetus from the placenta, selection pressures within the fetus result in the homogenous population.
Like the fetal bodies at the late stage of infection, the ZIKV populations in the fetal brains were largely comprised of homogenous populations.
Of the eight fetal bodies that contained mostly homogenous populations with dominant variants, the matched fetal brains shared the same dominant variants (Tables 4–6).
