The TC83_Cm control (quote 2) is defective in import and remains cytoplasmic even when export is inhibited, demonstrating the distinction. There is no evidence in the passage that SINE compounds block nuclear import of capsid; rather they trap capsid in the nucleus. Additionally, the passage does not present data showing prevention of capsid interaction with host transcription factors as the mechanism for titer reduction. Therefore the asserted mechanism (blocking import and preventing interaction with host transcription factors) is not supported by the presented data.

467.Option: The SINE compounds reduce viral titers by enhancing the host innate immune response through upregulation of interferon-stimulated genes.
Evidence: All four transcripts were strongly induced following TC83 infection (Fig 7A). Cells treated with KPT-185 also displayed increased levels of all four transcripts, with only IFNβ being significantly reduced compared to DMSO treated cells.
These results indicate that ISGs are capable of being induced in the presence of SINE compounds.

These data combined with the altered capsid localization and the observed decrease in viral RNA in extracellular supernatants, indicate that SINE compounds can sequester capsid, removing it from the pool of available structural proteins, leading to decrease in virus assembly and/or release of mature virions.
467.Rationale: While the passage documents that interferon-stimulated genes (ISGs) can be induced in infected cells and that KPT-185-treated infected cells show ISG transcripts (quote 1–2), the authors present stronger, direct experimental evidence that SINE compounds sequester capsid and reduce extracellular viral RNA and released capsid, indicating interference with assembly/release (quote 3). The data do not demonstrate that ISG upregulation is the primary cause of reduced viral titers; ISG induction is described but not shown to be necessary or sufficient for the antiviral effect. Therefore enhancement of innate immunity is not the most strongly supported mechanism by the presented data.

467.Option: The SINE compounds reduce viral titers by directly inhibiting the activity of the viral nonstructural proteins involved in RNA replication.
Evidence: Extracellular viral RNA was reduced upon treatment with Leptomycin B and KPT-185 at 4 and 8 hpi relative to DMSO controls (Fig 3A), with a greater relative reduction at 8 hpi.

In contrast, at 4 hpi, intracellular RNA levels were approximately equal regardless of treatment, with a decrease seen by 8 hpi in both Leptomycin B and KPT-185 treated cells relative to DMSO controls (Fig 3B).
Capsid levels were also unaffected in whole cell lysates from DMSO and KPT-301 treated cells and minimally affected in Leptomycin B or KPT-185 samples (Fig 3C).
KPT-185 was unable to reduce luminescence when cells were infected at a higher MOI (MOI of 5 or 10) and assayed at 8 hpi (Fig 4B).
467.Rationale: Direct inhibition of viral RNA replication by targeting nonstructural proteins would be expected to reduce intracellular viral RNA and early reporter signals. Instead, the data show intracellular RNA is similar at early time points (4 hpi) with reductions in extracellular RNA and released capsid, consistent with a late-stage block (assembly/release) rather than direct inhibition of RNA replication (quotes 1–3). The inability of KPT-185 to reduce luciferase signal at high MOI/early time point (quote 4) further argues against direct inhibition of replication as the primary mechanism. Thus option 3 is not supported by the presented experiments.

467.Option: The SINE compounds reduce viral titers by inducing apoptosis in infected cells, leading to premature cell death and reduced viral production.
Evidence: Four SINE compounds (S1A Fig) were first screened for toxicity in two separate cell lines, treated for 24 hours, and determined to be non-toxic at 2.5 μM (S2 Fig, [39, 40]).
inhibitor treatment also increased cellular viability in infected cells compared to infected cells treated with the vehicle only [18].

These data combined with the altered capsid localization and the observed decrease in viral RNA in extracellular supernatants, indicate that SINE compounds can sequester capsid, removing it from the pool of available structural proteins, leading to decrease in virus assembly and/or release of mature virions.
467.Rationale: The passage reports that SINE compounds were non-toxic at the active concentration used and that inhibitor treatment previously increased cellular viability in infected cells (quotes 1–2), which is inconsistent with induction of apoptosis or premature cell death as the cause of reduced viral output. Instead, multiple lines of evidence point to capsid sequestration and impairment of assembly/release as the mechanism (quote 3). Therefore apoptosis-induced reduction of viral production is not supported by the data.

468.Option: The presence of mGMCSF fusion increases the immune response to the LdPxn1 antigen primarily due to its role as a secretory module that enhances antigen delivery to professional antigen-presenting cells.
Evidence: In addition to its role as a vaccine adjuvant, GMCSF can also be used as a secretory module for vaccines used in intramuscular injection. GMCSF possesses a 17 amino acid leader sequence that mediates secretion of the protein [22].

Western blotting on culture supernatant and lysate of cells transfected with LdPxn1 plasmid DNA confirmed that the vaccine antigens are expressed in mammalian system. Moreover, GMCSF facilitates the secretion of the vaccine antigen.
Although not statistically significant, the presence of mGMCSF fusion appreciably increased the total IgG response as compared to immunization with the antigen in the absence of GMCSF.

GMCSF has been used in the form of recombinant protein or plasmid DNA as an adjuvant in candidate vaccines for malaria [17], HIV [18], mycobacteria [19], Leishmania [20], as well as cancer [21]. These studies have shown that GMCSF significantly increases immunogenicity of the vaccine antigens and protection from the cognate microorganism.
468.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that GMCSF both (a) can act as a secretory module (leader sequence mediates secretion) and (b) facilitates secretion of the vaccine antigen in the CHO expression experiments. It also documents that mGMCSF fusion increased immune responses. However, the claim that the increased immune response is 'primarily due to its role as a secretory module that enhances antigen delivery to professional antigen-presenting cells' asserts a specific primary mechanism that the passage does not establish. The passage also describes GMCSF's immunomodulatory roles (activation/maturation of dendritic cells, recruitment of immune cells) and cites prior work showing its adjuvant activity. Thus while secretion is plausible and documented, the passage does not provide evidence that secretion (antigen delivery to APCs) is the primary mechanism for the enhanced immune response, so the option overstates causality and is not derivable from the text.

468.Option: The protective efficacy of the LdPxn1 vaccine is primarily determined by the magnitude of the humoral immune response, as evidenced by high IgG2a titers and sustained antibody levels after infection.
Evidence: At Week-6 post-immunization, mice that were immunized with plasmids expressing peroxidoxin-1 DNA in the presence or absence of mGMCSF fusion induced significantly higher antigen-specific total IgG response than the respective controls, pcDNA or pcDNA-mGMCSF (p<0.05).
Three weeks after recombinant protein boost (Week-9), a dramatic increase in antibody response was seen in mice primed with the DNA vaccine antigens in the presence or absence of fusion GMCSF.

The LdPxn1-specific antibody response remained high after infection. Mice that received the plasmids expressing LdPxn1 antigen showed high IgG, IgG1 and IgG2a response before infection as well as three and eight weeks after infection. These mice produced a remarkably higher IgG2a response than control mice (Fig. 5C).

In this study, we have demonstrated that immunization of mice with LdPxn1 antigen in the presence of fusion mGMCSF adjuvant in DNA-protein prime-boost strategy induces a strong immune response with significant increase in the frequency of CD4+ T cells that express IFN-γ, TNF-α, and IL-2. More importantly, the antigen induced a significantly higher proportion of multipotent CD4+ T cells that simultaneously express all the three Th-1 cytokines and resulted in a significantly increased protection of mice from L. major challenge infection.
Mice primed with the plasmid expressing LdPxn1 in the presence of mGMCSF fusion DNA adjuvant followed by a booster immunization with rLdPxn1 showed the least footpad swelling (Fig.8).
468.Rationale: The passage documents robust and sustained antibody (including IgG2a) responses after vaccination and infection. However, it also explicitly links protection to induction of Th1-type CD4+ T cells producing IFN-γ, TNF-α, and IL-2 and to a higher proportion of multipotent Th1 CD4+ cells, and describes vaccinated mice with those cellular responses as showing increased protection (smaller footpad lesions). The passage does not present evidence that humoral magnitude (IgG2a titers) is the primary determinant of protection; rather, the data emphasize cell-mediated Th1 responses as associated with protection. Therefore the claim that protective efficacy is primarily determined by humoral magnitude is not supported by the passage.

468.Option: The induction of high levels of IL-10 by antigen-stimulated CD4+ T cells is a key factor in the protective efficacy of the LdPxn1 vaccine, as it enhances macrophage activation and parasite clearance.
Evidence: spleen cells isolated from mice immunized with pcDNA-mGMCSF-LdPxn1 produced significantly more IL-10 than those from control mice (P<0.05).
Unlike Th-1 cytokine response, no clear difference was seen between immunized mice and controls with regard to IL-10 expression.

Like antibody response, cell-mediated response remained high eight weeks after L. major infection (Fig. 6). Antigen-stimulated spleen cells from mice immunized with pcDNA-mGMCSF-LdPx1 produced significantly higher IFN-γ than the control mice (Fig. 6A). Unlike, pre-challenge response, no difference was seen between immunized and control mice regarding IL-10 production (Fig. 6B).
468.Rationale: The passage reports some IL-10 production in certain comparisons, but it does not present IL-10 as a mediator of protection nor link IL-10 to enhanced macrophage activation or parasite clearance. In fact, IL-10 differences are inconsistent (some pre-challenge differences, but no difference post-challenge), and the passage emphasizes Th1 cytokines (IFN-γ, TNF-α, IL-2) in association with protection. There is no statement in the passage that IL-10 enhances macrophage activation or is a key protective factor; thus this causal claim cannot be derived from the provided text.

468.Option: The partial protection observed in mice immunized with LdPxn1 is due to the antigen's ability to induce a balanced Th1/Th2 immune response, as indicated by the presence of both IgG1 and IgG2a antibodies.
Evidence: Like total IgG response, mice primed with plasmids expressing the vaccine antigens developed a significantly higher IgG1 response than the control mice that received pcDNA or pcDNA-mGMCSF (p<0.05).
only mice that were immunized with the plasmid expressing LdPxn1 in the presence of mGMCSF fusion showed significantly higher IgG2a response at Week-6 post immunization (p<0.05).
The LdPxn1-specific antibody response in the control groups appears to be predominantly IgG1.

In this study, we have demonstrated that immunization of mice with LdPxn1 antigen in the presence of fusion mGMCSF adjuvant in DNA-protein prime-boost strategy induces a strong immune response with significant increase in the frequency of CD4+ T cells that express IFN-γ, TNF-α, and IL-2. More importantly, the antigen induced a significantly higher proportion of multipotent CD4+ T cells that simultaneously express all the three Th-1 cytokines and resulted in a significantly increased protection of mice from L. major challenge infection.
468.Rationale: The passage documents induction of both IgG1 and IgG2a antibodies after vaccination, which are often considered Th2- and Th1-associated isotypes respectively. However, the authors emphasize induction of strong Th1-type cellular responses (IFN-γ, TNF-α, IL-2 and multipotent Th1 CD4+ cells) and link these cellular responses to protection. The passage does not present evidence that a 'balanced Th1/Th2' response is the reason for partial protection; rather, it highlights Th1-biased cellular immunity as correlated with protection. Therefore inferring that protection is due to a balanced Th1/Th2 response is not supported by the text.

468.Option: The level of protection conferred by the LdPxn1 antigen is strongly correlated with the induction of a high level expression of IFN-γ, TNF-α, and IL-2 cytokines by antigen-specific CD4+ T cells.
Evidence: In this study, we have demonstrated that immunization of mice with LdPxn1 antigen in the presence of fusion mGMCSF adjuvant in DNA-protein prime-boost strategy induces a strong immune response with significant increase in the frequency of CD4+ T cells that express IFN-γ, TNF-α, and IL-2. More importantly, the antigen induced a significantly higher proportion of multipotent CD4+ T cells that simultaneously express all the three Th-1 cytokines and resulted in a significantly increased protection of mice from L. major challenge infection.

The result clearly shows that immunization with two doses of pcDNA-mGMCSF-LdPxn1 DNA followed by a single booster immunization with rLdPxn1 protein induced significantly higher CD4+ T cell response with high level expression of IFN-γ, TNF-α, and IL-2 (Fig. 4).
Mice primed with the plasmid expressing LdPxn1 in the presence of mGMCSF fusion DNA adjuvant followed by a booster immunization with rLdPxn1 showed the least footpad swelling (Fig.8).

At Week-6 post challenge, mice immunized with plasmid expressing the antigen with or without mGMCSF adjuvant developed significantly smaller lesion than that of the respective control groups (p<0.05). Likewise, at Week-8 post infection, mice that were immunized with pcDNA-mGMCSF-Pxn1 had significantly smaller lesions than those that were immunized with the control DNA, pcDNA-mGMCSF (p<0.05) (Fig. 8).
468.Rationale: The passage directly links the vaccine-induced high-level expression of Th1 cytokines (IFN-γ, TNF-α, IL-2) in antigen-specific CD4+ T cells — including an increased proportion of multipotent Th1 CD4+ cells — with significantly increased protection (reduced footpad swelling and smaller lesions) after L. major challenge. These statements provide a clear basis for inferring a strong correlation between induction of these CD4+ Th1 cytokines and protective efficacy in this study.

469.Option: The trained dog's ability to detect nymphs of T. infestans without prior exposure suggests that the odor recognition stimulus is specific to the species and not shared across Triatominae, as the dog also detected T. sordida and T. guasayana.
Evidence: The trainer used live, laboratory-reared, uninfected male and female adult bugs throughout the training process.

Of the 70 bugs collected, 22 specimens (16 adults and 6 nymphs) corresponded to dark morph (DM) forms of T. infestans ... The remaining bugs comprised 18 specimens of Triatoma guasayana, and 30 specimens of Triatoma sordida (Table 2).

Following the discovery of triatomine colonies in the forested areas of the Chaco we attempted further trials to see if the dog was capable of identifying nymphs and adults independently, and what specific scent the dog was detecting.

The dog was exposed to nymphs, fresh and dry bug faeces, and isobutyric acid, in independent experiments. The dog consistently marked the location of the nymphs, but did not find the fresh or dry faeces on any occassion.
When the dog was exposed to a flask containing isobutyric acid the dog was able to locate the flask, but did not clearly indicate the location as when finding a live triatomine bug.
469.Rationale: The option asserts species-specific odor recognition 'without prior exposure' and that the dog detecting multiple species implies species-specificity. Both claims are not supported by the passage. First, the dog was explicitly trained with live, laboratory-reared adult T. infestans, so it did have prior exposure to T. infestans odor. Second, the dog located not only T. infestans but also T. sordida and T. guasayana in the field; this observation is inconsistent with the inference that the odor recognition stimulus is specific to one species and not shared across Triatominae. The passage also reports the dog detected nymphs and responded differently to isolated compounds (faeces, isobutyric acid), indicating detection is likely based on complex cues from live bugs rather than an odor unique to one species. Therefore the specific claim in the option cannot be derived from the passage.

469.Option: The failure of light traps and manual checks to detect sylvatic T. infestans indicates that these conventional methods are ineffective for any triatomine species, regardless of habitat or life stage.
Evidence: the search for this species using light traps and manual checking of fallen trees and burrows had been unsuccessful.
We report here a novel approach using a trained dog, which has revealed several sylvatic populations of T. infestans in the Paraguayan Chaco.
469.Rationale: The passage states that light traps and manual checks had been unsuccessful in detecting sylvatic T. infestans in this region, and that the trained dog approach revealed sylvatic T. infestans where those methods had failed. However, the option generalizes that conventional methods are ineffective for any triatomine species in any habitat or life stage. The passage does not provide evidence to support such a universal conclusion about all triatomine species, habitats, or life stages; it only documents failure of these conventional methods for detecting sylvatic T. infestans in this setting. Thus the broad generalization in the option is not supported by the provided text.

469.Option: The use of trained dogs to detect triatomines reduces sample collection bias and decreases the time spent searching for sylvatic specimens, which are present in very low densities and dispersed over wide areas.
Evidence: We report here a novel approach using a trained dog, which has revealed several sylvatic populations of T. infestans in the Paraguayan Chaco.
This has enabled us to find sylvatic T. infestans in the region of the Paraguayan Chaco through a quick, easy and low-cost procedure.
the search for this species using light traps and manual checking of fallen trees and burrows had been unsuccessful.

Following the discovery of triatomine colonies in the forested areas of the Chaco we attempted further trials to see if the dog was capable of identifying nymphs and adults independently ... The dog consistently marked the location of the nymphs...
A total of 70 triatomines was collected during 5 field trips with NERO.
The house nearest to capture sites was located 408 meters from the town of 10 Leguas, while the distances from capture sites to the nearest community averaged 2.8±1 km (Figure 1).
469.Rationale: The passage documents that conventional searches (light traps/manual checks) previously failed to detect sylvatic T. infestans, while the trained dog successfully revealed several sylvatic populations using a 'quick, easy and low-cost procedure.' The dog consistently marked nymphs as well as adults, indicating it can detect life stages that might be missed by methods biased toward flying adults (e.g., light traps). The team collected 70 specimens over five field trips, and capture sites were located several kilometers from communities, supporting that sylvatic bugs are dispersed and not concentrated. Taken together, these facts support the inference that the dog method can decrease search time (quick procedure), likely reduce certain sampling biases (detecting nymphs and hidden microhabitats), and is useful for detecting low-density, dispersed sylvatic populations. While the passage does not quantify bias reduction or time savings numerically, the qualitative evidence justifies the epidemiological inference in the option.

470.Option: The detection of a single positive case in a child from a previously treated area suggests that the mass drug administration program in Recife was ineffective in interrupting transmission.
Evidence: Only one child in the municipality of Camaragibe was positive for the presence of circulating filarial antigen (Table 2).
This child was a six-year-old girl from the Nova Descoberta district of the municipality of Recife (a post-MDA area). The child was born in Recife and had moved to Camaragibe with her family in 2014.
her parents had undergone three rounds of MDA while living in Recife.
Recife and Olinda completed three TASs with two-year intervals in 2018, while Jaboatão dos Guararapes will complete theirs in 2020 [1,8].
A total of 17,222 children who were enrolled in the 185 public schools in the nine municipalities were surveyed.
470.Rationale: The passage reports a single antigen-positive child among 17,222 surveyed and notes the child was born in and moved from a post-MDA area where parents had received MDA and TASs were completed. A single antigen-positive result does not, by itself, demonstrate program failure. The data show very low prevalence after interventions (only one positive overall), and the child was amicrofilaremic with negative family investigations, which is inconsistent with ongoing, uncontrolled transmission. Therefore the passage does not provide sufficient evidence to conclude that Recife's MDA program was ineffective in interrupting transmission.

470.Option: The lack of physical and administrative barriers between municipalities means that lymphatic filariasis can be eradicated across the entire Metropolitan Region of Recife through coordinated mass drug administration campaigns.
Evidence: Nine other municipalities (Abreu e Lima, Cabo de Santo Agostinho, Camaragibe, Igarassu, Ilha de Itamaracá, Ipojuca, Itapissuma, Moreno, and São Lourenço da Mata) are part of the Metropolitan Region of Recife and are areas that adjoin the endemic sites [10].
The objective of the Global Program to Eliminate Lymphatic Filariasis (GPELF) is to phase out this endemic disease as a public health problem by 2020.
Evidence in these municipalities is limited to historical data based on case reports [6,11–17]; thus, the epidemiology of LF in these areas is uncertain [18].
470.Rationale: While the passage states that the nine municipalities adjoin endemic sites and references the GPELF objective, it does not describe the existence or absence of physical or administrative barriers, nor does it provide evidence that coordinated MDA across the entire metropolitan region would necessarily achieve eradication. The passage explicitly notes that the epidemiology in these adjacent municipalities is uncertain, which undermines any definitive inference that eradication across the region is guaranteed by coordinated MDA. Therefore this claim cannot be derived from the provided text.

470.Option: The historical presence of microfilaremia and vector infection in the nine municipalities indicates that mass drug administration should be reinstated in these areas to prevent further spread of lymphatic filariasis.
Evidence: The review of the literature identified eight epidemiological surveys performed during the study period. ... in the remaining eight municipalities, there were cases of microfilaremia, corresponding for the prevalence of <1% [11–15].
The vector infection rate was 1.1% in a single survey conducted in the municipality of São Lourenço da Mata in 1967 [12].
In 2015, an investigation of antigens conducted in the municipality of Ipojuca did not identify any cases of LF [16].
Only one child in the municipality of Camaragibe was positive for the presence of circulating filarial antigen (Table 2).
A total of 17,222 children who were enrolled in the 185 public schools in the nine municipalities were surveyed.
470.Rationale: Although historical surveys reported low-prevalence microfilaremia and an old vector infection finding, the contemporary school-based survey of 17,222 children identified only a single antigen-positive child. The passage therefore documents very low current evidence of transmission. The recommendation to reinstate MDA is a programmatic decision that requires additional epidemiological thresholds, risk assessment, and guidance; the passage does not provide direct evidence or criteria that would mandate reinstating MDA in these municipalities. Thus this conclusion cannot be directly derived from the presented data.

470.Option: The use of immunochromatographic card tests (ICTs) in schoolchildren is a reliable method for confirming the elimination of lymphatic filariasis in areas with uncertain endemicity, as demonstrated by the absence of positive cases in the nine municipalities studied.
Evidence: Immunochromatographic card tests (ICTs) were used to test for circulating filarial antigen (BinaxNOW Filariasis, Alere Scarborough, Orlando, United States). For these tests, 100 μL of capillary blood was collected.

We enrolled children aged 6–10 years (compared to those aged 6–7 years recommended for TAS) [22] from all municipal public schools in the urban area to increase the chances of detecting infected individuals.
Only one child in the municipality of Camaragibe was positive for the presence of circulating filarial antigen (Table 2).
The child was amicrofilaremic, and ultrasound examination did not reveal any adult worm nests in the cervical, axillary, or inguinal lymphatic chains.
470.Rationale: The passage describes use of ICTs and reports the survey results, but it does not claim that a single cross-sectional ICT-based school survey is sufficient to 'confirm elimination' in areas of uncertain endemicity. The study used children aged 6–10 (a broader age range than TAS recommends) to increase detection chances, which indicates methodological caution rather than definitive validation. Additionally, elimination validation requires multiple criteria (described elsewhere in the passage) beyond negative ICT results. Therefore the stronger claim that ICTs in this survey reliably confirm elimination is not supported by the text.

470.Option: Even in areas with low endemicity that have stopped mass drug administration, ongoing surveillance is necessary to prevent the risk of disease recrudescence due to migration and the presence of vector breeding sites.
Evidence: Consequently, surveillance and verification of the interruption of transmission started with molecular xenomonitoring (identification of parasite DNA in vector mosquitoes) and thick drop tests in two endemic municipalities of the Metropolitan region of Recife after three TASs were stopped.

Only one child in the municipality of Camaragibe was positive for the presence of circulating filarial antigen (Table 2). This child was a six-year-old girl from the Nova Descoberta district of the municipality of Recife (a post-MDA area). The child was born in Recife and had moved to Camaragibe with her family in 2014.
there were cases of microfilaremia, corresponding for the prevalence of <1% [11–15].
The vector infection rate was 1.1% in a single survey conducted in the municipality of São Lourenço da Mata in 1967 [12].
470.Rationale: The passage documents very low current antigen prevalence but also historical microfilaremia and an earlier vector infection finding. It describes that surveillance and verification efforts (including xenomonitoring) were initiated after TASs stopped. The single positive child was from a post-MDA area and had moved between municipalities, illustrating migration as a potential source of reintroduction. Together, these statements support the epidemiological public health implication that ongoing surveillance is warranted in areas that have ceased MDA to detect and prevent recrudescence driven by human movement and existing vector presence.

471.Option: Despite high levels of knowledge about schistosomiasis transmission, schoolchildren in Mbita Sub-county continue to engage in high-risk behaviors due to the lack of safe water sources and sanitation infrastructure, indicating a critical gap between knowledge and practice.
Evidence: Nearly 60–70% of pupils in 2015 and 2016 correctly answered “contact with contaminated water” as a transmission mode.
In terms of practice, more than 70%, 80%, and 70% of pupils bathed, fetched water and washed clothes/utensils, respectively at Lake Victoria every day or sometimes in both years.
71 (76%) depended on Lake Victoria as a water source for daily school life (Fig 2).
61 of 93 (66%) schools asked pupils to fetch/bring water for use in schools and of those, 53 schools had Lake Victoria as the nearest water source (Fig 3).

In 2016, 3.3% of households had tap water and according to the Health and Demographic Surveillance System (HDSS) initiated by the NUITM-KEMRI project (Institute of Tropical Medicine, Nagasaki University, Japan and Kenya Medical Research Institute, Kenya), there were nine functional boreholes available in the area.

about 85% of the pupils reported that they regularly use toilet while in school, but the figure statistically decreased to about 67% while at home in both years (p = 0.004 in 2015, p < 0.001 in 2016 by χ2 test).
471.Rationale: The passage shows that a substantial proportion of pupils correctly identify contact with contaminated water as a transmission mode (knowledge). Despite this, most pupils frequently bathe, fetch water, and wash in Lake Victoria (high-risk behaviors). The school- and community-level reliance on Lake Victoria for water (71 schools; many pupils asked to fetch lake water) and the very low household tap-water coverage (3.3%) indicate limited availability of safe water sources. The lower toilet use at home versus at school further points to sanitation gaps outside school. These statements together support the inference that there is a gap between knowledge and practice driven by lack of safe water/sanitation infrastructure, undermining preventive strategies.

471.Option: The high rate of toilet use at school compared to at home suggests that community-level sanitation infrastructure is more developed than household-level infrastructure in Mbita Sub-county.
Evidence: about 85% of the pupils reported that they regularly use toilet while in school, but the figure statistically decreased to about 67% while at home in both years (p = 0.004 in 2015, p < 0.001 in 2016 by χ2 test).

In 2016, 3.3% of households had tap water and according to the Health and Demographic Surveillance System (HDSS) initiated by the NUITM-KEMRI project (Institute of Tropical Medicine, Nagasaki University, Japan and Kenya Medical Research Institute, Kenya), there were nine functional boreholes available in the area.
471.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that toilet use is higher in school (≈85%) than at home (≈67%), but it does not provide data about broader community-level sanitation infrastructure (e.g., public latrines, community sewerage) beyond schools and households. The observed difference supports that school sanitation facilities are better used/available than household facilities, but it does not substantiate the broader claim that community-level sanitation infrastructure is more developed than household-level infrastructure. Thus the conclusion overextends the evidence.

471.Option: The contradiction between school water-fetching policies and health education messages indicates that school administrators are unaware of the risks of schistosomiasis transmission through lake water.
Evidence: 61 of 93 (66%) schools asked pupils to fetch/bring water for use in schools and of those, 53 schools had Lake Victoria as the nearest water source (Fig 3).

In terms of prevention, “Using protective boots and gloves when we contact with water from lake”, “avoiding contact with contaminated water” and “using toilet for urination/defecation” were the top three major answers for both years.

Two kinds of textbooks published by different publishers were available in the study area, and their use depended on preference of the school. Both textbooks covered cause, transmission, and prevention.
471.Rationale: The passage documents a factual tension: many schools rely on/pupils fetch lake water while prevention messages include avoiding contact with contaminated water. However, the claim that school administrators are unaware of the risks is a causal explanation not supported by the passage. The text does not report administrators' knowledge or intentions; the reliance on lake water could be due to lack of alternatives or resource constraints rather than unawareness. Therefore the specific inference about administrators' awareness cannot be derived from the provided evidence.

471.Option: The use of school-based health education through science textbooks has been ineffective in improving students' knowledge about schistosomiasis, as evidenced by the high prevalence of misconceptions about transmission and symptoms.
Evidence: Two kinds of textbooks published by different publishers were available in the study area, and their use depended on preference of the school. Both textbooks covered cause, transmission, and prevention. However, one of the textbooks wrongly described that drinking contaminated water is a mode of transmission, and the other missed the symptoms of intestinal schistosomiasis (S1 Table).
Pupils cited schoolteachers (more than 90%) or hospital/health centre (about 80%) as their main sources of information.
Nearly 60–70% of pupils in 2015 and 2016 correctly answered “contact with contaminated water” as a transmission mode.

More than 75% of pupils in both 2015 and 2016 answered that blood in urine and blood in stool were main signs of schistosomiasis. However, more than half of the pupils wrongly chose “blood in urine” as a main sign of dominant schistosomiasis in Mbita (data is available only for 2016).
471.Rationale: The passage states that schistosomiasis is taught via science textbooks and that teachers are the main information source, yet it documents substantive knowledge gaps and misconceptions: only ~60–70% correctly identified contact with contaminated water, textbooks contain errors (one stating drinking contaminated water transmits schistosomiasis; the other omitting intestinal symptoms), and many pupils incorrectly identified the dominant local sign. These findings indicate that school-based education (as implemented via these textbooks and instruction) has not fully achieved accurate student knowledge, supporting the inference of ineffective education in improving correct understanding.

472.Option: The presence of a frame-shift mutation in ML0825c, encoding a zinc-sensing transcriptional regulator, suggests that the recurrent strain in patient 2188 had evolved increased virulence due to functional impairment of the SmtB protein.
Evidence: Another frame-shift arising from a dinucleotide insertion was found in ML0825c, the ortholog of rv2358 in M. tuberculosis that codes for the protein SmtB, a zinc-sensing transcriptional regulator and member of the AsrR/SmtB family.[23, 24].
Modeling of M. leprae StmB in silico (S2 Fig) showed that the frame-shift leads to loss of H138 and should thus impair protein function.
472.Rationale: The passage documents a frame-shift in ML0825c and states that modeling indicates loss of a key residue and likely impairment of SmtB function. However, the passage does not state or provide evidence that this functional impairment resulted in increased virulence. Inferring increased virulence from impaired function is speculative and not supported by the provided text. Therefore the specific claim that the recurrent strain "had evolved increased virulence" cannot be derived from the Passage Body.

472.Option: The extended observation period allowed identification of relapses/reinfection, and whole-genome sequencing enabled differentiation between relapse and reinfection in a field setting with high resolution.
Evidence: During follow-up, four of the treated patients presented with new symptoms between four and eight years after completion of U-MDT and three of these were re-investigated in this study.

In this study, we investigated three recurrent cases of leprosy from the U-MDT/CT-BR trial to determine whether recurrence was due to drug resistance, bacterial persistence or to reinfection. To achieve this, we compared whole genome sequencing analysis of M. leprae collected from skin lesions at the initial diagnosis and during the recurrence of the disease and correlated the sequence data with the clinical, microbiologic and serologic findings.

The recurrent strain 1126–2011 was clearly distinct from the primary strain 1126–2007, and differed in 44 SNPs, 4 InDels and 6 VNTR loci (Fig 1 and S5 Table). Furthermore, 1126–2007 and 1126–2011 share no SNPs that might indicate close relatedness or direct ancestry.

Strains 3208–2007 and 3208–2015 differed in only two SNPs and one VNTR locus (Fig 1 and S5 Table). Both SNPs ... were present in 3208–2015, indicating that 3208–2015 was certainly the direct progeny of 3208–2007.
Analysis of 2188–2007 and 2188–2014 revealed identical genome sequences (Fig 1).
472.Rationale: The passage describes long-term clinical monitoring with new symptoms detected 4–8 years after treatment, and explicitly states the study compared whole-genome sequences from initial and recurrent lesions. The genomic comparisons show clear distinctions in one case (many SNPs) consistent with reinfection and near-identical or direct progeny relationships in others (few or no SNPs) consistent with relapse/persistence. These statements directly support the inference that extended follow-up plus whole-genome sequencing allowed differentiation of relapse versus reinfection with high resolution in this field study.

472.Option: The high antibody titers to PGL-1 and LID-1 antigens during and after treatment suggest that patients remained immunologically responsive to M. leprae, which may have contributed to the recurrence of leprosy.
Evidence: In addition, the first diagnosis revealed that the three MB patients showed high IgM and IgG antibody levels to PGL-1 and LID-1 antigens, respectively (S1 Fig).
Overall, the antibody titers gradually declined but remained above the threshold for positivity for at least one of the antigens during the study period except for patient 1126.
This patient showed an antibody titer below the threshold just before the recurrence of the disease (39 months after U-MDT) and then both antibody titers increased by the time of recurrent disease.
By contrast, despite oscillating levels of PGL-1 antibody for 3208, antibody titers, especially to LID-1, remained high for 3208 and 2188 during the entire study period.
472.Rationale: The Passage Body documents that antibody titers (PGL-1 and LID-1) were high at diagnosis and in many instances remained above positivity thresholds during follow-up, and that titers rose again by the time of recurrence in at least one patient. These quotes support the factual part of the option (high antibody titers and persistence). However, the causal assertion that remaining "immunologically responsive" "may have contributed to the recurrence" is not supported by the passage. The text treats antibody titers as biomarkers used to monitor disease state, not as factors causing recurrence. Therefore the suggested causal contribution to recurrence is speculative and not derivable solely from the Passage Body.

472.Option: The use of a custom-synthesized oligonucleotide array to remove host DNA from libraries improved the sensitivity of whole-genome sequencing in clinical specimens, enabling reliable detection of low-abundance M. leprae strains.
Evidence: To remove host DNA from the libraries, we used a custom-synthesized oligonucleotide array (Agilent) spanning the entire M. leprae genome.[18]

High throughput sequencing is becoming increasingly efficient and cost-effective with purified DNA but is more challenging with clinical specimens such as DNA extracted directly from skin biopsies, especially from formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded (FFPE) samples.
Sufficient whole genome read coverage was obtained from the six M. leprae samples for genotyping and comparative genomic analyses (S4 Table).
472.Rationale: The passage explains that sequencing from clinical (FFPE) specimens is challenging and describes the use of a custom oligonucleotide array to remove host DNA from libraries. It also states that sufficient whole-genome read coverage was obtained for genotyping and comparative analyses from the clinical samples. Together these points support the methodological inference that removing host DNA with the custom array improved the effective sensitivity of sequencing in clinical specimens and enabled reliable detection/analysis of M. leprae genomes that may be present at low abundance in such samples.

473.Option: The high sensitivity of the CareStart G6PD test ensures that all patients with true G6PD deficiency are correctly identified, which supports the safe and effective use of standard daily primaquine in the majority of treated patients.
Evidence: Diagnostic parameters showed 100% sensitivity (4/4; 95%CI 39.8–100.0) and 68.1% specificity (62/91; 95%CI 57.5–77.5), with a prevalence of 4.2% (95%CI 1.2–10.4). Negative and positive predictive values were 100% (95%CI 94.2–100.0) and 12.1% (95%CI 3.4–28.2), respectively.
In this scenario, 29 (87%) false deficient patients out of 33 diagnosed in the RDT missed the chance of radical cure due to low specificity.

Chloroquine for three days plus seven days PQ (0.5mg/kg/day) or weekly primaquine (0.75mg/kg/week for eight weeks) were used for G6PD normal and deficient patients, respectively, as per national guidelines [21].
473.Rationale: While the passage reports 100% sensitivity (quote 1) indicating true G6PD-deficient individuals were detected in the sampled subset, the same data show poor specificity (68.1%) and a large number of false deficient results (29/33) that caused patients to 'miss the chance of radical cure' (quote 2). Because misclassification as deficient changes the prescribed regimen from standard daily primaquine to weekly primaquine (quote 3), the low specificity undermines the claim that the test supports use of standard daily primaquine in the majority. The evidence therefore does not support the full statement in the option.

473.Option: The implementation of the CareStart G6PD test in the Brazilian Amazon has led to improved patient adherence to weekly primaquine regimens due to increased trust in the diagnostic process.
Evidence: Eleven patients said performing the test helped them have greater confidence in the treatment of malaria, since many had a negative experience with the disease (Fig 4).
Most patients (80.5%) understood that the reason for the test was to check resistance to the medication or any type of “allergy”.
473.Rationale: The passage documents increased patient confidence and understanding of the test (quotes 1–2), but it does not present any data on actual adherence to weekly primaquine regimens after implementation. No measures or outcomes of adherence or comparisons before/after implementation are reported. Therefore one cannot infer from the provided evidence that implementation led to improved adherence.

473.Option: The lack of a control line in the CareStart G6PD test prevents the identification of invalid results, which may lead to misinterpretation of test outcomes and compromise the reliability of G6PD screening in field conditions.
Evidence: The test does not have a control line.
Intermediate results cannot be distinguished clearly, and thus faint color changes were also considered deficient.
An invalid result was considered when blood did not run correctly through the testing window.

“I had difficulty only when an unclear color appeared, it was light pink almost white, there was a middle ground, so we were advised that when I had these doubts, it was good to retake the test or seek an opinion from another professional” (HCP 12).

A consensus among HCPs was that the test should be kept with them and not in the laboratory. However, having only one buffer vial per CareStart kit is something that makes it unfeasible, as it is not possible to divide a test kit between the agents.
473.Rationale: The passage explicitly states the test lacks a control line (quote 1) and that intermediate/faint color results are hard to distinguish and were treated as deficient (quote 2). It defines invalid results in operational terms (quote 3) and reports HCPs experienced difficulty interpreting unclear colors (quote 4). These points together support the inference that absence of a control line and ambiguous color readouts can contribute to misinterpretation and reduce reliability in field conditions.

473.Option: Although the CareStart G6PD rapid diagnostic test showed 100% sensitivity, its poor specificity led to a high number of false deficient results, resulting in many patients being incorrectly classified as unable to receive standard daily primaquine treatment.
Evidence: Diagnostic parameters showed 100% sensitivity (4/4; 95%CI 39.8–100.0) and 68.1% specificity (62/91; 95%CI 57.5–77.5), with a prevalence of 4.2% (95%CI 1.2–10.4).
In this scenario, 29 (87%) false deficient patients out of 33 diagnosed in the RDT missed the chance of radical cure due to low specificity.

Chloroquine for three days plus seven days PQ (0.5mg/kg/day) or weekly primaquine (0.75mg/kg/week for eight weeks) were used for G6PD normal and deficient patients, respectively, as per national guidelines [21].
473.Rationale: The passage reports 100% sensitivity but only 68.1% specificity (quote 1). It explicitly quantifies that 29 of 33 RDT-diagnosed deficient patients were false deficient and 'missed the chance of radical cure' (quote 2). Since being labeled G6PD deficient changes treatment to a non-daily (weekly) primaquine regimen (quote 3), the evidence directly supports the option's claim that poor specificity led to many patients being incorrectly classified as unable to receive standard daily primaquine.

473.Option: The use of the CareStart G6PD test in routine malaria treatment units has been shown to significantly reduce hospitalization rates related to PQ-induced hemolysis in the Brazilian Amazon.
Evidence: Although AHA can lead to severe anemia and acute kidney injury [4,5], with an estimated impact on hospitalization costs of US$ 6 million per year to the Brazilian public health budget [6], there is currently no routine G6PDd screening performed in vivax malaria patients prior to treatment in Brazil, where all patients are given standard short-course PQ treatment (0.5 mg/kg/day for 7 days) [7].

The present study aimed at evaluating the routine implementation of the CareStart G6PD test at malaria treatment units (MTUs) of a municipality in the countryside of the Brazilian Amazon, assessing the reliability of interpretation by health care professionals (HCPs), the test performance in the field, and the perception about the test by HCPs and users pre- and post-implementation.
473.Rationale: The passage describes background hospitalization impacts and states the study objectives focused on implementation feasibility, test reliability, field performance, and perceptions (quotes 1–2). It does not report any data or analyses on hospitalization rates related to PQ-induced hemolysis before versus after implementation, nor does it claim reductions in such hospitalizations. Therefore the assertion that use of the test 'has been shown to significantly reduce hospitalization rates' is not supported by the provided evidence.

474.Option: Higher FHP coverage may be associated with increased leprosy transmission due to greater contact between individuals in primary care settings, leading to more frequent exposure and infection.
Evidence: The FHP is a nationwide program, aimed at broadening access to public health services, especially in deprived areas, by offering free community based primary care” [32].
FHP contributes to leprosy control by supporting early detection and treatment of cases, contact tracing, control of disabilities and other preventive measures [34].
Increased access to primary care (PHC) achieved in Brazil mainly by FHP implementation has been shown to increase new case detection rate of leprosy [35].
The analysis shows a significant increase in NCDR of leprosy as FHP coverage increases.
474.Rationale: The passage describes the FHP as community-based primary care whose role is early detection, treatment, contact tracing and prevention. It explicitly links higher FHP coverage to increased detection (NCDR), not to increased transmission. There is no evidence in the passage that primary care contacts increase transmission; rather, the documented mechanism is improved case finding. Therefore the causal claim that higher FHP coverage increases transmission through clinic contact is not supported by the passage.

474.Option: The rise in new case detection rates with higher FHP coverage is likely due to increased reporting bias, as health professionals in well-covered areas are more likely to diagnose and report leprosy cases for performance evaluation purposes.
Evidence: FHP contributes to leprosy control by supporting early detection and treatment of cases, contact tracing, control of disabilities and other preventive measures [34].
Increased access to primary care (PHC) achieved in Brazil mainly by FHP implementation has been shown to increase new case detection rate of leprosy [35].
The analysis shows a significant increase in NCDR of leprosy as FHP coverage increases.

In the adjusted model, compared with the low tertile of FHP coverage, in the medium tertile of FHP coverage (72.03–95.08%) there was an increase of 5% over the period (RR  = 1.05 95% CI  = 1.02–1.09) and for the higher tertile and increase of 12% over the period (RR  = 1.12 95% CI  = 1.08–1.17).
474.Rationale: The passage supports that increased FHP coverage leads to increased detection through improved access and active case-finding; this is consistent with increased reporting/ascertainment. However, the specific assertion that increases are due to 'reporting bias' driven by health professionals diagnosing and reporting for performance evaluation is not mentioned or evidenced in the passage. The text attributes the phenomenon to improved access and detection rather than to performance-motivated reporting, so the more specific claim about reporting bias for performance evaluation cannot be derived from the passage.

474.Option: The increase in new case detection rate of leprosy with higher FHP coverage reflects a failure of the Family Health Program to effectively manage infectious diseases, leading to delayed diagnosis and treatment.
Evidence: FHP contributes to leprosy control by supporting early detection and treatment of cases, contact tracing, control of disabilities and other preventive measures [34].
Increased access to primary care (PHC) achieved in Brazil mainly by FHP implementation has been shown to increase new case detection rate of leprosy [35].
The analysis shows a significant increase in NCDR of leprosy as FHP coverage increases.
474.Rationale: The passage characterizes the FHP as enhancing early detection, treatment and other control measures and explicitly states that FHP implementation has been shown to increase new case detection. There is no evidence in the passage that higher FHP coverage reflects failure of disease management or delayed diagnosis; on the contrary, increased detection is presented as an expected outcome of better primary care access. Thus the claim that the increase indicates program failure is not supported by the passage.

474.Option: The increase in new case detection rate of leprosy associated with higher Family Health Program coverage likely reflects improved case detection rather than a true increase in incidence.
Evidence: FHP contributes to leprosy control by supporting early detection and treatment of cases, contact tracing, control of disabilities and other preventive measures [34].
Increased access to primary care (PHC) achieved in Brazil mainly by FHP implementation has been shown to increase new case detection rate of leprosy [35].
The analysis shows a significant increase in NCDR of leprosy as FHP coverage increases.

In the adjusted model, compared with the low tertile of FHP coverage, in the medium tertile of FHP coverage (72.03–95.08%) there was an increase of 5% over the period (RR  = 1.05 95% CI  = 1.02–1.09) and for the higher tertile and increase of 12% over the period (RR  = 1.12 95% CI  = 1.08–1.17).
474.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the Family Health Program supports early detection and that increased access to primary care via FHP has been shown to increase new case detection. The observed statistical association (increasing NCDR with greater FHP coverage) is therefore most plausibly explained by improved case-finding/ascertainment rather than a true increase in underlying incidence. The passage provides direct textual support for this interpretation.

474.Option: The observed increase in new case detection rate of leprosy with higher FHP coverage may be due to a genuine rise in leprosy incidence caused by increased population mobility and urbanization in Brazilian municipalities.
Evidence: The mean urbanization rate reached 61.3% in 2011.
All selected covariates except urbanization rate were significantly associated with the new case detection rate of leprosy.
Increased access to primary care (PHC) achieved in Brazil mainly by FHP implementation has been shown to increase new case detection rate of leprosy [35].
The analysis shows a significant increase in NCDR of leprosy as FHP coverage increases.
474.Rationale: While the passage notes increasing urbanization, it also reports that urbanization was the only selected covariate not significantly associated with NCDR. Moreover, the authors interpret increasing NCDR with higher FHP coverage as related to improved access and detection rather than rising incidence. There is no evidence presented linking population mobility or urbanization to a genuine increase in leprosy incidence in the studied municipalities. Therefore this causal explanation is not supported by the passage.

475.Option: The fact that only one study demonstrated a significant impact of outdoor fogging on vector populations indicates that space-spraying is ineffective and should be abandoned in dengue control programs.
Evidence: Two subsequent reviews [29, 30] focused on peri-domestic space spraying and concluded that there was no evidence to support its use in dengue outbreak control, either as a standalone intervention or in combination with other interventions.

Outdoor nocturnal ultra-low volume fogging significantly reduced numbers of adult Ae. albopictus in the intervention group by -13.90 (95% CI -21.86, -5.94) (S4 Fig) but did not measure effects on immature stages [74].

The typical response to dengue outbreaks is to target the adult mosquito population by space-spraying or fogging with insecticide, delivered outside or inside the home, with the aim of severely reducing the vector population at the time of delivery.

This method is not designed to deliver persistent insecticide residues on treated surfaces and if the outbreak continues, it must be repeated at intervals that coincide with the vector life cycle [19].
475.Rationale: The passage documents limited and mixed evidence for space-spraying: reviews concluded there is no evidence to support its use, yet at least one study showed a significant reduction in adult Ae. albopictus. The passage does not provide sufficient, consistent RCT-quality evidence to definitively declare space-spraying ineffective and to support the policy recommendation to abandon it. That policy conclusion goes beyond what can be derived from the presented evidence, which instead indicates limited and heterogeneous evidence, not categorical ineffectiveness warranting abandonment.

475.Option: The lack of robust evidence from randomized controlled trials evaluating the effectiveness of vector control interventions, particularly space-spraying, means that the true impact of these methods on dengue transmission remains unknown.
Evidence: None of the included reports that investigated the impact of vector control on dengue incidence were randomised controlled studies.

Two subsequent reviews [29, 30] focused on peri-domestic space spraying and concluded that there was no evidence to support its use in dengue outbreak control, either as a standalone intervention or in combination with other interventions.

Subgroup analyses were planned to explore potential sources of heterogeneity (i.e. effects of seasonality, mosquito species, duration of intervention, coverage), but analyses were not carried out because of the low number of studies available for analysis. For the same reason, sensitivity analyses that excluded studies with a high risk of bias were pre-planned to assess the robustness of results, but were not carried out.
475.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that studies assessing impact on dengue incidence lacked randomized controlled designs, and that reviews found no evidence supporting space-spraying. Additionally, planned analytic approaches to address heterogeneity and bias could not be performed because of the low number and quality of studies. Together, these statements support the inference that the true effect of many widely used vector control methods on dengue transmission remains uncertain.

475.Option: The high number of studies evaluating vector indices rather than dengue incidence reflects a strategic focus on measurable outcomes that are directly linked to disease transmission, indicating a well-aligned research agenda.
Evidence: The primary outcome was dengue incidence (any reported case data; clinical or lab-confirmed/ serologically positive cases); secondary outcomes were a range of vector indices: Breteau Index (BI), House Index (HI), Container Index (CI), tank positivity, number of mosquito adults, pupae per person index (PPPI), presence of Aedes immatures and ovitrap positivity rates.

Nineteen studies reported dengue incidence, 17 studies reported BI, 16 studies reported HI, 11 studies reported CI, 1 study reported tank positivity, 3 studies reported number of mosquito adults, 6 studies reported pupal indices, 3 studies reported ovitrap data.
What are the best currently available dengue vector control tools, as measured by their impact on dengue infections, and not simply on vector populations?
475.Rationale: While the passage documents many studies measuring vector indices, it explicitly highlights concern that interventions should be evaluated by their impact on dengue infections 'and not simply on vector populations.' The text frames the emphasis on vector indices as a limitation rather than evidence of a well-aligned research agenda. Therefore the claim that this focus indicates a strategically well-aligned agenda is not supported by the passage.

475.Option: The significant negative associations found between the use of insecticide aerosols and mosquito coils and higher odds of dengue incidence suggest that these tools may actually increase the risk of infection, possibly due to reduced adoption of more effective preventive measures.
Evidence: Conversely, the use of knockdown sprays [52] (OR 2.03; 95% CI 1.44, 2.86) or mosquito coils [52,69] (OR 1.44; 95% CI 1.09, 1.91; p = 0.01) was significantly associated with an increased odds of dengue incidence.
475.Rationale: The passage reports a statistically significant association between knockdown sprays/mosquito coils and higher odds of dengue incidence. However, it does not provide evidence on causality or mechanisms (for example, that these tools increase risk by displacing more effective measures). The suggested explanation is speculative and not supported by data in the passage, so the causal interpretation cannot be derived from the presented evidence.

475.Option: The absence of randomized controlled trials for space-spraying indicates that the method is too costly or logistically complex to evaluate, rather than a lack of scientific rigor in the research community.
Evidence: Hence, space-spraying and larviciding require trained personnel to deliver potentially toxic insecticides using specialized equipment and are dependent on vertical municipality-driven programs.
None of the included reports that investigated the impact of vector control on dengue incidence were randomised controlled studies.
475.Rationale: The passage notes that space-spraying requires specialized equipment and trained personnel, which could make evaluation complex. However, it does not state that the absence of randomized trials is due to cost or logistical difficulty rather than other reasons (e.g. scientific priorities, ethical considerations, or limited funding). The proposed causal explanation about why RCTs are absent is not supported directly by the passage and thus cannot be inferred with confidence.

476.Option: The inconsistent support of partner programs for NTD control in Ogun State has led to a decline in the effectiveness of MDA programs over time.
Evidence: Ogun State, Nigeria, was selected as the study location due to its high prevalence of STH (19.2%) and SCH (32.2%) and the inconsistent support of partner programs for NTD control.
While MDA is the primary strategy for controlling and eliminating STH and SCH, the treatment has been inconsistent.

Despite significant progress in eliminating SCH and STH, there is a need to investigate the implementation challenges that hinder the uptake, uptake, and coverage of the integrated MDA programs at the community level.
476.Rationale: The passage explicitly notes 'the inconsistent support of partner programs' and that 'the treatment has been inconsistent,' and it calls for investigation of implementation challenges. However, it does not provide evidence that this inconsistent support has produced a measurable decline in MDA effectiveness over time. The statement asserts a causal trend (a decline in effectiveness) that is not demonstrated by the provided text. Therefore the claim cannot be derived from the passage.

476.Option: Despite concerns about side effects and drug characteristics, the majority of participants acknowledged the benefits of the MDA program and expressed willingness to accept the drugs, indicating that perceived benefits outweigh perceived risks for most community members.
Evidence: The community broadly embraced the MDA program.
Through the records, it is of high acceptability, and there has been positive support from community members.

2.2.1 Side Effects Experience. The primary issue raised when asked about the acceptability was the side effects of the drugs, which motivates some mothers to discourage their children from taking the drugs, but a more significant portion of the parents said they have no issues accepting the drugs after considering the benefits they stand to gain from it.
Despite some side effects, the drug is effective and potent, leading to successful outcomes.
476.Rationale: The passage documents both concerns about side effects and drug characteristics and multiple statements indicating high acceptability and broad embrace of the MDA program. The explicit sentence noting that 'a more significant portion of the parents said they have no issues accepting the drugs after considering the benefits' supports the inference that, for most participants, perceived benefits outweigh perceived risks.

476.Option: The use of herbal treatments by community members undermines the credibility of the MDA program and contributes to low drug acceptability.
Evidence: People get treated through herbal treatment (Yoruba herbs) and clinic care.
The herbs, our traditional healers use herbs for it. They cook and drink it without measurement, and as they are drinking it, it will just come out through urine or feces.

I always use the modern method because we have been advised to do so. In addition, locally made drugs do not have dosage, which might even cause more harm to our children than good. Therefore, I believe modern medication is right for these diseases.
The community broadly embraced the MDA program.
Through the records, it is of high acceptability, and there has been positive support from community members.
476.Rationale: The passage confirms that some community members use herbal treatments, but it also documents broad embrace and high acceptability of the MDA program and instances where community members prefer or endorse modern medication. The text does not state that herbal use has undermined the program's credibility or that herbal treatment is a primary contributor to low acceptability. Therefore the strong causal assertion in the option is not supported by the passage.

477.Option: The lack of PCR testing in routine clinical practice at NSW Health Pathology may have contributed to a higher rate of missed Q fever cases in the undiagnosed cohort.
Evidence: PCR is often positive in symptomatic Q fever patients before seroconversion [15] and with this suite of tests, it is unlikely any acute Q fever cases were missed.

Concurrently, the data received on Q fever tests already performed by NSW Health Pathology showed that Phase 1 and Phase 2 IgG and IgM assays (and occasionally IgA) had been done routinely when requested, but not PCR.

From all samples tested four recent Q fever infections were identified (0.74%, 95% CI: 0.29–1.88%) but only one could be ruled in as a missed acute Q fever infection responsible for the current episode of illness, with certainty (1/542, 0.18%, 95% CI: 0.03–1.03%).

Overall, 70 (9.6%, 95% CI: 7.65–11.93%) patients tested positive for acute Q fever in the group that were referred for testing based on clinical suspicion, a number statistically significantly higher than the missed infections during the same period (p<0.0001, Chi square test).
477.Rationale: The passage states that NSW Health Pathology did not routinely perform PCR and that PCR can detect infection before seroconversion; however, the study's comprehensive testing (including PCR) of the undiagnosed cohort found very few missed acute infections (only 1 definite missed case among 542). There is no direct evidence in the passage showing that the routine absence of PCR at NSW Health Pathology causally led to a higher rate of missed Q fever in the undiagnosed cohort. The data show a large difference in detection between clinician-requested testing and the undiagnosed cohort, but do not establish that lack of PCR in routine practice was the contributing factor to missed cases; other factors (e.g., clinicians selecting higher-risk patients for Q fever testing) are also plausible and are discussed in the passage.

477.Option: The absence of significant differences in Q fever detection rates between 2018–2020 and 2021–2023 suggests that the study's findings are not influenced by changes in diagnostic behavior over time.
Evidence: Finally, almost half the samples (352/731, 48.2%) tested for Q fever by NSW Health Pathology were from the period (2021–2023) when we were also collecting samples for missed Q fever testing.

However, NSW Health pathology did not diagnose significantly more acute Q fever cases in 2021–2023 compared to 2016–2020 (p = 0.279, chi square test) or when compared to the 3 years (2018–2020) immediately preceding the start of the study (p = 0.888, chi square test).
477.Rationale: The passage provides statistical comparisons showing no significant increase in diagnosed acute Q fever cases in the later period compared with earlier periods (including 2018–2020). From this, it is reasonable to infer that the study's findings are unlikely to be driven by major changes in diagnostic behavior across those time periods, since detection rates remained statistically similar.

477.Option: The higher proportion of males in the clinically suspected group suggests that men are more likely to develop symptomatic Q fever, leading to increased diagnostic testing in this population.
Evidence: In this cohort, the mean age was 45.9 years (SD: ± 16.7 years, range: 10–70) with 479 (65.5%) males.
Both groups had a similar mean age and geographical distribution, but there was a higher proportion of males in the group that were clinically suspected to have Q fever.

A subgroup analysis of Q fever positive patients (Table 2) showed similar probability of testing positive for acute Q fever regardless of age, sex, calendar year of sampling, and geographical location (except Southern regional postcodes having a higher probability than rest of regional NSW).

Those in contact with domesticated livestock and with wild animals such as farmers, abattoir workers, pig shooters, butchers, wildlife and forestry workers, and veterinarians are at particularly high risk of infection. [2]
477.Rationale: While the passage documents a higher proportion of males among those clinically suspected and notes occupations at higher risk (which may be male-dominated), it does not provide direct evidence that men are intrinsically more likely to develop symptomatic Q fever. Moreover, the subgroup analysis found similar probabilities of testing positive regardless of sex. Therefore, the observed male predominance among clinician-suspected tests could reflect occupational exposure patterns or clinician testing practices rather than a sex-based greater likelihood of symptomatic disease. The causal statement in the option is not supported by the provided data.

477.Option: In this study, Q fever was most likely to be diagnosed via a clinician-initiated request than by non-specific testing of samples from undiagnosed patients with an influenza-like illness.
Evidence: From all samples tested four recent Q fever infections were identified (0.74%, 95% CI: 0.29–1.88%) but only one could be ruled in as a missed acute Q fever infection responsible for the current episode of illness, with certainty (1/542, 0.18%, 95% CI: 0.03–1.03%).

During the corresponding period (2016–2023), samples from 731 patients living in regional NSW had been referred to NSW Health Pathology for Q fever testing based on clinical suspicion ... Overall, 70 (9.6%, 95% CI: 7.65–11.93%) patients tested positive for acute Q fever in the group that were referred for testing based on clinical suspicion, a number statistically significantly higher than the missed infections during the same period (p<0.0001, Chi square test).
477.Rationale: The data show a markedly higher detection rate among samples tested because clinicians suspected Q fever (9.6% positive) compared with the undiagnosed cohort subjected to non-specific retrospective testing (0.18–0.74% positive). The difference is statistically significant, supporting the inference that clinician-initiated testing was far more likely to yield Q fever diagnoses in this study population than broad non-specific testing of undiagnosed ILI samples.

477.Option: The high subclinical to clinical ratio of Q fever suggests that most infections are not detected in routine clinical practice, leading to underestimation of true disease burden in regional populations.
Evidence: Most Q fever infections (60%) are minimally symptomatic or asymptomatic [1].

As Q fever infection is associated with a high subclinical to clinical ratio, [1] this hospitalisation rate suggests a strong bias towards consideration of acute Q fever only in those with severe illness.
Thus, awareness of Q fever infection and its consideration as a differential diagnosis in primary care is critical both for reliable epidemiological estimates and also for individual patient care.
477.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that most infections are minimally symptomatic or asymptomatic and that there is a high subclinical-to-clinical ratio, leading to a bias toward diagnosing only severe cases. It also emphasizes the need for awareness to obtain reliable epidemiological estimates. From these statements, it is reasonable to infer that many infections are not detected in routine clinical practice and that the true burden in regional populations is likely underestimated.

478.Option: The incidence rate of scabies in LTCHs in South Korea has increased, and this rate was significantly higher in hospitals without an infection control department than in hospitals with such a department.
Evidence: The incidence of scabies is increasing annually, and the highest rate was reported in 2018 (32.7%).
There was a difference in the incidence of scabies according to the number of hospital beds (p = 0.005) and the presence of an infection control department (p = 0.012).
Approximately 84% of the hospitals without an infection control department had cases of scabies.
478.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that scabies incidence is increasing annually and cites the highest rate in 2018, supporting the first clause. It further reports a statistically significant difference in incidence by presence of an infection control department (p = 0.012) and provides the specific observation that approximately 84% of hospitals without an infection control department had scabies cases. Together these statements justify the inference that incidence has increased and was significantly higher in hospitals lacking an infection control department.

478.Option: LTCHs with a larger number of beds (200–300) reported fewer scabies incidences than those with 100–200 beds, suggesting that larger facilities have better infection control systems.
Evidence: There was a difference in the incidence of scabies according to the number of hospital beds (p = 0.005).
Regarding the number of hospital beds, 63.3% of the LTCHs with less than 100–200 hospital beds had patients with infection.
In all, 110 LTCHs in 14 of the 17 administrative districts in Korea participated in this study. Among the 110 LTCHs, 60.0% had 100–200 beds, and 80.0% had grade 1 nursing staff.
478.Rationale: While the passage notes a statistically significant difference in scabies incidence by bed number (p = 0.005) and gives the proportion for the 100–200 bed category (63.3% with infections; 60.0% of LTCHs had 100–200 beds), it does not provide the specific incidence for a 200–300 bed category or any direct comparison showing fewer incidences in larger (200–300) facilities. The passage therefore does not support the specific claim that larger facilities (200–300 beds) reported fewer cases or that this implies better infection control; that conclusion cannot be derived from the provided data.

479.Option: The considerable geographic variation in HEV seroprevalence underscores the need for targeted surveillance and intervention programs to make efficient use of limited resources.
Evidence: The three distinct ecological regions of Nepal exhibited variation in seroprevalence. Seroprevalence was significantly lower (P<0.001) in clusters sampled in the flat Terai region in the south of the country at 14.2% (95% CI: 12.7–15.8), compared with the Mountain region in the north of the country at 24.6% (95% CI: 18.8–30.5), and Hilly region in between at 28.9% (95% CI: 26.6–31.2).
Estimates were highest in Kathmandu, reaching 50% in some grid cells, and lower than the national average in the eastern terai ecological region near the border with India at around 10%.

Population-based HEV epidemiologic data are needed to support informed decision making on strengthening the national waterborne disease surveillance system and to generate a comprehensive control plan.
479.Rationale: The passage documents marked geographic heterogeneity in HEV seroprevalence across ecological regions and within the country (e.g., Terai 14.2% vs Hill/Mountain higher; Kathmandu up to 50%). It also explicitly states that population-based epidemiologic data are needed to inform surveillance and control planning. From an epidemiologic and public health resource-allocation perspective, such geographic variation supports the inference that surveillance and interventions should be targeted to high-burden areas to use limited resources efficiently.

479.Option: The absence of a significant association between wealth quintile, water source, and toilet facility with HEV IgG seroprevalence suggests that socioeconomic factors do not play a role in HEV transmission in Nepal.
Evidence: We didn’t find any statistically significant association between wealth quintile, water source, and toilet facility with HEV IgG seroprevalence (Table 1).
As the study was conducted using bio-banked samples from previously conducted cross-sectional survey with limited HEV risk exposure assessment, no attempt for casual inference was done.
479.Rationale: While the authors report no statistically significant association between those measured socioeconomic/water/sanitation variables and HEV IgG seroprevalence in this study, they also note important study limitations (cross-sectional design, limited exposure assessment, and no attempt at causal inference). Absence of an observed association in this study does not justify the stronger conclusion that socioeconomic factors do not play any role in HEV transmission in Nepal. That causal inference is not supported by the study design or the data presented.

479.Option: The geostatistical model used to predict HEV seroprevalence assumes smooth spatial variation, which may mask localized outbreaks and lead to underestimation of risk in high-transmission hotspots such as Kathmandu.
Evidence: We fitted Bayesian geostatistical models to grid cell level seroprevalence data to create a national map of smoothed HEV seroprevalence (Fig 3).

The null model, with spatial random effects only, had reasonable predictive ability with mean absolute error of 9%, a bias of 0.0034 as measured using leave one out cross-validation, and a Pearson’s correlation coefficient of 0.64 between the predicted and true seroprevalence.
Estimates were highest in Kathmandu, reaching 50% in some grid cells, and lower than the national average in the eastern terai ecological region near the border with India at around 10%.
479.Rationale: The passage describes producing a smoothed national map using Bayesian geostatistical models and explicitly reports that the model identified high seroprevalence in Kathmandu (up to 50% in some grid cells) and had reasonable out-of-sample predictive performance. Although smoothing can theoretically obscure very localized variation, the passage does not provide evidence that the modeling approach masked hotspots or underestimated risk; in fact, it reports high estimates for Kathmandu. The claim that the model 'may mask localized outbreaks and lead to underestimation' is speculative and not directly supported by the information in the passage.

479.Option: Children in Nepal are more likely to serorevert to HEV IgG antibodies, which could explain the lower seroprevalence observed in younger age groups and suggests that seroprevalence studies may underestimate the true burden of infection in children.
Evidence: Overall, 20.8% (95% CI: 19.54–22.16) of the individuals tested positive for anti-HEV IgG.
